source.title: Evaluation Portfolio Prioritization Framework
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Purpose:
This document defines a lightweight, repeatable framework for maintaining and prioritizing Redwood's evaluation portfolio across eval-harness runs, prompt-benchmarks, onboarding checks, quantization profiles, and regression triage exercises. The goal is to ensure eval work is aligned to product impact, risk, and operational cost so we run the right tests at the right cadence and react quickly to detected regressions.

Scope:
- Applies to all evaluation activities owned by Applied ML & Evals, Model Onboarding, and Runtime teams.
- Covers both offline (batch) evals and lightweight online/serving simulations.
- Does not replace specific test implementations or labeling processes; it provides a prioritization and scheduling layer.

Key definitions:
- Evaluation asset: any runnable test, prompt set, or benchmark (examples: long-context-robustness suite, prompt-coverage matrix, quant-safety evaluation).
- Portfolio: the set of evaluation assets actively tracked for a model or workload.
- Evaluation cadence: frequency at which an asset should run (daily, weekly, on-push, on-promotion).

Design principles:
1) Outcome-driven: prioritize evals that measure customer-facing failure modes (hallucination, personal data leakage, latency regressions).
2) Cost-aware: account for compute and annotation cost when scheduling heavyweight sims vs. lightweight smoke checks.
3) Traceable: every portfolio decision must include owner, rationale, and acceptance thresholds.
4) Adaptive: dynamically reweight priority based on recent regression signals and product launches.

Portfolio inventory template (maintain as CSV or Confluence table):
- id: short id (e.g., PFB-021)
- name: human-friendly name
- type: prompt-benchmark | simulation | quant | smoke | regression-capture
- owner: team or engineer
- cadence: daily | weekly | on-push | on-promotion | ad-hoc
- cost-estimate: cpu/hours or GPU/hours + labeling cost
- impact: high | medium | low
- critical-thresholds: succinct metric thresholds that constitute a failure

Prioritization matrix (scoring model):
Each asset receives a score (0-100) computed as a weighted sum of four axes:
- Impact (40%): product exposure and user-facing harm if the asset regresses (scale 0-10)
- Risk (25%): likelihood that a deployment step, quantization, or kernel swap will affect this asset (0-10)
- Freshness (15%): how often the underlying prompts/data change or new model variants are introduced (0-10)
- Cost-efficiency (20%): inverse cost normalized to 0-10 (highly efficient = 10)

Scoring guide (example):
- Impact: 9-10 = customer-critical features (chat summaries, billing changes); 6-8 = important UX paths; <=5 = internal diagnostic.
- Risk: 9-10 = model architecture change, new quant profile, or runtime kernel change; <=3 = stable component.
- Freshness: 9-10 = prompt library updated weekly; <=3 = historical/archive tests.
- Cost-efficiency: uses recorded GPU-hours per run; normalize against portfolio median.

Sample prioritization table (human-readable):
| id      | name                                 | impact | risk | freshness | cost-eff | score |
|---------|--------------------------------------|--------|------|----------|---------|-------|
| PFB-021 | Long-context robustness              | 9      | 7    | 8        | 5       | 8.0   |
| PFB-058 | Embedding semantic-stability         | 7      | 4    | 6        | 9       | 6.8   |

Determining cadence from score:
- Score >= 8.0: daily or on-promotion + nightly smoke.
- Score 6.0–7.9: weekly with on-promotion checks.
- Score 4.0–5.9: biweekly/monthly scheduled run.
- Score <4.0: ad-hoc or archive.

Operational procedure: selecting evals for a model promotion
1) Gather candidate assets: model owner submits a promotion checklist that references portfolio ids.
2) Auto-filter: pipeline auto-selects all assets with score >= 6.0 and all assets flagged as "promotion-mandatory".
3) Schedule: heavy simulations run first on shadow infra, smoke checks run in parallel.
4) Decision gates: each asset has a clear assertion (pass/fail) and tolerances; failures produce an evidence bundle (logs + diffed outputs).
5) Triage: failed assets create a ticket assigned to the owner_team indicated in the inventory table and appear in the regression-triage board.

Runtime integration and automation tips:
- Tagging: evaluation assets must include metadata tags: model_family, workload_tag, infra_profile, and cost_bucket. These are used by the scheduler to route runs to dedicated pools or cheaper burst workers.
- Priority overrides: during incidents or security findings, runbook can mark assets with "incident-priority" which temporarily sets cadence to highest.
- Observability: every run emits a run_id, git_sha, model_version, and the evaluation profile used. Store these as traceable artifacts in S3 and index in the eval-metadata store.

Example eval job spec (YAML-like snippet):
---
eval_id: PFB-021
model: redwood-g-bison-v2
profile: long-context-robustness
cadence: daily
infra: gpu-v100-pool
owner: applied-ml/evals
assertions:
  - metric: answer_consistency
    threshold: 0.92
  - metric: latency_p95
    threshold: 650ms
---

Cost-control knobs:
- Split heavy workloads into stratified batches with representative seeds to get early signals before full runs complete.
- Cache shared prefix computations across evals that reuse prompts or datasets.
- Use quant-safety truncated runs for pre-flight checks, followed by full precision only on promotion.

Quality gate and regression escalation flow:
- If an asset with impact >= 8 fails: auto-open a critical regression ticket, notify on #regression-ops, and suspend promotion pipelines for that model version.
- For medium-impact failures: open a ticket and create a canary rollout plan.
- For low-impact failures: create a ticket for next-planned sprint unless repeated failures exceed a rate threshold.

Postmortem and portfolio health review:
- Monthly portfolio review checklist:
  1. Top 10 assets by score — confirm cadence and ownership.
  2. Recent failures and time-to-restore summary.
  3. Cost burn by asset and recommendations to prune or merge low-value tests.
  4. New candidate assets from feature launches or support tickets.

Metrics to monitor (examples):
- Eval coverage: number of active assets per model family.
- Time-to-detect: median time from deployment to first failing eval.
- False-positive rate: proportion of eval failures deemed non-actionable after triage.
- Cost per meaningful-failure: GPU-hours spent per high-impact regression detected.

Ownership and escalation contacts:
- Framework owner: Priya Natarajan (applied-ml/evals)
- On-call triage: regression-triage team (see runbook for rotation)
- Scheduler owner: eng-platform/schedulers

References and related pages:
- See eval-harness/serving-simulation-eval-harness-2026 for simulation configuration examples.
- See model-onboarding/inference-readiness-eval-matrix-and-promotion-playbook-2026 for promotion gates and onboarding checklists.

Appendix A — Minimal JSON evaluation manifest (for CI integration):
{
  \"eval_id\": \"PFB-021\",
  \"model\": \"redwood-g-bison-v2\",
  \"profile\": \"long-context-robustness\",
  \"cadence\": \"daily\",
  \"infra\": \"gpu-v100-pool\",
  \"assertions\": [
    {\"metric\": \"answer_consistency\", \"threshold\": 0.92},
    {\"metric\": \"latency_p95\", \"threshold\": \"650ms\"}
  ]
}

Appendix B — Quick CLI examples:
- Trigger one-off: eval-run --id PFB-021 --model redwood-g-bison-v2 --output s3://eval-artifacts/PFB-021/run-$(date +%s)
- List scheduled cadence: eval-scheduler list --model-family redwood-g

Notes and change log:
- 2025-11-03: initial framework and scoring model documented (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-02-10: added incident-priority override and cost-control recommendations (Marta Lopez).

source.title: Instrumentation Sunset and Cleanup Playbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview
--------
This playbook describes the approved, low-risk process to retire (sunset) unused or harmful instrumentation signals, remove stale dashboards/alerts, and reclaim telemetry budget. It's intended for platform engineers, telemetry owners, and oncall operators. Follow the steps below to minimize alert churn, SLO regressions, and data-loss surprises.

When to use this playbook
-------------------------
- A metric or log signal has no active consumer for >= 90 days.
- An alert fires repeatedly but has no owner or actionability (signal noise).
- A dashboard is irrelevant after a major architecture change (service split, deprecation).
- Telemetry cost budget is exhausted by high-cardinality data that provides low value.

Goals and constraints
---------------------
- Ensure no customer-facing SLOs are degraded by telemetry removal.
- Maintain auditability: every sunset must have an associated ticket and rollback plan.
- Prefer retirement to deletion where possible (reduce retention, disable ingestion, keep archived raw data for 90 days).

Prerequisites and ownership
---------------------------
- Signal owner: The team or person who created/consumes the signal. If unknown, platform team will take temporary ownership and run a discovery window.
- Ticket: Create a tracking ticket in the platform backlog with links to dashboards, alerts, and PRs.
- Canary window: Minimum 14-day discovery/notice window before irreversible deletion.

Key roles and contacts
- Signal owner (required): Accepts responsibility for validation and rollout steps.
- Platform telemetry lead: Coordinates cross-team impact and capacity planning (pager: @oncall_platform).
- Oncall operator: Executes rollback and monitors during the canary window.
- Observability SRE reviewer: Confirms metrics/alerts removal is safe.

Step-by-step process
--------------------
1) Discovery and annotation (days 0–3)
   - Run the telemetry ownership scan: query our metrics registry for signals with zero consumers in the last 90 days.
   - Add annotation in the metric registry: "sunset:proposed" with ticket ID.
   - Post a short note on the platform-team Slack channel with the signal list and the proposed sunset date (14 days out).

2) Impact assessment (days 1–5)
   - For each signal, fill the Impact Table (see template below). Include dashboard references and alerts.
   - Run a dependency trace (prometheus/rbac -> alertmanager routes -> alert pages) to ensure nothing unexpected will break.

3) Canary / Notice window (days 6–20)
   - Lower ingestion rate: if possible switch the signal to lowered sampling or 30-day retention to surface consumers that depend on long-tail data.
   - Convert alerts that use this signal to a non-firing mode: set severity to informational or add a temporary mute with an owner.
   - Monitor for unexpected consumer activity for at least 14 days.
   - If an owner claims the signal, cancel sunset and update docs.

4) Disable ingestion and retention step (days 21–28)
   - Disable producers at source by toggling feature flags or config (preferred). Example: toggle in config-service: 
     - curl -X PATCH https://config.redwood.internal/api/flags/metrics-foo -d '{"enabled": false}'
   - If producers cannot be toggled, add a write-side filter in the ingest pipeline to discard or sample events.
   - Reduce retention in the storage policy to 7 days for archived signals.

5) Dashboard and alert cleanup (days 28–35)
   - Mark dashboards as "deprecated" (add banner and owner field) for 7 more days.
   - Remove alerts using the signal after re-verifying related SLOs; create PRs and reference the ticket.
   - Update dashboard catalogs and alert directories to remove entries.

6) Final deletion or archive (day 36+)
   - If no dependency surfaced, perform final deletion of the timeseries and dashboard objects. Prefer archive-first where the storage system supports it.
   - Record the deletion PR, archive link, and snapshot in the ticket.

Impact Table (template)
-----------------------
| Signal name | Owner | Dashboard refs | Alerts affected | Consumer teams | Risk level | Rollback action |
|-------------|-------|----------------|-----------------|----------------|------------|-----------------|
| metrics/foo_latency | payments-team | payments/latency-dashboard | alert:payments-high-latency | payments, billing | Medium | Re-enable producer flag + backfill |

Risk categories and mitigations
- High: Potential to affect public SLOs. Require manual signoff from SRE and product.
- Medium: Internal tooling or non-critical dashboards. Canary as normal.
- Low: Developer debug-only signals. Remodel to archive after 7 days.

Alert migration checklist (text)
-------------------------------
- Identify alert rules referencing the signal: prometheus rule groups, alertmanager receivers.
- For each alert: change severity to info and add annotation "sunset in progress" for the canary window.
- Create an alternative alert where necessary using fallback signals (document fallback math).
- Add noise suppression policy: not more than 1 alertdedup per hour during migration.

Example Prometheus rule change (before -> canary):
# BEFORE
- alert: FooLatencyHigh
  expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(foo_latency_bucket[5m])) by (le, job)) > 1.2
  for: 5m
# CANARY (temporary)
- alert: FooLatencyHigh_Canary
  expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(foo_latency_bucket[5m])) by (le, job)) > 1.2
  for: 5m
  labels:
    severity: info
  annotations:
    note: "Sunset candidate: watch-only; will be removed if no owner claims it during 14d window"

Rollback playbook (if consumer appears)
-------------------------------------
1) Identify which consumer surfaced and confirm the dependency.
2) Re-enable ingestion at source: flip feature flag or remove filter.
3) Backfill critical data where needed using archived exports (S3 path documented in ticket).
4) Re-enable alerts and dashboards.
5) Update the metric registry to set owner and increase retention.

Backfill and data export guidance
--------------------------------
- Preferred: ask the producer team to replay events from local logs or job archives.
- Platform-level export example (Prometheus snapshot):
  - curl -sS -XPOST "http://prometheus.redwood.internal/api/v1/admin/tsdb/snapshot"
  - Copy snapshot from /var/lib/prometheus/snapshots/<id> to s3://observability-archive/<ticket-id>/

Telemetry cost-saving targets and KPIs
------------------------------------
- Target: reclaim 15% platform telemetry cost each quarter via sunsetting and sampling.
- KPIs to track: ingestion volume (GB/day), number of signals retired, dashboards archived, alert noise reduction (alerts/day).

Post-cleanup retrospective and documentation
------------------------------------------
- Within 21 days of final deletion, the signal owner or platform lead must run a short retrospective covering: what claimed the signal, any rollback steps, time saved, and lessons learned.
- Update the telemetry catalog with the final status and link to archives.

Appendix: Automation recipes and scripts
--------------------------------------
- Ownership scan (example query):
  - TSDB: select distinct(metric_name) from metrics where last_consumer_ts < now() - interval '90 days';
  - PromQL: count_over_time(alerts{alertname!~""}[90d]) == 0 // combined with consumer registry
- Example CI PR template snippet for alert removal:
  - Title: remove alert FooLatencyHigh (sunset ticket #12345)
  - Body: rationale, canary dates, rollback steps, owner signoff, snapshot link.

Contact and escalation

title: Instrumentation Sunset and Cleanup Playbook
preview:
Overview
--------
This playbook describes the approved, low-risk process to retire (sunset) unused or harmful instrumentation signals, remove stale dashboards/alerts, and reclaim telemetry budget. It's intended for platform engineers, telemetry owners, and oncall operators. Follow the steps below to minimize alert churn, SLO regressions, and data-loss surprises.

When to use this playbook
-------------------------
- A metric or log signal has no active consumer for >= 90 days.
- An alert fires repeatedly but has no owner or actionability (signal noise).
- A dashboard is irrelevant after a major architecture change (service split, deprecation).
- Telemetry cost budget is exhausted by high-cardinality data that provides low value.

Goals and constraints
---------------------
- Ensure no customer-facing SLOs are degraded by telemetry removal.
- Maintain auditability: every sunset must have an associated ticket and rollback plan.
- Prefer retirement to deletion where possible (reduce retention, disable ingestion, keep archived raw data for 90 days).

Prerequisites and ownership
---------------------------
- Signal owner: The team or person who created/consumes the signal. If unknown, platform team will take temporary ownership and run a discovery window.
- Ticket: Create a tracking ticket in the platform backlog with links to dashboards, alerts, and PRs.
- Canary window: Minimum 14-day discovery/notice window before irreversible deletion.

Key roles and contacts
- Signal owner (required): Accepts responsibility for validation and rollout steps.
- Platform telemetry lead: Coordinates cross-team impact and capacity planning (pager: @oncall_platform).
- Oncall operator: Executes rollback and monitors during the canary window.
- Observability SRE reviewer: Confirms metrics/alerts removal is safe.

Step-by-step process
--------------------
1) Discovery and annotation (days 0–3)
   - Run the telemetry ownership scan: query our metrics registry for signals with zero consumers in the last 90 days.
   - Add annotation in the metric registry: "sunset:proposed" with ticket ID.
   - Post a short note on the platform-team Slack channel with the signal list and the proposed sunset date (14 days out).

2) Impact assessment (days 1–5)
   - For each signal, fill the Impact Table (see template below). Include dashboard references and alerts.
   - Run a dependency trace (prometheus/rbac -> alertmanager routes -> alert pages) to ensure nothing unexpected will break.

3) Canary / Notice window (days 6–20)
   - Lower ingestion rate: if possible switch the signal to lowered sampling or 30-day retention to surface consumers that depend on long-tail data.
   - Convert alerts that use this signal to a non-firing mode: set severity to informational or add a temporary mute with an owner.
   - Monitor for unexpected consumer activity for at least 14 days.
   - If an owner claims the signal, cancel sunset and update docs.

4) Disable ingestion and retention step (days 21–28)
   - Disable producers at source by toggling feature flags or config (preferred). Example: toggle in config-service: 
     - curl -X PATCH https://config.redwood.internal/api/flags/metrics-foo -d '{"enabled": false}'
   - If producers cannot be toggled, add a write-side filter in the ingest pipeline to discard or sample events.
   - Reduce retention in the storage policy to 7 days for archived signals.

5) Dashboard and alert cleanup (days 28–35)
   - Mark dashboards as "deprecated" (add banner and owner field) for 7 more days.
   - Remove alerts using the signal after re-verifying related SLOs; create PRs and reference the ticket.
   - Update dashboard catalogs and alert directories to remove entries.

6) Final deletion or archive (day 36+)
   - If no dependency surfaced, perform final deletion of the timeseries and dashboard objects. Prefer archive-first where the storage system supports it.
   - Record the deletion PR, archive link, and snapshot in the ticket.

Impact Table (template)
-----------------------
| Signal name | Owner | Dashboard refs | Alerts affected | Consumer teams | Risk level | Rollback action |
|-------------|-------|----------------|-----------------|----------------|------------|-----------------|
| metrics/foo_latency | payments-team | payments/latency-dashboard | alert:payments-high-latency | payments, billing | Medium | Re-enable producer flag + backfill |

Risk categories and mitigations
- High: Potential to affect public SLOs. Require manual signoff from SRE and product.
- Medium: Internal tooling or non-critical dashboards. Canary as normal.
- Low: Developer debug-only signals. Remodel to archive after 7 days.

Alert migration checklist (text)
-------------------------------
- Identify alert rules referencing the signal: prometheus rule groups, alertmanager receivers.
- For each alert: change severity to info and add annotation "sunset in progress" for the canary window.
- Create an alternative alert where necessary using fallback signals (document fallback math).
- Add noise suppression policy: not more than 1 alertdedup per hour during migration.

Example Prometheus rule change (before -> canary):
# BEFORE
- alert: FooLatencyHigh
  expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(foo_latency_bucket[5m])) by (le, job)) > 1.2
  for: 5m
# CANARY (temporary)
- alert: FooLatencyHigh_Canary
  expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(foo_latency_bucket[5m])) by (le, job)) > 1.2
  for: 5m
  labels:
    severity: info
  annotations:
    note: "Sunset candidate: watch-only; will be removed if no owner claims it during 14d window"

Rollback playbook (if consumer appears)
-------------------------------------
1) Identify which consumer surfaced and confirm the dependency.
2) Re-enable ingestion at source: flip feature flag or remove filter.
3) Backfill critical data where needed using archived exports (S3 path documented in ticket).
4) Re-enable alerts and dashboards.
5) Update the metric registry to set owner and increase retention.

Backfill and data export guidance
--------------------------------
- Preferred: ask the producer team to replay events from local logs or job archives.
- Platform-level export example (Prometheus snapshot):
  - curl -sS -XPOST "http://prometheus.redwood.internal/api/v1/admin/tsdb/snapshot"
  - Copy snapshot from /var/lib/prometheus/snapshots/<id> to s3://observability-archive/<ticket-id>/

Telemetry cost-saving targets and KPIs
------------------------------------
- Target: reclaim 15% platform telemetry cost each quarter via sunsetting and sampling.
- KPIs to track: ingestion volume (GB/day), number of signals retired, dashboards archived, alert noise reduction (alerts/day).

Post-cleanup retrospective and documentation
------------------------------------------
- Within 21 days of final deletion, the signal owner or platform lead must run a short retrospective covering: what claimed the signal, any rollback steps, time saved, and lessons learned.
- Update the telemetry catalog with the final status and link to archives.

Appendix: Automation recipes and scripts
--------------------------------------
- Ownership scan (example query):
  - TSDB: select distinct(metric_name) from metrics where last_consumer_ts < now() - interval '90 days';
  - PromQL: count_over_time(alerts{alertname!~""}[90d]) == 0 // combined with consumer registry
- Example CI PR template snippet for alert removal:
  - Title: remove alert FooLatencyHigh (sunset ticket #12345)
  - Body: rationale, canary dates, rollback steps, owner signoff, snapshot link.

Contact and escalation
----------------------
- Platform telemetry channel: #platform-telemetry (primary)
- Pager: @oncall_platform (for production-impact concerns)
- Escalation to Observability SRE: @sre-observability on Slack and email sle@redwood.internal

Document history
----------------
- 2025-11-12: created by Priya Natarajan
- 2025-11-20: added alert migration checklist (reviewed by Alex Rivera)
- 2026-02-10: clarified rollback/backfill guidance and added KPIs (Maya Chen)

source.title: Evaluation Asset Registry and Evaluator Service Contract
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Summary:
This document defines the Evaluation Asset Registry (EAR) — a canonical catalog and lifecycle for evaluation artifacts — and the Evaluator Service Contract (ESC) that runtime and eval components must implement to consume, produce, and promote assets safely across shadow, canary, and production stages. The EAR + ESC are intended to reduce duplicated probe creation, improve reproducibility, and provide clear operational guarantees for model onboarding, quantization, and regression triage workflows.

Goals and non-goals:
- Goals:
  - Provide a single, queryable registry of evaluation assets (probe sets, slice tags, sample annotations, golden outputs, evaluator configs, telemetry fingerprints, quant-compatibility reports).
  - Standardize metadata such that evaluators, CI, and the serving runtime can make deterministic promotion/rollback decisions.
  - Define SLOs and behavioral expectations (the ESC) for asset producers and consumers (latency, freshness, compatibility checks).
- Non-goals:
  - This is not a replacement for the model artifact repository (weights). EAR references model snapshots but does not store binaries.
  - EAR is not itself an annotation UI — it references annotator outputs hosted in the annotation store.

Asset types and minimal metadata contract:
Each asset in the registry must include the following canonical metadata fields. Below we provide the minimal schema and rationale. Implementations should store metadata as JSON-LD-style flat maps; consumer code must treat unknown extra fields as opaque strings.

Required fields (string values):
- asset_id: stable short id (e.g., ea-2026-000142)
- asset_type: one of [probe_set, slice_definition, golden_reference, eval_config, quant_report, telemetry_fingerprint, sample_bundle]
- title: human-friendly short title
- description: 2–6 sentence summary describing provenance and intended use
- author: full name of producer
- owner_team: team responsible for upkeep (applied-ml, runtime, platform, etc.)
- created_at: YYYY-MM-DD
- last_updated: YYYY-MM-DD
- stage: one of [draft, shadow, canary, production, deprecated]
- tags: comma-separated short tags (e.g., longcontext, finance, multilingual)
- privacy_level: one of [public, internal, restricted] — governs who can mount the asset in private deployments
- checksum: SHA256 over the canonical artifact bundle URI (not the metadata)
- canonical_uri: resolvable URI to download or mount the artifact (s3://, gs://, or internal artifact-store path)

Optional but strongly recommended fields:
- seed_samples_count: integer as string (e.g., "128")
- languages: comma-separated ISO codes
- quant_compatibility: [none|soft|verified] — indicates if quant-eval has run against this asset
- evaluation_goals: short enum list (e.g., coherence, safety, latency-sensitivity)
- linked_model_snapshot: model-snap-id(s) that the asset was created against (comma-separated)
- expiration_date: YYYY-MM-DD (if temporary)

Example metadata JSON (stored as a flat string blob in the registry):
{
  "asset_id": "ea-2026-000142",
  "asset_type": "probe_set",
  "title": "longcontext-fewshot-coverage-v2",
  "description": "Stratified few-shot prompt families targeting long-context degradation across English/ES/FR tenants.",
  "author": "Priya Natarajan",
  "owner_team": "applied-ml",
  "created_at": "2026-01-10",
  "last_updated": "2026-01-30",
  "stage": "shadow",
  "tags": "longcontext,fewshot,multilingual",
  "privacy_level": "internal",
  "checksum": "sha256:3a1f...",
  "canonical_uri": "s3://redwood-eval-assets/ea-2026-000142/bundle-v2.tar.gz",
  "seed_samples_count": "512",
  "languages": "en,es,fr",
  "quant_compatibility": "soft",
  "linked_model_snapshot": "snap-ml-2026-0154"
}

Evaluator Service Contract (ESC): runtime and eval components that read or write assets must meet these expectations. The contract is intentionally small and actionable.

Producer obligations (asset writers):
1) Metadata completeness: before promoting an asset to shadow/canary, the producer must supply all required fields and run the automated metadata linter (lint-eval-asset CLI).
2) Compatibility verification: for assets labeled with quant_compatibility != "none", run the QuantCompatibilityRunner (see example CLI) and attach the quant_report asset_id as a linked artifact.
3) Privacy gating: assets with privacy_level == "restricted" must be hashed/DP-redacted upstream; the producer must include a redaction_manifest URI in the metadata.
4) Promotion tests: an automated smoke eval (10k token budget) must complete with success criteria (no runtime exceptions, coverage >= 75% of declared slices) before stage transitions.

Consumer obligations (asset readers):
1) Freshness check: consumers must verify last_updated >= policy_age for the execution context. Default policy_age:
   - production: 30 days
   - canary: 14 days
   - shadow: 90 days
2) Checksum validation: consumer must verify the bundle checksum prior to use and abort on mismatch.
3) Quant fallback behavior: if quant_compatibility == "none" and consumer workload is quantized, the ESC requires either (a) skip asset with a logged skip reason, or (b) run a temporary quant-sim replay and annotate results with uncertainty tags.
4) Observability hooks: consumers must emit the following events to eval-metrics: asset.mount.start, asset.mount.complete, asset.evaluate.start, asset.evaluate.complete, asset.evaluate.failure. Each event MUST include asset_id and stage tags.

Operational SLOs and thresholds (for evaluator infra):
- Asset catalog query latency: p95 < 120ms
- Asset bundle download success rate: >= 99.5% over 24h window
- Smoke eval completion time (10k token test): p95 < 3 minutes in shadow environments
- Checksum validation failure rate: < 0.01% (incidents flagged if > 0.1%)
- Promotion pipeline mean time to promote (draft -> production when automated gates pass): target <= 48 hours (manual review adds headroom)

Promotion workflow (recommended steps):
1) Create asset in draft stage. Run lint-eval-asset and attach quant_report if available.
2) Run automated smoke eval in shadow against linked_model_snapshot. Address any failing slices. Update metadata and increment version in canonical_uri.
3) Promote to canary and schedule a small canary eval (budgeted per-model token cap). Monitor eval-metrics for fidelity deltas and failure modes for 72 hours.
4) If canary passes (no critical fidelity regressions, checksum stable, quant fallback behavior acceptable), update stage=production and notify owner_team. Create a promotion audit record in the registry (promotion_event_id).

Rollback criteria and quick remediation steps:
- Immediate rollback to previous asset version if any of the following happen in production within a 24h window:
  - fidelity delta > 12% on top-priority slices
  - golden-reference mismatch rate > 5% for safety-critical slices
  - sustained asset bundle download failures > 0.5%
Remediation: demote offending asset to deprecated, instruct serving to remount previous production asset, open a regression triage ticket with tags: regression, eval-asset, urgent.

Integration examples and CLI snippets:
- Register an asset (example):
  $ lint-eval-asset validate --path bundle-v1/manifest.json
  $ ear-cli register --metadata bundle-v1/manifest.json --uri s3://redwood-eval-assets/ea-2026-000142/bundle-v1.tar.gz

- Querying by tag and stage (example):
  $ ear-cli search --tag longcontext --stage shadow --owner_team applied-ml --format=json | jq '.[] | {asset_id, title, canonical_uri}'

- Minimal quant compatibility check (example output):
  {"asset_id":"ea-2026-000142","quant_compatibility":"soft","quant_report":"ea-qr-2026-0099","notes":"KV-cache replay shows 2.3% coherence loss at q4; acceptable under soft policy"}

Governance and ownership:
- Registry owner: evaluated by applied-ml infra; operational stewardship lives with the platform team for availability SLOs and applied-ml for content quality.
- Promotion approvers: a promotion to production requires one applied-ml reviewer and one runtime reviewer (can be automated if smoke eval metrics and quant_report pass all gates).

Privacy, compliance, and access controls:
- Assets marked restricted are only mountable in private deployment targets after an access approval workflow. Access must be recorded in the registry audit log with approver and justification.
- Redaction manifests must include hashing/scrubbing algorithms and (if applicable) DP epsilon used.

Onboarding checklist for teams producing assets (quick start):
1) Read the EAR metadata spec and install ear-cli + lint-eval-asset.
2) Prepare canonical bundle with README, manifest.json, and sample index.
3) Run quant runner if the asset targets quantized deployments; attach quant_report.
4) Register artifact in EAR as draft, run shadow smoke eval, iterate until coverage & fidelity targets are met.
5) Promote to canary and observe for 72 hours.

Appendix: compatibility matrix (excerpt described in text)
- When to require quant_report: any asset intended for quantized serving or labeled tags containing any of [kv-cache, longcontext, streaming-functioncall].
- DP/redaction required: assets containing user-generated text, PII, or financial transaction samples.
- Storage lifecycle: production assets are retained for 2 years; shadow assets are retained for 180 days unless explicitly extended.

Frequently asked questions (short):
Q: Can an asset reference multiple model snapshots?
A: Yes — list them in linked_model_snapshot. Consumers should prefer the snapshot matching their runtime.

Q: What if a probe set grows after registration?
A: Create a new version (increment the asset_id version suffix) and register a new canonical_uri. Do not mutate production bundles in-place.

Contact and escalation:
- Principal owner: Priya Natarajan (applied-ml) — priya@redwood.ai
- Platform SRE: Jin Park — jin.park@redwood.ai
- For immediate operational issues open an incident via PagerDuty with service id: eval-asset-registry

This document is intended to be referenced by the eval harness, model onboarding playbooks, quality guardrails, and regression triage runbooks. Implementers should link back to this page when creating or updating assets so that automated pipelines can discover expected lifecycle signals.

  "linked_model_snapshot": "snap-ml-2026-0154"
}

Evaluator Service Contract (ESC): runtime and eval components that read or write assets must meet these expectations. The contract is intentionally small and actionable.

Producer obligations (asset writers):
1) Metadata completeness: before promoting an asset to shadow/canary, the producer must supply all required fields and run the automated metadata linter (lint-eval-asset CLI).
2) Compatibility verification: for assets labeled with quant_compatibility != "none", run the QuantCompatibilityRunner (see example CLI) and attach the quant_report asset_id as a linked artifact.
3) Privacy gating: assets with privacy_level == "restricted" must be hashed/DP-redacted upstream; the producer must include a redaction_manifest URI in the metadata.
4) Promotion tests: an automated smoke eval (10k token budget) must complete with success criteria (no runtime exceptions, coverage >= 75% of declared slices) before stage transitions.

Consumer obligations (asset readers):
1) Freshness check: consumers must verify last_updated >= policy_age for the execution context. Default policy_age:
   - production: 30 days
   - canary: 14 days
   - shadow: 90 days
2) Checksum validation: consumer must verify the bundle checksum prior to use and abort on mismatch.
3) Quant fallback behavior: if quant_compatibility == "none" and consumer workload is quantized, the ESC requires either (a) skip asset with a logged skip reason, or (b) run a temporary quant-sim replay and annotate results with uncertainty tags.
4) Observability hooks: consumers must emit the following events to eval-metrics: asset.mount.start, asset.mount.complete, asset.evaluate.start, asset.evaluate.complete, asset.evaluate.failure. Each event MUST include asset_id and stage tags.

Operational SLOs and thresholds (for evaluator infra):
- Asset catalog query latency: p95 < 120ms
- Asset bundle download success rate: >= 99.5% over 24h window
- Smoke eval completion time (10k token test): p95 < 3 minutes in shadow environments
- Checksum validation failure rate: < 0.01% (incidents flagged if > 0.1%)
- Promotion pipeline mean time to promote (draft -> production when automated gates pass): target <= 48 hours (manual review adds headroom)

Promotion workflow (recommended steps):
1) Create asset in draft stage. Run lint-eval-asset and attach quant_report if available.
2) Run automated smoke eval in shadow against linked_model_snapshot. Address any failing slices. Update metadata and increment version in canonical_uri.
3) Promote to canary and schedule a small canary eval (budgeted per-model token cap). Monitor eval-metrics for fidelity deltas and failure modes for 72 hours.
4) If canary passes (no critical fidelity regressions, checksum stable, quant fallback behavior acceptable), update stage=production and notify owner_team. Create a promotion audit record in the registry (promotion_event_id).

Rollback criteria and quick remediation steps:
- Immediate rollback to previous asset version if any of the following happen in production within a 24h window:
  - fidelity delta > 12% on top-priority slices
  - golden-reference mismatch rate > 5% for safety-critical slices
  - sustained asset bundle download failures > 0.5%
Remediation: demote offending asset to deprecated, instruct serving to remount previous production asset, open a regression triage ticket with tags: regression, eval-asset, urgent.

Integration examples and CLI snippets:
- Register an asset (example):
  $ lint-eval-asset validate --path bundle-v1/manifest.json
  $ ear-cli register --metadata bundle-v1/manifest.json --uri s3://redwood-eval-assets/ea-2026-000142/bundle-v1.tar.gz

- Querying by tag and stage (example):
  $ ear-cli search --tag longcontext --stage shadow --owner_team applied-ml --format=json | jq '.[] | {asset_id, title, canonical_uri}'

- Minimal quant compatibility check (example output):
  {"asset_id":"ea-2026-000142","quant_compatibility":"soft","quant_report":"ea-qr-2026-0099","notes":"KV-cache replay shows 2.3% coherence loss at q4; acceptable under soft policy"}

Governance and ownership:
- Registry owner: evaluated by applied-ml infra; operational stewardship lives with the platform team for availability SLOs and applied-ml for content quality.
- Promotion approvers: a promotion to production requires one applied-ml reviewer and one runtime reviewer (can be automated if smoke eval metrics and quant_report pass all gates).

Privacy, compliance, and access controls:
- Assets marked restricted are only mountable in private deployment targets after an access approval workflow. Access must be recorded in the registry audit log with approver and justification.
- Redaction manifests must include hashing/scrubbing algorithms and (if applicable) DP epsilon used.

Onboarding checklist for teams producing assets (quick start):
1) Read the EAR metadata spec and install ear-cli + lint-eval-asset.
2) Prepare canonical bundle with README, manifest.json, and sample index.
3) Run quant runner if the asset targets quantized deployments; attach quant_report.
4) Register artifact in EAR as draft, run shadow smoke eval, iterate until coverage & fidelity targets are met.
5) Promote to canary and observe for 72 hours.

Appendix: compatibility matrix (excerpt described in text)
- When to require quant_report: any asset intended for quantized serving or labeled tags containing any of [kv-cache, longcontext, streaming-functioncall].
- DP/redaction required: assets containing user-generated text, PII, or financial transaction samples.
- Storage lifecycle: production assets are retained for 2 years; shadow assets are retained for 180 days unless explicitly extended.

Frequently asked questions (short):
Q: Can an asset reference multiple model snapshots?
A: Yes — list them in linked_model_snapshot. Consumers should prefer the snapshot matching their runtime.

Q: What if a probe set grows after registration?
A: Create a new version (increment the asset_id version suffix) and register a new canonical_uri. Do not mutate production bundles in-place.

Contact and escalation:
- Principal owner: Priya Natarajan (applied-ml) — priya@redwood.ai
- Platform SRE: Jin Park — jin.park@redwood.ai
- For immediate operational issues open an incident via PagerDuty with service id: eval-asset-registry

This document is intended to be referenced by the eval harness, model onboarding playbooks, quality guardrails, and regression triage runbooks. Implementers should link back to this page when creating or updating assets so that automated pipelines can discover expected lifecycle signals.
text:
Summary:
This document defines the Evaluation Asset Registry (EAR) — a canonical catalog and lifecycle for evaluation artifacts — and the Evaluator Service Contract (ESC) that runtime and eval components must implement to consume, produce, and promote assets safely across shadow, canary, and production stages. The EAR + ESC are intended to reduce duplicated probe creation, improve reproducibility, and provide clear operational guarantees for model onboarding, quantization, and regression triage workflows.

Goals and non-goals:
- Goals:
  - Provide a single, queryable registry of evaluation assets (probe sets, slice tags, sample annotations, golden outputs, evaluator configs, telemetry fingerprints, quant-compatibility reports).
  - Standardize metadata such that evaluators, CI, and the serving runtime can make deterministic promotion/rollback decisions.
  - Define SLOs and behavioral expectations (the ESC) for asset producers and consumers (latency, freshness, compatibility checks).
- Non-goals:
  - This is not a replacement for the model artifact repository (weights). EAR references model snapshots but does not store binaries.
  - EAR is not itself an annotation UI — it references annotator outputs hosted in the annotation store.

Asset types and minimal metadata contract:
Each asset in the registry must include the following canonical metadata fields. Below we provide the minimal schema and rationale. Implementations should store metadata as JSON-LD-style flat maps; consumer code must treat unknown extra fields as opaque strings.

Required fields (string values):
- asset_id: stable short id (e.g., ea-2026-000142)
- asset_type: one of [probe_set, slice_definition, golden_reference, eval_config, quant_report, telemetry_fingerprint, sample_bundle]
- title: human-friendly short title
- description: 2–6 sentence summary describing provenance and intended use
- author: full name of producer
- owner_team: team responsible for upkeep (applied-ml, runtime, platform, etc.)
- created_at: YYYY-MM-DD
- last_updated: YYYY-MM-DD
- stage: one of [draft, shadow, canary, production, deprecated]
- tags: comma-separated short tags (e.g., longcontext, finance, multilingual)
- privacy_level: one of [public, internal, restricted] — governs who can mount the asset in private deployments
- checksum: SHA256 over the canonical artifact bundle URI (not the metadata)
- canonical_uri: resolvable URI to download or mount the artifact (s3://, gs://, or internal artifact-store path)

Optional but strongly recommended fields:
- seed_samples_count: integer as string (e.g., "128")
- languages: comma-separated ISO codes
- quant_compatibility: [none|soft|verified] — indicates if quant-eval has run against this asset
- evaluation_goals: short enum list (e.g., coherence, safety, latency-sensitivity)
- linked_model_snapshot: model-snap-id(s) that the asset was created against (comma-separated)
- expiration_date: YYYY-MM-DD (if temporary)

Example metadata JSON (stored as a flat string blob in the registry):
{
  "asset_id": "ea-2026-000142",
  "asset_type": "probe_set",
  "title": "longcontext-fewshot-coverage-v2",
  "description": "Stratified few-shot prompt families targeting long-context degradation across English/ES/FR tenants.",
  "author": "Priya Natarajan",
  "owner_team": "applied-ml",
  "created_at": "2026-01-10",
  "last_updated": "2026-01-30",
  "stage": "shadow",
  "tags": "longcontext,fewshot,multilingual",
  "privacy_level": "internal",
  "checksum": "sha256:3a1f...",
  "canonical_uri": "s3://redwood-eval-assets/ea-2026-000142/bundle-v2.tar.gz",
  "seed_samples_count": "512",
  "languages": "en,es,fr",
  "quant_compatibility": "soft",
  "linked_model_snapshot": "snap-ml-2026-0154"
}

4) Promotion tests: an automated smoke eval (10k token budget) must complete with success criteria (no runtime exceptions, coverage >= 75% of declared slices) before stage transitions.

Consumer obligations (asset readers):

   - production: 30 days
   - canary: 14 days
   - shadow: 90 days
2) Checksum validation: consumer must verify the bundle checksum prior to use and abort on mismatch.
3) Quant fallback behavior: if quant_compatibility == "none" and consumer workload is quantized, the ESC requires either (a) skip asset with a logged skip reason, or (b) run a temporary quant-sim replay and annotate results with uncertainty tags.
4) Observability hooks: consumers must emit the following events to eval-metrics: asset.mount.start, asset.mount.complete, asset.evaluate.start, asset.evaluate.complete, asset.evaluate.failure. Each event MUST include asset_id and stage tags.

source.title: Streaming Error Taxonomy and Retry Contract
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview:
This page defines a company-wide error taxonomy and client-facing retry contract specifically for streaming endpoints (server-sent events, HTTP/2/gRPC streaming, and chunked streaming). It supplements existing API compatibility and typed-error guidelines by focusing on streaming-specific failure modes, resume semantics, and SLO-safe retry behavior. The goal is to make client behaviour predictable, reduce unnecessary load from aggressive retries, and provide observability hooks that map streaming errors to operational responses and SLIs.

Audience:
- API owners and backend engineers implementing streaming endpoints
- Client SDK authors and integrators
- SRE and monitoring owners responsible for SLIs/alerting

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to all external-facing streaming endpoints and internal long-lived RPC streams used for inference (chat, generation streams, long-poll embeddings).
- Does NOT replace general HTTP status conventions; it augments them with stream-level semantics, resume tokens, and partial-result handling.

Key principles:
1) Explicit classification: every stream-termination event must be classified using a canonical "stream_error_type" (see table below) and include a machine-readable error payload in the final frame or trailing metadata.
2) Idempotency + resume: where feasible, streams must support resume tokens or sequence checkpoints that allow clients to retry without reprocessing already-delivered tokens.
3) Backpressure-aware retries: retry guidance must be safe for SLOs—clients should use exponential backoff with jitter and obey service-supplied Retry-After or Rate-Hint headers when present.
4) Observability linkage: every error must correlate to a set of telemetry fields (trace id, stream id, shard, kernel-version, model-variant) to enable triage and automated alerts.

Canonical stream error taxonomy (summary table):
| stream_error_type | HTTP/gRPC mapping | Meaning | Client action (default) | SRE severity |
|-------------------|-------------------|---------|-------------------------|--------------|
| TRANSIENT_NETWORK | 502/503/gRPC UNAVAILABLE | Network hiccup; partial frames may be lost | Retry with resume token if present, otherwise backoff & restart | P2 |
| RATE_LIMITED | 429/gRPC RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED | Service is overloaded or rate quota exceeded | Honor Retry-After/Rate-Hint; exponential backoff; consider degrading quality | P2 |
| PARTIAL_RESULT_CANCELLED | 499/gRPC CANCELLED | Client or network closed mid-stream; server stopped delivery | Do not retry automatically; client may resume if token available | P3 |
| MODEL_DEGRADATION | 200 + application error / gRPC OK + payload error | Server returned lower-quality output or switched to a degraded variant | Best-effort retry to higher-quality variant (respecting cost), or surface to user | P3 |
| CHECKPOINT_MISMATCH | 400/gRPC INVALID_ARGUMENT | Resume token invalid or sequence gap detected | Full restart from start; surface error to client | P1 |
| INTERNAL_FT | 500/gRPC INTERNAL | Server runtime failure (kernel crash, OOM) | Retry with exponential backoff; if repeated, fail and surface | P1 |
| AUTHZ_DENIED | 401/403/gRPC PERMISSION_DENIED | Authentication/authorization failure | Do not retry; require token refresh or user action | P1 |
| STREAM_TIMEOUT | 504/gRPC DEADLINE_EXCEEDED | Stream exceeded server-imposed idle or total time | Restart with shorter client-side timeout; do not infinite-repeat | P2 |

Detailed contract for clients:
1) Error payload shape
- All streaming close frames or trailing metadata MUST include a JSON object under the key "x-stream-error" (or gRPC trailing metadata key "x-stream-error-bin" for binary) with this flat schema: {
  "stream_error_type": "<one of canonical types>",
  "message": "human-friendly explanation",
  "resume_token": "<opaque-token>" (optional),
  "retry_after_seconds": "<int>" (optional),
  "request_id": "<internal-request-id>"
}
- Example (HTTP chunked):
  {"x-stream-error": "{\"stream_error_type\": \"TRANSIENT_NETWORK\", \"message\": \"connection reset by peer\", \"resume_token\": \"rt:abc123\", \"request_id\": \"req-2026-01-17-42\"}"}

2) Resume semantics
- Resume token semantics: a resume token encodes the last-delivered sequence position for the stream and is opaque to the client. It MUST be safe to replay the request from that point without duplication of side-effects on the backend (idempotency).
- Server obligations when providing a resume_token:
  a) retain the minimal replay window for at least 2 minutes past token issuance by default (configurable per service)
  b) include in telemetry: resume_token, retention-window, and token-ttl at issuance time
- Client obligations:
  a) if resume_token present, attempt at most 3 resume attempts with exponential backoff (base 200ms, max 5s), unless server sends explicit retry_after_seconds > 0.
  b) if resume fails due to CHECKPOINT_MISMATCH, perform a full restart and surface to user.

3) Retry budget and SLO safety
- Clients must incorporate a retry budget to avoid cascading retries: no more than 5% extra requests per-minute per-caller beyond normal traffic for automated retries. This is a soft limit; platform may return RATE_LIMITED to enforce.
- Internal SRE rule: automated client retries that cause 10% increase in tail latency or 3x increase in queue depth trigger alert and automatic throttling of resume responses.

4) Idempotency and side-effects
- Streaming endpoints that can trigger server-side side-effects must publish whether the stream is "idempotent" in the initial handshake (response header "X-Stream-Idempotent: true/false").
- If not idempotent, clients must avoid blind retries and require explicit user confirmation or server-provided safe-retry markers.

5) Backoff algorithm (recommended)
- Use decorrelated jittered exponential backoff:
  wait = min(max_backoff, base * 2^attempt) * random(0.5, 1.5)
  where base = 200ms, max_backoff = 5000ms, attempts <= 5.
- If server includes retry_after_seconds, add that to the computed wait and respect it as a floor.

6) Quality fallback strategies
- If MODEL_DEGRADATION is signaled, server MAY include the active variant and a suggested lower-cost variant in the error payload. Client options:
  a) surface degraded output with UI hint; do not retry automatically;
  b) call an explicit "regenerate" with higher quality variant if user requests and the client can pay.

Observability and telemetry requirements:
- Every terminal event for a stream must emit metrics and traces with these fields: stream_id, request_id, user_id (if available), model_variant, kernel_build, node_shard, stream_error_type, resume_token_present (bool), duration_ms, bytes_sent, bytes_received.
- SLIs to track (per-service and per-model-variant):
  - streaming_success_rate (target: 99.5% p99 over 1h)
  - resume_success_rate (target: 99% p95 over 24h)
  - incremental_retries_due_to_network (target: <0.5% of requests)
  - tail_stream_latency_p99 (service-specific budgets; default 2.5s for single-response-turn streams)

Testing, verification, and rollout checklist:
1) Unit tests: error payload is emitted and parsable for all close paths.
2) Integration tests: simulate network drop, partial frames, and CHECKPOINT_MISMATCH to verify client behavior in SDK tests.
3) Load tests: measure impact of retries under 2x, 5x failure scenarios and assert tail latency remains within budget when retries occur.
4) Canary rollout: enable streaming error payloads on a narrow user segment (5% traffic) for 72h, monitor resume_success_rate and streaming_success_rate.
5) Gradual expansion: 5% -> 25% -> 75% -> 100% with 24h observation windows and automated rollback on SLO degradation.

Runbook excerpts (for SRE):
- Symptom: large spike in TRANSIENT_NETWORK closures
  1) Query traces for stream_error_type = TRANSIENT_NETWORK and filter by node_shard.
  2) If >5% of streams in last 5m originate from a single shard, evacuate shard and check recent kernel_release changes.
  3) If resume tokens are present but resume_success_rate < 90%, extend token retention window temporarily and eject suspect kernel build.

- Symptom: elevated CHECKPOINT_MISMATCH errors
  1) Identify latest schema or checkpoint encoding changes via artifact release logs.
  2) If change is recent, roll back the release to previous artifact and escalate to runtime team.

Implementation recipes and examples:
- Example streaming close header (HTTP chunked):
  Trailer: x-stream-error
  x-stream-error: {"stream_error_type":"RATE_LIMITED","message":"burst quota exceeded","retry_after_seconds":30,"request_id":"req-2026-01-21-11"}

- Example gRPC trailing metadata (pseudo):
  grpc-status: 8
  x-stream-error-bin: <base64(json-payload)>

- Example client pseudo-code for resume attempt:
  1) onStreamClose(meta):
     if meta.x-stream-error.resume_token:
       for attempt in 1..3:
         wait(randomized_backoff(attempt))
         resp = openStream({resumeToken: meta.resume_token})
         if resp.ok: attach and continue
       surface "Resume failed" and fall back to full restart
     else: full restart or surface to user

Enforcement and governance:
- API gate: all streaming endpoints must implement the x-stream-error payload by Q3 2026. Non-compliant services will be flagged by the nightly API conformance job.
- SDKs: official Redwood SDKs will implement default retry logic conforming to this contract; third-party clients are encouraged but not forced to use SDKs.
- Auditing: Weekly reports will summarize top stream_error_types by service and client platform; Product and SRE will review during the reliability sync.

Ownership and contacts:
- Specification owner: Priya Natarajan (eng/platform)
- Runtime/implementation owner: Runtime Team (eng-serving-runtime) – primary contact: Ethan Park
- Observability owner: Platform SRE – primary contact: Marta Silva
- Rollout coordinator: Product Infrastructure – Samira Ahmed

Appendix: change log
- 2025-09-17: Drafted initial taxonomy and payload schema (Priya).
- 2025-11-10: Added resume token retention and TTL guidance (Ethan).
- 2026-02-08: Finalized SLI targets and rollout checklist; changed base backoff to 200ms (Marta).

References:
- API compatibility and deprecation policy (see related_pages)
- Partial result checkpointing and client resume playbook (see related_pages)

Feedback and revision process:
- Send suggested edits as PRs to the spec repo and include a short impact note. Major changes (affecting client retry behavior or resume semantics) require reviewers from Runtime, SRE, and SDK teams and a two-week review period.

  \"message\": \"human-friendly explanation\",
  \"resume_token\": \"<opaque-token>\" (optional),
  \"retry_after_seconds\": \"<int>\" (optional),
  \"request_id\": \"<internal-request-id>\"
}
- Example (HTTP chunked):
  {\"x-stream-error\": \"{\\\"stream_error_type\\\": \\\"TRANSIENT_NETWORK\\\", \\\"message\\\": \\\"connection reset by peer\\\", \\\"resume_token\\\": \\\"rt:abc123\\\", \\\"request_id\\\": \\\"req-2026-01-17-42\\\"}\"}

2) Resume semantics
- Resume token semantics: a resume token encodes the last-delivered sequence position for the stream and is opaque to the client. It MUST be safe to replay the request from that point without duplication of side-effects on the backend (idempotency).
- Server obligations when providing a resume_token:
  a) retain the minimal replay window for at least 2 minutes past token issuance by default (configurable per service)
  b) include in telemetry: resume_token, retention-window, and token-ttl at issuance time
- Client obligations:
  a) if resume_token present, attempt at most 3 resume attempts with exponential backoff (base 200ms, max 5s), unless server sends explicit retry_after_seconds > 0.
  b) if resume fails due to CHECKPOINT_MISMATCH, perform a full restart and surface to user.

3) Retry budget and SLO safety
- Clients must incorporate a retry budget to avoid cascading retries: no more than 5% extra requests per-minute per-caller beyond normal traffic for automated retries. This is a soft limit; platform may return RATE_LIMITED to enforce.
- Internal SRE rule: automated client retries that cause 10% increase in tail latency or 3x increase in queue depth trigger alert and automatic throttling of resume responses.

4) Idempotency and side-effects
- Streaming endpoints that can trigger server-side side-effects must publish whether the stream is \"idempotent\" in the initial handshake (response header \"X-Stream-Idempotent: true/false\").
- If not idempotent, clients must avoid blind retries and require explicit user confirmation or server-provided safe-retry markers.

5) Backoff algorithm (recommended)
- Use decorrelated jittered exponential backoff:
  wait = min(max_backoff, base * 2^attempt) * random(0.5, 1.5)
  where base = 200ms, max_backoff = 5000ms, attempts <= 5.
- If server includes retry_after_seconds, add that to the computed wait and respect it as a floor.

6) Quality fallback strategies
- If MODEL_DEGRADATION is signaled, server MAY include the active variant and a suggested lower-cost variant in the error payload. Client options:
  a) surface degraded output with UI hint; do not retry automatically;
  b) call an explicit \"regenerate\" with higher quality variant if user requests and the client can pay.

Observability and telemetry requirements:
- Every terminal event for a stream must emit metrics and traces with these fields: stream_id, request_id, user_id (if available), model_variant, kernel_build, node_shard, stream_error_type, resume_token_present (bool), duration_ms, bytes_sent, bytes_received.
- SLIs to track (per-service and per-model-variant):
  - streaming_success_rate (target: 99.5% p99 over 1h)
  - resume_success_rate (target: 99% p95 over 24h)
  - incremental_retries_due_to_network (target: <0.5% of requests)
  - tail_stream_latency_p99 (service-specific budgets; default 2.5s for single-response-turn streams)

Testing, verification, and rollout checklist:
1) Unit tests: error payload is emitted and parsable for all close paths.
2) Integration tests: simulate network drop, partial frames, and CHECKPOINT_MISMATCH to verify client behavior in SDK tests.
3) Load tests: measure impact of retries under 2x, 5x failure scenarios and assert tail latency remains within budget when retries occur.
4) Canary rollout: enable streaming error payloads on a narrow user segment (5% traffic) for 72h, monitor resume_success_rate and streaming_success_rate.
5) Gradual expansion: 5% -> 25% -> 75% -> 100% with 24h observation windows and automated rollback on SLO degradation.

Runbook excerpts (for SRE):
- Symptom: large spike in TRANSIENT_NETWORK closures
  1) Query traces for stream_error_type = TRANSIENT_NETWORK and filter by node_shard.
  2) If >5% of streams in last 5m originate from a single shard, evacuate shard and check recent kernel_release changes.
  3) If resume tokens are present but resume_success_rate < 90%, extend token retention window temporarily and eject suspect kernel build.

- Symptom: elevated CHECKPOINT_MISMATCH errors
  1) Identify latest schema or checkpoint encoding changes via artifact release logs.
  2) If change is recent, roll back the release to previous artifact and escalate to runtime team.

Implementation recipes and examples:
- Example streaming close header (HTTP chunked):
  Trailer: x-stream-error
  x-stream-error: {\"stream_error_type\":\"RATE_LIMITED\",\"message\":\"burst quota exceeded\",\"retry_after_seconds\":30,\"request_id\":\"req-2026-01-21-11\"}

- Example gRPC trailing metadata (pseudo):
  grpc-status: 8
  x-stream-error-bin: <base64(json-payload)>

- Example client pseudo-code for resume attempt:
  1) onStreamClose(meta):
     if meta.x-stream-error.resume_token:
       for attempt in 1..3:
         wait(randomized_backoff(attempt))
         resp = openStream({resumeToken: meta.resume_token})
         if resp.ok: attach and continue
       surface \"Resume failed\" and fall back to full restart
     else: full restart or surface to user

Enforcement and governance:
- API gate: all streaming endpoints must implement the x-stream-error payload by Q3 2026. Non-compliant services will be flagged by the nightly API conformance job.
- SDKs: official Redwood SDKs will implement default retry logic conforming to this contract; third-party clients are encouraged but not forced to use SDKs.
- Auditing: Weekly reports will summarize top stream_error_types by service and client platform; Product and SRE will review during the reliability sync.

Ownership and contacts:
- Specification owner: Priya Natarajan (eng/platform)
- Runtime/implementation owner: Runtime Team (eng-serving-runtime) – primary contact: Ethan Park
- Observability owner: Platform SRE – primary contact: Marta Silva
- Rollout coordinator: Product Infrastructure – Samira Ahmed

Appendix: change log
- 2025-09-17: Drafted initial taxonomy and payload schema (Priya).
- 2025-11-10: Added resume token retention and TTL guidance (Ethan).
- 2026-02-08: Finalized SLI targets and rollout checklist; changed base backoff to 200ms (Marta).

References:
- API compatibility and deprecation policy (see related_pages)
- Partial result checkpointing and client resume playbook (see related_pages)

Feedback and revision process:
- Send suggested edits as PRs to the spec repo and include a short impact note. Major changes (affecting client retry behavior or resume semantics) require reviewers from Runtime, SRE, and SDK teams and a two-week review period.
text:
Overview:
This page defines a company-wide error taxonomy and client-facing retry contract specifically for streaming endpoints (server-sent events, HTTP/2/gRPC streaming, and chunked streaming). It supplements existing API compatibility and typed-error guidelines by focusing on streaming-specific failure modes, resume semantics, and SLO-safe retry behavior. The goal is to make client behaviour predictable, reduce unnecessary load from aggressive retries, and provide observability hooks that map streaming errors to operational responses and SLIs.

Audience:
- API owners and backend engineers implementing streaming endpoints
- Client SDK authors and integrators
- SRE and monitoring owners responsible for SLIs/alerting

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to all external-facing streaming endpoints and internal long-lived RPC streams used for inference (chat, generation streams, long-poll embeddings).
- Does NOT replace general HTTP status conventions; it augments them with stream-level semantics, resume tokens, and partial-result handling.

Key principles:
1) Explicit classification: every stream-termination event must be classified using a canonical \"stream_error_type\" (see table below) and include a machine-readable error payload in the final frame or trailing metadata.
2) Idempotency + resume: where feasible, streams must support resume tokens or sequence checkpoints that allow clients to retry without reprocessing already-delivered tokens.
3) Backpressure-aware retries: retry guidance must be safe for SLOs—clients should use exponential backoff with jitter and obey service-supplied Retry-After or Rate-Hint headers when present.
4) Observability linkage: every error must correlate to a set of telemetry fields (trace id, stream id, shard, kernel-version, model-variant) to enable triage and automated alerts.

Canonical stream error taxonomy (summary table):
| stream_error_type | HTTP/gRPC mapping | Meaning | Client action (default) | SRE severity |
|-------------------|-------------------|---------|-------------------------|--------------|
| TRANSIENT_NETWORK | 502/503/gRPC UNAVAILABLE | Network hiccup; partial frames may be lost | Retry with resume token if present, otherwise backoff & restart | P2 |
| RATE_LIMITED | 429/gRPC RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED | Service is overloaded or rate quota exceeded | Honor Retry-After/Rate-Hint; exponential backoff; consider degrading quality | P2 |
| PARTIAL_RESULT_CANCELLED | 499/gRPC CANCELLED | Client or network closed mid-stream; server stopped delivery | Do not retry automatically; client may resume if token available | P3 |
| MODEL_DEGRADATION | 200 + application error / gRPC OK + payload error | Server returned lower-quality output or switched to a degraded variant | Best-effort retry to higher-quality variant (respecting cost), or surface to user | P3 |
| CHECKPOINT_MISMATCH | 400/gRPC INVALID_ARGUMENT | Resume token invalid or sequence gap detected | Full restart from start; surface error to client | P1 |
| INTERNAL_FT | 500/gRPC INTERNAL | Server runtime failure (kernel crash, OOM) | Retry with exponential backoff; if repeated, fail and surface | P1 |
| AUTHZ_DENIED | 401/403/gRPC PERMISSION_DENIED | Authentication/authorization failure | Do not retry; require token refresh or user action | P1 |
| STREAM_TIMEOUT | 504/gRPC DEADLINE_EXCEEDED | Stream exceeded server-imposed idle or total time | Restart with shorter client-side timeout; do not infinite-repeat | P2 |

Detailed contract for clients:
1) Error payload shape
- All streaming close frames or trailing metadata MUST include a JSON object under the key \"x-stream-error\" (or gRPC trailing metadata key \"x-stream-error-bin\" for binary) with this flat schema: {
  \"stream_error_type\": \"<one of canonical types>\",
  \"message\": \"human-friendly explanation\",
  \"resume_token\": \"<opaque-token>\" (optional),
  \"retry_after_seconds\": \"<int>\" (optional),
  \"request_id\": \"<internal-request-id>\"
}
- Example (HTTP chunked):
  {\"x-stream-error\": \"{\\\"stream_error_type\\\": \\\"TRANSIENT_NETWORK\\\", \\\"message\\\": \\\"connection reset by peer\\\", \\\"resume_token\\\": \\\"rt:abc123\\\", \\\"request_id\\\": \\\"req-2026-01-17-42\\\"}\"}

2) Resume semantics
- Resume token semantics: a resume token encodes the last-delivered sequence position for the stream and is opaque to the client. It MUST be safe to replay the request from that point without duplication of side-effects on the backend (idempotency).
- Server obligations when providing a resume_token:
  a) retain the minimal replay window for at least 2 minutes past token issuance by default (configurable per service)
  b) include in telemetry: resume_token, retention-window, and token-ttl at issuance time
- Client obligations:
  a) if resume_token present, attempt at most 3 resume attempts with exponential backoff (base 200ms, max 5s), unless server sends explicit retry_after_seconds > 0.
  b) if resume fails due to CHECKPOINT_MISMATCH, perform a full restart and surface to user.

3) Retry budget and SLO safety
- Clients must incorporate a retry budget to avoid cascading retries: no more than 5% extra requests per-minute per-caller beyond normal traffic for automated retries. This is a soft limit; platform may return RATE_LIMITED to enforce.
- Internal SRE rule: automated client retries that cause 10% increase in tail latency or 3x increase in queue depth trigger alert and automatic throttling of resume responses.

4) Idempotency and side-effects
- Streaming endpoints that can trigger server-side side-effects must publish whether the stream is \"idempotent\" in the initial handshake (response header \"X-Stream-Idempotent: true/false\").
- If not idempotent, clients must avoid blind retries and require explicit user confirmation or server-provided safe-retry markers.

5) Backoff algorithm (recommended)
- Use decorrelated jittered exponential backoff:
  wait = min(max_backoff, base * 2^attempt) * random(0.5, 1.5)
  where base = 200ms, max_backoff = 5000ms, attempts <= 5.
- If server includes retry_after_seconds, add that to the computed wait and respect it as a floor.

6) Quality fallback strategies
- If MODEL_DEGRADATION is signaled, server MAY include the active variant and a suggested lower-cost variant in the error payload. Client options:
  a) surface degraded output with UI hint; do not retry automatically;
  b) call an explicit \"regenerate\" with higher quality variant if user requests and the client can pay.

Observability and telemetry requirements:
- Every terminal event for a stream must emit metrics and traces with these fields: stream_id, request_id, user_id (if available), model_variant, kernel_build, node_shard, stream_error_type, resume_token_present (bool), duration_ms, bytes_sent, bytes_received.
- SLIs to track (per-service and per-model-variant):
  - streaming_success_rate (target: 99.5% p99 over 1h)
  - resume_success_rate (target: 99% p95 over 24h)
  - incremental_retries_due_to_network (target: <0.5% of requests)
  - tail_stream_latency_p99 (service-specific budgets; default 2.5s for single-response-turn streams)

Testing, verification, and rollout checklist:
1) Unit tests: error payload is emitted and parsable for all close paths.
2) Integration tests: simulate network drop, partial frames, and CHECKPOINT_MISMATCH to verify client behavior in SDK tests.
3) Load tests: measure impact of retries under 2x, 5x failure scenarios and assert tail latency remains within budget when retries occur.
4) Canary rollout: enable streaming error payloads on a narrow user segment (5% traffic) for 72h, monitor resume_success_rate and streaming_success_rate.
5) Gradual expansion: 5% -> 25% -> 75% -> 100% with 24h observation windows and automated rollback on SLO degradation.

Runbook excerpts (for SRE):
- Symptom: large spike in TRANSIENT_NETWORK closures
  1) Query traces for stream_error_type = TRANSIENT_NETWORK and filter by node_shard.
  2) If >5% of streams in last 5m originate from a single shard, evacuate shard and check recent kernel_release changes.
  3) If resume tokens are present but resume_success_rate < 90%, extend token retention window temporarily and eject suspect kernel build.

- Symptom: elevated CHECKPOINT_MISMATCH errors
  1) Identify latest schema or checkpoint encoding changes via artifact release logs.
  2) If change is recent, roll back the release to previous artifact and escalate to runtime team.

Implementation recipes and examples:
- Example streaming close header (HTTP chunked):
  Trailer: x-stream-error
  x-stream-error: {\"stream_error_type\":\"RATE_LIMITED\",\"message\":\"burst quota exceeded\",\"retry_after_seconds\":30,\"request_id\":\"req-2026-01-21-11\"}

- Example gRPC trailing metadata (pseudo):
  grpc-status: 8
  x-stream-error-bin: <base64(json-payload)>

- Example client pseudo-code for resume attempt:
  1) onStreamClose(meta):
     if meta.x-stream-error.resume_token:
       for attempt in 1..3:
         wait(randomized_backoff(attempt))
         resp = openStream({resumeToken: meta.resume_token})
         if resp.ok: attach and continue
       surface \"Resume failed\" and fall back to full restart
     else: full restart or surface to user

Enforcement and governance:
- API gate: all streaming endpoints must implement the x-stream-error payload by Q3 2026. Non-compliant services will be flagged by the nightly API conformance job.
- SDKs: official Redwood SDKs will implement default retry logic conforming to this contract; third-party clients are encouraged but not forced to use SDKs.
- Auditing: Weekly reports will summarize top stream_error_types by service and client platform; Product and SRE will review during the reliability sync.

Ownership and contacts:
- Specification owner: Priya Natarajan (eng/platform)
- Runtime/implementation owner: Runtime Team (eng-serving-runtime) – primary contact: Ethan Park
- Observability owner: Platform SRE – primary contact: Marta Silva
- Rollout coordinator: Product Infrastructure – Samira Ahmed

Appendix: change log
- 2025-09-17: Drafted initial taxonomy and payload schema (Priya).
- 2025-11-10: Added resume token retention and TTL guidance (Ethan).
- 2026-02-08: Finalized SLI targets and rollout checklist; changed base backoff to 200ms (Marta).

References:
- API compatibility and deprecation policy (see related_pages)
- Partial result checkpointing and client resume playbook (see related_pages)
