source.title: Procurement Approval Matrix and Billing-Trigger Guide
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Purpose
-------
This page defines Redwood Inference's standardized procurement approval matrix, the lifecycle steps for contract execution, and the precise billing triggers that feed the revenue recognition (revrec) process. It is intended for Finance, Legal, Procurement owners, and Engineering or Product teams who initiate supplier commitments. The goal is to reduce cycle time, ensure correct accounting treatment at go-live, and avoid revenue/billing misalignment across hosted, dedicated, and private product lines.

Scope
-----
Applies to: all third-party software and hardware purchases, cloud/hosted commitments, professional services, and vendor contracts that create a legal obligation or financial commitment for Redwood Inference (including pilot agreements and proof-of-concept statements of work).
Excludes: petty cash, employee expense reimbursements, and subscriptions procured on employee cards under $500 (see petty-expense policy).

Key Definitions
---------------
- Commitment: any signed agreement or PO that obligates Redwood to pay or receive services.
- Billing Trigger: the event which causes the vendor to generate invoices and which typically corresponds to the point at which Redwood recognizes expense or revenue impact.
- Effective Date vs. Service Commencement: Effective Date is when the contract is executed; Service Commencement is when vendor obligations start (these can be different).

Summary of Approval Matrix
--------------------------
Approval thresholds are financial (total contract value over term) and categorical (SaaS, infra, professional services, capital equipment). Routing times assume standard 5 business day SLA for each approver unless flagged. Use the Procurement Portal to initiate all requests.

Table: Approval Matrix (text tabular view)
Value band / Category | Requestor -> Approvers (order) | Required Attachments | Typical SLA
---------------------|--------------------------------|----------------------|---------
<$25k (non-capex) | Requesting Manager -> Procurement -> Finance (inform) | Quote, business justification | 3–7 days
$25k–$150k | Requesting Manager -> Procurement -> Finance -> Legal (if T&C change) -> Procurement Head | Quote, ROI or cost-center approval, security checklist | 7–12 days
$150k–$750k | Requesting Director -> Procurement -> Finance -> Legal -> Head of Product/Eng -> CFO (if multiyear) | Contract redline, SOC/attestation, procurement risk assessment | 10–20 days
>$750k or capital equipment | VP-level sponsor -> Procurement Director -> Legal -> CFO -> CEO (if > $2M/3yr) | Full diligence pack, board notice (if required) | 15–45 days
Special categories: any contract involving customer data residency, on-prem deployment, or KMS/HSM access must route to Security & Compliance prior to Legal review (add 5 business days).

Roles & Responsibilities
------------------------
- Requesting Manager: complete procurement request in portal, attach justification, and define cost-center and budget tag.
- Procurement: primary owner of vendor selection, PO issuance, and supplier onboarding checklist.
- Finance: verifies budget availability, assigns GL codes, and confirms revrec/billing implications.
- Legal: reviews and negotiates T&Cs; signs if within delegated authority.
- Security & Compliance: performs data-security risk review for relevant categories.
- Contracts Admin: executes signature workflows and uploads countersigned agreement to contract registry.

Standard Contract Lifecycle (5-step process)
-------------------------------------------
1) Intake & Business Justification
   - Requestor opens procurement ticket (Portal -> New Request) and attaches: vendor quote, scope of work, cost-center approval, and expected go-live date.
2) Risk & Compliance Screening (automatic)
   - Procurement auto-runs vendor risk checks (sanctions, AML, basic security posture). If flagged, Security & Compliance engages.
3) Negotiation & Approvals
   - Legal negotiates commercial terms and redlines. Approvers are notified in the order defined by the Approval Matrix.
4) Signature & PO Issuance
   - Contracts Admin finalizes signature (DocuSign). Procurement issues PO referencing contract ID and expected billing trigger.
5) Onboarding & Accounting Setup
   - Vendor is added to AP, GL mapping applied, invoicing instructions recorded, and revrec treatment confirmed in the contract registry.

Billing Triggers and RevRec Alignment
------------------------------------
Each contract requires an explicit Billing Trigger field to be set at signature. Typical triggers and how Finance treats them for expense recognition and billing purposes:
- Invoice on execution: vendor invoices after signature. Finance posts expense when invoice received; revrec not applicable (vendor expense only).
- Monthly in arrears based on usage: vendor invoices monthly for actual usage. Finance accrues monthly; for Redwood customer-facing revenue (if vendor pass-through), match usage reports to invoiced amounts.
- Milestone-based invoicing: vendor invoices on achievement of defined milestones. Finance recognizes expense as milestones are met; procurements that create customer billable services must map vendor milestones to customer billing milestones in product contracts to avoid mismatched revenue timing.
- Delivery + Acceptance: hardware or on-prem appliances invoiced on delivery and customer acceptance. Capitalization policy applies for items meeting fixed-asset thresholds (see capitalization policy).
- Supply or Capacity Reservation (committed capacity): vendor invoices per agreed commitment schedule even if unused. Finance treats unused commitment as expense on vendor invoice date unless contractual credits or rollovers exist.

Examples (practical scenarios)
----------------------------
1) Dedicated GPU pool reserved capacity (3-year commitment)
   - Approval: VP of Engineering -> Procurement -> Legal -> CFO.
   - Billing trigger: quarterly in advance per committed capacity.
   - Finance treatment: expense recognized when billed; related customer revenue recognized when we host customer workloads (ensure internal transfer pricing mirrors committed schedule).
2) Professional services (integration) tied to a customer implementation
   - Approval: Director Product -> Procurement -> Legal.
   - Billing trigger: milestone-based.
   - Finance treatment: vendor expenses recognized per milestone; Redwood invoices customer per product contract milestone — Product and Legal must ensure milestone definitions match.

Checklist for Requestors (minimum attachments)
--------------------------------------------
- Vendor quote or SOW
- Business justification and expected ROI or product impact
- Cost center and budget approver name
- Expected service commencement date and billing trigger recommendation
- Security questionnaire (if handling customer data)
- Any required export-control or compliance notes

Delegated Signature Authority (summary)
-------------------------------------
- Legal Counsel: up to $500k for standard SaaS templates (with no high-risk clauses)
- VP/Director of Finance: approve non-binding MSA addenda < $250k
- CFO: approves multiyear commitments > $250k and <= $2M
- CEO: approvals > $2M or any arrangement with strategic/board impact
Full authority matrix and delegation letters are in the Contracts Admin folder.

SLA Targets and Escalation Path
-------------------------------
- Standard legal review (non-complex): 7 business days.
- High-risk contracts (data residency, HSM, on-prem): 15 business days.
- If any stage exceeds SLA, Procurement will escalate to the approver’s manager and add an expedited tag. For > 10 business day delays in total, the Procurement Director performs a weekly review with stakeholder owners.

Common Pitfalls & Mitigations
----------------------------
- Mismatched milestones between vendor invoice schedule and customer billing: mitigation — require cross-signed milestone appendix and Finance sign-off pre-execution.
- Hidden termination or auto-renew clauses without notice periods: mitigation — Legal to enforce 30–90 day advance notice for renewals and include renewal reminders in contract registry.
- Security attachments missing: mitigation — block signature until Security & Compliance clears or issues a formal exception.

Operational Templates and Tools
------------------------------
- Procurement Portal: https://procurement.redwood.internal (internal) — use for all intake and auto-routing.
- Contract Registry spreadsheet / Confluence index (Contract-ID lookup) — upload signed agreements and set Billing Trigger field.
- Standard SOW and SaaS MSA templates are available in the Contracts library (link in related pages).

Change Log
----------
- 2025-11-12: Document created by Priya Natarajan (initial published).
- 2026-02-08: Updated approval thresholds and added committed capacity guidance for dedicated GPU pools; minor SLA adjustments.

Appendix: Example Billing Trigger JSON (for Contract Registry)
---------------------------------------------------------
Use the following example when filling the Billing Trigger field in the registry:

{
  "contract_id": "RWI-2026-0123",
  "billing_trigger": "quarterly_in_advance",
  "service_commencement": "2026-04-01",
  "revrec_note": "expense recognized on invoice; customer revenue recognized per usage"
}

Contact
-------
Procurement team (procurement@redwood.ai), Finance contracts (finance-contracts@redwood.ai), and Legal (legal-ops@redwood.ai). For urgent escalations, contact the Procurement Director (procurement-director@redwood.ai).

References
----------
- Capitalization policy (finance-and-legal/capitalization-policy-2025)
- Contract registry process (finance-and-legal/procurement/contract-registry-howto-2025)
- RevRec principles (finance-and-legal/revrec-and-billing/revrec-principles-2024)

Each contract requires an explicit Billing Trigger field to be set at signature. Typical triggers and how Finance treats them for expense recognition and billing purposes:
- Invoice on execution: vendor invoices after signature. Finance posts expense when invoice received; revrec not applicable (vendor expense only).
- Monthly in arrears based on usage: vendor invoices monthly for actual usage. Finance accrues monthly; for Redwood customer-facing revenue (if vendor pass-through), match usage reports to invoiced amounts.
- Milestone-based invoicing: vendor invoices on achievement of defined milestones. Finance recognizes expense as milestones are met; procurements that create customer billable services must map vendor milestones to customer billing milestones in product contracts to avoid mismatched revenue timing.
- Delivery + Acceptance: hardware or on-prem appliances invoiced on delivery and customer acceptance. Capitalization policy applies for items meeting fixed-asset thresholds (see capitalization policy).
- Supply or Capacity Reservation (committed capacity): vendor invoices per agreed commitment schedule even if unused. Finance treats unused commitment as expense on vendor invoice date unless contractual credits or rollovers exist.

Examples (practical scenarios)
----------------------------
1) Dedicated GPU pool reserved capacity (3-year commitment)
   - Approval: VP of Engineering -> Procurement -> Legal -> CFO.
   - Billing trigger: quarterly in advance per committed capacity.
   - Finance treatment: expense recognized when billed; related customer revenue recognized when we host customer workloads (ensure internal transfer pricing mirrors committed schedule).
2) Professional services (integration) tied to a customer implementation
   - Approval: Director Product -> Procurement -> Legal.
   - Billing trigger: milestone-based.
   - Finance treatment: vendor expenses recognized per milestone; Redwood invoices customer per product contract milestone — Product and Legal must ensure milestone definitions match.

Checklist for Requestors (minimum attachments)
--------------------------------------------
- Vendor quote or SOW
- Business justification and expected ROI or product impact
- Cost center and budget approver name
- Expected service commencement date and billing trigger recommendation
- Security questionnaire (if handling customer data)
- Any required export-control or compliance notes

Delegated Signature Authority (summary)
-------------------------------------
- Legal Counsel: up to $500k for standard SaaS templates (with no high-risk clauses)
- VP/Director of Finance: approve non-binding MSA addenda < $250k
- CFO: approves multiyear commitments > $250k and <= $2M
- CEO: approvals > $2M or any arrangement with strategic/board impact
Full authority matrix and delegation letters are in the Contracts Admin folder.

SLA Targets and Escalation Path
-------------------------------
- Standard legal review (non-complex): 7 business days.
- High-risk contracts (data residency, HSM, on-prem): 15 business days.
- If any stage exceeds SLA, Procurement will escalate to the approver’s manager and add an expedited tag. For > 10 business day delays in total, the Procurement Director performs a weekly review with stakeholder owners.

Common Pitfalls & Mitigations
----------------------------
- Mismatched milestones between vendor invoice schedule and customer billing: mitigation — require cross-signed milestone appendix and Finance sign-off pre-execution.
- Hidden termination or auto-renew clauses without notice periods: mitigation — Legal to enforce 30–90 day advance notice for renewals and include renewal reminders in contract registry.
- Security attachments missing: mitigation — block signature until Security & Compliance clears or issues a formal exception.

Operational Templates and Tools
------------------------------
- Procurement Portal: https://procurement.redwood.internal (internal) — use for all intake and auto-routing.
- Contract Registry spreadsheet / Confluence index (Contract-ID lookup) — upload signed agreements and set Billing Trigger field.
- Standard SOW and SaaS MSA templates are available in the Contracts library (link in related pages).

Change Log
----------
- 2025-11-12: Document created by Priya Natarajan (initial published).
- 2026-02-08: Updated approval thresholds and added committed capacity guidance for dedicated GPU pools; minor SLA adjustments.

Appendix: Example Billing Trigger JSON (for Contract Registry)
---------------------------------------------------------
Use the following example when filling the Billing Trigger field in the registry:

{
  "contract_id": "RWI-2026-0123",
  "billing_trigger": "quarterly_in_advance",
  "service_commencement": "2026-04-01",
  "revrec_note": "expense recognized on invoice; customer revenue recognized per usage"
}

Contact
-------
Procurement team (procurement@redwood.ai), Finance contracts (finance-contracts@redwood.ai), and Legal (legal-ops@redwood.ai). For urgent escalations, contact the Procurement Director (procurement-director@redwood.ai).

References
----------
- Capitalization policy (finance-and-legal/capitalization-policy-2025)
- Contract registry process (finance-and-legal/procurement/contract-registry-howto-2025)
- RevRec principles (finance-and-legal/revrec-and-billing/revrec-principles-2024)
text:
Purpose
-------
This page defines Redwood Inference's standardized procurement approval matrix, the lifecycle steps for contract execution, and the precise billing triggers that feed the revenue recognition (revrec) process. It is intended for Finance, Legal, Procurement owners, and Engineering or Product teams who initiate supplier commitments. The goal is to reduce cycle time, ensure correct accounting treatment at go-live, and avoid revenue/billing misalignment across hosted, dedicated, and private product lines.

Scope
-----
Applies to: all third-party software and hardware purchases, cloud/hosted commitments, professional services, and vendor contracts that create a legal obligation or financial commitment for Redwood Inference (including pilot agreements and proof-of-concept statements of work).
Excludes: petty cash, employee expense reimbursements, and subscriptions procured on employee cards under $500 (see petty-expense policy).

Key Definitions
---------------
- Commitment: any signed agreement or PO that obligates Redwood to pay or receive services.
- Billing Trigger: the event which causes the vendor to generate invoices and which typically corresponds to the point at which Redwood recognizes expense or revenue impact.
- Effective Date vs. Service Commencement: Effective Date is when the contract is executed; Service Commencement is when vendor obligations start (these can be different).

Summary of Approval Matrix
--------------------------
Approval thresholds are financial (total contract value over term) and categorical (SaaS, infra, professional services, capital equipment). Routing times assume standard 5 business day SLA for each approver unless flagged. Use the Procurement Portal to initiate all requests.

Table: Approval Matrix (text tabular view)
Value band / Category | Requestor -> Approvers (order) | Required Attachments | Typical SLA
---------------------|--------------------------------|----------------------|---------
<$25k (non-capex) | Requesting Manager -> Procurement -> Finance (inform) | Quote, business justification | 3–7 days
$25k–$150k | Requesting Manager -> Procurement -> Finance -> Legal (if T&C change) -> Procurement Head | Quote, ROI or cost-center approval, security checklist | 7–12 days
$150k–$750k | Requesting Director -> Procurement -> Finance -> Legal -> Head of Product/Eng -> CFO (if multiyear) | Contract redline, SOC/attestation, procurement risk assessment | 10–20 days
>$750k or capital equipment | VP-level sponsor -> Procurement Director -> Legal -> CFO -> CEO (if > $2M/3yr) | Full diligence pack, board notice (if required) | 15–45 days
Special categories: any contract involving customer data residency, on-prem deployment, or KMS/HSM access must route to Security & Compliance prior to Legal review (add 5 business days).

Roles & Responsibilities
------------------------
- Requesting Manager: complete procurement request in portal, attach justification, and define cost-center and budget tag.
- Procurement: primary owner of vendor selection, PO issuance, and supplier onboarding checklist.
- Finance: verifies budget availability, assigns GL codes, and confirms revrec/billing implications.
- Legal: reviews and negotiates T&Cs; signs if within delegated authority.
- Security & Compliance: performs data-security risk review for relevant categories.
- Contracts Admin: executes signature workflows and uploads countersigned agreement to contract registry.

Standard Contract Lifecycle (5-step process)
-------------------------------------------
1) Intake & Business Justification
   - Requestor opens procurement ticket (Portal -> New Request) and attaches: vendor quote, scope of work, cost-center approval, and expected go-live date.
2) Risk & Compliance Screening (automatic)
   - Procurement auto-runs vendor risk checks (sanctions, AML, basic security posture). If flagged, Security & Compliance engages.
3) Negotiation & Approvals
   - Legal negotiates commercial terms and redlines. Approvers are notified in the order defined by the Approval Matrix.
4) Signature & PO Issuance
   - Contracts Admin finalizes signature (DocuSign). Procurement issues PO referencing contract ID and expected billing trigger.
5) Onboarding & Accounting Setup
   - Vendor is added to AP, GL mapping applied, invoicing instructions recorded, and revrec treatment confirmed in the contract registry.

Billing Triggers and RevRec Alignment
------------------------------------
Each contract requires an explicit Billing Trigger field to be set at signature. Typical triggers and how Finance treats them for expense recognition and billing purposes:
- Invoice on execution: vendor invoices after signature. Finance posts expense when invoice received; revrec not applicable (vendor expense only).
- Monthly in arrears based on usage: vendor invoices monthly for actual usage. Finance accrues monthly; for Redwood customer-facing revenue (if vendor pass-through), match usage reports to invoiced amounts.
- Milestone-based invoicing: vendor invoices on achievement of defined milestones. Finance recognizes expense as milestones are met; procurements that create customer billable services must map vendor milestones to customer billing milestones in product contracts to avoid mismatched revenue timing.
- Delivery + Acceptance: hardware or on-prem appliances invoiced on delivery and customer acceptance. Capitalization policy applies for items meeting fixed-asset thresholds (see capitalization policy).
- Supply or Capacity Reservation (committed capacity): vendor invoices per agreed commitment schedule even if unused. Finance treats unused commitment as expense on vendor invoice date unless contractual credits or rollovers exist.

Examples (practical scenarios)
----------------------------
1) Dedicated GPU pool reserved capacity (3-year commitment)
   - Approval: VP of Engineering -> Procurement -> Legal -> CFO.
   - Billing trigger: quarterly in advance per committed capacity.
   - Finance treatment: expense recognized when billed; related customer revenue recognized when we host customer workloads (ensure internal transfer pricing mirrors committed schedule).
2) Professional services (integration) tied to a customer implementation
   - Approval: Director Product -> Procurement -> Legal.
   - Billing trigger: milestone-based.
   - Finance treatment: vendor expenses recognized per milestone; Redwood invoices customer per product contract milestone — Product and Legal must ensure milestone definitions match.

Checklist for Requestors (minimum attachments)
--------------------------------------------
- Vendor quote or SOW
- Business justification and expected ROI or product impact
- Cost center and budget approver name
- Expected service commencement date and billing trigger recommendation
- Security questionnaire (if handling customer data)
- Any required export-control or compliance notes

Delegated Signature Authority (summary)
-------------------------------------
- Legal Counsel: up to $500k for standard SaaS templates (with no high-risk clauses)
- VP/Director of Finance: approve non-binding MSA addenda < $250k
- CFO: approves multiyear commitments > $250k and <= $2M
- CEO: approvals > $2M or any arrangement with strategic/board impact
Full authority matrix and delegation letters are in the Contracts Admin folder.

- If any stage exceeds SLA, Procurement will escalate to the approver’s manager and add an expedited tag. For > 10 business day delays in total, the Procurement Director performs a weekly review with stakeholder owners.

----------------------------

- Hidden termination or auto-renew clauses without notice periods: mitigation — Legal to enforce 30–90 day advance notice for renewals and include renewal reminders in contract registry.

Operational Templates and Tools

- Contract Registry spreadsheet / Confluence index (Contract-ID lookup) — upload signed agreements and set Billing Trigger field.
- Standard SOW and SaaS MSA templates are available in the Contracts library (link in related pages).

Change Log
----------

- 2026-02-08: Updated approval thresholds and added committed capacity guidance for dedicated GPU pools; minor SLA adjustments.

Appendix: Example Billing Trigger JSON (for Contract Registry)
---------------------------------------------------------

{
  "contract_id": "RWI-2026-0123",
  "billing_trigger": "quarterly_in_advance",
  "service_commencement": "2026-04-01",
  "revrec_note": "expense recognized on invoice; customer revenue recognized per usage"
}

Contact
-------
Procurement team (procurement@redwood.ai), Finance contracts (finance-contracts@redwood.ai), and Legal (legal-ops@redwood.ai). For urgent escalations, contact the Procurement Director (procurement-director@redwood.ai).

References
----------

- Contract registry process (finance-and-legal/procurement/contract-registry-howto-2025)

source.title: Econometric Latency-Cost Sensitivity Model and Simulation Runbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Summary:
This document defines an econometric model for estimating how changes in runtime decisions (kernel selection, batching, quantization, routing) map to observed latency and per-token cost, and provides a reproducible simulation runbook for validating sensitivity hypotheses before production rollouts. The goal is to provide a single reference for analysts and runtime engineers to: quantify marginal cost/latency effects, prioritize optimization work, and validate safe rollout envelopes for changes that affect operator economics or SLOs.

Contents:
1) Introduction and intent
2) Model specification and assumptions
3) Required telemetry and inputs
4) Simulation lab setup and dataset construction
5) Runbook: experiment steps (numbered)
6) Analysis and decision criteria
7) Example configs and sample output
8) Ownership and escalation
9) Appendix: quick CLI snippets and sanity checks

1) Introduction and intent
- Problem statement: Changes to kernel implementations, scheduler policies, batching strategies, or quantization profiles have non-linear effects on both tail latency and per-token cost. This doc formalizes a pragmatic, testable econometric model and a simulation protocol to estimate those effects using production traces + controlled synthetic workloads.
- Who should use this: runtime engineers, performance analysts, SREs, product owners evaluating cost-optimization proposals.
- Outcome: A reproducible vector of sensitivity metrics: delta P50/P95/P99 latency per unit change, marginal cost per 1k tokens, and joint probability of SLO violation under traffic variance.

2) Model specification and assumptions
- Model form (high level): latency ~ alpha + beta1 * effective_batch_size + beta2 * kernel_choice_factor + beta3 * seq_len + beta4 * kvcache_hit_rate + beta5 * quant_level + epsilon
- Cost proxy: per_token_cost_estimate = fixed_dispatch_cost / expected_tokens + dispatch_amortization(batch_size) + kernel_compute_cost(kernel_choice, seq_len, quant_level) + infra_markup
- Interpretability: beta coefficients are marginal effects; we prefer GLM with log-link for latency and cost when distributions are skewed. Use robust standard errors clustered by trace session.
- Key assumptions to record before running experiments:
  - Trace representativeness: sampled traffic should match production distribution for sequence lengths and request inter-arrival times within 5–10% L2 distance.
  - Stationarity window: workload period chosen should be short enough to avoid major model drift (recommended 4–24 hours) unless explicitly modelling diurnal effects.
  - Kernel comparability: kernel_choice_factor encodes candidate kernel implementations that satisfy semantic parity for outputs (validation gate required).

3) Required telemetry and inputs
- Mandatory fields from production traces (per request):
  - timestamp (ms), request_id, model_id, model_variant, kernel_id, batch_size, seq_len, token_count, kvcache_hit (bool), kernel_start_ms, kernel_end_ms, total_latency_ms, host_id, accelerator_type
- Aggregates needed for modelling:
  - session_id-level prefix counts, per-host occupancy, infra_cost_tags (spot vs reserved), network RTTs
- Additional signals to collect for sensitivity attribution:
  - scheduler_decision_tag, admission_reason, preemption_count, soft_degradation_flag
- Recommended sampling/retention policy for experiments: retain full traces for 90 days in the lab bucket; keep 7-day high-fidelity window for direct replay.

4) Simulation lab setup and dataset construction
- Data sources: production trace snapshot (anonymized), synthetic corpus (diverse seq_len and prompt shapes), and microbenchmarks for each kernel variant (cold, warm, high-concurrency).
- Dataset construction steps (summary):
  1. Select representative day(s) and extract 4-hour windows covering peak and off-peak traffic.
  2. Resample requests to produce target QPS shapes (steady, ramp, burst) preserving inter-arrival distribution at 10ms resolution.
  3. Inject controlled variance: scale seq_len distribution by +/- 20% in separate cohorts; create an extreme-burst cohort (10x for 30s every 5 minutes).
  4. Annotate each request with kernel hints and quant_profile tags for the simulation scenarios.
- Lab topology: use a small isolated cluster that mirrors production instance types (same accelerator families). Use network shaping to emulate production RTT and packet loss if relevant.

5) Runbook: experiment steps (numbered)
1. Preflight checks (owners: experiment lead, runtime owner)
   a. Confirm trace window selection and anonymization complete.
   b. Verify kernel builds are semantically equivalent (unit test and model-output diff below threshold).
   c. Validate lab cluster health and telemetry ingestion.
2. Baseline collection
   a. Run baseline scenario: production kernel + current batching + quant profile; collect 2 full runs (steady + burst) to measure variance.
   b. Compute baseline metrics: mean/median/std for latency quantiles, per_token_cost_estimate (see formula), kernel utilization, queue lengths.
3. Candidate configuration injection
   a. For each candidate change (kernel variant, batch reshaping, quant level), run 3 independent repeats across identical QPS shapes.
   b. Capture microbenchmarks before and after: cold-path start, warm-path P50 kernel time, kernel tail.
4. Sensitivity estimation
   a. Fit the GLM to pooled run data with scenario tags. Use clustered robust errors at host-level.
   b. Produce coefficient table and marginal effect plots (delta latency per +1 batch, per quant step, per kernel switch).
5. Stress and edge-case probing
   a. Run chaos-scenarios: preemption bursts, eviction-induced cold starts, and simulated NVLink degradation.
   b. Record joint SLO violation frequency and time-to-recovery.
6. Regression attribution and anomaly hunting
   a. If any scenario raises tail latency > baseline P99 + 15% or increases per-token cost > 10% with no quality gain, mark as failed and collect full traces for RCA.
7. Signoff matrix and rollout plan
   a. If sensitivity metrics fall within acceptance envelope (see section 6), prepare canary rollout plan with automatic rollback triggers and observability dashboards.

6) Analysis and decision criteria
- Primary acceptance envelope (default):
  - P95 latency increase <= 5% and P99 <= 10% relative to baseline for expected traffic;
  - Marginal per-token cost delta <= +3% for hosted pricing-sensitive tiers;
  - No increase in observed model output divergence beyond validation tolerance.
- Escalation thresholds (immediate abort):
  - P99 increases by > 25% or joint SLO violation probability increases > 2x under burst scenarios;
  - Any increase in error rate / silent degradation flag set by runtime checks.
- Statistical checks:
  - Use bootstrapped confidence intervals for marginal estimates; require 95% CI not crossing zero for performance improvements to be considered significant.
  - For tail metrics use peak-over-threshold EVT-inspired checks plus sample quantile CI.
- Economics view:
  - When comparing two candidates with similar latency, prefer the one with lower estimated cost-per-1M tokens, but apply a product-weighted factor (weight latency more for interactive products; see appendix for weight table).

7) Example configs and sample output
- Example simulation scenario (YAML):
  scenario_name: kernel-led-quant-test
  qps_shape: burst-peak-2x
  kernel_candidates: [kernel_v1_float16, kernel_v2_int8_opt]
  batch_policy: [auto, target_batch=16, max_latency_ms=120]
  quant_profiles: [q4_0, q8_1]
  replay_window: 2025-11-04T02:00:00Z/2025-11-04T06:00:00Z

- Example result snippet (CSV-friendly summary):
  metric,baseline, candidate_v2,delta,ci_low,ci_high
  p50_latency_ms,24,22,-8.3%,-9.5%,-7.1%
  p95_latency_ms,78,85,+9.0%,+6.1%,+11.8%
  p99_latency_ms,210,250,+19.0%,+12.0%,+26.5%
  estimated_cost_per_1M_tokens,$420,$385,-8.3%,-10.1%,-6.2%

8) Ownership and escalation
- Primary owner: runtime-performance team (team contact: runtime-performance@redwood.example.com).
- Experiment lead: listed per-run on the experiment ticket; responsible for preflight, results, and rollout proposal.
- SLA owner and SRE review: required before any production canary that affects P95.
- Escalation route: experiment lead -> runtime tech lead -> SRE oncall -> VP of Engineering if unresolved.

9) Appendix: quick CLI snippets and sanity checks
- Replaying a trace to lab cluster (sanitized):
  cat trace.jsonl | jq -c '. | del(.user_data)' | lab-replay --cluster lab-west-1 --qps 120 --seed 42
- Simple GLM fit command (python/pandas/statsmodels):
  python -c "import pandas as pd, statsmodels.formula.api as smf; df=pd.read_csv('runs.csv'); m=smf.glm('np.log(total_latency_ms) ~ batch_size + C(kernel_id) + seq_len + kvcache_hit', data=df).fit(cov_type='cluster', cov_kwds={'groups':df['host_id']}); print(m.summary())"
- Sanity checks before signoff:
  - Kernel unit tests pass and model-output-diff < 0.1% of tokens differing.
  - Telemetry ingestion shows no missing kernel tags for >99% of requests in the run.
  - Canary dashboard widgets prewired: P50/P95/P99, per-token-cost, kernel-utilization, queue-lengths, and a per-host heatmap.

Frequently asked questions (short)
- Q: How long does a full experiment take?
  A: End-to-end from baseline to signoff usually 6–18 hours depending on repeats and stress tests.
- Q: Can I rely only on microbenchmarks?
  A: No. Microbenchmarks are necessary but insufficient because they do not model admission, scheduling, or KV cache effects under real traffic shapes.

Change log
- 2025-11-12: Initial draft and baseline model spec (Priya N.).
- 2026-02-07: Added bootstrapped CI guidance and canary acceptance envelope; tightened preflight checks (Priya N., reviewed by Marcela G.).

Related artifacts to attach to the experiment ticket:
- baseline_runs.csv (zipped), candidate_runs.csv (zipped), kernel_microbenchmarks.tar.gz, dashboard_snapshot.png, signed-off validation diff report.json

Notes and caveats
- This model is intentionally pragmatic: do not treat coefficients as structural causal estimates without additional causal identification work. For changes with product-level impact (e.g., new quantization profiles that alter model outputs), engage Applied ML for an evaluation gate before rollout.

If you have questions or want this runbook adapted for Private deployments (VPC/on-prem), contact the runtime-performance team for a tailored lab topology and cost-tag mapping.

  quant_profiles: [q4_0, q8_1]
  replay_window: 2025-11-04T02:00:00Z/2025-11-04T06:00:00Z

- Example result snippet (CSV-friendly summary):
  metric,baseline, candidate_v2,delta,ci_low,ci_high
  p50_latency_ms,24,22,-8.3%,-9.5%,-7.1%
  p95_latency_ms,78,85,+9.0%,+6.1%,+11.8%
  p99_latency_ms,210,250,+19.0%,+12.0%,+26.5%
  estimated_cost_per_1M_tokens,$420,$385,-8.3%,-10.1%,-6.2%

8) Ownership and escalation
- Primary owner: runtime-performance team (team contact: runtime-performance@redwood.example.com).
- Experiment lead: listed per-run on the experiment ticket; responsible for preflight, results, and rollout proposal.
- SLA owner and SRE review: required before any production canary that affects P95.
- Escalation route: experiment lead -> runtime tech lead -> SRE oncall -> VP of Engineering if unresolved.

9) Appendix: quick CLI snippets and sanity checks
- Replaying a trace to lab cluster (sanitized):
  cat trace.jsonl | jq -c '. | del(.user_data)' | lab-replay --cluster lab-west-1 --qps 120 --seed 42
- Simple GLM fit command (python/pandas/statsmodels):
  python -c "import pandas as pd, statsmodels.formula.api as smf; df=pd.read_csv('runs.csv'); m=smf.glm('np.log(total_latency_ms) ~ batch_size + C(kernel_id) + seq_len + kvcache_hit', data=df).fit(cov_type='cluster', cov_kwds={'groups':df['host_id']}); print(m.summary())"
- Sanity checks before signoff:
  - Kernel unit tests pass and model-output-diff < 0.1% of tokens differing.
  - Telemetry ingestion shows no missing kernel tags for >99% of requests in the run.
  - Canary dashboard widgets prewired: P50/P95/P99, per-token-cost, kernel-utilization, queue-lengths, and a per-host heatmap.

Frequently asked questions (short)
- Q: How long does a full experiment take?
  A: End-to-end from baseline to signoff usually 6–18 hours depending on repeats and stress tests.
- Q: Can I rely only on microbenchmarks?
  A: No. Microbenchmarks are necessary but insufficient because they do not model admission, scheduling, or KV cache effects under real traffic shapes.

Change log
- 2025-11-12: Initial draft and baseline model spec (Priya N.).
- 2026-02-07: Added bootstrapped CI guidance and canary acceptance envelope; tightened preflight checks (Priya N., reviewed by Marcela G.).

Related artifacts to attach to the experiment ticket:
- baseline_runs.csv (zipped), candidate_runs.csv (zipped), kernel_microbenchmarks.tar.gz, dashboard_snapshot.png, signed-off validation diff report.json

Notes and caveats
- This model is intentionally pragmatic: do not treat coefficients as structural causal estimates without additional causal identification work. For changes with product-level impact (e.g., new quantization profiles that alter model outputs), engage Applied ML for an evaluation gate before rollout.

If you have questions or want this runbook adapted for Private deployments (VPC/on-prem), contact the runtime-performance team for a tailored lab topology and cost-tag mapping.
text:
Summary:
This document defines an econometric model for estimating how changes in runtime decisions (kernel selection, batching, quantization, routing) map to observed latency and per-token cost, and provides a reproducible simulation runbook for validating sensitivity hypotheses before production rollouts. The goal is to provide a single reference for analysts and runtime engineers to: quantify marginal cost/latency effects, prioritize optimization work, and validate safe rollout envelopes for changes that affect operator economics or SLOs.

Contents:
1) Introduction and intent
2) Model specification and assumptions
3) Required telemetry and inputs
4) Simulation lab setup and dataset construction
5) Runbook: experiment steps (numbered)
6) Analysis and decision criteria
7) Example configs and sample output
8) Ownership and escalation
9) Appendix: quick CLI snippets and sanity checks

1) Introduction and intent
- Problem statement: Changes to kernel implementations, scheduler policies, batching strategies, or quantization profiles have non-linear effects on both tail latency and per-token cost. This doc formalizes a pragmatic, testable econometric model and a simulation protocol to estimate those effects using production traces + controlled synthetic workloads.
- Who should use this: runtime engineers, performance analysts, SREs, product owners evaluating cost-optimization proposals.
- Outcome: A reproducible vector of sensitivity metrics: delta P50/P95/P99 latency per unit change, marginal cost per 1k tokens, and joint probability of SLO violation under traffic variance.

2) Model specification and assumptions
- Model form (high level): latency ~ alpha + beta1 * effective_batch_size + beta2 * kernel_choice_factor + beta3 * seq_len + beta4 * kvcache_hit_rate + beta5 * quant_level + epsilon
- Cost proxy: per_token_cost_estimate = fixed_dispatch_cost / expected_tokens + dispatch_amortization(batch_size) + kernel_compute_cost(kernel_choice, seq_len, quant_level) + infra_markup
- Interpretability: beta coefficients are marginal effects; we prefer GLM with log-link for latency and cost when distributions are skewed. Use robust standard errors clustered by trace session.
- Key assumptions to record before running experiments:
  - Trace representativeness: sampled traffic should match production distribution for sequence lengths and request inter-arrival times within 5–10% L2 distance.
  - Stationarity window: workload period chosen should be short enough to avoid major model drift (recommended 4–24 hours) unless explicitly modelling diurnal effects.
  - Kernel comparability: kernel_choice_factor encodes candidate kernel implementations that satisfy semantic parity for outputs (validation gate required).

3) Required telemetry and inputs
- Mandatory fields from production traces (per request):
  - timestamp (ms), request_id, model_id, model_variant, kernel_id, batch_size, seq_len, token_count, kvcache_hit (bool), kernel_start_ms, kernel_end_ms, total_latency_ms, host_id, accelerator_type
- Aggregates needed for modelling:
  - session_id-level prefix counts, per-host occupancy, infra_cost_tags (spot vs reserved), network RTTs
- Additional signals to collect for sensitivity attribution:
  - scheduler_decision_tag, admission_reason, preemption_count, soft_degradation_flag
- Recommended sampling/retention policy for experiments: retain full traces for 90 days in the lab bucket; keep 7-day high-fidelity window for direct replay.

4) Simulation lab setup and dataset construction
- Data sources: production trace snapshot (anonymized), synthetic corpus (diverse seq_len and prompt shapes), and microbenchmarks for each kernel variant (cold, warm, high-concurrency).
- Dataset construction steps (summary):
  1. Select representative day(s) and extract 4-hour windows covering peak and off-peak traffic.
  2. Resample requests to produce target QPS shapes (steady, ramp, burst) preserving inter-arrival distribution at 10ms resolution.
  3. Inject controlled variance: scale seq_len distribution by +/- 20% in separate cohorts; create an extreme-burst cohort (10x for 30s every 5 minutes).
  4. Annotate each request with kernel hints and quant_profile tags for the simulation scenarios.
- Lab topology: use a small isolated cluster that mirrors production instance types (same accelerator families). Use network shaping to emulate production RTT and packet loss if relevant.

5) Runbook: experiment steps (numbered)
1. Preflight checks (owners: experiment lead, runtime owner)
   a. Confirm trace window selection and anonymization complete.
   b. Verify kernel builds are semantically equivalent (unit test and model-output diff below threshold).
   c. Validate lab cluster health and telemetry ingestion.
2. Baseline collection
   a. Run baseline scenario: production kernel + current batching + quant profile; collect 2 full runs (steady + burst) to measure variance.
   b. Compute baseline metrics: mean/median/std for latency quantiles, per_token_cost_estimate (see formula), kernel utilization, queue lengths.
3. Candidate configuration injection
   a. For each candidate change (kernel variant, batch reshaping, quant level), run 3 independent repeats across identical QPS shapes.
   b. Capture microbenchmarks before and after: cold-path start, warm-path P50 kernel time, kernel tail.
4. Sensitivity estimation
   a. Fit the GLM to pooled run data with scenario tags. Use clustered robust errors at host-level.
   b. Produce coefficient table and marginal effect plots (delta latency per +1 batch, per quant step, per kernel switch).
5. Stress and edge-case probing
   a. Run chaos-scenarios: preemption bursts, eviction-induced cold starts, and simulated NVLink degradation.
   b. Record joint SLO violation frequency and time-to-recovery.
6. Regression attribution and anomaly hunting
   a. If any scenario raises tail latency > baseline P99 + 15% or increases per-token cost > 10% with no quality gain, mark as failed and collect full traces for RCA.
7. Signoff matrix and rollout plan
   a. If sensitivity metrics fall within acceptance envelope (see section 6), prepare canary rollout plan with automatic rollback triggers and observability dashboards.

6) Analysis and decision criteria
- Primary acceptance envelope (default):
  - P95 latency increase <= 5% and P99 <= 10% relative to baseline for expected traffic;
  - Marginal per-token cost delta <= +3% for hosted pricing-sensitive tiers;
  - No increase in observed model output divergence beyond validation tolerance.
- Escalation thresholds (immediate abort):
  - P99 increases by > 25% or joint SLO violation probability increases > 2x under burst scenarios;
  - Any increase in error rate / silent degradation flag set by runtime checks.
- Statistical checks:
  - Use bootstrapped confidence intervals for marginal estimates; require 95% CI not crossing zero for performance improvements to be considered significant.
  - For tail metrics use peak-over-threshold EVT-inspired checks plus sample quantile CI.
- Economics view:
  - When comparing two candidates with similar latency, prefer the one with lower estimated cost-per-1M tokens, but apply a product-weighted factor (weight latency more for interactive products; see appendix for weight table).

7) Example configs and sample output
- Example simulation scenario (YAML):
  scenario_name: kernel-led-quant-test
  qps_shape: burst-peak-2x
  kernel_candidates: [kernel_v1_float16, kernel_v2_int8_opt]
  batch_policy: [auto, target_batch=16, max_latency_ms=120]
  quant_profiles: [q4_0, q8_1]
  replay_window: 2025-11-04T02:00:00Z/2025-11-04T06:00:00Z

- Example result snippet (CSV-friendly summary):
  metric,baseline, candidate_v2,delta,ci_low,ci_high
  p50_latency_ms,24,22,-8.3%,-9.5%,-7.1%
  p95_latency_ms,78,85,+9.0%,+6.1%,+11.8%
  p99_latency_ms,210,250,+19.0%,+12.0%,+26.5%
  estimated_cost_per_1M_tokens,$420,$385,-8.3%,-10.1%,-6.2%

8) Ownership and escalation
- Primary owner: runtime-performance team (team contact: runtime-performance@redwood.example.com).
- Experiment lead: listed per-run on the experiment ticket; responsible for preflight, results, and rollout proposal.
- SLA owner and SRE review: required before any production canary that affects P95.
- Escalation route: experiment lead -> runtime tech lead -> SRE oncall -> VP of Engineering if unresolved.

9) Appendix: quick CLI snippets and sanity checks
- Replaying a trace to lab cluster (sanitized):
  cat trace.jsonl | jq -c '. | del(.user_data)' | lab-replay --cluster lab-west-1 --qps 120 --seed 42
- Simple GLM fit command (python/pandas/statsmodels):
  python -c "import pandas as pd, statsmodels.formula.api as smf; df=pd.read_csv('runs.csv'); m=smf.glm('np.log(total_latency_ms) ~ batch_size + C(kernel_id) + seq_len + kvcache_hit', data=df).fit(cov_type='cluster', cov_kwds={'groups':df['host_id']}); print(m.summary())"
- Sanity checks before signoff:
  - Kernel unit tests pass and model-output-diff < 0.1% of tokens differing.
  - Telemetry ingestion shows no missing kernel tags for >99% of requests in the run.
  - Canary dashboard widgets prewired: P50/P95/P99, per-token-cost, kernel-utilization, queue-lengths, and a per-host heatmap.

Frequently asked questions (short)
- Q: How long does a full experiment take?
  A: End-to-end from baseline to signoff usually 6–18 hours depending on repeats and stress tests.
- Q: Can I rely only on microbenchmarks?
  A: No. Microbenchmarks are necessary but insufficient because they do not model admission, scheduling, or KV cache effects under real traffic shapes.

Change log
- 2025-11-12: Initial draft and baseline model spec (Priya N.).
- 2026-02-07: Added bootstrapped CI guidance and canary acceptance envelope; tightened preflight checks (Priya N., reviewed by Marcela G.).

Related artifacts to attach to the experiment ticket:
- baseline_runs.csv (zipped), candidate_runs.csv (zipped), kernel_microbenchmarks.tar.gz, dashboard_snapshot.png, signed-off validation diff report.json

Notes and caveats
- This model is intentionally pragmatic: do not treat coefficients as structural causal estimates without additional causal identification work. For changes with product-level impact (e.g., new quantization profiles that alter model outputs), engage Applied ML for an evaluation gate before rollout.

If you have questions or want this runbook adapted for Private deployments (VPC/on-prem), contact the runtime-performance team for a tailored lab topology and cost-tag mapping.

source.title: Fleet Canary Allocator Terraform Module and Runbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Summary

This document defines the design, Terraform module interface, CI/CD integration, and on-call runbook for the Fleet Canary Allocator: a small control-plane component that performs safe, SLO-driven gradual rollouts of allocation rules and GPU placement policies across heterogeneous GPU pools. The primary goal is to minimize blast radius for policy changes that affect tenant placement, preemption behavior, and burst capacity while keeping rollout automation auditable and reversible.

Why this exists

- We frequently change allocation policies (fair-share weights, priority buckets, spot fallback policy) and kernel/runtime flags that affect performance and preemption probability.
- A full fleet-wide change can cause regressions in latency/SLOs or tenant evictions if there is an unseen interaction with topology or the scheduler.
- Existing canary tooling targets provisioning; this module ties Terraform-driven policy changes to a staged placement rollout and provides an automated fallback and remediation path.

Scope and Goals

- Provide a Terraform module that deploys the Canary Allocator with opinionated defaults for Redwood GPU fleets.
- Expose variables for canary population sizing, ramp cadence, health checks, and failover targets.
- Integrate with the CI/CD canary pipeline so every policy change is gated by plan-time validation and short-circuitable rollout.
- Offer an on-call runbook covering detection, mitigation, and postmortem guidance for a failed canary.

Non-goals

- Replacing core scheduler logic: the allocator orchestrates placement policy application and traffic-shifting, but does not modify underlying scheduler algorithms.
- End-user tenant-level billing decisions.

High-level design

Components

1) Canary Allocator Controller (K8s Deployment)
   - Watches Policy CRDs and orchestrates staged application across target GPU pools.
   - Uses allocation tiers: canary, ramp, steady, reserve.
   - Exposes REST endpoints for manual pause/rollback and metrics (Prometheus).

2) Policy CRD (YAML)
   - Holds allocation policy payload (weights, priority buckets, preemption thresholds, KV cache affinity tags).

3) Terraform Module
   - Encapsulates K8s manifests, RBAC, service account IAM bindings, and optional external canary targets (CloudRun Lambda).

4) CI/CD Integration (Buildkite + TF Cloud workspace)
   - Plan + policy linting stage -> canary deployment stage -> automated health gate -> ramp stage -> steady stage.

Allocation tier semantics

- canary: 1–3 nodes or 0.5–2% of active capacity, whichever is larger. Short window (10–30 minutes) for quick validation.
- ramp: incremental increase to 10–25% over configurable period (1–24 hours) with continuous health checks.
- steady: remaining production capacity.
- reserve: preconfigured fallback capacity identified at plan time for immediate traffic shift on failure.

SLOs and health checks

- Primary SLOs to monitor during canary: 95th percentile token latency (SLO delta threshold +20%), error rate (>= 5xx) increase > 0.5% absolute, tenant eviction rate delta > 1 per hour, and queue-time percentiles.
- Checks performed every 60s. If any check crosses threshold for 3 consecutive samples, the health gate fails.

Terraform module interface (example)

Note: this is a high-level excerpt — full module lives in the terraform registry module path referenced below.

Required variables (Terraform var names)

- cluster_name (string) — k8s cluster to deploy into
- namespace (string) — namespace for controller and CRDs
- canary_capacity_pct (number) — target percent for initial canary population (default 1.0)
- ramp_duration_minutes (number) — time to reach ramp target (default 120)
- health_checks (list) — list of named health checks to run (default ["latency_p95", "error_rate"])
- reserve_target (string) — label selector for reserve pool nodes
- iam_bindings (map) — optional map of IAM roles to service-account emails

Outputs

- controller_service_endpoint (string)
- canary_policy_crd_id (string)

Sample terraform usage (snippet)

module "fleet_canary_allocator" {
  source = "git::https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra-modules.git//fleet-canary-allocator"
  cluster_name = var.k8s_cluster_name
  namespace = "fleet-allocator"
  canary_capacity_pct = 1.5
  ramp_duration_minutes = 180
  health_checks = ["latency_p95","error_rate","eviction_rate"]
  reserve_target = "gpu-pool:reserve-v2"
}

Policy CRD example (extracted)

apiVersion: infra.redwood.ai/v1alpha1
kind: AllocationPolicy
metadata:
  name: preempt-threshold-adjust-2026-02
spec:
  policyPayload:
    preemption_threshold: 0.65
    priority_weights:
      enterprise: 1.0
      standard: 0.7
    kv_cache_affinity: ["tenant-critical"]
  rolloutStrategy:
    tiers: ["canary", "ramp", "steady"]
    canaryTargetSelector: "topology.kubernetes.io/zone==us-west1-b"
    rampSteps: 4
    stepDurationMinutes: 45

CI/CD flow (Buildkite)

1) pre-commit: policy linting (policy-schema-validator).
2) plan: terraform plan in isolated workspace. Policy diff is recorded as structured JSON artifact.
3) canary-deploy job: applies the policy as a canary-only CRD.
4) canary-health-gate: queries Prometheus metrics + log-based signals for the canary window. If pass, continue; if fail, mark pipeline and trigger rollback job.
5) ramp-job: incrementally expands target selectors per rampSteps.
6) promote-job: marks policy as steady and writes rollout metadata to audit log (CloudSQL).

Observability and dashboards

- Prometheus metrics exposed by controller: allocator_canary_state{tier=}, allocator_health_check_failures_total, allocator_rollout_progress_percentage.
- Grafana dashboard panels: Canary Latency Delta, Canary Error Spike, Eviction Delta Heatmap (by tenant), Node-level GPU Utilization (canary vs baseline).
- Alert rules (examples):
  - ALERT CanaryLatencyRegression
    IF canary_latency_p95_delta > 0.20 FOR 2m
    LABELS {severity=\"critical\"}
    ANNOTATIONS {summary=\"Canary latency P95 regression >20%\"}

Runbook: on-call steps for failed canary

1) Detect
   - Pager triggers via CanaryLatencyRegression or CanaryErrorSpike alerts.

2) Triage (first 10 minutes)
   - Identify policy CRD name and canary selector: kubectl -n fleet-allocator get allocationpolicy -o wide
   - Query controller logs: kubectl -n fleet-allocator logs deploy/fleet-canary-allocator --since=15m | tail -n 500
   - Check metrics: run promql queries for allocator_* metrics and tenant evictions.

3) Immediate mitigation (if health gate failed)
   - Pause rollout: curl -X POST $CONTROLLER_SERVICE_ENDPOINT/api/v1/rollouts/{policy}/pause
   - Shift traffic off canary: kubectl label nodes -l <canary-selector> canary.offload=true (label is interpreted by node router to deprioritize)
   - If pause attempt fails or errors persist, run emergency rollback:
     terraform workspace select <canary-workspace> && terraform apply -var 'force_revert=true' -auto-approve

4) Post-mortem and permanent fix
   - Open incident ticket with labels [canary, allocator, gpu-fleet] and assign to owner team.
   - Collect artifacts: terraform plan diff JSON, policy CRD YAML, controller logs (last 2h), kube events (kubectl get events --all-namespaces --since=2h).
   - Run regression test harness (see infra/eval-harness) against the exact canary node topology to reproduce.

Safety and guardrails

- All Terraform changes that create/modify AllocationPolicy CRDs must run in a dedicated TF Cloud workspace with run-time approval configured.
- The module enforces an explicit reserve_target parameter; disallow empty reserve targets in production environments.
- Canary window default is short and must be explicitly extended in PR body to be allowed beyond 4 hours.

Ownership and contacts

- Primary owner: eng-infra (allocator-service subteam) — oncall rotation: infra-allocator@redwood.com
- Secondary owner: eng-serving-runtime for interactions with scheduler and kernel flags

Migration plan for existing policies

1) Audit all current AllocationPolicy records (kubectl get allocationpolicies --all-namespaces -o yaml).
2) Create a TF import mapping file (tools/imports/canary-policy-imports.tf) to import existing policy state into TF state.
3) Run a dry-plan in the module workspace to validate no-op diffs.

Security and IAM

- Controller runs under a dedicated service account with least privilege. Module creates IAM binding only when iam_bindings variable is provided.
- All controller endpoints are behind mTLS and authenticated via K8s service account tokens issued from the cluster OIDC provider.

Operational playbook for upgrades

- Module semantic versioning: major bump required for any backward-incompatible CRD change.
- Canary for allocator controller itself: deploy controller change using the same module but use the allocator to rollout controller changes as well (self-hosted canary).

Appendix: Useful CLI snippets

- Pause rollout:
  curl -s -X POST $CONTROLLER_SERVICE_ENDPOINT/api/v1/rollouts/my-policy/pause \n    -H \"Authorization: Bearer $(gcloud auth print-identity-token)\"

- Query canary health summary (PromQL):
  sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket{job=\"inference\",outcome=\"error\"}[5m])) by (tenant) / sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_count{job=\"inference\"}[5m]))

- Force revert via TF (last resort):
  terraform workspace select canary-my-policy && terraform apply -var 'force_revert=true' -auto-approve

Where the module lives

- Repo: github.com/redwood-inference/infra-modules (path: modules/fleet-canary-allocator)
- Design doc: linked internal design doc id: infra-allocator-design-2025-09 (refer to related_pages above)

Notes and open questions

- Should the allocator be able to operate across clusters (multi-cluster canary)? Current MVP is single-cluster.
- Does the allocator need to surface tenant-level rollback recommendations (per-tenant fallbacks)? Next-phase roadmap item.

If you are implementing or changing allocation policies, follow the CI/CD flow above and schedule a shadow run against a synthetic traffic profile before opening a production rollout.

-- Priya Natarajan, eng-infra (allocator-service)

6) promote-job: marks policy as steady and writes rollout metadata to audit log (CloudSQL).

Observability and dashboards

- Prometheus metrics exposed by controller: allocator_canary_state{tier=}, allocator_health_check_failures_total, allocator_rollout_progress_percentage.
- Grafana dashboard panels: Canary Latency Delta, Canary Error Spike, Eviction Delta Heatmap (by tenant), Node-level GPU Utilization (canary vs baseline).
- Alert rules (examples):
  - ALERT CanaryLatencyRegression
    IF canary_latency_p95_delta > 0.20 FOR 2m
    LABELS {severity=\"critical\"}
    ANNOTATIONS {summary=\"Canary latency P95 regression >20%\"}

Runbook: on-call steps for failed canary

1) Detect
   - Pager triggers via CanaryLatencyRegression or CanaryErrorSpike alerts.

2) Triage (first 10 minutes)
   - Identify policy CRD name and canary selector: kubectl -n fleet-allocator get allocationpolicy -o wide
   - Query controller logs: kubectl -n fleet-allocator logs deploy/fleet-canary-allocator --since=15m | tail -n 500
   - Check metrics: run promql queries for allocator_* metrics and tenant evictions.

3) Immediate mitigation (if health gate failed)
   - Pause rollout: curl -X POST $CONTROLLER_SERVICE_ENDPOINT/api/v1/rollouts/{policy}/pause
   - Shift traffic off canary: kubectl label nodes -l <canary-selector> canary.offload=true (label is interpreted by node router to deprioritize)
   - If pause attempt fails or errors persist, run emergency rollback:
     terraform workspace select <canary-workspace> && terraform apply -var 'force_revert=true' -auto-approve

4) Post-mortem and permanent fix
   - Open incident ticket with labels [canary, allocator, gpu-fleet] and assign to owner team.
   - Collect artifacts: terraform plan diff JSON, policy CRD YAML, controller logs (last 2h), kube events (kubectl get events --all-namespaces --since=2h).
   - Run regression test harness (see infra/eval-harness) against the exact canary node topology to reproduce.

Safety and guardrails

- All Terraform changes that create/modify AllocationPolicy CRDs must run in a dedicated TF Cloud workspace with run-time approval configured.
- The module enforces an explicit reserve_target parameter; disallow empty reserve targets in production environments.
- Canary window default is short and must be explicitly extended in PR body to be allowed beyond 4 hours.

Ownership and contacts

- Primary owner: eng-infra (allocator-service subteam) — oncall rotation: infra-allocator@redwood.com
- Secondary owner: eng-serving-runtime for interactions with scheduler and kernel flags

Migration plan for existing policies

1) Audit all current AllocationPolicy records (kubectl get allocationpolicies --all-namespaces -o yaml).
2) Create a TF import mapping file (tools/imports/canary-policy-imports.tf) to import existing policy state into TF state.
3) Run a dry-plan in the module workspace to validate no-op diffs.

Security and IAM

- Controller runs under a dedicated service account with least privilege. Module creates IAM binding only when iam_bindings variable is provided.
- All controller endpoints are behind mTLS and authenticated via K8s service account tokens issued from the cluster OIDC provider.

Operational playbook for upgrades

- Module semantic versioning: major bump required for any backward-incompatible CRD change.
- Canary for allocator controller itself: deploy controller change using the same module but use the allocator to rollout controller changes as well (self-hosted canary).

Appendix: Useful CLI snippets

- Pause rollout:
  curl -s -X POST $CONTROLLER_SERVICE_ENDPOINT/api/v1/rollouts/my-policy/pause 
    -H \"Authorization: Bearer $(gcloud auth print-identity-token)\"

- Query canary health summary (PromQL):
  sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket{job=\"inference\",outcome=\"error\"}[5m])) by (tenant) / sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_count{job=\"inference\"}[5m]))

- Force revert via TF (last resort):
  terraform workspace select canary-my-policy && terraform apply -var 'force_revert=true' -auto-approve

Where the module lives

- Repo: github.com/redwood-inference/infra-modules (path: modules/fleet-canary-allocator)
- Design doc: linked internal design doc id: infra-allocator-design-2025-09 (refer to related_pages above)

Notes and open questions

- Should the allocator be able to operate across clusters (multi-cluster canary)? Current MVP is single-cluster.
- Does the allocator need to surface tenant-level rollback recommendations (per-tenant fallbacks)? Next-phase roadmap item.

If you are implementing or changing allocation policies, follow the CI/CD flow above and schedule a shadow run against a synthetic traffic profile before opening a production rollout.

-- Priya Natarajan, eng-infra (allocator-service)
text:
Summary

This document defines the design, Terraform module interface, CI/CD integration, and on-call runbook for the Fleet Canary Allocator: a small control-plane component that performs safe, SLO-driven gradual rollouts of allocation rules and GPU placement policies across heterogeneous GPU pools. The primary goal is to minimize blast radius for policy changes that affect tenant placement, preemption behavior, and burst capacity while keeping rollout automation auditable and reversible.

Why this exists

- We frequently change allocation policies (fair-share weights, priority buckets, spot fallback policy) and kernel/runtime flags that affect performance and preemption probability.
- A full fleet-wide change can cause regressions in latency/SLOs or tenant evictions if there is an unseen interaction with topology or the scheduler.
- Existing canary tooling targets provisioning; this module ties Terraform-driven policy changes to a staged placement rollout and provides an automated fallback and remediation path.

Scope and Goals

- Provide a Terraform module that deploys the Canary Allocator with opinionated defaults for Redwood GPU fleets.
- Expose variables for canary population sizing, ramp cadence, health checks, and failover targets.
- Integrate with the CI/CD canary pipeline so every policy change is gated by plan-time validation and short-circuitable rollout.
- Offer an on-call runbook covering detection, mitigation, and postmortem guidance for a failed canary.

Non-goals

- Replacing core scheduler logic: the allocator orchestrates placement policy application and traffic-shifting, but does not modify underlying scheduler algorithms.
- End-user tenant-level billing decisions.

High-level design

Components

1) Canary Allocator Controller (K8s Deployment)
   - Watches Policy CRDs and orchestrates staged application across target GPU pools.
   - Uses allocation tiers: canary, ramp, steady, reserve.
   - Exposes REST endpoints for manual pause/rollback and metrics (Prometheus).

2) Policy CRD (YAML)
   - Holds allocation policy payload (weights, priority buckets, preemption thresholds, KV cache affinity tags).

3) Terraform Module
   - Encapsulates K8s manifests, RBAC, service account IAM bindings, and optional external canary targets (CloudRun Lambda).

4) CI/CD Integration (Buildkite + TF Cloud workspace)
   - Plan + policy linting stage -> canary deployment stage -> automated health gate -> ramp stage -> steady stage.

Allocation tier semantics

- canary: 1–3 nodes or 0.5–2% of active capacity, whichever is larger. Short window (10–30 minutes) for quick validation.
- ramp: incremental increase to 10–25% over configurable period (1–24 hours) with continuous health checks.
- steady: remaining production capacity.
- reserve: preconfigured fallback capacity identified at plan time for immediate traffic shift on failure.

SLOs and health checks

- Primary SLOs to monitor during canary: 95th percentile token latency (SLO delta threshold +20%), error rate (>= 5xx) increase > 0.5% absolute, tenant eviction rate delta > 1 per hour, and queue-time percentiles.
- Checks performed every 60s. If any check crosses threshold for 3 consecutive samples, the health gate fails.

Terraform module interface (example)

Note: this is a high-level excerpt — full module lives in the terraform registry module path referenced below.

Required variables (Terraform var names)

- cluster_name (string) — k8s cluster to deploy into
- namespace (string) — namespace for controller and CRDs
- canary_capacity_pct (number) — target percent for initial canary population (default 1.0)
- ramp_duration_minutes (number) — time to reach ramp target (default 120)
- health_checks (list) — list of named health checks to run (default ["latency_p95", "error_rate"])
- reserve_target (string) — label selector for reserve pool nodes
- iam_bindings (map) — optional map of IAM roles to service-account emails

Outputs

- controller_service_endpoint (string)
- canary_policy_crd_id (string)

Sample terraform usage (snippet)

module "fleet_canary_allocator" {
  source = "git::https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra-modules.git//fleet-canary-allocator"
  cluster_name = var.k8s_cluster_name
  namespace = "fleet-allocator"
  canary_capacity_pct = 1.5
  ramp_duration_minutes = 180
  health_checks = ["latency_p95","error_rate","eviction_rate"]
  reserve_target = "gpu-pool:reserve-v2"
}

Policy CRD example (extracted)

apiVersion: infra.redwood.ai/v1alpha1
kind: AllocationPolicy
metadata:
  name: preempt-threshold-adjust-2026-02
spec:
  policyPayload:
    preemption_threshold: 0.65
    priority_weights:
      enterprise: 1.0
      standard: 0.7
    kv_cache_affinity: ["tenant-critical"]
  rolloutStrategy:
    tiers: ["canary", "ramp", "steady"]
    canaryTargetSelector: "topology.kubernetes.io/zone==us-west1-b"
    rampSteps: 4
    stepDurationMinutes: 45

CI/CD flow (Buildkite)

1) pre-commit: policy linting (policy-schema-validator).
2) plan: terraform plan in isolated workspace. Policy diff is recorded as structured JSON artifact.
3) canary-deploy job: applies the policy as a canary-only CRD.
4) canary-health-gate: queries Prometheus metrics + log-based signals for the canary window. If pass, continue; if fail, mark pipeline and trigger rollback job.
5) ramp-job: incrementally expands target selectors per rampSteps.
6) promote-job: marks policy as steady and writes rollout metadata to audit log (CloudSQL).

Observability and dashboards

- Prometheus metrics exposed by controller: allocator_canary_state{tier=}, allocator_health_check_failures_total, allocator_rollout_progress_percentage.
- Grafana dashboard panels: Canary Latency Delta, Canary Error Spike, Eviction Delta Heatmap (by tenant), Node-level GPU Utilization (canary vs baseline).
- Alert rules (examples):
  - ALERT CanaryLatencyRegression
    IF canary_latency_p95_delta > 0.20 FOR 2m
    LABELS {severity=\"critical\"}
    ANNOTATIONS {summary=\"Canary latency P95 regression >20%\"}

Runbook: on-call steps for failed canary

1) Detect
   - Pager triggers via CanaryLatencyRegression or CanaryErrorSpike alerts.

2) Triage (first 10 minutes)
   - Identify policy CRD name and canary selector: kubectl -n fleet-allocator get allocationpolicy -o wide
   - Query controller logs: kubectl -n fleet-allocator logs deploy/fleet-canary-allocator --since=15m | tail -n 500
   - Check metrics: run promql queries for allocator_* metrics and tenant evictions.

3) Immediate mitigation (if health gate failed)
   - Pause rollout: curl -X POST $CONTROLLER_SERVICE_ENDPOINT/api/v1/rollouts/{policy}/pause
   - Shift traffic off canary: kubectl label nodes -l <canary-selector> canary.offload=true (label is interpreted by node router to deprioritize)
   - If pause attempt fails or errors persist, run emergency rollback:
     terraform workspace select <canary-workspace> && terraform apply -var 'force_revert=true' -auto-approve

4) Post-mortem and permanent fix
   - Open incident ticket with labels [canary, allocator, gpu-fleet] and assign to owner team.
   - Collect artifacts: terraform plan diff JSON, policy CRD YAML, controller logs (last 2h), kube events (kubectl get events --all-namespaces --since=2h).
   - Run regression test harness (see infra/eval-harness) against the exact canary node topology to reproduce.

Safety and guardrails

- All Terraform changes that create/modify AllocationPolicy CRDs must run in a dedicated TF Cloud workspace with run-time approval configured.
- The module enforces an explicit reserve_target parameter; disallow empty reserve targets in production environments.
- Canary window default is short and must be explicitly extended in PR body to be allowed beyond 4 hours.

Ownership and contacts

- Primary owner: eng-infra (allocator-service subteam) — oncall rotation: infra-allocator@redwood.com
- Secondary owner: eng-serving-runtime for interactions with scheduler and kernel flags

Migration plan for existing policies

1) Audit all current AllocationPolicy records (kubectl get allocationpolicies --all-namespaces -o yaml).
2) Create a TF import mapping file (tools/imports/canary-policy-imports.tf) to import existing policy state into TF state.
3) Run a dry-plan in the module workspace to validate no-op diffs.

Security and IAM

- Controller runs under a dedicated service account with least privilege. Module creates IAM binding only when iam_bindings variable is provided.
- All controller endpoints are behind mTLS and authenticated via K8s service account tokens issued from the cluster OIDC provider.

Operational playbook for upgrades

- Canary for allocator controller itself: deploy controller change using the same module but use the allocator to rollout controller changes as well (self-hosted canary).

Appendix: Useful CLI snippets

- Pause rollout:
  curl -s -X POST $CONTROLLER_SERVICE_ENDPOINT/api/v1/rollouts/my-policy/pause 
    -H \"Authorization: Bearer $(gcloud auth print-identity-token)\"

- Query canary health summary (PromQL):
  sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket{job=\"inference\",outcome=\"error\"}[5m])) by (tenant) / sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_count{job=\"inference\"}[5m]))

- Force revert via TF (last resort):
  terraform workspace select canary-my-policy && terraform apply -var 'force_revert=true' -auto-approve

Where the module lives

- Repo: github.com/redwood-inference/infra-modules (path: modules/fleet-canary-allocator)
- Design doc: linked internal design doc id: infra-allocator-design-2025-09 (refer to related_pages above)

Notes and open questions

- Should the allocator be able to operate across clusters (multi-cluster canary)? Current MVP is single-cluster.
- Does the allocator need to surface tenant-level rollback recommendations (per-tenant fallbacks)? Next-phase roadmap item.

If you are implementing or changing allocation policies, follow the CI/CD flow above and schedule a shadow run against a synthetic traffic profile before opening a production rollout.
