- Annual audit: full evidence and retention policy review executed by Compliance and external auditor if required.

Appendix: quick reference

- Emergency rollback command:
  traffic-router cutback --from green --to blue --reason \"slo-exceeded\" --ticket REL-2026-117
- Evidence bundle retrieval:
  evidencectl fetch --bundle EB-20260112-REL117 --dest ./local-evidence --auth $(gcloud auth print-access-token)
- Contact list:
  - Security: priya.natarajan@redwood.ai
  - SRE oncall: model-serving-oncall@pagerduty
  - Compliance: compliance-ops@redwood.ai

If you are executing a blue/green deployment with special constraints (multi-region, regulated customer, or cross-account access) contact Security for a pre-cutover review at least 72 hours prior to the scheduled window.

source.title: Proactive anomaly quarantine and capacity hedging runbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Summary:

This runbook describes the proactive anomaly quarantine and capacity hedging process used by Redwood Inference SRE to detect early signals of service instability, isolate affected traffic (quarantine), and allocate short-term capacity hedges to preserve SLOs while a remediation is performed. It ties directly to SLO thresholds and error budget policies and is intended for oncall, incident commanders, and automation owners.

Scope:
- Applies to customer-facing inference endpoints (hosted API) and inter-service routing between control-plane components and serving runtime.
- Not a replacement for full incident response; this is a containment and mitigation layer used before escalation to a SEV.

Why this exists:
- Transient anomalies (kernel/backpressure, noisy tenants, model-level regressions) can erode SLOs quickly. Rapid isolation + capacity hedging preserves customer experience and error budget while enabling safe remediation.

Runbook entry criteria (triggers):
1) Alert-based triggers:
   - P50 latency > SLO target for 3 consecutive 1-minute windows AND request error rate delta > 2x baseline in same windows.
   - Anomalous increase in 5xx rate > 0.5% absolute and >50% relative change.
   - Autoscaler failing to provision new workers for 2 minutes (API error 503 from autoscaler).
2) Observability-derived triggers:
   - Spike in prefix-cache-miss ratio + drop in request throughput for a model.
   - Rapid growth in queue depth (GPU-worker queue) crossing high watermark (see thresholds table).

Monitoring thresholds and quick reference table:
| Signal | Threshold (short) | Action | SLO impact concern |
|---|---:|---|---|
|P99 latency| > 2x SLO for 5m | Quarantine candidate model/route | High|
|Request error rate| > 0.5% absolute increase, >50% relative| Quarantine tenant routing | Medium|
|Worker queue depth| > 250 (per-shard)| Hedge capacity + scale workers| High|
|Autoscaler failures| > 2 min of failed allocations| Engage oncall + temporary hedging| High|

Ownership and roles:
- Incident Commander (IC): accountable for decisions to quarantine & hedge. Typical: SRE oncall.
- Owner service: team owning the endpoint/model. Responsible for root cause analysis and remediation.
- Platform automation: owns runbook automation, job templates, and rollback hooks.
- Observability owner: maintains dashboards and alerts used by this runbook.

Preparatory steps (pre-incident automation):
- Ensure the following Grafana dashboards are visible in the SRE folder: "Serving runtime: latency matrix", "Autoscaler: allocation failures", "Tenant request distribution".
- Make sure the quarantine routing policy templates exist in the platform repo: infra/traffic-policies/quarantine.yaml and hedging policies in infra/autoscale/hedge-policies.yaml.
- Validate that API keys and runbook automation roles are present in the vault path: secret/sre/runbooks/quarantine.
- Confirm canary cluster for hedged capacity is healthy (health-check endpoint returns 200 within 50ms).

Quarantine & hedging decision flow (high level):
1) Detect: Confirm trigger conditions and gather evidence (dashboards, logs, timeline).
2) Assess: Determine scope (single tenant, model variant, region, or control-plane service).
3) Quarantine: Apply routing policy to isolate affected traffic to a quarantine pool or redirect to fallback model.
4) Hedge capacity: Provision short-lived capacity (warm pool or burst nodes) using defined hedge policy to maintain throughput.
5) Observe: Validate SLO metrics improving; maintain quarantine for 30–120 minutes while remediation occurs.
6) Remediate: Owner service executes root cause remediation or rollback.
7) Restore: Gradually remove quarantine and deprovision hedged capacity with monitoring and rollback windows.

Detailed procedures (play-by-play):
A. Evidence gathering (10 minutes)
   1. Open the endpoint dashboard and note 1m/5m/15m P50/P95/P99 and error rate.
   2. Run these queries in Observability (example Prometheus):

   prometheus query: (single-line)
   
   sum(rate(http_request_errors_total{service=~"serving-.*"}[1m])) by (service)

   latency distribution snippet:

   histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="serving"}[1m])) by (le, handler))

   3. Capture top-5 abnormal tenants/models via request.count by tenant/model (summary).

B. Quarantine application (5–10 minutes via automation)
   1. If affected scope == tenant or route, run quarantine template (infra CLI):

   # Quarantine a tenant by id
   infra traffic apply --policy=quarantine.yaml --selector=tenant_id:12345 --reason=auto-detected

   2. If affected scope == model variant, route traffic away:

   infra traffic route --from=model:my-model-v2 --to=model:my-model-v1 --weight=0

   3. Confirm policy applied: check traffic-policy status and request distribution dashboard.

C. Capacity hedging (3–8 minutes to provision warm nodes)
   1. Identify hedging policy based on region and workload class. Example hedging commands:

   # Provision 3 burst nodes in us-west1 for serving pool mypool
   infra autoscale hedge apply --pool=mypool --count=3 --ttl=2h --reason=quarantine-hedge-$(date +%F-%H%M)

   2. Validate health of new nodes and ensure they register to load balancer.

   3. Temporarily increase prefix cache size for the pool if cache miss signal present:

   infra runtime config set --pool=mypool --prefix-cache-size=1.5x

D. Observe and iterate (continuous)
   - Monitor P95/P99, request error rate, queue depth, and token consumption for the hedged pool.
   - If metrics improve within 10 minutes and errors drop to baseline, maintain hedges for the configured TTL but start remediation.
   - If metrics worsen, escalate to SEV+response and run rollback of routing changes.

E. Rolling back quarantine and deprovisioning hedges (safety-first)
   1. Remove quarantine by reversing the traffic policy:

   infra traffic remove --policy=quarantine.yaml --selector=tenant_id:12345 --reason=restore

   2. Gradually reintroduce traffic weight over 10-minute windows (20% increments) while monitoring error budget burn rates.

   3. Deprovision hedged nodes:

   infra autoscale hedge remove --pool=mypool --tag=quarantine-hedge-2026

   4. Run a 30-minute watch window post-full-restore before closing the runbook.

Automation guardrails and tests:
- All quarantine and hedging templates must include a TTL and an automatic rollback if CPU or memory errors increase after application.
- Automation should reject policy changes if the impact path touches customers in the "restricted" compliance tier unless approved by security.
- Unit tests: simulate a noisy tenant and validate that quarantine removes >95% of offending requests from normal pool in CI.
- Integration test: dry-run of hedge provisioning via staging API and verify that autoscaler increments are honored in 60s.

SLO & error budget tie-ins:
- Use this runbook when short-term hedging can prevent SLO burn > 10% in a 1-hour window.
- If hedging fails to prevent error budget burn surpassing the breach threshold configured in SLO governance, escalate to the IC and follow the error-budget escalation playbook.
- Record all runbook actions in the incident timeline with tags: quarantine, hedge, TTL, owner. These are used by the SLO analytics pipeline for post-incident SLO reconciliation.

Post-action/Review checklist (immediately after restore):
1. Owner service files a 72-hour remediation plan if root cause unresolved.
2. SRE attaches runbook execution logs to the incident ticket.
3. Update automation if any manual steps were required beyond templates.
4. Schedule a short review (15–30m) within 48 hours to determine if additional reliability initiatives are needed.

Common failure modes and mitigations:
- Hedge nodes fail to register: check firewall rules, instance IAM, and service account quotas. Fall back to throttling policy to preserve SLO.
- Quarantine policy mis-targets: immediately revert and notify impacted owners; validate selector logic in staging first.
- Cache thrash after hedging: reduce prefix-cache-size to baseline and let caches cool down; consider warming cache with synthetic requests.

Runbook metrics (what we track for effectiveness):
- Time from trigger -> quarantine applied (target < 10min).
- Time from quarantine -> hedged capacity healthy (target < 12min).
- Reduction in offending requests after quarantine (target > 90% reduction).
- Error budget saved (tokens) during hedging window.

Related runbooks and docs:
- SLO governance and error budget lifecycle (link in related_pages).
- Service degradation and fallback runbook.

Appendix: example quarantine policy (YAML snippet)

```yaml
# infra/traffic-policies/quarantine.yaml
policy: quarantine
selector:
  - type: tenant_id
actions:
  - action: route_to_pool
    pool: quarantine-pool
    ttl: 2h
  - action: set_rate_limit
    qps: 10
```

Contact matrix (primary escalation):
- SRE oncall rota: sre-oncall@redwood.ai
- Serving runtime lead: runtime-lead@redwood.ai
- Platform automation: platform-automation@redwood.ai

Document history:
- 2025-10-14: Created by Priya Natarajan (draft).
- 2025-11-02: Added hedging policy templates and automation CLI examples (reviewed by Ethan Morales).
- 2026-02-03: Finalized TTL guardrails and added SLO tie-ins; published.

Appendix: example quarantine policy (YAML snippet)

```yaml
# infra/traffic-policies/quarantine.yaml
policy: quarantine
selector:
  - type: tenant_id
actions:
  - action: route_to_pool
    pool: quarantine-pool
    ttl: 2h
  - action: set_rate_limit
    qps: 10
```

Contact matrix (primary escalation):
- SRE oncall rota: sre-oncall@redwood.ai
- Serving runtime lead: runtime-lead@redwood.ai
- Platform automation: platform-automation@redwood.ai

Document history:
- 2025-10-14: Created by Priya Natarajan (draft).
- 2025-11-02: Added hedging policy templates and automation CLI examples (reviewed by Ethan Morales).
- 2026-02-03: Finalized TTL guardrails and added SLO tie-ins; published.
text:
Summary:

This runbook describes the proactive anomaly quarantine and capacity hedging process used by Redwood Inference SRE to detect early signals of service instability, isolate affected traffic (quarantine), and allocate short-term capacity hedges to preserve SLOs while a remediation is performed. It ties directly to SLO thresholds and error budget policies and is intended for oncall, incident commanders, and automation owners.

Scope:
- Applies to customer-facing inference endpoints (hosted API) and inter-service routing between control-plane components and serving runtime.
- Not a replacement for full incident response; this is a containment and mitigation layer used before escalation to a SEV.

Why this exists:
- Transient anomalies (kernel/backpressure, noisy tenants, model-level regressions) can erode SLOs quickly. Rapid isolation + capacity hedging preserves customer experience and error budget while enabling safe remediation.

Runbook entry criteria (triggers):
1) Alert-based triggers:
   - P50 latency > SLO target for 3 consecutive 1-minute windows AND request error rate delta > 2x baseline in same windows.
   - Anomalous increase in 5xx rate > 0.5% absolute and >50% relative change.
   - Autoscaler failing to provision new workers for 2 minutes (API error 503 from autoscaler).
2) Observability-derived triggers:
   - Spike in prefix-cache-miss ratio + drop in request throughput for a model.
   - Rapid growth in queue depth (GPU-worker queue) crossing high watermark (see thresholds table).

Monitoring thresholds and quick reference table:
| Signal | Threshold (short) | Action | SLO impact concern |
|---|---:|---|---|
|P99 latency| > 2x SLO for 5m | Quarantine candidate model/route | High|
|Request error rate| > 0.5% absolute increase, >50% relative| Quarantine tenant routing | Medium|
|Worker queue depth| > 250 (per-shard)| Hedge capacity + scale workers| High|
|Autoscaler failures| > 2 min of failed allocations| Engage oncall + temporary hedging| High|

Ownership and roles:
- Incident Commander (IC): accountable for decisions to quarantine & hedge. Typical: SRE oncall.
- Owner service: team owning the endpoint/model. Responsible for root cause analysis and remediation.
- Platform automation: owns runbook automation, job templates, and rollback hooks.
- Observability owner: maintains dashboards and alerts used by this runbook.

Preparatory steps (pre-incident automation):
- Ensure the following Grafana dashboards are visible in the SRE folder: \"Serving runtime: latency matrix\", \"Autoscaler: allocation failures\", \"Tenant request distribution\".
- Make sure the quarantine routing policy templates exist in the platform repo: infra/traffic-policies/quarantine.yaml and hedging policies in infra/autoscale/hedge-policies.yaml.
- Validate that API keys and runbook automation roles are present in the vault path: secret/sre/runbooks/quarantine.
- Confirm canary cluster for hedged capacity is healthy (health-check endpoint returns 200 within 50ms).

Quarantine & hedging decision flow (high level):
1) Detect: Confirm trigger conditions and gather evidence (dashboards, logs, timeline).
2) Assess: Determine scope (single tenant, model variant, region, or control-plane service).
3) Quarantine: Apply routing policy to isolate affected traffic to a quarantine pool or redirect to fallback model.
4) Hedge capacity: Provision short-lived capacity (warm pool or burst nodes) using defined hedge policy to maintain throughput.
5) Observe: Validate SLO metrics improving; maintain quarantine for 30–120 minutes while remediation occurs.
6) Remediate: Owner service executes root cause remediation or rollback.
7) Restore: Gradually remove quarantine and deprovision hedged capacity with monitoring and rollback windows.

Detailed procedures (play-by-play):
A. Evidence gathering (10 minutes)
   1. Open the endpoint dashboard and note 1m/5m/15m P50/P95/P99 and error rate.
   2. Run these queries in Observability (example Prometheus):

   prometheus query: (single-line)
   
   sum(rate(http_request_errors_total{service=~\"serving-.*\"}[1m])) by (service)

   latency distribution snippet:

   histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job=\"serving\"}[1m])) by (le, handler))

   3. Capture top-5 abnormal tenants/models via request.count by tenant/model (summary).

B. Quarantine application (5–10 minutes via automation)
   1. If affected scope == tenant or route, run quarantine template (infra CLI):

   # Quarantine a tenant by id
   infra traffic apply --policy=quarantine.yaml --selector=tenant_id:12345 --reason=auto-detected

   2. If affected scope == model variant, route traffic away:

   infra traffic route --from=model:my-model-v2 --to=model:my-model-v1 --weight=0

   3. Confirm policy applied: check traffic-policy status and request distribution dashboard.

C. Capacity hedging (3–8 minutes to provision warm nodes)
   1. Identify hedging policy based on region and workload class. Example hedging commands:

   # Provision 3 burst nodes in us-west1 for serving pool mypool
   infra autoscale hedge apply --pool=mypool --count=3 --ttl=2h --reason=quarantine-hedge-$(date +%F-%H%M)

   2. Validate health of new nodes and ensure they register to load balancer.

   3. Temporarily increase prefix cache size for the pool if cache miss signal present:

   infra runtime config set --pool=mypool --prefix-cache-size=1.5x

D. Observe and iterate (continuous)
   - Monitor P95/P99, request error rate, queue depth, and token consumption for the hedged pool.
   - If metrics improve within 10 minutes and errors drop to baseline, maintain hedges for the configured TTL but start remediation.
   - If metrics worsen, escalate to SEV+response and run rollback of routing changes.

E. Rolling back quarantine and deprovisioning hedges (safety-first)
   1. Remove quarantine by reversing the traffic policy:

   infra traffic remove --policy=quarantine.yaml --selector=tenant_id:12345 --reason=restore

   2. Gradually reintroduce traffic weight over 10-minute windows (20% increments) while monitoring error budget burn rates.

   3. Deprovision hedged nodes:

   infra autoscale hedge remove --pool=mypool --tag=quarantine-hedge-2026

   4. Run a 30-minute watch window post-full-restore before closing the runbook.

Automation guardrails and tests:
- All quarantine and hedging templates must include a TTL and an automatic rollback if CPU or memory errors increase after application.
- Automation should reject policy changes if the impact path touches customers in the \"restricted\" compliance tier unless approved by security.
- Unit tests: simulate a noisy tenant and validate that quarantine removes >95% of offending requests from normal pool in CI.
- Integration test: dry-run of hedge provisioning via staging API and verify that autoscaler increments are honored in 60s.

SLO & error budget tie-ins:
- Use this runbook when short-term hedging can prevent SLO burn > 10% in a 1-hour window.
- If hedging fails to prevent error budget burn surpassing the breach threshold configured in SLO governance, escalate to the IC and follow the error-budget escalation playbook.
- Record all runbook actions in the incident timeline with tags: quarantine, hedge, TTL, owner. These are used by the SLO analytics pipeline for post-incident SLO reconciliation.

Post-action/Review checklist (immediately after restore):
1. Owner service files a 72-hour remediation plan if root cause unresolved.
2. SRE attaches runbook execution logs to the incident ticket.
3. Update automation if any manual steps were required beyond templates.
4. Schedule a short review (15–30m) within 48 hours to determine if additional reliability initiatives are needed.

Common failure modes and mitigations:
- Hedge nodes fail to register: check firewall rules, instance IAM, and service account quotas. Fall back to throttling policy to preserve SLO.
- Quarantine policy mis-targets: immediately revert and notify impacted owners; validate selector logic in staging first.
- Cache thrash after hedging: reduce prefix-cache-size to baseline and let caches cool down; consider warming cache with synthetic requests.

Runbook metrics (what we track for effectiveness):
- Time from trigger -> quarantine applied (target < 10min).
- Time from quarantine -> hedged capacity healthy (target < 12min).
- Reduction in offending requests after quarantine (target > 90% reduction).
- Error budget saved (tokens) during hedging window.

Related runbooks and docs:
- SLO governance and error budget lifecycle (link in related_pages).
- Service degradation and fallback runbook.

Appendix: example quarantine policy (YAML snippet)

```yaml
# infra/traffic-policies/quarantine.yaml
policy: quarantine
selector:
  - type: tenant_id
actions:
  - action: route_to_pool
    pool: quarantine-pool
    ttl: 2h
  - action: set_rate_limit
    qps: 10
```

Contact matrix (primary escalation):
- SRE oncall rota: sre-oncall@redwood.ai
- Serving runtime lead: runtime-lead@redwood.ai
- Platform automation: platform-automation@redwood.ai

Document history:
- 2025-10-14: Created by Priya Natarajan (draft).
- 2025-11-02: Added hedging policy templates and automation CLI examples (reviewed by Ethan Morales).
- 2026-02-03: Finalized TTL guardrails and added SLO tie-ins; published.

source.title: Authorization Snapshotting and Retention Playbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview

This playbook defines a cross-functional standard for creating, storing, and packaging point-in-time snapshots of authorization state and related telemetry across Redwood Inference services. It covers: RBAC and auth configuration capture, associated audit log evidence, retention schedules with data residency considerations, SDLC gates to ensure evidenceable changes, incident response usage, and exception handling. The goal is reproducible artifacts for audits and investigations while minimizing surface area for sensitive data exposure.

Scope and Intent

- Systems: identity provider (IdP), internal entitlement service (entd), gateway authz policies, tenant metadata store, CI/CD change history, and audit log pipelines (Splunk/BigQuery).
- Use cases: compliance audits, breach investigation, post-incident root cause, quarterly evidence packaging for regulated customers.
- Exclusions: application-level feature flags unrelated to access control, ephemeral debug traces older than 48 hours unless tagged for incident triage.

Definitions

- Authorization Snapshot: an immutable bundle containing RBAC configuration, role bindings, service-account keys metadata, and a time-anchored pointer to corresponding audit log segments.
- Evidence Bundle: the Snapshot plus audit log extracts, change-provenance records, and retention metadata required to demonstrate control operation and enforcement.

Principles

1) Minimal exposure: sanitize secrets and PII before storing snapshots in long-term evidence storage.
2) Verifiable lineage: every Snapshot must include commit hashes, CI run IDs, and a hash of the snapshot file for tamper detection.
3) Region-aware retention: follow data residency rules per tenant/region. Evidence for EU tenants must remain in EU-bound storage.
4) Operational usability: incident responders must be able to obtain a Snapshot within 1 hour (target) for active incidents.

Retention and Residency Matrix (summary)

| Artifact Type | Retention (default) | Retention (sensitive tenants) | Storage Location | Notes |
|--------------|--------------------:|------------------------------:|------------------|------|
| RBAC config snapshots | 3 years | 7 years (gov customers) | encrypted S3 / region-specific bucket | include commit hash and signer id |
| Audit log extracts (index pointers) | 1 year | 7 years | long-term log retention (BigQuery EU/US) | extract uses time-windowed export to preserve PII controls |
| Evidence bundles (packaged) | 7 years | 10 years | secure evidence vault (KMS-protected) | only accessible to security+legal roles |
| Ephemeral debug traces | 14 days | 14 days | ephemeral store | truncated / redacted by default |

Snapshot Creation: process and automation

Owner: Eng Security (sec-engineering) / entitlement-service SRE
Frequency: automated nightly snapshots + on-change snapshots triggered by PR merge to main affecting auth code or config

Steps (automated):
1. Pull canonical RBAC objects from entd API: roles, role-bindings, policy versions.
2. Export IdP group-to-claim mapping and service account metadata (rotate keys excluded; only metadata fingerprints).
3. Record commit hashes for any infra-as-code (Terraform) or config repos used to apply changes.
4. Trigger export of relevant audit log window: +/- 15 minutes around change events; for nightly snapshots export that day’s activity window.
5. Build snapshot.json and compute SHA256 (store SHA in snapshot manifest).
6. Sanitize: apply redaction rules (PII patterns, secrets).
7. Encrypt with evidence-vault KMS key and store in region-appropriate bucket.

CLI example (pseudo):

# create a one-off snapshot for tenant=acme-corp
redwood-cli authz snapshot create --tenant acme-corp --window 2026-02-01T00:00:00Z/2026-02-01T23:59:59Z --output /tmp/acme-snap-20260201.json

# compute verification hash
sha256sum /tmp/acme-snap-20260201.json > /tmp/acme-snap-20260201.sha256

Evidence Packaging: checklist for audit-ready bundles

Required contents for an Evidence Bundle:
- snapshot.json (RBAC + metadata)
- snapshot.sha256 (hash)
- audit-extract.ndjson (time-bounded)
- ci-provenance.txt (CI run ID, commit hashes, PR ids)
- retention-manifest.yaml (artifact type, retention expiration iso8601, storage region)
- signer-record.json (service identity that created snapshot)

Packaging procedure (numbered):
1. Assemble files into a timestamped directory naming convention: evidence/{tenant}/{YYYYMMDDThhmmss}Z/
2. Generate a signed manifest using the evidence service's signing key: evidence-sign sign --path evidence/...
3. Store package in evidence-vault and update the global evidence index with storage pointer and access controls.
4. Notify security-legal slack channel with read-only link for auditors.

Audit Log Integration and Query Patterns

- Logging sources: gateway-authz (envoy filter), entd change events, IdP SCIM events, CI/CD apply logs, infrastructure orchestration logs.
- Recommended index fields: timestamp, tenant_id, principal_type, principal_id, action, resource_id, decision, policy_version, request_id

Example Splunk/BigQuery query (pseudo):
SELECT timestamp, principal_id, action, resource_id, decision, policy_version
FROM `redwood-logs.authz_decisions`
WHERE tenant_id = "acme-corp" AND timestamp BETWEEN "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z" AND "2026-02-01T00:30:00Z"
ORDER BY timestamp ASC

Secure SDLC and Change Controls

Gates to ensure snapshotability and evidenceability:
- Any Terraform/Helm change touching entd or gateway-authz requires: PR checklist items (unit tests + canary policy tests + evidence generation script updated if schema changes).
- All changes that alter role definitions must be accompanied by a smallfootprint change-provenance file committed to the repo (provenance.json) that lists who approved, when, and link to design doc.
- CI step: after merge, CI job must call "redwood-cli authz snapshot create --auto" to create the post-merge snapshot and attach evidence to the merge commit.

Incident Response: using snapshots during investigations

- Priority: for a live incident impacting authorization decisions, incident commander (IC) can request a fast-track snapshot (target delivery < 60 minutes).
- Fast-track steps:
  a. Incident responder runs redwood-cli authz snapshot create --fast --window <incident-window>.
  b. Evidence automation exports an audit-log slice with increased fidelity (including debug tokens) to a short-lived secure bucket.
  c. Security engineer verifies SHA and attaches snapshot to incident ticket.
- Preservation: mark the fast-track evidence as immutable for the duration of the investigation.

Risk and Exceptions Workflow

- Risk scoring: each authorization change is scored for blast radius (tenant scope, service impact), sensitivity (PII scope), and rollback complexity. Changes scoring > threshold require security review and extended retention of snapshots.
- Exception handling:
  1. Submit exception request via the Risk Portal with fields: component, justification, requested retention deviation, compensating controls.
  2. Security reviews request within 5 business days.
  3. If approved, add explicit retention override and link to evidence bundle; log decision in exception register.

Operational Roles and Responsibilities

- Security (sec-engineering): owns snapshot tooling, evidence-vault, and retention enforcement.
- Entitlement SRE: ensures entd exports are available and validates snapshot integrity.
- Infra/Platform: configures regional buckets and KMS keys.
- Legal/Compliance: approves evidence retention schedules for customers under contract.
- Incident Response: consumes snapshots and coordinates preservation for investigations.

Access Control for Evidence

- Principle of least privilege: evidence-vault access is RBAC-gated (security:read, security:manage, legal:read).
- Access approvals are timeboxed (max 30 days) and recorded in the evidence index with a justification string.

Tamper Detection and Monitoring

- Each Snapshot manifest stores SHA256 and signer metadata. Changes to stored artifacts trigger a notarization job that re-verifies checksums daily. Any mismatch opens a P1 investigation workflow.
- Monitoring alerts: evidence-integrity-alert (pager), snapshot-creation-failure (slack to #sec-tooling), retention-expiry-warning (email to data-retention-steerco).

Sampling and Cost Controls

- For low-risk tenants, full snapshotting may be sampled (e.g., weekly) with metadata snapshots daily. Sampling configuration stored in retention-manifest.yaml and requires a documented risk acceptance.

Appendices

A. Redaction rules summary:
- Remove full email local-parts unless tenant consents.
- Replace service account key material with fingerprints and last-rotate timestamps.
- Strip request bodies unless flagged by incident responder.

B. Example retention-manifest.yaml (excerpt):
---
artifact: rbac-snapshot
tenant: acme-corp
created_at: 2026-02-01T02:05:00Z
retention_expires: 2029-02-01T02:05:00Z
storage_region: eu-west-1
redaction_level: standard
---

C. Troubleshooting quickwins
- Snapshot creation fails: check entd API tokens and evidence-vault KMS permissions.
- Hash mismatch on verification: do not delete artifact; open an evidence-integrity P1 and preserve both the artifact and system logs.

D. Change log
- 2025-11-15: Playbook created by Priya Natarajan (initial draft).
- 2026-02-02: Added fast-track incident snapshot path and tamper-detection alerting (reviewers: Ethan Caldwell, Marta Ruiz).

Contact and escalation

- Primary: sec-engineering Slack: #sec-tooling, PagerDuty: sec-oncall
- Evidence vault owner: evidence-service@redwood.ai

Related policies and links

- RBAC standard (link internal): /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/least-privilege-evidence-and-access-risk-playbook-2026
- Audit log retention policy: /confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/immutable-audit-store-design-evidence-catalog-2026
- SDLC gating process: /confluence/security-and-compliance/secure-sdlc/sdlc-data-residency-audit-artifacts-manifest-2026

End of document

Overview

This playbook defines a cross-functional standard for creating, storing, and packaging point-in-time snapshots of authorization state and related telemetry across Redwood Inference services. It covers: RBAC and auth configuration capture, associated audit log evidence, retention schedules with data residency considerations, SDLC gates to ensure evidenceable changes, incident response usage, and exception handling. The goal is reproducible artifacts for audits and investigations while minimizing surface area for sensitive data exposure.

Scope and Intent

- Systems: identity provider (IdP), internal entitlement service (entd), gateway authz policies, tenant metadata store, CI/CD change history, and audit log pipelines (Splunk/BigQuery).
- Use cases: compliance audits, breach investigation, post-incident root cause, quarterly evidence packaging for regulated customers.
- Exclusions: application-level feature flags unrelated to access control, ephemeral debug traces older than 48 hours unless tagged for incident triage.

Definitions

- Authorization Snapshot: an immutable bundle containing RBAC configuration, role bindings, service-account keys metadata, and a time-anchored pointer to corresponding audit log segments.
- Evidence Bundle: the Snapshot plus audit log extracts, change-provenance records, and retention metadata required to demonstrate control operation and enforcement.

Principles

1) Minimal exposure: sanitize secrets and PII before storing snapshots in long-term evidence storage.
2) Verifiable lineage: every Snapshot must include commit hashes, CI run IDs, and a hash of the snapshot file for tamper detection.
3) Region-aware retention: follow data residency rules per tenant/region. Evidence for EU tenants must remain in EU-bound storage.
4) Operational usability: incident responders must be able to obtain a Snapshot within 1 hour (target) for active incidents.

Retention and Residency Matrix (summary)

| Artifact Type | Retention (default) | Retention (sensitive tenants) | Storage Location | Notes |
|--------------|--------------------:|------------------------------:|------------------|------|
| RBAC config snapshots | 3 years | 7 years (gov customers) | encrypted S3 / region-specific bucket | include commit hash and signer id |
| Audit log extracts (index pointers) | 1 year | 7 years | long-term log retention (BigQuery EU/US) | extract uses time-windowed export to preserve PII controls |
| Evidence bundles (packaged) | 7 years | 10 years | secure evidence vault (KMS-protected) | only accessible to security+legal roles |
| Ephemeral debug traces | 14 days | 14 days | ephemeral store | truncated / redacted by default |

Snapshot Creation: process and automation

Owner: Eng Security (sec-engineering) / entitlement-service SRE
Frequency: automated nightly snapshots + on-change snapshots triggered by PR merge to main affecting auth code or config

Steps (automated):
1. Pull canonical RBAC objects from entd API: roles, role-bindings, policy versions.
2. Export IdP group-to-claim mapping and service account metadata (rotate keys excluded; only metadata fingerprints).
3. Record commit hashes for any infra-as-code (Terraform) or config repos used to apply changes.
4. Trigger export of relevant audit log window: +/- 15 minutes around change events; for nightly snapshots export that day’s activity window.
5. Build snapshot.json and compute SHA256 (store SHA in snapshot manifest).
6. Sanitize: apply redaction rules (PII patterns, secrets).
7. Encrypt with evidence-vault KMS key and store in region-appropriate bucket.

CLI example (pseudo):

# create a one-off snapshot for tenant=acme-corp
redwood-cli authz snapshot create --tenant acme-corp --window 2026-02-01T00:00:00Z/2026-02-01T23:59:59Z --output /tmp/acme-snap-20260201.json

# compute verification hash
sha256sum /tmp/acme-snap-20260201.json > /tmp/acme-snap-20260201.sha256

Evidence Packaging: checklist for audit-ready bundles

Required contents for an Evidence Bundle:
- snapshot.json (RBAC + metadata)
- snapshot.sha256 (hash)
- audit-extract.ndjson (time-bounded)
- ci-provenance.txt (CI run ID, commit hashes, PR ids)
- retention-manifest.yaml (artifact type, retention expiration iso8601, storage region)
- signer-record.json (service identity that created snapshot)

Packaging procedure (numbered):
1. Assemble files into a timestamped directory naming convention: evidence/{tenant}/{YYYYMMDDThhmmss}Z/
2. Generate a signed manifest using the evidence service's signing key: evidence-sign sign --path evidence/...
3. Store package in evidence-vault and update the global evidence index with storage pointer and access controls.
4. Notify security-legal slack channel with read-only link for auditors.

Audit Log Integration and Query Patterns

- Logging sources: gateway-authz (envoy filter), entd change events, IdP SCIM events, CI/CD apply logs, infrastructure orchestration logs.
- Recommended index fields: timestamp, tenant_id, principal_type, principal_id, action, resource_id, decision, policy_version, request_id

Example Splunk/BigQuery query (pseudo):
SELECT timestamp, principal_id, action, resource_id, decision, policy_version
FROM `redwood-logs.authz_decisions`
WHERE tenant_id = "acme-corp" AND timestamp BETWEEN "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z" AND "2026-02-01T00:30:00Z"
ORDER BY timestamp ASC

Secure SDLC and Change Controls

Gates to ensure snapshotability and evidenceability:
- Any Terraform/Helm change touching entd or gateway-authz requires: PR checklist items (unit tests + canary policy tests + evidence generation script updated if schema changes).
- All changes that alter role definitions must be accompanied by a smallfootprint change-provenance file committed to the repo (provenance.json) that lists who approved, when, and link to design doc.
- CI step: after merge, CI job must call "redwood-cli authz snapshot create --auto" to create the post-merge snapshot and attach evidence to the merge commit.

Incident Response: using snapshots during investigations

- Priority: for a live incident impacting authorization decisions, incident commander (IC) can request a fast-track snapshot (target delivery < 60 minutes).
- Fast-track steps:
  a. Incident responder runs redwood-cli authz snapshot create --fast --window <incident-window>.
  b. Evidence automation exports an audit-log slice with increased fidelity (including debug tokens) to a short-lived secure bucket.
  c. Security engineer verifies SHA and attaches snapshot to incident ticket.
- Preservation: mark the fast-track evidence as immutable for the duration of the investigation.

Risk and Exceptions Workflow

- Risk scoring: each authorization change is scored for blast radius (tenant scope, service impact), sensitivity (PII scope), and rollback complexity. Changes scoring > threshold require security review and extended retention of snapshots.
- Exception handling:
  1. Submit exception request via the Risk Portal with fields: component, justification, requested retention deviation, compensating controls.
  2. Security reviews request within 5 business days.
  3. If approved, add explicit retention override and link to evidence bundle; log decision in exception register.

Operational Roles and Responsibilities

- Security (sec-engineering): owns snapshot tooling, evidence-vault, and retention enforcement.
- Entitlement SRE: ensures entd exports are available and validates snapshot integrity.
- Infra/Platform: configures regional buckets and KMS keys.
- Legal/Compliance: approves evidence retention schedules for customers under contract.
- Incident Response: consumes snapshots and coordinates preservation for investigations.

Access Control for Evidence

- Principle of least privilege: evidence-vault access is RBAC-gated (security:read, security:manage, legal:read).
- Access approvals are timeboxed (max 30 days) and recorded in the evidence index with a justification string.

Tamper Detection and Monitoring

- Each Snapshot manifest stores SHA256 and signer metadata. Changes to stored artifacts trigger a notarization job that re-verifies checksums daily. Any mismatch opens a P1 investigation workflow.
- Monitoring alerts: evidence-integrity-alert (pager), snapshot-creation-failure (slack to #sec-tooling), retention-expiry-warning (email to data-retention-steerco).

Sampling and Cost Controls

- For low-risk tenants, full snapshotting may be sampled (e.g., weekly) with metadata snapshots daily. Sampling configuration stored in retention-manifest.yaml and requires a documented risk acceptance.

Appendices

A. Redaction rules summary:
- Remove full email local-parts unless tenant consents.
- Replace service account key material with fingerprints and last-rotate timestamps.
- Strip request bodies unless flagged by incident responder.

B. Example retention-manifest.yaml (excerpt):
---
artifact: rbac-snapshot
tenant: acme-corp
created_at: 2026-02-01T02:05:00Z
retention_expires: 2029-02-01T02:05:00Z
storage_region: eu-west-1
redaction_level: standard
---

C. Troubleshooting quickwins
- Snapshot creation fails: check entd API tokens and evidence-vault KMS permissions.
- Hash mismatch on verification: do not delete artifact; open an evidence-integrity P1 and preserve both the artifact and system logs.

D. Change log
- 2025-11-15: Playbook created by Priya Natarajan (initial draft).
- 2026-02-02: Added fast-track incident snapshot path and tamper-detection alerting (reviewers: Ethan Caldwell, Marta Ruiz).

Contact and escalation

- Primary: sec-engineering Slack: #sec-tooling, PagerDuty: sec-oncall
- Evidence vault owner: evidence-service@redwood.ai

Related policies and links

- RBAC standard (link internal): /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/least-privilege-evidence-and-access-risk-playbook-2026
- Audit log retention policy: /confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/immutable-audit-store-design-evidence-catalog-2026
- SDLC gating process: /confluence/security-and-compliance/secure-sdlc/sdlc-data-residency-audit-artifacts-manifest-2026

End of document

source.title: Cost-aware streaming payload schema and metadata guideline
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Overview:

This guideline defines a standardized payload schema and runtime metadata conventions for cost-aware streaming inference requests across Redwood’s public and private deployments. It is intended for API engineers, SDK maintainers, runtime routing components, and observability pipelines. The goal is to make per-request cost signals explicit, machine-readable, and compatible with our streaming and batch transports.

Why this matters:
- Enables accurate per-request cost attribution and billing for streamed tokens and partial outputs.
- Allows runtime routing decisions (e.g., route to cheaper quantized variant) based on declared request constraints.
- Improves observability: deterministic dimensions for SLOs, per-token latencies, and partial-output error attribution.

Scope:
- Applies to: text generation streaming endpoints (server-sent events, chunked HTTP, gRPC streams) and chunked embedding/encoding flows.
- Excludes: non-streaming admin endpoints, internal control-plane RPCs.

Design principles:
- Backwards compatible: new fields are optional and namespaced under "x_redwood_*" to avoid collisions.
- Minimal runtime overhead: metadata is compact and typed; large payloads should be referenced by ID rather than embedded.
- Deterministic semantics: each field has a clear meaning and effect on routing, batching, and billing.

Canonical payload example (JSON over HTTP chunked or initial gRPC request):

{
  "prompt": "Translate to French: 'Good morning'",
  "max_tokens": 64,
  "stream": true,
  "x_redwood_cost_profile": "low-latency",
  "x_redwood_token_budget": 1000,
  "x_redwood_request_priority": "standard",
  "x_redwood_customer_request_id": "cust-1234-req-5678",
  "x_redwood_prompt_hash": "sha256:abc...",
  "x_redwood_expected_output_schema": "text/plain"
}

Field definitions (table):
| field | type | required | semantics | runtime effect |
| prompt | string | required | user input or instruction | used for model selection and token estimation |
| max_tokens | integer | optional | cap on generated tokens | enforces downstream truncation |
| stream | boolean | optional | whether incremental chunks are returned | enables streaming mode on runtime |
| x_redwood_cost_profile | string | optional | one of [low-latency, balanced, low-cost] | influences model variant selection and routing policies |
| x_redwood_token_budget | integer | optional | hard token budget for entire request | used by admission control and batching estimator |
| x_redwood_request_priority | string | optional | one of [bulk, standard, realtime] | affects queueing and preemption policies |
| x_redwood_customer_request_id | string | optional | customer-side id for tracing | correlated with billing & logs |
| x_redwood_prompt_hash | string | optional | deterministic hash for prompt caching | prefix/KV cache key derivation |
| x_redwood_expected_output_schema | string | optional | mime/type or structured schema id | used for tool-calling and schema validation |

Streaming semantics and partial-chunk metadata:
- Each stream chunk MUST include a lightweight metadata envelope when possible. For HTTP chunking this should be a JSON prefix line starting with "x-rw-meta:" followed by a compact JSON object.
- Envelope fields: chunk_index (int), cumulative_tokens_emitted (int), partial_cost_units_estimate (float), final (bool).
- Example chunk prefix: x-rw-meta:{"chunk_index":2,"cumulative_tokens_emitted":24,"partial_cost_units_estimate":0.006,"final":false}

Backpressure, batching, and admission control:
1. The runtime uses x_redwood_token_budget + max_tokens to estimate resource needs before admission to the serving queue.
2. For low-cost profiles, runtime may delay routing to lower-cost variants or batch longer; SDKs should provide optional hint x_redwood_max_latency_ms.
3. For realtime priority, admission must favor low-latency shards and may reject low-cost profile requests under heavy load with a 429 and structured error (see Errors section).

Error reporting and deduplication:
- Rejections must use typed error codes with a JSON body: {"code":"INSUFFICIENT_CAPACITY","retry_after_ms":2500,"request_id":"..."}.
- Client retries must include x_redwood_customer_request_id to allow server-side idempotency.

Observability and telemetry requirements:
- Mandatory traces: incoming_request -> route_selection -> model_infer_stream -> chunk_emission. Each span should carry x_redwood_customer_request_id and x_redwood_cost_profile.
- Metrics to emit per request: request_cost_estimate (gcu), tokens_emitted, stream_chunks, request_latency_p50/p95/p99, admission_decision (allowed|queued|rejected).
- Logging guidance: do not log full prompt bodies outside secure logging pipelines; only store prompt_hash and a short prompt_preview (<= 128 chars) for debugging.

Migration and rollout plan:
1. Phase 1 (SDK opt-in): SDKs and internal clients add optional x_redwood_* headers and support chunk prefixes. Backwards compatibility maintained.
2. Phase 2 (Runtime opt-in): Routers and admission controllers start reading cost_profile and token_budget for non-critical routing decisions.
3. Phase 3 (Enforcement): Admission control enforces token budgets for prioritized queues and billing surfaces the cost signals.

Checklist for implementation teams:
- API/SDK: add header/JSON fields and document examples; publish migration guide.
- Runtime: extend admission controller, update route selector to use cost_profile, support chunk metadata parsing.
- Observability: add mandatory spans and the metrics list; update dashboards and billing pipelines.
- Security: ensure prompt_hashing uses a KDF with per-tenant salt; redact prompt_preview in logs.

Operational impact and SLO considerations:
- New metrics enable a per-profile SLO for median cost-per-token with an alert on >15% drift quarter-over-quarter.
- Streaming latency SLOs should be defined per priority: realtime p95 <= 120ms; standard p95 <= 350ms.

Ownership and references:
- Owner: API Platform (engineering)
- Contact: priya@redwood.ai
- References: API rate-tiering playbook, telemetry ingestion guidelines, runtime batching contract ADR.

Appendix: Common edge cases and Q&A:
Q: What if token budget is exceeded mid-stream?
A: Runtime should emit a final chunk with final=true and error code STREAM_TOKEN_BUDGET_EXCEEDED; try to close stream gracefully.

Q: Are x_redwood_* names stable?
A: Yes. Additions may be made with minor-version compatibility rules; removals are deprecated for 6 months before enforcement.

This guideline will be reviewed quarterly or after major runtime changes.

source.title: Occupancy and Concurrency Audit Playbook for Serving Runtime
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview:

This playbook describes a repeatable audit process to assess and remediate GPU occupancy and request concurrency issues in the Redwood serving runtime. It is intended for on-call engineers, performance engineers, and SREs responsible for multi-tenant inference pools and kernel scheduler tuning. The goal is to detect pathological occupancy/concurrency patterns early, quantify their impact on latency/cost, and prescribe targeted mitigations.

Why this matters:

- Low GPU occupancy (SM utilization) or poor warp efficiency increases per-token cost and can drastically raise tail latency for short requests.
- Excessive concurrency without appropriate kernel crediting can cause kernel contention, eviction churn, and KV-cache thrash.
- Multi-tenant pools and dynamic batching increase the surface area for occupancy anomalies; automated audits help prevent regressions when we change kernel mappers, quantization profiles, or shard placement rules.

Scope and audience:

- Scope: GPUs used by Redwood-hosted runtime (heterogeneous pools), Dedicated clusters on RDW, and Private deployment models where we operate the inference stack.
- Audience: runtime performance team, serving runtime owners, SRE, release engineers.

Key concepts and metrics (short primer):

- SM Occupancy: fraction of Streaming Multiprocessor compute capacity active. Monitored with nvidia-smi dmon/ DCGM metric: gpu_sm_active and nvprof metrics like sm_efficiency.
- Warp Efficiency: fraction of executed threads performing useful work (warp_execution_efficiency).
- GPU Active Time / Kernel Time: total kernel execution time versus idle/wait time.
- CUDA Stream Utilization: concurrency of CUDA streams per process (useful for kernel overlap).
- Kernel Queue Length: number of enqueued kernels waiting execution per GPU.
- Token-per-second per-GPU and per-request token latency: application-visible metrics correlated with kernel metrics.

Acceptance thresholds (operational guidance):

- Avg SM Occupancy: target 30%–80% depending on model size. Small models (<=4B) should aim for >45% with batching; large models (>=70B) may be 20%–40% due to register pressure.
- Warp Efficiency: target >70%; investigate below 60%.
- Kernel Queue Length (p95): <= 8 for latency SLOs; if >16, treat as congestion.
- Kernel Preemption Events: zero preferred; any spike should be investigated.

Audit cadence and triggers:

- Scheduled audit: weekly automated run across production pools.
- Release-triggered audit: run after kernel mapper, quantization, or scheduler changes.
- Incident-triggered audit: triggered by sustained SLO degradation (p95 latency increase >20% or error spike).

Automated data collection (what to gather):

1) DCGM / Prometheus exporter snapshots for each GPU: sm_efficiency, sm_active, gpu_util, mem_used, sm_clock, power.
2) Kernel-level traces (perfetto / CUPTI) sampled for 60s across representative nodes.
3) Per-request runtime traces (trace-id keyed) including batch size, sequence length, model variant, and scheduling priority.
4) Kernel queue metrics and kernel metadata (kernel name, duration, per-kernel average latency).
5) KV-cache hit/miss rates and warmup time per request shard.
6) System-level metrics: NUMA statistics, PCIe throughput, nvlink/ nvlink errors.

How to run an audit (step-by-step):

1. Kick off collection:
   - Run the automated collector job (prod-audit-collector) for the target pool. CLI: 

   ```bash
   ./tools/profiler/run-audit --pool gpu-pool-2 --duration 60s --out /tmp/audit-gpu-pool-2.json
   ```

   - Ensure collector captures DCGM metrics at 250ms granularity and samples 1 CUPTI trace per physical GPU.

2. Baseline correlation (automated):
   - Run the baseline comparator tool to correlate p95 latency and per-GPU sm_efficiency. Example: 

   ```bash
   ./tools/perf/compare-baseline --current /tmp/audit-gpu-pool-2.json --baseline s3://perf-baselines/gpu-pool-2/2026-01-01.json
   ```

   - Output: CSV with columns (metric, current_p50, current_p95, baseline_p95, relative_change).

3. High-level triage (manual):
   - Open the generated dashboard link from the collector output. Inspect: SM occupancy heatmap, kernel queue length timeseries, per-model aggregated warp efficiency.
   - If p95 latency correlates with occupancy drop (sm_efficiency falls while kernel queue increases), flag as kernel contention.

4. Deep dive: kernel-level analysis:
   - Identify top 5 kernels by total time in the trace. Use the kernel map to attribute kernels to model layers (attention, MLP, softmax).
   - For each kernel, inspect duration distribution, occupancy-dependent latency (plot duration vs active warps).
   - Look for kernels with large variance or heavy tail (p99 > 4x median). Those are primary candidates for micro-optimization or fallback tuning.

5. Concurrency accounting check:
   - Verify kernel-credit accounting logs to ensure per-request credit consumption matches runtime expectations. Query: 

   ```promql
   sum by (pool) (increase(kernel_credit_consumed_total[5m]))
   ```

   - If observed concurrency > configured_max_concurrency by >20%, investigate admission control or credit leak bugs.

6. KV-cache correlation:
   - Join per-request latency with KV-cache hit rate. A simultaneous drop in hit-rate and SM occupancy often indicates aggressive eviction policies during high concurrency.

7. NUMA/PCIe check:
   - If occupancy low but GPU util high and kernel times are fragmented, inspect NUMA binding and transfer stalls. Use `numactl --hardware` and `perf record` to validate.

Automated remediation recommendations (ordered):

1) Apply immediate throttling: reduce admission concurrency for the affected model/pool by 20% (fast rollback).
2) Switch to a compatible lower-parallel kernel variant (runtime supports alternative kernel variants labeled fast-path, conservative).
3) Increase batching window for short-sequence workloads for 30s to evaluate impact.
4) Adjust kernel mapper hints to prefer fewer concurrent small kernels on the same GPU (see kernel mapper tuning snippet below).

Kernel mapper tuning example (config snippet):

- purpose: prefer sequential execution for kernels with low occupancy and high variance
- config keys (YAML):

  kernel_mapper:
    prioritize_small_kernel_serialization: true
    small_kernel_threshold_ms: 1.5
    concurrency_grouping_window_ms: 12
    backpressure_on_queue_length: 10

Metrics and dashboard recipes (Grafana/PromQL examples):

- SM occupancy heatmap: avg(sm_efficiency) by (gpu_id) over 5m
- Kernel queue alarm: (max_over_time(kernel_queue_length[5m]) > 12)
- Warp inefficiency alert (event): increase(warp_stall_cycles_total[5m]) / increase(instruction_issued_total[5m]) > 0.35
- Correlation panel: overlay p95(request_latency) with 1 - avg(sm_efficiency) (both normalized) to surface inverse relationships.

Incident play examples (short):

- Symptom: sudden p95 spike for short-text generation where throughput hasn't changed.
  Triage: run audit collector for 60s; find that kernel queue length jumped and sm_efficiency dropped to 18% on two GPUs.
  Remediation: throttle concurrency for model route, apply kernel mapper change to serialize small kernels. Result: p95 restored within 3 minutes.

- Symptom: slow cold-start for mini-batches after rollout of a quantized kernel.
  Triage: KV-cache miss surge and multiple small kernels with long tail.
  Remediation: revert to previous kernel variant for the model, schedule controlled rollout with traffic shadowing.

Decision criteria matrix (when to change runtime configs vs when to open a kernel engineering ticket):

- Quick config change (runtime): if issue is resolved by admission control, mapper hint flips, or batch-window adjustments and change reduces p95 by >15% in canary.
- Kernel engineering ticket: if top kernel p99 > 3x median and variance does not change with concurrency reduction or mapper hints, escalate to kernel team with trace bundle.

Ownership and escalation paths:

- Primary: serving-runtime-performance@redwood.internal (team lead: Priya Natarajan).
- Kernel engineering: kernel-core@redwood.internal (owner: Diego Morales).
- Oncall escalation: page SRE oncall if SLO breached after the second remediation step.

Artifacts to attach to the audit ticket:

- audit collector JSON and generated dashboard link
- top-5 kernel trace bundle (CUPTI/Perfetto) with timestamped ranges
- per-request trace subset (10–100 representative trace ids) with correlated KV-cache and scheduler decisions

Rollback playbook (if remediation regresses production):

1) Immediately revert the admission control change via runtime CLI: `runtimectl set-concurrency --pool <pool> --value <previous>`
2) Revert kernel mapper config using config rollback tag in S3 and trigger a config reload: `runtimectl reload-config --tag <previous-tag>`
3) If kernel variant was replaced, rotate traffic back to previous variant and monitor p95 for 10 minutes.

Checklist for a good audit report (what we expect in the ticket):

- Executive summary (one paragraph): impact, affected pools/models, summary of root cause.
- Data appendix: top graphs and a CSV of key metrics.
- Remediation applied and measurable effect (before/after numbers).
- Recommendations (short-term / long-term).
- Owner and ETA for follow-up (if kernel ticket created).

Related pages:

- /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling/adaptive-kernel-mapper-and-scaling-playbook-2026
- /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/numa-aware-shard-placement-eviction-playbook-2026
- /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/profiling-playbook/runtime-profiling-playbook-guidelines-2025

Appendix: Useful commands and example outputs

- DCGM sample query (JSON):

```bash
dcgmi dmon -e 250 -s 60 -o /tmp/dcgmi-gpu-pool-2.json
```

- Example PromQL for kernel queue p95: 

```promql
histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(kernel_queue_length_bucket[5m])) by (le, gpu))
```

- CUPTI/Perfetto capture invocation (example):

```bash
perfetto --txt -c /etc/perfetto/prod-gpu-capture.cfg --out /tmp/perfetto-gpu-$(date +%s).trace
```

Change log:

- 2025-11-10: Document created (initial draft).
- 2026-01-15: Added kernel mapper tuning snippet and PromQL recipes.
- 2026-02-02: Hardened remediation ordering and clarified ownership/escalation.

Notes and future work:

- Integrate audit collector with auto-remediation playbooks (low-risk throttles) once we have robust canary testing.
- Add ML-driven anomaly detection for warp efficiency drop that triggers a lightweight sampler trace.

Contacts:

- Priya Natarajan (serving-runtime-performance lead) — priya@redwood.internal
- Diego Morales (kernel engineering) — diego@redwood.internal

title: Occupancy and Concurrency Audit Playbook for Serving Runtime

Overview:

This playbook describes a repeatable audit process to assess and remediate GPU occupancy and request concurrency issues in the Redwood serving runtime. It is intended for on-call engineers, performance engineers, and SREs responsible for multi-tenant inference pools and kernel scheduler tuning. The goal is to detect pathological occupancy/concurrency patterns early, quantify their impact on latency/cost, and prescribe targeted mitigations.

Why this matters:

- Low GPU occupancy (SM utilization) or poor warp efficiency increases per-token cost and can drastically raise tail latency for short requests.
- Excessive concurrency without appropriate kernel crediting can cause kernel contention, eviction churn, and KV-cache thrash.
- Multi-tenant pools and dynamic batching increase the surface area for occupancy anomalies; automated audits help prevent regressions when we change kernel mappers, quantization profiles, or shard placement rules.

Scope and audience:

- Scope: GPUs used by Redwood-hosted runtime (heterogeneous pools), Dedicated clusters on RDW, and Private deployment models where we operate the inference stack.
- Audience: runtime performance team, serving runtime owners, SRE, release engineers.

Key concepts and metrics (short primer):

- SM Occupancy: fraction of Streaming Multiprocessor compute capacity active. Monitored with nvidia-smi dmon/ DCGM metric: gpu_sm_active and nvprof metrics like sm_efficiency.
- Warp Efficiency: fraction of executed threads performing useful work (warp_execution_efficiency).
- GPU Active Time / Kernel Time: total kernel execution time versus idle/wait time.
- CUDA Stream Utilization: concurrency of CUDA streams per process (useful for kernel overlap).
- Kernel Queue Length: number of enqueued kernels waiting execution per GPU.
- Token-per-second per-GPU and per-request token latency: application-visible metrics correlated with kernel metrics.

Acceptance thresholds (operational guidance):

- Avg SM Occupancy: target 30%–80% depending on model size. Small models (<=4B) should aim for >45% with batching; large models (>=70B) may be 20%–40% due to register pressure.
- Warp Efficiency: target >70%; investigate below 60%.
- Kernel Queue Length (p95): <= 8 for latency SLOs; if >16, treat as congestion.
- Kernel Preemption Events: zero preferred; any spike should be investigated.

- Incident-triggered audit: triggered by sustained SLO degradation (p95 latency increase >20% or error spike).

- Diego Morales (kernel engineering) — diego@redwood.internal

source.title: Tabletop: SLO-to-Capacity Runway Playbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Summary
-------
This playbook describes how to design, run, and learn from SLO-aligned tabletop simulations that trace an outage hypothesis from customer-visible impact through telemetry, error-budget consumption, and capacity runway decisions. The focus is practical: runbooks, PromQL examples, decision thresholds, and post-exercise actions that connect incident review findings to capacity planning and reliability initiatives.

Scope
-----
- Services: inference-api, routing-service, model-cache, realtime-ingest, dedicated-proxy
- Scenarios: latency tail regression, KV cache invalidation, cold-start surge, multi-tenant eviction cascade, model-quality regression causing retries
- Participants: SRE on-call, service owner, product PM, platform capacity engineer, customer success (for customer-impact simulations)

Goals
-----
1. Validate that SLO alerting maps to actionable capacity thresholds and runbook steps.
2. Exercise the decision path for short-term runway (minutes-hours) vs. runway extensions (hours-days).
3. Practice coordinated customer-facing communications and post-incident remediation that tie back to capacity allocations and model rollbacks.
4. Produce concrete, prioritized remediation actions for the reliability initiatives backlog.

Pre-exercise preparation (2–5 days)
-------------------------------
1. Define the scenario and hypothesis in one paragraph. Example: "Embedding generation latency p95 spikes from 120ms to 900ms after a quantized model rollout; error rate remains low but timeouts lead to client retries and SLO burn."
2. Identify SLOs and owners (table below).
3. Prepare telemetry dashboard and queries (Prometheus/Grafana). Ensure synthetic canaries simulate the same profile as production tokens.
4. Reserve a 90-minute slot and invite representatives from each owning team. Choose a facilitator who is not the service owner.

SLO mapping reference (example)
-----------------------------
| SLO name | Objective | Primary signal | Alert threshold | Escalation owner | Runway action at alert |
|----------|-----------|----------------|-----------------|------------------|-----------------------|
| inference-api-latency | 99th <= 800ms | request_duration_seconds{route="/v1/generate"} | p99 > 800ms for 3m | inference-service-oncall | Scale warm-pool + throttle low-priority tokens |
| embedding-throughput | 95th <= 250ms | embedding_duration_seconds | p95 > 250ms for 5m | embedding-team | Failover to quantized-variant or route to dedicated-capacity |
| model-serving-availability | 99.9% availability | up{job=~"model-server.*"} | <99.9% over 30m | runtime-team | Activate cross-region failover |

Telemetry examples
------------------
- PromQL: p99 latency for generate route:
  - "histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket{route=\"/v1/generate\"}[5m])) by (le))"
- Error-budget burn rate (1h):
  - "increase(slo_error_count_total{name=\"inference-api-latency\"}[1h]) / increase(slo_total_window[1h])"
- Canaries: use the snythc-canary job with the production prompt set (~200 tokens) to reproduce tail behavior.

Tabletop run format (90 minutes)
--------------------------------
1. 5m — Intro: facilitator reads scenario, scope, and success criteria.
2. 10m — Initial telemetry: on-call presents the dashboard and the first alarm.
3. 20m — Diagnosis window: teams propose hypotheses and quick checks.
   - Check model-change metadata: "/infra/model-versions/latest"
   - Confirm KV-cache hit-rate: PromQL "sum(rate(kv_cache_hits[5m]))/sum(rate(kv_cache_requests[5m]))"
4. 20m — Decision and action: select one primary mitigation for runway extension and one for immediate customer protection. Record time and owner.
5. 20m — Escalation + comms: draft an internal incident summary and a short customer bulletin.
6. 15m — Retro & next steps: capture findings, estimate effort, and assign follow-ups to the reliability backlog.

Runbook actions tied to capacity runway
--------------------------------------
- Immediate (0–30m):
  - Increase replicas of model-serving by +25% (if warm pool available) — Owner: runtime-oncall
  - Apply token-priority throttle for exploratory API keys: /runbooks/apply-token-throttle.sh --policy=exploratory
  - Enable short-circuit responses for heavy downstream features (embeddings) — Owner: inference-service
- Short-term (30m–6h):
  - Expand warm pool by provisioning reserved GPUs in the same region (if reserved capacity pools exist) — Owner: capacity-ops
  - Route a subset of traffic to dedicated tenants with guaranteed headroom — Owner: traffic-routing
- Medium-term (6h–72h):
  - Roll back model variant to last-known-good quantization profile — Owner: model-onboarding
  - Increase pre-warming cadence for larger prompt slices to reduce cold starts — Owner: performance-team

Operational tooling snippets
--------------------------
- Apply token throttle (example):
  - CLI: 
    $ infractl throttle set --service inference-api --label exploratory --qps 10 --reason "tabletop mitigation"
- Provision warm pool (fast path):
  - YAML snippet used by capacity-ops:
    "\n    warm_pool_request:\n      region: us-west-2\n      gpu_type: a100-80gb\n      count: 6\n      duration: 6h\n    "
- Alert creation template (Grafana):
  - Condition: p99 latency > 800ms for 3m
  - Notification: #oncall-sre, slack: @oncall-lead, runbook link

Decision criteria matrix (shortened)
------------------------------------
- If p99 > 2x SLO and error-budget consumption > 5%/hour: prioritize traffic shaping and immediate capacity expansion.
- If p99 > 2x SLO but error budgets < 1%/hour (cold-start storm): prioritize warm-pool expansion and cache prefill.
- If increased latency co-occurs with model-version change: prefer rollback over capacity expansion unless rollback risk assessed high.

Post-exercise outputs (deliverables)
----------------------------------
1. Incident-style action log (timestamp, actor, action, rationale, result).
2. Quantified capacity runway gap: compute additional GPU-hours required to keep SLOs for the next 24/72 hours at current demand. Example calculation included in appendix.
3. Prioritized remediation tickets with estimated effort and owner.
4. A short customer-facing FAQ template for recurring scenarios.

Appendix A — example capacity runway calc (string template)
--------------------------------------------------------
Inputs:
- Baseline throughput at p95: 10k reqs/min
- Observed p99 increase factor: 3x
- Average GPU concurrency (model variant A): 40 tokens/s
- Warm pool headroom: 120 GPU-seconds available
Simple runway estimate (24h):
- Extra GPU-seconds required = baseline_throughput * (p99_factor - 1) * token_cost_per_req * 3600 * 24
- Convert GPU-seconds to GPU-hours and compare to warm-pool.

Appendix B — example incident summary template
----------------------------------------------
- Title: [TABLETOP] scenario short title
- Time window: start / end (UTC)
- Services involved: list
- SLOs impacted: list with measured burn
- Hypothesis tested: one-line
- Actions taken: bullet list with timestamps
- Outcome: did actions restore SLO or reduce burn?
- Postmortem owner and due date

After-action guidance
---------------------
1. Link detected gaps to the reliability initiatives board. Classify each ticket as Capacity, Observability, Model, or Runbook.
2. For anything requiring >3d of engineering work, require a small design doc and a cross-team review.
3. Schedule a follow-up tabletop within 30 days for high-severity findings to validate remediation.

Ownership and cadence
---------------------
- Quarterly program lead: Priya Natarajan (eng-sre) — owns tabletop curriculum and metrics.
- Monthly exercises: each service owner runs at least one scenario per quarter with SRE participation.
- Annual stress test: cross-region full-day drill coordinated with platform and product.

Related resources
-----------------
- Runbooks: /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/slo-rollout-readiness-and-capacity-checklist-2026
- Observability spec: /confluence/eng-sre/operational-tooling/slo-telemetry-calibration-and-capacity-feedback-runbook-2025
- Incident comms templates: /confluence/eng-sre/incident-review/post-incident-automation-review-standards-2026

Contact
-------
For questions or to schedule a tabletop exercise, ping #eng-sre-runbooks or email priya.natarajan@redwood.ai
