source.title: Operational Commons and Process Patterns 2026
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview

Purpose: This document consolidates company-wide process patterns and canonical templates that operational teams (finance, security, people-ops, engineering, and product) should follow when executing cross-functional activities: access and permissions, change management, data management, procurement & expenses, vendor management, travel, and the internal templates used to standardize execution.

Audience: All Redwood employees who own or execute operational processes, and technical leads who implement controls in services and tools.

Scope: Applies to all corporate functions and product teams operating under Redwood Inference legal entities. This is the canonical set of process patterns; local variations require documented exceptions and a risk-owner signoff.

-----

1) Access & Permissions

Goal: Grant least privilege access quickly while maintaining auditability and fast recovery for incidents.

Key Roles and Owners:
- Access Owner: Team manager (regionally delegated)
- Access Audit Owner: Security Team
- Provisioning Tool Owner: Eng-Platform

Pattern: Request -> Approve -> Provision -> Review

Procedure (standard):
1. Submit access request via the Access Request form (Confluence template: "access-request-template") with justification and least-privilege role.
2. Manager-level approval required for all role grants; for admin-level roles, security review is mandatory.
3. Eng-Platform or HR (for people systems) performs provisioning within 48 business hours.
4. Access grants automatically expire after 90 days unless renewed.
5. Quarterly review: Security runs an automated orphan-account and role-scope report; team managers must attest within 14 days.

SLA and Controls:
- Provisioning SLA: 48 business hours for standard requests, 5 business days for sensitive roles.
- Periodic Audit: Quarterly attestation, monthly automated scans.

Revocation and Emergency Access:
- Emergency access (break glass) requires written post-facto justification within 24 hours and is logged to the central audit log.

-----

2) Change Management (services, infra, and major configs)

Goal: Minimize unexpected impact from changes while enabling rapid iteration on serving stacks and business systems.

Change categories:
- Fast-track: Non-customer-facing, low-risk changes (doc updates, non-prod infra tuning).
- Standard: Routine production changes with rollbacks and monitoring in place.
- Major: Schema migrations, model rollouts, capacity-related infra changes, changes affecting SLAs.

Approval Matrix:
- Fast-track: Owner approval + automated CI checks.
- Standard: Owner + peer reviewer + responsible SRE on-call signoff. Rollout plan and rollback playbook required.
- Major: Owner + Eng-Platform + Product + Change Advisory Board (CAB) signoff. CAB meets twice weekly; emergency CAB can be convened.

Deployment checklist (for Standard and Major):
1. Describe change, impact, and rollback plan in the change request template.
2. Pre-deploy canary in a controlled slice (1–5% traffic or designated test cluster).
3. Instrumentation: Ensure tracing, profiling, and key metric dashboards are updated.
4. Run smoke tests and the regression prompt-set where model-serving behavior might change.
5. Perform progressive rollout using feature flags and monitor error budget and latency SLOs for 30 minutes after each step.
6. If any threshold breach occurs, trigger automatic rollback and page the on-call.

Escalation thresholds (example):
- Latency increase > 20% from baseline for 5 minutes -> immediate rollback.
- Error rate > 1% for 3 minutes -> rollback + incident review.

Change windows: Standard business hours preferred; Major changes require notified maintenance windows and customer-facing communications.

-----

3) Data Management (classification, retention, handling)

Principles: Minimize retained PII, apply defense-in-depth, and document lineage for datasets used for training/eval.

Classification tiers:
- Public: Documentation, marketing assets.
- Internal: Product specs, runbooks, non-PII operational data.
- Restricted: Customer data, secrets, regulated datasets.

Retention and Access Rules (summary table in plain text):
| Data Type | Retention Default | Access Pattern | Owner |
| Customer logs (non-PII) | 180 days | Read for support & SRE | Customer Success |
| Customer PII | 90 days (default) | Minimal, audited | Security |
| Model training datasets | 365 days (review) | Restricted; request-based | Applied ML Team |
| Audit logs | 2 years | Security & Compliance | Security |

Data Handling Requirements:
- All Restricted data must be stored in approved repositories (VPC-only buckets, customer-dedicated storage) and encrypted at rest with KMS keys.
- Masking/pseudonymization is required before using customer telemetry for model training unless explicit consent/contractual terms allow.
- Data deletion requests must be tracked via the Data Deletion Request template and completed within contractual timelines.

Lineage and Provenance:
- Every dataset used for model onboarding must include a provenance document: source, transformation steps, retention policy, and contact owner.

-----

4) Procurement & Expenses

Objective: Streamline purchases and expense reimbursement while providing delegation controls and budget visibility.

Purchase thresholds and authority matrix:
- <= $5,000: Team manager approval.
- $5,001–$50,000: Department head approval + procurement review.
- > $50,000: VP-level approval + legal/compliance review.

Approved procurement flow:
1. Create a Purchase Request (PR) using the Purchase Request template with business justification, budget code, and vendor info.
2. Procurement verifies vendor due diligence checklist (insurance, security posture questionnaire if needed).
3. Procurement creates PO and routes to vendor.
4. Finance records the commit against the budget and tracks payments.

Expense policy highlights:
- Reimbursable items: travel, client entertainment (with receipts and business justification), approved training.
- Non-reimbursable: personal expenses, fines, or items without documentation.
- Expense submission: within 30 days of expenditure; late submissions require manager exception.

Travel approvals:
- Any trip > $2,500 requires explicit manager and travel coordinator approval and a travel safety review for international travel.

-----

5) Vendor Management

Vendor lifecycle stages: Selection -> Contracting -> Onboarding -> Monitoring -> Offboarding

Minimum due diligence checklist (selection stage):
- Legal & security review (standard clause checklist).
- SOC2 or equivalent evidence for vendors handling Restricted data.
- Financial health check for long-term commitments.

Onboarding artifacts required:
- Signed contract with SLAs and termination rights.
- Security questionnaire and remediation plan.
- Contact and escalation list (vendor-side and Redwood-side).

Monitoring cadence:
- Strategic vendors: quarterly business reviews (QBRs).
- Operational vendors: monthly KPI review and incident syncs as needed.

Offboarding checklist:
- Data return or certified deletion; revoke credentials and ensure accounts terminated.
- Final invoice reconciliation and retention of contract for 7 years (per legal retention schedule).

-----

6) Travel & Event Policy (high-level)

Principles: Safety first, cost-consciousness second, equitable access to travel for required business needs.

Booking rules:
- Use approved travel portal; economy class for flights under 5 hours, premium economy or business only with pre-approved manager exception.
- Hotel selection must use corporate rates when available.

Safety & Approvals:
- International travel requires manager approval and travel safety check for high-risk countries.
- Emergency travel assistance details and insurance coverage are available through People Ops.

Expenses and per diems:
- Meal per diem caps differ by region; itemize expenses and provide receipts for amounts over $75 USD equivalent.

Conference sponsorships and exhibitor booths:
- Additional procurement flow required for significant spend on booths or sponsorships. See Procurement section.

-----

7) Internal Templates and Checklists (where to find them)

Canonical templates (use and link names):
- access-request-template (Access & Permissions)
- change-request-template (Change Management)
- procurement-purchase-request (Procurement & Expenses)
- vendor-onboarding-pack (Vendor Management)
- data-provenance-checklist (Data Management)
- travel-approval-form (Travel)
- post-incident-review (Incident Response)

Template usage rules:
- Teams must not create local forks of templates without documenting the differences and obtaining an owner-team signoff.
- All templates have a metadata header that includes template owner, last revision date, and related policy link.

-----

Governance and Exceptions

Exception process:
1. Document the exception request using the Exception Request template, including risk assessment and mitigation.
2. Route to owner_team and governance board (depending on risk tier).
3. Approval requires a documented compensating control and an expiration date.

Periodic review cycle:
- Policy and templates review cadence is annual. High-risk controls (access, data handling) are reviewed semi-annually.

Metrics and KPIs (suggested):
- Time-to-provision (median hours) for access requests.
- Change success rate (percentage of changes with no rollback).
- Vendor security compliance coverage (%) for vendors handling Restricted data.
- Expense processing time (median days).

Appendix: Example change request checklist (text)
1. Change summary and owner contact.
2. Impact analysis and affected services list.
3. Rollback steps with clear commands or runbook references.
4. Monitoring dashboards and metric thresholds.
5. Communication plan (internal and customer-facing if relevant).

Contact and ownership matrix:
- Policy curator: Priya Natarajan (finance-legal)
- Access & Security escalation: Marta Alvarez (Security)
- Procurement lead: Evan Chen (Finance)
- Change CAB coordinator: Liam O'Connor (Eng-Platform)

Revision history:
- 2025-10-15: Created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2026-01-10: Added data retention defaults and travel threshold updates (reviewers: Evan Chen, Marta Alvarez).
- 2026-02-01: Published as company policy and linked canonical templates.

Related reading and links:
- Company Ops Index (search: company-operations-reference-2025)
- Security: Risk & Exceptions playbook

End of document.

title: Operational Commons and Process Patterns 2026
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This document consolidates company-wide process patterns and canonical templates that operational teams (finance, security, people-ops, engineering, and product) should follow when executing cross-functional activities: access and permissions, change management, data management, procurement & expenses, vendor management, travel, and the internal templates used to standardize execution.

Audience: All Redwood employees who own or execute operational processes, and technical leads who implement controls in services and tools.

Scope: Applies to all corporate functions and product teams operating under Redwood Inference legal entities. This is the canonical set of process patterns; local variations require documented exceptions and a risk-owner signoff.

-----

1) Access & Permissions

Goal: Grant least privilege access quickly while maintaining auditability and fast recovery for incidents.

Key Roles and Owners:
- Access Owner: Team manager (regionally delegated)
- Access Audit Owner: Security Team
- Provisioning Tool Owner: Eng-Platform

Pattern: Request -> Approve -> Provision -> Review

Procedure (standard):
1. Submit access request via the Access Request form (Confluence template: "access-request-template") with justification and least-privilege role.
2. Manager-level approval required for all role grants; for admin-level roles, security review is mandatory.
3. Eng-Platform or HR (for people systems) performs provisioning within 48 business hours.
4. Access grants automatically expire after 90 days unless renewed.
5. Quarterly review: Security runs an automated orphan-account and role-scope report; team managers must attest within 14 days.

SLA and Controls:
- Provisioning SLA: 48 business hours for standard requests, 5 business days for sensitive roles.
- Periodic Audit: Quarterly attestation, monthly automated scans.

Revocation and Emergency Access:
- Emergency access (break glass) requires written post-facto justification within 24 hours and is logged to the central audit log.

-----

2) Change Management (services, infra, and major configs)

Goal: Minimize unexpected impact from changes while enabling rapid iteration on serving stacks and business systems.

Change categories:
- Fast-track: Non-customer-facing, low-risk changes (doc updates, non-prod infra tuning).
- Standard: Routine production changes with rollbacks and monitoring in place.
- Major: Schema migrations, model rollouts, capacity-related infra changes, changes affecting SLAs.

Approval Matrix:
- Fast-track: Owner approval + automated CI checks.
- Standard: Owner + peer reviewer + responsible SRE on-call signoff. Rollout plan and rollback playbook required.
- Major: Owner + Eng-Platform + Product + Change Advisory Board (CAB) signoff. CAB meets twice weekly; emergency CAB can be convened.

Deployment checklist (for Standard and Major):
1. Describe change, impact, and rollback plan in the change request template.
2. Pre-deploy canary in a controlled slice (1–5% traffic or designated test cluster).
3. Instrumentation: Ensure tracing, profiling, and key metric dashboards are updated.
4. Run smoke tests and the regression prompt-set where model-serving behavior might change.
5. Perform progressive rollout using feature flags and monitor error budget and latency SLOs for 30 minutes after each step.
6. If any threshold breach occurs, trigger automatic rollback and page the on-call.

Escalation thresholds (example):
- Latency increase > 20% from baseline for 5 minutes -> immediate rollback.
- Error rate > 1% for 3 minutes -> rollback + incident review.

Change windows: Standard business hours preferred; Major changes require notified maintenance windows and customer-facing communications.

-----

3) Data Management (classification, retention, handling)

Principles: Minimize retained PII, apply defense-in-depth, and document lineage for datasets used for training/eval.

Classification tiers:
- Public: Documentation, marketing assets.
- Internal: Product specs, runbooks, non-PII operational data.
- Restricted: Customer data, secrets, regulated datasets.

Retention and Access Rules (summary table in plain text):
| Data Type | Retention Default | Access Pattern | Owner |
| Customer logs (non-PII) | 180 days | Read for support & SRE | Customer Success |
| Customer PII | 90 days (default) | Minimal, audited | Security |
| Model training datasets | 365 days (review) | Restricted; request-based | Applied ML Team |
| Audit logs | 2 years | Security & Compliance | Security |

Data Handling Requirements:
- All Restricted data must be stored in approved repositories (VPC-only buckets, customer-dedicated storage) and encrypted at rest with KMS keys.
- Masking/pseudonymization is required before using customer telemetry for model training unless explicit consent/contractual terms allow.
- Data deletion requests must be tracked via the Data Deletion Request template and completed within contractual timelines.

Lineage and Provenance:
- Every dataset used for model onboarding must include a provenance document: source, transformation steps, retention policy, and contact owner.

-----

4) Procurement & Expenses

Objective: Streamline purchases and expense reimbursement while providing delegation controls and budget visibility.

Purchase thresholds and authority matrix:
- <= $5,000: Team manager approval.
- $5,001–$50,000: Department head approval + procurement review.
- > $50,000: VP-level approval + legal/compliance review.

Approved procurement flow:
1. Create a Purchase Request (PR) using the Purchase Request template with business justification, budget code, and vendor info.
2. Procurement verifies vendor due diligence checklist (insurance, security posture questionnaire if needed).
3. Procurement creates PO and routes to vendor.
4. Finance records the commit against the budget and tracks payments.

Expense policy highlights:
- Reimbursable items: travel, client entertainment (with receipts and business justification), approved training.
- Non-reimbursable: personal expenses, fines, or items without documentation.
- Expense submission: within 30 days of expenditure; late submissions require manager exception.

Travel approvals:
- Any trip > $2,500 requires explicit manager and travel coordinator approval and a travel safety review for international travel.

-----

5) Vendor Management

Vendor lifecycle stages: Selection -> Contracting -> Onboarding -> Monitoring -> Offboarding

Minimum due diligence checklist (selection stage):
- Legal & security review (standard clause checklist).
- SOC2 or equivalent evidence for vendors handling Restricted data.
- Financial health check for long-term commitments.

Onboarding artifacts required:
- Signed contract with SLAs and termination rights.
- Security questionnaire and remediation plan.
- Contact and escalation list (vendor-side and Redwood-side).

Monitoring cadence:
- Strategic vendors: quarterly business reviews (QBRs).
- Operational vendors: monthly KPI review and incident syncs as needed.

Offboarding checklist:
- Data return or certified deletion; revoke credentials and ensure accounts terminated.
- Final invoice reconciliation and retention of contract for 7 years (per legal retention schedule).

-----

6) Travel & Event Policy (high-level)

Principles: Safety first, cost-consciousness second, equitable access to travel for required business needs.

Booking rules:
- Use approved travel portal; economy class for flights under 5 hours, premium economy or business only with pre-approved manager exception.
- Hotel selection must use corporate rates when available.

Safety & Approvals:
- International travel requires manager approval and travel safety check for high-risk countries.
- Emergency travel assistance details and insurance coverage are available through People Ops.

Expenses and per diems:
- Meal per diem caps differ by region; itemize expenses and provide receipts for amounts over $75 USD equivalent.

Conference sponsorships and exhibitor booths:
- Additional procurement flow required for significant spend on booths or sponsorships. See Procurement section.

-----

7) Internal Templates and Checklists (where to find them)

Canonical templates (use and link names):
- access-request-template (Access & Permissions)
- change-request-template (Change Management)
- procurement-purchase-request (Procurement & Expenses)
- vendor-onboarding-pack (Vendor Management)
- data-provenance-checklist (Data Management)
- travel-approval-form (Travel)
- post-incident-review (Incident Response)

Template usage rules:
- Teams must not create local forks of templates without documenting the differences and obtaining an owner-team signoff.
- All templates have a metadata header that includes template owner, last revision date, and related policy link.

-----

Governance and Exceptions

Exception process:
1. Document the exception request using the Exception Request template, including risk assessment and mitigation.
2. Route to owner_team and governance board (depending on risk tier).
3. Approval requires a documented compensating control and an expiration date.

Periodic review cycle:
- Policy and templates review cadence is annual. High-risk controls (access, data handling) are reviewed semi-annually.

Metrics and KPIs (suggested):
- Time-to-provision (median hours) for access requests.
- Change success rate (percentage of changes with no rollback).
- Vendor security compliance coverage (%) for vendors handling Restricted data.
- Expense processing time (median days).

Appendix: Example change request checklist (text)
1. Change summary and owner contact.
2. Impact analysis and affected services list.
3. Rollback steps with clear commands or runbook references.
4. Monitoring dashboards and metric thresholds.
5. Communication plan (internal and customer-facing if relevant).

Contact and ownership matrix:
- Policy curator: Priya Natarajan (finance-legal)
- Access & Security escalation: Marta Alvarez (Security)
- Procurement lead: Evan Chen (Finance)
- Change CAB coordinator: Liam O'Connor (Eng-Platform)

Revision history:
- 2025-10-15: Created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2026-01-10: Added data retention defaults and travel threshold updates (reviewers: Evan Chen, Marta Alvarez).
- 2026-02-01: Published as company policy and linked canonical templates.

Related reading and links:
- Company Ops Index (search: company-operations-reference-2025)
- Security: Risk & Exceptions playbook

End of document.

source.title: Incident Seeding and Warm Recovery Playbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook explains our controlled incident seeding practice and a standard warm-recovery runloop for LLM-serving incidents. Incident seeding is a deliberate, low-risk exercise where we inject small, observable faults to validate detection, routing, and warm-handoff paths without impacting customer-facing SLOs. Warm recovery is a set of prioritized, pre-authorized mitigations that restore service quality while preserving user sessions and KV cache state.

Scope
- Services: routing-proxy, runtime-gateway, kv-cache, model-pool, rate-limiter.
- Regions: us-west-2, eu-central-1.
- Tenancy: multitenant hosted API and Dedicated pools.

Definitions
- Seeding event: a controlled fault or degradation introduced for testing (e.g., 20% KV cache eviction, isolated model fallback).
- Warm handoff: transferring active incident ownership from automated systems to oncall with minimal impact to users.
- Pre-seed window: scheduled 15–30 minute window with notification and rollback controls.

Ownership and Roles
- Incident Owner (primary oncall): implements warm recovery steps and coordinates customer comms.
- Seeding Operator (SRE): schedules and executes seed operations during game-day windows.
- Recovery SME (runtime team): provides model/fallback expertise and approves rollbacks.
- Communications Lead (support/CS): publishes status updates using templates below.

Pre-seed checklist (before any seeding event)
1. Confirm pre-seed window and notify stakeholders (email, Slack #ops, status page placeholder).
2. Verify a clean baseline: SLOs within threshold for last 48 hours.
3. Snapshot observability: export latency histograms, token-cost metrics, KV hit rate, and recent traces.
4. Pre-authorize mitigations: cold-start rollback, model-fallback map, and scaling playbook accessible.
5. Ensure rollback procedure is documented and tested locally.
6. Open a short-lived incident on our tracker (tag: seeded).

Seeding patterns (low-risk examples)
- KV cache partial eviction: Evict cache for a single availability-zone pool for 2 minutes to validate cache miss surge handling.
- Model variant redirect: Route 10% of dummy traffic to a quantized-but-compatible model to verify fallback quality metrics.
- Rate limiter spike: Temporarily increase throttling threshold on a synthetic client to observe backpressure propagation.

Warm Recovery Runbook (ordered steps, target RTO: 12 minutes for partial degradations)
Preparation: The Incident Owner must have access to the runbook, cloud accounts, and console dashboards.
1) Acknowledge and classify (0–2 minutes)
   - Confirm seeded flag on incident ticket.
   - Set bridge if incident >5 minutes expected.
   - Record initial SLO deviations (p50/p95/p99 latency, KV hit rate, error rate).
2) Stabilize traffic routing (2–6 minutes)
   - If KV miss storm observed, route affected tenants to a fallback read-through path: runbook command example: curl -X POST https://runbook.internal/redwood/route -d '{"target":"fallback-readthrough","pool":"us-west-2-fallback"}'
   - Reduce cross-tenant blast radius by enabling tenant-level throttles for non-critical customers.
3) Preserve model state (6–9 minutes)
   - Pin active model variants for sessions where possible to avoid re-compilation churn. Kubernetes example: kubectl annotate pod/<model-pod> "model-lock=seeded-session-<ticket>"
   - If a model fallback is in use, check quality signals in eval-stream (prefix-match QA checks).
4) Targeted autoscale and cache warmup (9–14 minutes)
   - Trigger predictive warm-up for model-pool using synthetic warm calls: ./scripts/prime_pool.sh --pool us-west-2-fallback --rate 20
   - Gradually ramp traffic back from fallback in 10% increments while monitoring p95 latency and coherence metrics.
5) Validate and close (14–20 minutes)
   - Confirm metrics returned to acceptable thresholds and close seeded tag.
   - Publish status update (see templates).

Escalation and rollback criteria (fast paths)
- Rollback to pre-seed state immediately if: sustained p95 latency > 2x baseline for 10+ minutes OR error-rate spike results in >0.5% 5xx for customer-facing requests.
- Escalate to Recovery SME if model coherence score drops > 15% vs baseline during fallback.

Multi-tenant sequencing notes
- Always perform seeded cache eviction on a non-critical tenant first. Observe system-wide propagation for 5 minutes before broader target.
- Use tenant labels to correlate customer impact and avoid mass throttle flapping.

Game Day Scenarios and Exercise Design
- Scenario A: KV Eviction Cascade (30-minute lab)
  - Objective: exercise detection, routing, warm handoff and customer comms.
  - Steps: seed 20% eviction in one AZ, simulate 100 synthetic concurrent sessions across 10 tenants, observe backpressure.
  - Success criteria: automated routing reduces p95 by 30% within 10 minutes, no customer-facing errors persist beyond 15 minutes.
- Scenario B: Quantized Model Fallback (45-minute lab)
  - Objective: ensure quality monitoring and rollback triggers for model fallbacks.

On-call rotation integration
- Pre-seed windows are scheduled against oncall shifts; primary oncall must opt-in to broadcast.
- If a seed overlaps with a shift handoff, follow the Warm Handoff checklist (below) before seeding begins.

Warm Handoff checklist (when transferring an active seeded incident)
- Summarize actions taken, commands executed, and current metric deltas.
- Ensure next oncall has access tokens and runbook anchor link.
- Transfer any active synthetic warm-up scripts and their PIDs or orchestrator run IDs.

Customer Status Templates (use these verbatim on status.redwood.ai and in CSR replies)
1) Initial placeholder (within 10 minutes):
   - status.redwood.ai: "We are investigating elevated latency affecting a subset of inference requests in US-West. Engineers are executing a controlled test to validate route-level mitigations. We do not expect persistent customer impact at this time. Updates every 15 minutes."
2) Mitigation in progress (after stabilization actions started):
   - "Mitigation implemented: traffic is temporarily routed to a fallback pool to preserve request reliability. We are monitoring latency and coherence metrics; no action required from customers."
3) Degraded impact (if customers affected):
   - "We are aware that some requests may see higher latency or partial errors. We have a mitigation in place and are prioritizing affected customers. If you observe severe issues, reply to this thread with request IDs."
4) Resolution and follow-up:
   - "Service restored to expected levels. We executed a controlled test and reverted seeded changes. A post-incident note and timeline will be published within 72 hours."

Post-seed evidence & postmortem guidance
- Required evidence artifacts (attach to ticket):
  - timestamped metric exports (latency histograms, KV hit rate, throughput)
  - bridge transcript and participant list
  - commands and runbook versions used (git commit hash for runbook)
  - synthetic request IDs and sample payloads (sanitized)
  - quality signal snapshots for model fallbacks (eval-stream excerpts)
- Postmortem structure (suggested sections):
  1. Summary and impact (what customers experienced)
  2. Timeline (minute-by-minute)
  3. Root cause analysis (technical and process)
  4. Mitigations executed (with links to commands and artifacts)
  5. Action items and owners (target date)

Operational thresholds and quick reference table
- SLOs referenced during seed testing: p95 latency <= baseline * 1.5, KV hit rate >= baseline - 10%, 5xx rate <= 0.25%
- Rapid rollback triggers: p95 > 2x or sustained 5xx > 0.5% or customer complaints > 3 within 15 minutes.

Quick commands (examples)
- Check model-pool health: kubectl get pods -l app=model-pool -o wide
- Route traffic to fallback (internal API): curl -X POST https://ops-api.internal/v1/route -H 'Authorization: Bearer <TOKEN>' -d '{"pool":"fallback-usw2","tenants":["synthetic-tenant-1"]}'
- Export latency histogram (Prometheus): curl -s http://prometheus.internal/api/v1/query?query=histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))

Related pages and assets
- Link to the canonical incident command playbook (related_pages above).
- Repository: git@internal:redwood/incidents/seeded-runbooks.git (contains scripts and priming helpers).
- Observability dashboard: Dashboards > Incident > Seeded-runbook-latency-template.

Maintenance and governance
- Seeds must be scheduled and approved by SRE leads. Low-risk seeds may be run weekly; higher-risk seeds quarterly.
- All seeds are recorded in the Incident Seeding ledger (internal spreadsheet) with tagging for audit.

Appendix: example status page JSON template
{ "status": "investigating", "summary": "Elevated latency observed for a subset of inference requests in US-West", "impact": "Partial degradation for a subset of customers; mitigation underway", "components": ["runtime-gateway","model-pool-usw2"], "update_interval_mins": 15 }

Contact and escalation
- Oncall channel: #ops-oncall (pager: SRE rota)
- Escalation path: primary oncall -> SRE lead -> runtime team -> CTO on-call for Exec notification.

Document ownership and review cadence
- Owner: engineering (SRE). Review: quarterly or after each seeded exercise.

title: Incident Seeding and Warm Recovery Playbook
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook explains our controlled incident seeding practice and a standard warm-recovery runloop for LLM-serving incidents. Incident seeding is a deliberate, low-risk exercise where we inject small, observable faults to validate detection, routing, and warm-handoff paths without impacting customer-facing SLOs. Warm recovery is a set of prioritized, pre-authorized mitigations that restore service quality while preserving user sessions and KV cache state.

Scope
- Services: routing-proxy, runtime-gateway, kv-cache, model-pool, rate-limiter.
- Regions: us-west-2, eu-central-1.
- Tenancy: multitenant hosted API and Dedicated pools.

Definitions
- Seeding event: a controlled fault or degradation introduced for testing (e.g., 20% KV cache eviction, isolated model fallback).
- Warm handoff: transferring active incident ownership from automated systems to oncall with minimal impact to users.
- Pre-seed window: scheduled 15–30 minute window with notification and rollback controls.

Ownership and Roles
- Incident Owner (primary oncall): implements warm recovery steps and coordinates customer comms.
- Seeding Operator (SRE): schedules and executes seed operations during game-day windows.
- Recovery SME (runtime team): provides model/fallback expertise and approves rollbacks.
- Communications Lead (support/CS): publishes status updates using templates below.

Pre-seed checklist (before any seeding event)
1. Confirm pre-seed window and notify stakeholders (email, Slack #ops, status page placeholder).
2. Verify a clean baseline: SLOs within threshold for last 48 hours.
3. Snapshot observability: export latency histograms, token-cost metrics, KV hit rate, and recent traces.
4. Pre-authorize mitigations: cold-start rollback, model-fallback map, and scaling playbook accessible.
5. Ensure rollback procedure is documented and tested locally.
6. Open a short-lived incident on our tracker (tag: seeded).

Seeding patterns (low-risk examples)
- KV cache partial eviction: Evict cache for a single availability-zone pool for 2 minutes to validate cache miss surge handling.
- Model variant redirect: Route 10% of dummy traffic to a quantized-but-compatible model to verify fallback quality metrics.
- Rate limiter spike: Temporarily increase throttling threshold on a synthetic client to observe backpressure propagation.

Warm Recovery Runbook (ordered steps, target RTO: 12 minutes for partial degradations)
Preparation: The Incident Owner must have access to the runbook, cloud accounts, and console dashboards.
1) Acknowledge and classify (0–2 minutes)
   - Confirm seeded flag on incident ticket.
   - Set bridge if incident >5 minutes expected.
   - Record initial SLO deviations (p50/p95/p99 latency, KV hit rate, error rate).
2) Stabilize traffic routing (2–6 minutes)
   - If KV miss storm observed, route affected tenants to a fallback read-through path: runbook command example: curl -X POST https://runbook.internal/redwood/route -d '{"target":"fallback-readthrough","pool":"us-west-2-fallback"}'
   - Reduce cross-tenant blast radius by enabling tenant-level throttles for non-critical customers.
3) Preserve model state (6–9 minutes)
   - Pin active model variants for sessions where possible to avoid re-compilation churn. Kubernetes example: kubectl annotate pod/<model-pod> "model-lock=seeded-session-<ticket>"
   - If a model fallback is in use, check quality signals in eval-stream (prefix-match QA checks).
4) Targeted autoscale and cache warmup (9–14 minutes)
   - Trigger predictive warm-up for model-pool using synthetic warm calls: ./scripts/prime_pool.sh --pool us-west-2-fallback --rate 20
   - Gradually ramp traffic back from fallback in 10% increments while monitoring p95 latency and coherence metrics.
5) Validate and close (14–20 minutes)
   - Confirm metrics returned to acceptable thresholds and close seeded tag.
   - Publish status update (see templates).

Escalation and rollback criteria (fast paths)
- Rollback to pre-seed state immediately if: sustained p95 latency > 2x baseline for 10+ minutes OR error-rate spike results in >0.5% 5xx for customer-facing requests.
- Escalate to Recovery SME if model coherence score drops > 15% vs baseline during fallback.

Multi-tenant sequencing notes
- Always perform seeded cache eviction on a non-critical tenant first. Observe system-wide propagation for 5 minutes before broader target.
- Use tenant labels to correlate customer impact and avoid mass throttle flapping.

Game Day Scenarios and Exercise Design
- Scenario A: KV Eviction Cascade (30-minute lab)
  - Objective: exercise detection, routing, warm handoff and customer comms.
  - Steps: seed 20% eviction in one AZ, simulate 100 synthetic concurrent sessions across 10 tenants, observe backpressure.
  - Success criteria: automated routing reduces p95 by 30% within 10 minutes, no customer-facing errors persist beyond 15 minutes.
- Scenario B: Quantized Model Fallback (45-minute lab)
  - Objective: ensure quality monitoring and rollback triggers for model fallbacks.

On-call rotation integration
- Pre-seed windows are scheduled against oncall shifts; primary oncall must opt-in to broadcast.
- If a seed overlaps with a shift handoff, follow the Warm Handoff checklist (below) before seeding begins.

Warm Handoff checklist (when transferring an active seeded incident)
- Summarize actions taken, commands executed, and current metric deltas.
- Ensure next oncall has access tokens and runbook anchor link.
- Transfer any active synthetic warm-up scripts and their PIDs or orchestrator run IDs.

Customer Status Templates (use these verbatim on status.redwood.ai and in CSR replies)
1) Initial placeholder (within 10 minutes):
   - status.redwood.ai: "We are investigating elevated latency affecting a subset of inference requests in US-West. Engineers are executing a controlled test to validate route-level mitigations. We do not expect persistent customer impact at this time. Updates every 15 minutes."
2) Mitigation in progress (after stabilization actions started):
   - "Mitigation implemented: traffic is temporarily routed to a fallback pool to preserve request reliability. We are monitoring latency and coherence metrics; no action required from customers."
3) Degraded impact (if customers affected):
   - "We are aware that some requests may see higher latency or partial errors. We have a mitigation in place and are prioritizing affected customers. If you observe severe issues, reply to this thread with request IDs."
4) Resolution and follow-up:
   - "Service restored to expected levels. We executed a controlled test and reverted seeded changes. A post-incident note and timeline will be published within 72 hours."

Post-seed evidence & postmortem guidance
- Required evidence artifacts (attach to ticket):
  - timestamped metric exports (latency histograms, KV hit rate, throughput)
  - bridge transcript and participant list
  - commands and runbook versions used (git commit hash for runbook)
  - synthetic request IDs and sample payloads (sanitized)
  - quality signal snapshots for model fallbacks (eval-stream excerpts)
- Postmortem structure (suggested sections):
  1. Summary and impact (what customers experienced)
  2. Timeline (minute-by-minute)
  3. Root cause analysis (technical and process)
  4. Mitigations executed (with links to commands and artifacts)
  5. Action items and owners (target date)

Operational thresholds and quick reference table
- SLOs referenced during seed testing: p95 latency <= baseline * 1.5, KV hit rate >= baseline - 10%, 5xx rate <= 0.25%
- Rapid rollback triggers: p95 > 2x or sustained 5xx > 0.5% or customer complaints > 3 within 15 minutes.

Quick commands (examples)
- Check model-pool health: kubectl get pods -l app=model-pool -o wide
- Route traffic to fallback (internal API): curl -X POST https://ops-api.internal/v1/route -H 'Authorization: Bearer <TOKEN>' -d '{"pool":"fallback-usw2","tenants":["synthetic-tenant-1"]}'
- Export latency histogram (Prometheus): curl -s http://prometheus.internal/api/v1/query?query=histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))

Related pages and assets
- Link to the canonical incident command playbook (related_pages above).
- Repository: git@internal:redwood/incidents/seeded-runbooks.git (contains scripts and priming helpers).
- Observability dashboard: Dashboards > Incident > Seeded-runbook-latency-template.

Maintenance and governance
- Seeds must be scheduled and approved by SRE leads. Low-risk seeds may be run weekly; higher-risk seeds quarterly.
- All seeds are recorded in the Incident Seeding ledger (internal spreadsheet) with tagging for audit.

Appendix: example status page JSON template
{ "status": "investigating", "summary": "Elevated latency observed for a subset of inference requests in US-West", "impact": "Partial degradation for a subset of customers; mitigation underway", "components": ["runtime-gateway","model-pool-usw2"], "update_interval_mins": 15 }

Contact and escalation
- Oncall channel: #ops-oncall (pager: SRE rota)
- Escalation path: primary oncall -> SRE lead -> runtime team -> CTO on-call for Exec notification.

Document ownership and review cadence
- Owner: engineering (SRE). Review: quarterly or after each seeded exercise.

Last updated: 2026-01-28 (runbook refinement after Q4 game-day).

source.title: GPU build fan-out and stampede mitigation guide
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview

Purpose: This guide describes patterns, Terraform/IaC examples, monitoring signals, and an operational runbook to prevent and mitigate GPU stampedes and uncontrolled fan-out from CI pipelines and evaluation jobs. It targets infra engineers, CI owners, and platform SREs who operate GPU pools used for builds, evaluations, and canary workloads.

Summary: When large CI/eval jobs scale concurrently (nightly evals, multi-PR dependency checks, or mass rollouts), we observe sudden demand spikes that cause long queue times, warm-pool depletion, preemptions, and downstream latency for customer-facing inference. The mitigation strategy here is multi-layered: admission control + backpressure at CI, rate-limited fan-out adapters, autoscaler safeguards, pre-warm and warm-pool budgeting, token-bucket throttles, and telemetry-driven emergency throttles.

Key goals
- Ensure 95th percentile CI queue time to acquire a GPU stays below 4 minutes for short jobs (<= 30m).
- Bound impact of bulk-eval windows to < 10% of production capacity at any time.
- Provide fast operational controls to throttle/unwind runaway fan-outs within 2 minutes.

Scope and audience
- CI pipelines (Buildkite, GitHub Actions runner fleet) that request GPU nodes via internal provisioner.
- Evaluation clusters (ephemeral vs reserved) and scheduled eval grids.
- Autoscaler and GPU pool managers implemented in terraform + operator.
- Platform SRE, Eng-Infra, and Evaluation owners.

Patterns and controls (high level)
1) Source-side admission control (preferred):
   - Gate large fan-outs in the pipeline DSL: add a controlled "fanout" step that calculates effective concurrency and respects a per-repo concurrency quota.
   - Example policy: max 8 concurrent GPU jobs per repo by default; increase via request.
2) Rate-limited fanout adapter (thin service):
   - Implement an adapter between CI and the provisioner which enforces a token-bucket per-tenant and per-repo. The adapter emits telemetry for queue depth and throttle events.
3) Warm-pool budgeting and reservation:
   - Reserve a small fraction of capacity for latency-sensitive workloads (e.g., 10% of steady-state production) and a separate warm-pool for CI burst absorbtion.
4) Autoscaler soft/hard limits and plateau detection: 
   - Autoscaler must support: soft_scale_thresholds, hard_capacity_cap, plateau-detection (sustained increase > X mins), and cooldown windows.
5) Emergency throttles and circuit breakers:
   - Automated circuit breaker that reduces non-critical starter tokens (evals, scheduled CI) when the cluster cross-stage: GPU eviction rate > 2% OR avg token acquisition latency > 5m for 1 minute.

Operational metrics and dashboards
- Required metrics emitted by adapter and provisioner:
  - adapter.requests_total (counter)
  - adapter.tokens_acquired (counter)
  - adapter.tokens_throttled (counter)
  - provisioner.queue_depth (gauge)
  - provisioner.token_acquire_latency_seconds (histogram)
  - pool.warm_nodes (gauge)
  - pool.preemptions_per_minute (counter)
  - autoscaler.scale_events (counter)

SLOs and alerts (recommended thresholds)
- SLO: CI GPU token acquire P95 <= 4 minutes. Alert if P95 > 4m for 10m.
- Alert: queue_depth > 200 for any single cluster for 5m.
- Alert: preemption_rate > 2% sustained for 5m.
- Alert: tokens_throttled > 5% of requests for 1m (indicates adapter saturation).

Terraform / IaC patterns (examples)
- Purpose: keep module surface small and pluggable to both ephemeral eval and shared production pools. Use variables to express budgets and emergency throttle knobs.

Example terraform variables (text table)
- Variable: name | type | default | description
- max_concurrent_per_repo | number | 8 | Default admission concurrency per repo for CI tokens
- warm_pool_size | number | 20 | Number of pre-warmed GPU nodes reserved for CI/eval bursts
- emergency_throttle_pct | number | 15 | Percent of non-prod tokens to disable when circuit breaker trips

Example terraform snippet (pseudo-HCL)
-----BEGIN HCL-----
module "gpu_fanout_adapter" {
  source = "git::ssh://git@github.com/redwood/gpu-adapter.git//terraform/module"
  max_concurrent_per_repo = var.max_concurrent_per_repo
  warm_pool_size          = var.warm_pool_size
  emergency_throttle_pct  = var.emergency_throttle_pct
  monitoring_topic        = module.monitoring.topic
}
-----END HCL-----

CI pipeline changes (practical checklist)
- Replace raw parallel map steps with "fanout-step" that:
  1. requests N tokens from adapter APIs with a single bulk request,
  2. implements backoff (exponential with jitter) if tokens are denied,
  3. publishes telemetry labels (repo, pr, build_id).
- Example: Buildkite YAML patch (conceptual)
-----BEGIN YAML-----
steps:
  - label: "run-eval-grid"
    commands: 
      - scripts/request-gpu-tokens --count 16 --timeout 30m --tags ${BUILDKITE_PIPELINE_SLUG}
      - if: "\"$TOKENS_GRANTED\" == \"true\""
        commands: ["./run-grid.sh"]
      - else:
        commands: ["./notify-throttled.sh"]
-----END YAML-----

Adapter implementation notes
- Keep adapter state small: token-bucket counters in redis (per-tenant, per-repo), with TTLs to avoid leaks.
- Emit metrics at token request and token release. Tag metrics with owner, repo, job-type, and build_id.
- Add a soft-deny reason string to event logs so CI owners can detect whether denial is due to 'per_repo_quota', 'global_capacity_safety', or 'circuit_breaker'.

Warm-pool sizing guidance
- Base warm_pool_size calculation:
  - warm_pool_size = max(ceil(steady_state_prod_gpus * 0.08), 8)
  - Example: if production steady state is 250 GPUs, warm_pool_size = max(ceil(250*0.08)=20, 8) = 20.
- Use scheduled warm-pool daily windows (nightly evals) but cap the fraction of production used for scheduled batches to 10%.

Scaling safety: plateau detection and gradual scale-up
- When demand spike occurs, require autoscaler to do:
  1. fast warmup + limited scale (scale up to 30% over current)
  2. plateau detection (if tokens_acq_latency keeps increasing or queue_depth keeps growing, pause further scale and evaluate)
- Rationale: prevents us from autoscaling into a runaway eviction loop or cold-start cost spiral.

Runbook: Mitigate an active stampede (priority playbook)
1) Triage (0-2 minutes)
   - Owner: oncall infra/ci-cd SRE.
   - Dashboard to open: CI Token Health, Cluster queue_depth, Preemptions, Warm-pool utilization.
   - Immediate check: is global preemption_rate > 2%? Is token_acquire_latency P95 > 5m? If yes, escalate.
2) Contain (2-8 minutes)
   - Flip adapter emergency_throttle to 100% for non-prod and scheduled-evals. CLI: curl -XPOST https://internal-adapter/controls/throttle 
     Payload: {"scope":"non_prod","throttle_pct":100,"reason":"stampede-mitigation"}
   - If adapter not responsive: add iptables rate-limit on provisioner port for eval CI agents (last-resort).
3) Stabilize (8-30 minutes)
   - Reduce autoscaler max scale rate to 10% per 10m window.
   - Spin additional warm nodes only into a dedicated buffered pool; do not attach to production until preemption_rate < 1% for 10m.
4) Root-cause and fix (30m-4h)
   - Identify offending pipelines (use adapter logs -> tag build_id). 
   - Reconfigure pipelines: reduce per-repo fanout, batch tests into chunked runs, or move large eval grids to off-peak windows with prebooked capacity.
   - If a buggy PR is identified that caused exponential fan-out, block that PR by pipeline gate and notify respective owners.

Post-incident actions
- Postmortem must include: timeline, top 5 callers, % capacity consumed by offender, advisory to CI owners, and proposed change to admission control.
- Consider introducing scheduled capacity reservations (paid reservations) for large eval jobs to reduce contention with on-demand usage.

Testing and rollout checklist
- Unit and integ tests for adapter token-bucket behavior (race conditions around concurrent release).
- Load test harness: simulate X=200 concurrent repo builds with variable job durations and preemption interactions.
- Canary rollout: deploy adapter to single region and enable per-repo quotas set at 50% of expected. Monitor metrics for 48hrs before global rollout.

Ownership and contacts
- Primary owner: eng-infra/ci-cd team (oncall alias: oncall-ci-cd@redwood.com)
- Secondary escalation: platform-sre@redwood.com
- Pager escalation policy: if token_acquire_latency P95 > 10m for 15m, page platform-sre.

Related resources and links
- Implementation repo: git@github.com:redwood/gpu-adapter (internal)
- Dashboard: Grafana -> CI GPU Token Health (link internal)
- IaC modules: internal terraform registry -> gpu_fanout_adapter module
- Follow-up docs: runbook for warm-pool orchestration, CI pipeline best-practices for fan-out

Appendix: example telemetry alert rule (pseudo PromQL)
- Alert: CI_GPU_Token_Acquire_P95_High
  expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le) (rate(provisioner_token_acquire_latency_seconds_bucket[5m]))) > 240
  for: 10m
  labels: {severity: "page"}
  annotations: {summary: "CI GPU token acquire p95 > 4m"}

Notes and rationale
- We prioritize source-side admission control because it is the most deterministic and lowest-cost mitigation. Fallbacks (adapter throttles, warm pools) are safety nets.
- Warm pools reduce latency but cost money; budget decisions should be aligned with business priorities (e.g., scheduled evals contributing to core product quality may deserve higher reservations).

Document revision history
- 2024-05-10: Initial draft.
- 2024-09-03: Added terraform variables and adapter implementation notes.
- 2025-06-18: Updated warm-pool sizing heuristics and autoscaler plateau logic after incident IR-2025-06-gpu-spike.
- 2026-01-12: Finalized emergency throttle API and added rollout checklist.

title: GPU build fan-out and stampede mitigation guide
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This guide describes patterns, Terraform/IaC examples, monitoring signals, and an operational runbook to prevent and mitigate GPU stampedes and uncontrolled fan-out from CI pipelines and evaluation jobs. It targets infra engineers, CI owners, and platform SREs who operate GPU pools used for builds, evaluations, and canary workloads.

Summary: When large CI/eval jobs scale concurrently (nightly evals, multi-PR dependency checks, or mass rollouts), we observe sudden demand spikes that cause long queue times, warm-pool depletion, preemptions, and downstream latency for customer-facing inference. The mitigation strategy here is multi-layered: admission control + backpressure at CI, rate-limited fan-out adapters, autoscaler safeguards, pre-warm and warm-pool budgeting, token-bucket throttles, and telemetry-driven emergency throttles.

Key goals
- Ensure 95th percentile CI queue time to acquire a GPU stays below 4 minutes for short jobs (<= 30m).
- Bound impact of bulk-eval windows to < 10% of production capacity at any time.
- Provide fast operational controls to throttle/unwind runaway fan-outs within 2 minutes.

Scope and audience
- CI pipelines (Buildkite, GitHub Actions runner fleet) that request GPU nodes via internal provisioner.
- Evaluation clusters (ephemeral vs reserved) and scheduled eval grids.
- Autoscaler and GPU pool managers implemented in terraform + operator.
- Platform SRE, Eng-Infra, and Evaluation owners.

Patterns and controls (high level)
1) Source-side admission control (preferred):
   - Gate large fan-outs in the pipeline DSL: add a controlled "fanout" step that calculates effective concurrency and respects a per-repo concurrency quota.
   - Example policy: max 8 concurrent GPU jobs per repo by default; increase via request.
2) Rate-limited fanout adapter (thin service):
   - Implement an adapter between CI and the provisioner which enforces a token-bucket per-tenant and per-repo. The adapter emits telemetry for queue depth and throttle events.
3) Warm-pool budgeting and reservation:
   - Reserve a small fraction of capacity for latency-sensitive workloads (e.g., 10% of steady-state production) and a separate warm-pool for CI burst absorbtion.
4) Autoscaler soft/hard limits and plateau detection: 
   - Autoscaler must support: soft_scale_thresholds, hard_capacity_cap, plateau-detection (sustained increase > X mins), and cooldown windows.
5) Emergency throttles and circuit breakers:
   - Automated circuit breaker that reduces non-critical starter tokens (evals, scheduled CI) when the cluster cross-stage: GPU eviction rate > 2% OR avg token acquisition latency > 5m for 1 minute.

Operational metrics and dashboards
- Required metrics emitted by adapter and provisioner:
  - adapter.requests_total (counter)
  - adapter.tokens_acquired (counter)
  - adapter.tokens_throttled (counter)
  - provisioner.queue_depth (gauge)
  - provisioner.token_acquire_latency_seconds (histogram)
  - pool.warm_nodes (gauge)
  - pool.preemptions_per_minute (counter)
  - autoscaler.scale_events (counter)

SLOs and alerts (recommended thresholds)
- SLO: CI GPU token acquire P95 <= 4 minutes. Alert if P95 > 4m for 10m.
- Alert: queue_depth > 200 for any single cluster for 5m.
- Alert: preemption_rate > 2% sustained for 5m.
- Alert: tokens_throttled > 5% of requests for 1m (indicates adapter saturation).

Terraform / IaC patterns (examples)
- Purpose: keep module surface small and pluggable to both ephemeral eval and shared production pools. Use variables to express budgets and emergency throttle knobs.

Example terraform variables (text table)
- Variable: name | type | default | description
- max_concurrent_per_repo | number | 8 | Default admission concurrency per repo for CI tokens
- warm_pool_size | number | 20 | Number of pre-warmed GPU nodes reserved for CI/eval bursts
- emergency_throttle_pct | number | 15 | Percent of non-prod tokens to disable when circuit breaker trips

Example terraform snippet (pseudo-HCL)
-----BEGIN HCL-----
module "gpu_fanout_adapter" {
  source = "git::ssh://git@github.com/redwood/gpu-adapter.git//terraform/module"
  max_concurrent_per_repo = var.max_concurrent_per_repo
  warm_pool_size          = var.warm_pool_size
  emergency_throttle_pct  = var.emergency_throttle_pct
  monitoring_topic        = module.monitoring.topic
}
-----END HCL-----

CI pipeline changes (practical checklist)
- Replace raw parallel map steps with "fanout-step" that:
  1. requests N tokens from adapter APIs with a single bulk request,
  2. implements backoff (exponential with jitter) if tokens are denied,
  3. publishes telemetry labels (repo, pr, build_id).
- Example: Buildkite YAML patch (conceptual)
-----BEGIN YAML-----
steps:
  - label: "run-eval-grid"
    commands: 
      - scripts/request-gpu-tokens --count 16 --timeout 30m --tags ${BUILDKITE_PIPELINE_SLUG}
      - if: "\"$TOKENS_GRANTED\" == \"true\""
        commands: ["./run-grid.sh"]
      - else:
        commands: ["./notify-throttled.sh"]
-----END YAML-----

Adapter implementation notes
- Keep adapter state small: token-bucket counters in redis (per-tenant, per-repo), with TTLs to avoid leaks.
- Emit metrics at token request and token release. Tag metrics with owner, repo, job-type, and build_id.
- Add a soft-deny reason string to event logs so CI owners can detect whether denial is due to 'per_repo_quota', 'global_capacity_safety', or 'circuit_breaker'.

Warm-pool sizing guidance
- Base warm_pool_size calculation:
  - warm_pool_size = max(ceil(steady_state_prod_gpus * 0.08), 8)
  - Example: if production steady state is 250 GPUs, warm_pool_size = max(ceil(250*0.08)=20, 8) = 20.
- Use scheduled warm-pool daily windows (nightly evals) but cap the fraction of production used for scheduled batches to 10%.

Scaling safety: plateau detection and gradual scale-up
- When demand spike occurs, require autoscaler to do:
  1. fast warmup + limited scale (scale up to 30% over current)
  2. plateau detection (if tokens_acq_latency keeps increasing or queue_depth keeps growing, pause further scale and evaluate)
- Rationale: prevents us from autoscaling into a runaway eviction loop or cold-start cost spiral.

Runbook: Mitigate an active stampede (priority playbook)
1) Triage (0-2 minutes)
   - Owner: oncall infra/ci-cd SRE.
   - Dashboard to open: CI Token Health, Cluster queue_depth, Preemptions, Warm-pool utilization.
   - Immediate check: is global preemption_rate > 2%? Is token_acquire_latency P95 > 5m? If yes, escalate.
2) Contain (2-8 minutes)
   - Flip adapter emergency_throttle to 100% for non-prod and scheduled-evals. CLI: curl -XPOST https://internal-adapter/controls/throttle 
     Payload: {"scope":"non_prod","throttle_pct":100,"reason":"stampede-mitigation"}
   - If adapter not responsive: add iptables rate-limit on provisioner port for eval CI agents (last-resort).
3) Stabilize (8-30 minutes)
   - Reduce autoscaler max scale rate to 10% per 10m window.
   - Spin additional warm nodes only into a dedicated buffered pool; do not attach to production until preemption_rate < 1% for 10m.
4) Root-cause and fix (30m-4h)
   - Identify offending pipelines (use adapter logs -> tag build_id). 
   - Reconfigure pipelines: reduce per-repo fanout, batch tests into chunked runs, or move large eval grids to off-peak windows with prebooked capacity.
   - If a buggy PR is identified that caused exponential fan-out, block that PR by pipeline gate and notify respective owners.

Post-incident actions
- Postmortem must include: timeline, top 5 callers, % capacity consumed by offender, advisory to CI owners, and proposed change to admission control.
- Consider introducing scheduled capacity reservations (paid reservations) for large eval jobs to reduce contention with on-demand usage.

Testing and rollout checklist
- Unit and integ tests for adapter token-bucket behavior (race conditions around concurrent release).
- Load test harness: simulate X=200 concurrent repo builds with variable job durations and preemption interactions.
- Canary rollout: deploy adapter to single region and enable per-repo quotas set at 50% of expected. Monitor metrics for 48hrs before global rollout.

Ownership and contacts
- Primary owner: eng-infra/ci-cd team (oncall alias: oncall-ci-cd@redwood.com)
- Secondary escalation: platform-sre@redwood.com
- Pager escalation policy: if token_acquire_latency P95 > 10m for 15m, page platform-sre.

Related resources and links
- Implementation repo: git@github.com:redwood/gpu-adapter (internal)
- Dashboard: Grafana -> CI GPU Token Health (link internal)
- IaC modules: internal terraform registry -> gpu_fanout_adapter module
- Follow-up docs: runbook for warm-pool orchestration, CI pipeline best-practices for fan-out

Appendix: example telemetry alert rule (pseudo PromQL)
- Alert: CI_GPU_Token_Acquire_P95_High
  expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le) (rate(provisioner_token_acquire_latency_seconds_bucket[5m]))) > 240
  for: 10m
  labels: {severity: "page"}
  annotations: {summary: "CI GPU token acquire p95 > 4m"}

Notes and rationale
- We prioritize source-side admission control because it is the most deterministic and lowest-cost mitigation. Fallbacks (adapter throttles, warm pools) are safety nets.
- Warm pools reduce latency but cost money; budget decisions should be aligned with business priorities (e.g., scheduled evals contributing to core product quality may deserve higher reservations).

Document revision history
- 2024-05-10: Initial draft.
- 2024-09-03: Added terraform variables and adapter implementation notes.
- 2025-06-18: Updated warm-pool sizing heuristics and autoscaler plateau logic after incident IR-2025-06-gpu-spike.
- 2026-01-12: Finalized emergency throttle API and added rollout checklist.

source.title: SDK Offline Testbed & Integration Scenario Library
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview

Purpose: This page documents a reproducible, developer-friendly offline testbed and a curated scenario library for validating SDK integrations (embeddings, generation, streaming, and multi-model orchestration) without requiring access to hosted Redwood environments. The testbed supports fast-feedback loops, deterministic regression checks, and canonical fixes used by Support and DX.

Audience: SDK engineers, client integration engineers, support agents, and developer advocates.

What this document contains:
- High-level architecture and components of the offline testbed.
- Quickstart instructions to spin up a local harness.
- Scenario library (table of concrete test-cases with expected telemetry).
- Template stubs and sample code for Python and Node SDKs.
- Observability contract and canonical-fix triage playbook.
- Maintenance and ownership notes.

Design goals
- Deterministic: deterministic inputs and golden traces for regression detection.
- Low-friction: one-command start for common flows.
- Extensible: add new scenarios and model stubs easily.
- Telemetry-aligned: injects the same telemetry keys used in production to validate instrumentation.

Architecture and components

The testbed is intentionally minimal and portable:
1) Harness CLI: a single binary/script that orchestrates local services and scenarios.
2) Model stubs: small HTTP services that implement a subset of Redwood API surface (streams, chunked responses, embeddings).
3) Request replayer: records and replays client-to-runtime interactions with configurable timings.
4) Golden trace store: a filesystem-based store of request/response + token-level timing and cost metadata.
5) Telemetry shim: local collector that receives SDK telemetry (sampler hooks) and validates presence of required keys.
6) Scenario library: YAML manifests describing input, expected output assertions, and pass/fail thresholds.

Quickstart: local harness (macOS / Linux)
Prerequisites: Docker (for optional containerized stubs), Python 3.10+, Node 18+, git.

1) Clone the repo and install the harness: 
   git clone git@internal:redwood/devx/offline-testbed.git
   cd offline-testbed
   ./scripts/bootstrap.sh

2) Start the testbed (single command):
   ./bin/harness start --profile=fast

   Expected outcome: harness prints three endpoints: STUB_BASE=http://localhost:8081, TELEMETRY=http://localhost:4318, REPLAYER=http://localhost:8090

3) Run a curated scenario:
   ./bin/harness run --scenario=generate-classic-short-completion

   Harness output includes: scenario manifest, run id, PASS/FAIL summary and path to generated golden trace.

4) Run SDK integration smoke (Python):
   python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
   pip install -r examples/python/requirements.txt
   export REDWOOD_API_URL=http://localhost:8081
   python examples/python/generate_smoke.py --api-key test-key --timeout 5s

   Expected: script prints deterministic completion and telemetry assertion status.

Scenario library (selection)

| id | purpose | input summary | expected assertion | telemetry keys validated | notes |
|----|---------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------------|-------|
| generate-classic-short-completion | Validate single-turn generation and token budgeting | short prompt (<= 64 tokens) | exact-match against golden sample (normalized) | trace_id, latency_ms, tokens_input, tokens_output | used in CI smoke on PRs |
| streaming-chat-chunk-resume | Validate partial-chunk streaming + resume semantics | 3-turn chat stream with cut mid-token | reassembled output equals golden; resume flow produces same final text | stream_id, chunk_index, resume_flag | tests client backpressure handling |
| embeddings-stability-128 | Validate embedding determinism for small vectors | 10 sample sentences | L2 distance to golden <= 1e-6 | embed_set_id, model_ctx | used to detect quantization regressions |
| multi-model-fallback | Validate fallback routing when preferred model returns 503 | generation call to model-A that 503s; fallback to model-B | response from model-B with expected schema | route_policy, fallback_used | exercises Retry/Backoff wiring |
| long-context-kv-cache | Validate KV cache correctness across >8k tokens | staged conversation >10k tokens | KV cache hit rate >= 95% and correctness check | kv_cache_hit, seq_len | heavy memory-eviction scenario |
| embedding-batch-split | Validate batching semantics when client sends 500 items | large batch of sentences | batching windows created; final order preserved | batch_id, batch_size, split_count | asserts idempotent ordering |

Adding a scenario: manifest template (YAML)

# examples/scenarios/template.yaml
name: my-scenario
description: Short description
owner: team-name
runtime: python|node|bash
stages:
  - name: start-stubs
    steps:
      - run: scripts/start-stub --model foo --port 8082
  - name: execute-client
    steps:
      - run: examples/python/run_my_test.py --endpoint ${STUB_BASE}
assertions:
  - type: equals
    path: response.text
    expected: "Hello world"
telemetry_expectations:
  - key: trace_id
    present: true
  - key: tokens_output
    max: 128

SDK stubs and local adapter patterns

Python sample stub (Flask minimal streaming):

from flask import Flask, Response, request
app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route('/v1/generate', methods=['POST'])
def generate():
    prompts = request.json.get('prompts', [])
    def stream():
        for i, p in enumerate(prompts):
            # deterministic output for test harness
            chunk = f"{p[::-1]}"  # reversed prompt as canonical transformation
            yield f"data: {chunk}\n\n"

    return Response(stream(), mimetype='text/event-stream')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(port=8081)

Node sample: express stub that supports chunked transfer and a /health endpoint. See examples/node/stub.js.

Observability contract (required keys)

Use these checks in scenarios and CI. Telemetry keys below must be emitted by SDKs and the harness shim.
- trace_id (string): unique run id present on every request.
- sdk_version (string): semver of the SDK used for the run.
- latency_ms (number): wall-clock request latency.
- tokens_input, tokens_output (number): token accounting for unit economics tests.
- model_id (string): the model identifier used by the request.
- route_policy (string): routing decision tag when multi-model tests run.

Canonical-fix triage playbook (when a scenario regresses)

1) Reproduce: Run the scenario locally using the same harness commit and run id recorded by CI. Use the golden trace path recorded in the CI artifact.
2) Compare traces: run tools/trace_diff.py <golden> <current> to get token-level diffs and timing deltas.
3) Narrow root cause: check these layers in order: SDK surface (client-side changes), stub runtime (testbed changes), orchestration script, local shim telemetry.
4) If SDK client code changed in last PR: create a focused unit test using the failing scenario and add to SDK PR as required verification.
5) If stub or harness regression: revert the harness commit in a controlled branch and run CI smoke to confirm.
6) Publish the canonical fix: update the scenario manifest and golden trace, increment the scenario schema version, and add an entry to the changelog (scenarios/CHANGELOG.md).

Debugging commands and utilities

- List running services:
  curl http://localhost:8090/status
- Dump last golden trace:
  cat runs/<run-id>/trace.json | jq .
- Run trace comparator:
  python tools/trace_diff.py runs/<golden>/trace.json runs/<failed>/trace.json --summary
- Replay a recorded client session with original timings:
  ./bin/replayer replay --run runs/<id>/client_capture.har

CI integration guidance

- Include quick harness start (profile=ci) in PR checks that touch SDK serialization, streaming, or telemetry.
- Mark stable scenarios as "ci:smoke" in the YAML so they run on fast PR lanes.
- For heavier scenarios (long-context, embedding-batch-split), run nightly with artifacts uploaded to the golden trace bucket.

Ownership and maintenance

- Primary owner: eng-devx-testbed (rotation) — contact: devx-testbed@redwood.com
- Scenario curation: SDK product engineers and Support must jointly review new scenarios. Use the template in CONTRIBUTING.md.
- Versioning: scenario manifests carry a schema_version field; breaking changes require bumping and adding migration notes.

Governance and access

- Golden traces are stored in an internal S3 bucket with read-only access for support and read-write for devx-testbed.
- Do not push secrets into scenario manifests. Use secret placeholders and the local .env loader.

FAQ

Q: Why reverse prompts in the stub example?
A: We use a deterministic transformation (reverse, hash, or predictable tokenization) to make regression diffs obvious while avoiding shipping licensed model artifacts.

Q: Can I add a long-running real-model integration to this testbed?
A: Not in the public harness. For regulated or hosted-model tests, use the dedicated CI that runs against the staging Redwood API and tagged credentials. See related pages for that flow.

Appendix: scenario maintenance checklist

- Confirm deterministic seed and random sources are disabled.
- Validate telemetry keys are emitted for every upstream call.
- Produce and store golden trace under runs/<scenario>/<date>-<commit>.json
- Add a changelog entry and reviewer sign-off before merging updates.

Document history

- 2025-03-11: Created by Priya Natarajan
- 2025-09-02: Added streaming and resume scenario (review: Ethan Morales)
- 2026-01-12: Expanded observability contract and CI guidance (review: Leila Chen)

title: SDK Offline Testbed & Integration Scenario Library
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This page documents a reproducible, developer-friendly offline testbed and a curated scenario library for validating SDK integrations (embeddings, generation, streaming, and multi-model orchestration) without requiring access to hosted Redwood environments. The testbed supports fast-feedback loops, deterministic regression checks, and canonical fixes used by Support and DX.

Audience: SDK engineers, client integration engineers, support agents, and developer advocates.

What this document contains:
- High-level architecture and components of the offline testbed.
- Quickstart instructions to spin up a local harness.
- Scenario library (table of concrete test-cases with expected telemetry).
- Template stubs and sample code for Python and Node SDKs.
- Observability contract and canonical-fix triage playbook.
- Maintenance and ownership notes.

Design goals
- Deterministic: deterministic inputs and golden traces for regression detection.
- Low-friction: one-command start for common flows.
- Extensible: add new scenarios and model stubs easily.
- Telemetry-aligned: injects the same telemetry keys used in production to validate instrumentation.

Architecture and components

The testbed is intentionally minimal and portable:
1) Harness CLI: a single binary/script that orchestrates local services and scenarios.
2) Model stubs: small HTTP services that implement a subset of Redwood API surface (streams, chunked responses, embeddings).
3) Request replayer: records and replays client-to-runtime interactions with configurable timings.
4) Golden trace store: a filesystem-based store of request/response + token-level timing and cost metadata.
5) Telemetry shim: local collector that receives SDK telemetry (sampler hooks) and validates presence of required keys.
6) Scenario library: YAML manifests describing input, expected output assertions, and pass/fail thresholds.

Quickstart: local harness (macOS / Linux)
Prerequisites: Docker (for optional containerized stubs), Python 3.10+, Node 18+, git.

1) Clone the repo and install the harness: 
   git clone git@internal:redwood/devx/offline-testbed.git
   cd offline-testbed
   ./scripts/bootstrap.sh

2) Start the testbed (single command):
   ./bin/harness start --profile=fast

   Expected outcome: harness prints three endpoints: STUB_BASE=http://localhost:8081, TELEMETRY=http://localhost:4318, REPLAYER=http://localhost:8090

3) Run a curated scenario:
   ./bin/harness run --scenario=generate-classic-short-completion

   Harness output includes: scenario manifest, run id, PASS/FAIL summary and path to generated golden trace.

4) Run SDK integration smoke (Python):
   python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
   pip install -r examples/python/requirements.txt
   export REDWOOD_API_URL=http://localhost:8081
   python examples/python/generate_smoke.py --api-key test-key --timeout 5s

   Expected: script prints deterministic completion and telemetry assertion status.

Scenario library (selection)

| id | purpose | input summary | expected assertion | telemetry keys validated | notes |
|----|---------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------------|-------|
| generate-classic-short-completion | Validate single-turn generation and token budgeting | short prompt (<= 64 tokens) | exact-match against golden sample (normalized) | trace_id, latency_ms, tokens_input, tokens_output | used in CI smoke on PRs |
| streaming-chat-chunk-resume | Validate partial-chunk streaming + resume semantics | 3-turn chat stream with cut mid-token | reassembled output equals golden; resume flow produces same final text | stream_id, chunk_index, resume_flag | tests client backpressure handling |
| embeddings-stability-128 | Validate embedding determinism for small vectors | 10 sample sentences | L2 distance to golden <= 1e-6 | embed_set_id, model_ctx | used to detect quantization regressions |
| multi-model-fallback | Validate fallback routing when preferred model returns 503 | generation call to model-A that 503s; fallback to model-B | response from model-B with expected schema | route_policy, fallback_used | exercises Retry/Backoff wiring |
| long-context-kv-cache | Validate KV cache correctness across >8k tokens | staged conversation >10k tokens | KV cache hit rate >= 95% and correctness check | kv_cache_hit, seq_len | heavy memory-eviction scenario |
| embedding-batch-split | Validate batching semantics when client sends 500 items | large batch of sentences | batching windows created; final order preserved | batch_id, batch_size, split_count | asserts idempotent ordering |

Adding a scenario: manifest template (YAML)

# examples/scenarios/template.yaml
name: my-scenario
description: Short description
owner: team-name
runtime: python|node|bash
stages:
  - name: start-stubs
    steps:
      - run: scripts/start-stub --model foo --port 8082
  - name: execute-client
    steps:
      - run: examples/python/run_my_test.py --endpoint ${STUB_BASE}
assertions:
  - type: equals
    path: response.text
    expected: "Hello world"
telemetry_expectations:
  - key: trace_id
    present: true
  - key: tokens_output
    max: 128

SDK stubs and local adapter patterns

Python sample stub (Flask minimal streaming):

from flask import Flask, Response, request
app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route('/v1/generate', methods=['POST'])
def generate():
    prompts = request.json.get('prompts', [])
    def stream():
        for i, p in enumerate(prompts):
            # deterministic output for test harness
            chunk = f"{p[::-1]}"  # reversed prompt as canonical transformation
            yield f"data: {chunk}

"

    return Response(stream(), mimetype='text/event-stream')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(port=8081)

Node sample: express stub that supports chunked transfer and a /health endpoint. See examples/node/stub.js.

Observability contract (required keys)

Use these checks in scenarios and CI. Telemetry keys below must be emitted by SDKs and the harness shim.
- trace_id (string): unique run id present on every request.
- sdk_version (string): semver of the SDK used for the run.
- latency_ms (number): wall-clock request latency.
- tokens_input, tokens_output (number): token accounting for unit economics tests.
- model_id (string): the model identifier used by the request.
- route_policy (string): routing decision tag when multi-model tests run.

Canonical-fix triage playbook (when a scenario regresses)

1) Reproduce: Run the scenario locally using the same harness commit and run id recorded by CI. Use the golden trace path recorded in the CI artifact.
2) Compare traces: run tools/trace_diff.py <golden> <current> to get token-level diffs and timing deltas.
3) Narrow root cause: check these layers in order: SDK surface (client-side changes), stub runtime (testbed changes), orchestration script, local shim telemetry.
4) If SDK client code changed in last PR: create a focused unit test using the failing scenario and add to SDK PR as required verification.
5) If stub or harness regression: revert the harness commit in a controlled branch and run CI smoke to confirm.
6) Publish the canonical fix: update the scenario manifest and golden trace, increment the scenario schema version, and add an entry to the changelog (scenarios/CHANGELOG.md).

Debugging commands and utilities

- List running services:
  curl http://localhost:8090/status
- Dump last golden trace:
  cat runs/<run-id>/trace.json | jq .
- Run trace comparator:
  python tools/trace_diff.py runs/<golden>/trace.json runs/<failed>/trace.json --summary
- Replay a recorded client session with original timings:
  ./bin/replayer replay --run runs/<id>/client_capture.har

CI integration guidance

- Include quick harness start (profile=ci) in PR checks that touch SDK serialization, streaming, or telemetry.
- Mark stable scenarios as "ci:smoke" in the YAML so they run on fast PR lanes.
- For heavier scenarios (long-context, embedding-batch-split), run nightly with artifacts uploaded to the golden trace bucket.

Ownership and maintenance

- Primary owner: eng-devx-testbed (rotation) — contact: devx-testbed@redwood.com
- Scenario curation: SDK product engineers and Support must jointly review new scenarios. Use the template in CONTRIBUTING.md.
- Versioning: scenario manifests carry a schema_version field; breaking changes require bumping and adding migration notes.

Governance and access

- Golden traces are stored in an internal S3 bucket with read-only access for support and read-write for devx-testbed.
- Do not push secrets into scenario manifests. Use secret placeholders and the local .env loader.

FAQ

Q: Why reverse prompts in the stub example?
A: We use a deterministic transformation (reverse, hash, or predictable tokenization) to make regression diffs obvious while avoiding shipping licensed model artifacts.

Q: Can I add a long-running real-model integration to this testbed?
A: Not in the public harness. For regulated or hosted-model tests, use the dedicated CI that runs against the staging Redwood API and tagged credentials. See related pages for that flow.

Appendix: scenario maintenance checklist

- Confirm deterministic seed and random sources are disabled.
- Validate telemetry keys are emitted for every upstream call.
- Produce and store golden trace under runs/<scenario>/<date>-<commit>.json
- Add a changelog entry and reviewer sign-off before merging updates.

Document history

- 2025-03-11: Created by Priya Natarajan
- 2025-09-02: Added streaming and resume scenario (review: Ethan Morales)
- 2026-01-12: Expanded observability contract and CI guidance (review: Leila Chen)
