source.title: Scheduler Fault Injection Lab Procedures and Recovery Playbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Summary

This document is a laboratory-grade procedure set for deterministic fault injection against the runtime scheduler and kernel handoff surface. It defines controlled experiments, safety gates, measurement artifacts, and a recovery playbook to be used by the Serving Runtime SRE/Platform teams when validating scheduler resilience, degradation policies and telemetry coverage.

Purpose

- Validate scheduler behaviour under targeted faults (node, kernel, eviction, preemption, telemetry loss).
- Ensure graceful degradation policies preserve SLOs within defined error budgets.
- Verify observability and automated recovery paths capture and mitigate incidents.

Scope

Applies to internal lab clusters (non-production), the runtime components listed below and the scheduler API surfaces used by runtime-operator and kernel-dispatcher. Not for production injection without executive approval and a production blast radius mitigation plan.

Owned components

- scheduler-agent (control plane component that enforces admission and prioritization)
- kernel-dispatcher (executor that selects kernels and schedules GPU work)
- runtime-coordinator (session lifecycle and KV cache hints)
- kv-cache-service (local caching layer)
- operator (canary and rollout automation)

Safety and approval gates

- Only approved lab clusters with isolated tenant IDs may be used.
- At least one reviewer (from reviewers list) must sign off pre-run.
- Pre-run checklist must be completed and attached to experiment ticket.
- If experiment breaches safety thresholds (see "Abort thresholds"), stop immediately and follow recovery steps.

Abort thresholds (hard stops)

- P99 latency increase > 2x baseline sustained > 3 minutes for critical route 
- 5xx rate > 1% of requests for > 2 minutes across the cluster
- GPU thermal threshold > 85°C on any device
- Operator or control-plane crashloop detected for > 2 minutes

Lab prerequisites (environment)

- Kubernetes namespace: runtime-fault-lab-<ticket-id>
- Cluster label: lab=true, isolation: network-policy enabled
- Canary model: rdm-small-v1 (single-instance) and rdm-large-v2 (multi-shard)
- Traffic generator: loadgen-playbook v2.3 (configured for representative shape)
- Observability: grafana (scheduler, kernel, kvcache dashboards), jaeger tracing, metrics ingest to prometheus-lab

Config table (sample):

| Component | Default test config |
|---|---|
| scheduler-agent | admission-window=200ms, credit-bucket=10000 |
| kernel-dispatcher | max-concurrency=8, prefetch=1 |
| kv-cache-service | warmup=true, size=4GB |

Fault taxonomy and examples

1) Scheduler admission faults
- Simulate admission controller rejecting low-priority requests. Useful to verify priority inversion handling.
- Implementation: intercept gRPC admit call and return DENY for target cohort.

2) Kernel stall / slow-path injection
- Inject latency in kernel-launch path to replicate slow kernel/driver calls (e.g., +50-200ms).
- Implementation: eBPF shim or fault library hook in kernel-dispatcher.

3) KV cache evictions and warmup loss
- Force eviction of KV entries or block warmup to validate warm-start behavior and sequence-length penalties.
- Implementation: call kv-cache-service admin API: /admin/evict?pattern=prefix

4) Telemetry loss and delayed traces
- Drop X of N spans or metrics to ensure alerting and root cause still actionable.
- Implementation: apply a proxy rule to drop telemetry from one pod.

5) Control-plane intermittent restarts
- Cycle scheduler-agent or runtime-coordinator with SIGTERM and shortened graceful window to confirm state reconciliation.

Test recipes (numbered procedures)

Procedure A: Priority admission denial (small blast radius)

1. Create experiment ticket and link pre-run checklist.
2. Deploy two workloads: customer-canary (high-prio) and batch-processing (low-prio) to runtime-fault-lab.<ticket-id>.
3. Configure scheduler-agent fault shim to DENY batch-processing cohort for 5 minutes. Command: curl -XPOST http://scheduler-agent-admin.local/faults -d '{"type":"deny","cohort":"batch","duration_s":300}'
4. Start loadgen: 200 rps high-prio, 50 rps low-prio.
5. Observe metrics for 10 minutes: kernel_queue_depth, kernel_exec_time_ms, p50/p95/p99 latencies per cohort.
6. Expected outcome: high-prio P99 remains within 1.2x baseline; low-prio sees increased errors but remains isolated.
7. If abort thresholds hit, trigger recovery (see Recovery Playbook).

Procedure B: Kernel slow-path degradation (medium blast radius)

1. Schedule rdm-large-v2 multi-shard model to the lab pool.
2. Inject 150ms artificial delay into kernel-launch via fault library: kubectl exec -n runtime-fault-lab <kernel-dispatcher-pod> -- /opt/faults/inject --delay-ms=150 --target=launch
3. Drive a synthetic mixed workload with sequence length distribution matching production 80th percentile.
4. Capture traces for affected requests and compare kernel_exec_time_ms distribution to baseline.
5. Expected outcome: increased tail latency; scheduler should reduce admission or route to fallback model if available. Verify fallback routing executed.

Procedure C: Observability loss and triage exercise (low blast radius)

1. Apply a proxy rule to drop 30% of spans from one kernel-dispatcher pod: kubectl apply -f drop-spans-config.yaml
2. Run a diagnosis run: collect stack traces and residual metrics.
3. Verify that alerts degrade to metric-backed alerts (e.g., spike in p99 + increased error budget burn) rather than purely tracing-based.
4. Review detection latency for degraded telemetry.

Telemetry and measurement checklist (must collect)

- Baseline metrics snapshot (pre-run): p50/p95/p99 latency, 5xx rate, kernel_queue_depth, kvcache_hit_ratio, gpu_util_percent, gpu_temp_celsius.
- Continuous traces for representative request IDs (jaeger) with sampling rate >= 20% for experiment window.
- Kernel-level histograms: exec_time_ms histogram, launch_time_ms, kernel_retries.
- Control-plane logs: scheduler-agent audit trail and admission decisions.
- Experiment artifact: a tarball with raw traces and metric snapshots uploaded to experiment ticket.

Expected success criteria

- No critical-route P99 > 1.5x baseline for > 5 minutes.
- Error budget consumption for critical routes < 10% during experiment window.
- Recoverable state: no manual rollback required to return to baseline after experiment finishes.

Recovery playbook (fast path)

1. Stop fault injection immediately.
2. If control-plane components are unstable, restart scheduler-agent with playground command: kubectl rollout restart deployment/scheduler-agent -n runtime-fault-lab
3. Rehydrate KV cache: curl -XPOST http://kv-cache-service.local/admin/warmup
4. Scale up fallback route capacity if model fallback is available: operator apply --scale fallback-route=+2
5. If GPU thermal threshold exceeded, drain affected node and run thermal cooldown: kubectl cordon <node> && kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
6. Attach experiment artifacts and incident note to ticket; schedule a postmortem if abort thresholds were hit.

Post-experiment validation

- Run a 30-minute smoke workload and compare latencies to pre-run baseline.
- Confirm operator can resume normal canary rollouts.
- Ensure dashboards are clear (no lingering alerts).

Rollout guidance for production migration

- Require a staging test performed on a production-like fleet with synthetic traffic mirroring.
- Schedule at low-traffic window; have on-call SRE and Product owner present.
- Limit initial duration to 60s and validate quick rollback path.

CLI and example config snippets

YAML sample for kernel delay fault (lab-only):

---
apiVersion: faults.redwood.ai/v1
kind: KernelDelay
metadata:
  name: kernel-delay-150ms
spec:
  target: kernel-launch
  delay_ms: 150
  duration_s: 600
  scope: {"namespace":"runtime-fault-lab-1234","labelSelector":"test-cohort=experimental"}
---

Admin command to evict kvcache prefix:

curl -XPOST http://kv-cache-service.local/admin/evict -d '{"pattern":"session:rdm-large:*"}' -H 'Content-Type: application/json'

Dashboards and alerts to confirm coverage

- Dashboard: Runtime Scheduler Overview (panels: admission decisions, queue depth, per-cohort latency)
- Dashboard: Kernel Health (exec histograms, retry rate, GPU util, temp)
- Alert: KernelLaunchErrorsRate > 0.5% for 5m
- Alert: P99_CriticalRoute > threshold for 3m (notify on-call and platform slack channel)

Ownership and cadence

- Runbook owner: Priya Natarajan (runtime resilience lead)
- Responsible team: Serving Runtime SRE / Platform
- Review cadence: Quarterly or after any production incident that touches scheduler behaviour

Appendix: quick troubleshooting matrix

- Symptom: high p99, kernel_queue_depth spikes
  - Likely cause: kernel slow-path or burst in admission. Check kernel_exec_time_ms histograms and kernel-launch qps.
  - Quick mitigation: enable admission-tightening rule or scale kernel-dispatcher pool.

- Symptom: missing traces but metrics present
  - Likely cause: telemetry proxy rule or sidecar failure.
  - Quick mitigation: restart telemetry sidecar on affected pod and increase metric-based alert sensitivity temporarily.

References

- Runtime decision graph (internal) — link: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/runtime-decision-graph-and-observed-behaviour-catalog-2026
- Scheduler telemetry proofing — link: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling/scheduler-telemetry-synthesis-and-proofing-2025

Change history

- 2025-11-02: Initial draft by Priya Natarajan.
- 2025-12-15: Added kernel delay and kvcache warmup recipes (reviewer Marcus Chen).
- 2026-01-12: Updated abort thresholds and recovery steps (reviewer Alison Rivera).

- Operator or control-plane crashloop detected for > 2 minutes

Lab prerequisites (environment)

- Kubernetes namespace: runtime-fault-lab-<ticket-id>
- Cluster label: lab=true, isolation: network-policy enabled
- Canary model: rdm-small-v1 (single-instance) and rdm-large-v2 (multi-shard)
- Traffic generator: loadgen-playbook v2.3 (configured for representative shape)
- Observability: grafana (scheduler, kernel, kvcache dashboards), jaeger tracing, metrics ingest to prometheus-lab

Config table (sample):

| Component | Default test config |
|---|---|
| scheduler-agent | admission-window=200ms, credit-bucket=10000 |
| kernel-dispatcher | max-concurrency=8, prefetch=1 |
| kv-cache-service | warmup=true, size=4GB |

Fault taxonomy and examples

1) Scheduler admission faults
- Simulate admission controller rejecting low-priority requests. Useful to verify priority inversion handling.
- Implementation: intercept gRPC admit call and return DENY for target cohort.

2) Kernel stall / slow-path injection
- Inject latency in kernel-launch path to replicate slow kernel/driver calls (e.g., +50-200ms).
- Implementation: eBPF shim or fault library hook in kernel-dispatcher.

3) KV cache evictions and warmup loss
- Force eviction of KV entries or block warmup to validate warm-start behavior and sequence-length penalties.
- Implementation: call kv-cache-service admin API: /admin/evict?pattern=prefix

4) Telemetry loss and delayed traces
- Drop X of N spans or metrics to ensure alerting and root cause still actionable.
- Implementation: apply a proxy rule to drop telemetry from one pod.

5) Control-plane intermittent restarts
- Cycle scheduler-agent or runtime-coordinator with SIGTERM and shortened graceful window to confirm state reconciliation.

Test recipes (numbered procedures)

Procedure A: Priority admission denial (small blast radius)

1. Create experiment ticket and link pre-run checklist.
2. Deploy two workloads: customer-canary (high-prio) and batch-processing (low-prio) to runtime-fault-lab.<ticket-id>.
3. Configure scheduler-agent fault shim to DENY batch-processing cohort for 5 minutes. Command: curl -XPOST http://scheduler-agent-admin.local/faults -d '{"type":"deny","cohort":"batch","duration_s":300}'
4. Start loadgen: 200 rps high-prio, 50 rps low-prio.
5. Observe metrics for 10 minutes: kernel_queue_depth, kernel_exec_time_ms, p50/p95/p99 latencies per cohort.
6. Expected outcome: high-prio P99 remains within 1.2x baseline; low-prio sees increased errors but remains isolated.
7. If abort thresholds hit, trigger recovery (see Recovery Playbook).

Procedure B: Kernel slow-path degradation (medium blast radius)

1. Schedule rdm-large-v2 multi-shard model to the lab pool.
2. Inject 150ms artificial delay into kernel-launch via fault library: kubectl exec -n runtime-fault-lab <kernel-dispatcher-pod> -- /opt/faults/inject --delay-ms=150 --target=launch
3. Drive a synthetic mixed workload with sequence length distribution matching production 80th percentile.
4. Capture traces for affected requests and compare kernel_exec_time_ms distribution to baseline.
5. Expected outcome: increased tail latency; scheduler should reduce admission or route to fallback model if available. Verify fallback routing executed.

Procedure C: Observability loss and triage exercise (low blast radius)

1. Apply a proxy rule to drop 30% of spans from one kernel-dispatcher pod: kubectl apply -f drop-spans-config.yaml
2. Run a diagnosis run: collect stack traces and residual metrics.
3. Verify that alerts degrade to metric-backed alerts (e.g., spike in p99 + increased error budget burn) rather than purely tracing-based.
4. Review detection latency for degraded telemetry.

Telemetry and measurement checklist (must collect)

- Baseline metrics snapshot (pre-run): p50/p95/p99 latency, 5xx rate, kernel_queue_depth, kvcache_hit_ratio, gpu_util_percent, gpu_temp_celsius.
- Continuous traces for representative request IDs (jaeger) with sampling rate >= 20% for experiment window.
- Kernel-level histograms: exec_time_ms histogram, launch_time_ms, kernel_retries.
- Control-plane logs: scheduler-agent audit trail and admission decisions.
- Experiment artifact: a tarball with raw traces and metric snapshots uploaded to experiment ticket.

Expected success criteria

- No critical-route P99 > 1.5x baseline for > 5 minutes.
- Error budget consumption for critical routes < 10% during experiment window.
- Recoverable state: no manual rollback required to return to baseline after experiment finishes.

Recovery playbook (fast path)

1. Stop fault injection immediately.
2. If control-plane components are unstable, restart scheduler-agent with playground command: kubectl rollout restart deployment/scheduler-agent -n runtime-fault-lab
3. Rehydrate KV cache: curl -XPOST http://kv-cache-service.local/admin/warmup
4. Scale up fallback route capacity if model fallback is available: operator apply --scale fallback-route=+2
5. If GPU thermal threshold exceeded, drain affected node and run thermal cooldown: kubectl cordon <node> && kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
6. Attach experiment artifacts and incident note to ticket; schedule a postmortem if abort thresholds were hit.

Post-experiment validation

- Run a 30-minute smoke workload and compare latencies to pre-run baseline.
- Confirm operator can resume normal canary rollouts.
- Ensure dashboards are clear (no lingering alerts).

Rollout guidance for production migration

- Require a staging test performed on a production-like fleet with synthetic traffic mirroring.
- Schedule at low-traffic window; have on-call SRE and Product owner present.
- Limit initial duration to 60s and validate quick rollback path.

CLI and example config snippets

YAML sample for kernel delay fault (lab-only):

---
apiVersion: faults.redwood.ai/v1
kind: KernelDelay
metadata:
  name: kernel-delay-150ms
spec:
  target: kernel-launch
  delay_ms: 150
  duration_s: 600
  scope: {"namespace":"runtime-fault-lab-1234","labelSelector":"test-cohort=experimental"}
---

Admin command to evict kvcache prefix:

curl -XPOST http://kv-cache-service.local/admin/evict -d '{"pattern":"session:rdm-large:*"}' -H 'Content-Type: application/json'

Dashboards and alerts to confirm coverage

- Dashboard: Runtime Scheduler Overview (panels: admission decisions, queue depth, per-cohort latency)
- Dashboard: Kernel Health (exec histograms, retry rate, GPU util, temp)
- Alert: KernelLaunchErrorsRate > 0.5% for 5m
- Alert: P99_CriticalRoute > threshold for 3m (notify on-call and platform slack channel)

Ownership and cadence

- Runbook owner: Priya Natarajan (runtime resilience lead)
- Responsible team: Serving Runtime SRE / Platform
- Review cadence: Quarterly or after any production incident that touches scheduler behaviour

Appendix: quick troubleshooting matrix

- Symptom: high p99, kernel_queue_depth spikes
  - Likely cause: kernel slow-path or burst in admission. Check kernel_exec_time_ms histograms and kernel-launch qps.
  - Quick mitigation: enable admission-tightening rule or scale kernel-dispatcher pool.

- Symptom: missing traces but metrics present
  - Likely cause: telemetry proxy rule or sidecar failure.
  - Quick mitigation: restart telemetry sidecar on affected pod and increase metric-based alert sensitivity temporarily.

References

- Runtime decision graph (internal) — link: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/runtime-decision-graph-and-observed-behaviour-catalog-2026
- Scheduler telemetry proofing — link: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling/scheduler-telemetry-synthesis-and-proofing-2025

Change history

- 2025-11-02: Initial draft by Priya Natarajan.
- 2025-12-15: Added kernel delay and kvcache warmup recipes (reviewer Marcus Chen).
- 2026-01-12: Updated abort thresholds and recovery steps (reviewer Alison Rivera).

Summary

This document is a laboratory-grade procedure set for deterministic fault injection against the runtime scheduler and kernel handoff surface. It defines controlled experiments, safety gates, measurement artifacts, and a recovery playbook to be used by the Serving Runtime SRE/Platform teams when validating scheduler resilience, degradation policies and telemetry coverage.

Purpose

- Validate scheduler behaviour under targeted faults (node, kernel, eviction, preemption, telemetry loss).
- Ensure graceful degradation policies preserve SLOs within defined error budgets.
- Verify observability and automated recovery paths capture and mitigate incidents.

Scope

Applies to internal lab clusters (non-production), the runtime components listed below and the scheduler API surfaces used by runtime-operator and kernel-dispatcher. Not for production injection without executive approval and a production blast radius mitigation plan.

Owned components

- scheduler-agent (control plane component that enforces admission and prioritization)
- kernel-dispatcher (executor that selects kernels and schedules GPU work)
- runtime-coordinator (session lifecycle and KV cache hints)
- kv-cache-service (local caching layer)
- operator (canary and rollout automation)

Safety and approval gates

- Only approved lab clusters with isolated tenant IDs may be used.
- At least one reviewer (from reviewers list) must sign off pre-run.
- Pre-run checklist must be completed and attached to experiment ticket.
- If experiment breaches safety thresholds (see "Abort thresholds"), stop immediately and follow recovery steps.

Abort thresholds (hard stops)

- P99 latency increase > 2x baseline sustained > 3 minutes for critical route 
- 5xx rate > 1% of requests for > 2 minutes across the cluster
- GPU thermal threshold > 85°C on any device
- Operator or control-plane crashloop detected for > 2 minutes

- Cluster label: lab=true, isolation: network-policy enabled

source.title: Customer-managed audit log export: security controls and approval gates
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
# Purpose
This page defines the **mandatory security controls** and **approval gates** for enabling **customer-managed audit log export** from Redwood Inference to customer-controlled cloud storage (e.g., S3/GCS/Azure Blob) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

This is a control surface that touches: customer data boundaries, identity and access, encryption, and retention. **Do not enable export** without satisfying the requirements below.

# Scope
In scope:
- Export of **platform audit logs** (auth events, RBAC changes, API key lifecycle, console/admin actions, private control-plane actions).
- Delivery to a **customer-owned destination**.

Out of scope:
- Model inference payload logging (prompt/completion content). That remains disabled by default and requires a separate data logging exception.

# Definitions
- **Audit log**: event records emitted by Redwood control plane and selected data-plane components.
- **Customer-managed export**: Redwood pushes logs to an endpoint owned/controlled by the customer; the customer sets retention and access.
- **Export Connector**: Redwood-managed service that ships logs (see architecture page for details).

# Control requirements (must pass)
## 1) Data classification
- Audit logs are classified as **Customer-Sensitive Metadata**.
- Audit logs may include: user identifiers, IPs, org IDs, resource IDs, timestamps, and administrative actions.
- Audit logs must **not** include request bodies or model outputs.

## 2) Encryption
- In transit: TLS 1.2+ is required for any HTTPS endpoints.
- At rest: the customer destination must be configured with server-side encryption.
  - S3: SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS (customer preference).
  - GCS: Google-managed keys or CMEK.
  - Azure: Microsoft-managed keys or CMK.
- Redwood-side staging (if used by the connector) must be encrypted at rest using Redwood-managed KMS.

## 3) Least privilege access
- Hosted and Dedicated exports must use **assume-role / workload identity** patterns; no long-lived customer keys stored in Redwood.
- The role/policy must only permit:
  - write/put to a specific bucket/container prefix
  - list only if required by the connector health checks
- Policies must be reviewed by Security for correctness.

## 4) Network & destination allowlisting
- The destination must be customer-owned and reside in an allowed provider account/subscription/project per the contract.
- For Private deployments, outbound egress must be constrained by the customer (preferred) or by Redwood (if Redwood-managed networking is in scope).
- Public endpoints are allowed only if authenticated and protected with least-privilege credentials; **PrivateLink / private endpoints are preferred** when available.

## 5) Retention and deletion
- Default recommended destination retention: **90 days** (unless the customer requires more).
- If the customer requests retention < 30 days, note it in the request but it is allowed.
- If the customer requests retention > 1 year, Customer Success must confirm contract language supports it and that the customer understands storage cost/ownership.

## 6) Logging about logging (auditability of export)
- The platform must emit events for:
  - export enabled/disabled
  - destination changes (bucket/container/prefix)
  - auth/role changes used by the connector
  - delivery failures and retries
- These events must be visible in Redwood internal dashboards and retrievable for incident response.

# Approval gates (required)
## Gate A: Customer request validation (Customer Success + Support)
Required before Security review:
- customer company + deployment mode (Hosted / Dedicated / Private)
- target cloud + region
- desired log categories (standard only unless exception)
- retention intent
- contact for incident notifications

## Gate B: Security review (required for all modes)
Security must approve before any production enablement:
- destination configuration meets encryption and least privilege requirements
- no inference payload logging is included
- any cross-region delivery is evaluated (see Cross-region constraints)

Approval artifact:
- Jira internal-support ticket must include a Security comment: **“Approved for enablement”** with reviewer name + date.

## Gate C: Privacy/Legal check (conditional)
Required if any of the following are true:
- customer requests cross-region export that changes data residency guarantees
- customer requests inclusion of user email addresses or other PII fields beyond standard audit schema
- customer contract includes bespoke audit log clauses

# Cross-region constraints
- Default rule: **export must stay within the customer’s chosen region** that matches the deployment region.
- Cross-region export is allowed only when:
  1) Legal confirms it does not violate contractual residency terms
  2) Security confirms threat model is acceptable
  3) Customer acknowledges increased failure modes and latency

# Security exception process
If the customer cannot satisfy the above controls (e.g., insists on long-lived keys), file a Security exception under `security-and-compliance/risk-and-exceptions` and link it in the enablement ticket.

# Required evidence (attach/link in the Jira ticket)
- Destination policy snippet (IAM policy / role trust policy / workload identity binding)
- Confirmation of encryption at rest on destination
- Proof of ownership (account/subscription/project identifiers)
- For Private: network diagram or egress policy statement

# Related pages
- Audit log export architecture (Observability Standards)
- Hosted enablement runbook (Eng Platform)
- Dedicated customer setup runbook (Eng Infra)
- Private configuration guide (Eng Private Deployments)
- Support intake process (Customer Success & Support)

## 4) Network & destination allowlisting
- The destination must be customer-owned and reside in an allowed provider account/subscription/project per the contract.
- For Private deployments, outbound egress must be constrained by the customer (preferred) or by Redwood (if Redwood-managed networking is in scope).
- Public endpoints are allowed only if authenticated and protected with least-privilege credentials; **PrivateLink / private endpoints are preferred** when available.

## 5) Retention and deletion
- Default recommended destination retention: **90 days** (unless the customer requires more).
- If the customer requests retention < 30 days, note it in the request but it is allowed.
- If the customer requests retention > 1 year, Customer Success must confirm contract language supports it and that the customer understands storage cost/ownership.

## 6) Logging about logging (auditability of export)
- The platform must emit events for:
  - export enabled/disabled
  - destination changes (bucket/container/prefix)
  - auth/role changes used by the connector
  - delivery failures and retries
- These events must be visible in Redwood internal dashboards and retrievable for incident response.

# Approval gates (required)
## Gate A: Customer request validation (Customer Success + Support)
Required before Security review:
- customer company + deployment mode (Hosted / Dedicated / Private)
- target cloud + region
- desired log categories (standard only unless exception)
- retention intent
- contact for incident notifications

## Gate B: Security review (required for all modes)
Security must approve before any production enablement:
- destination configuration meets encryption and least privilege requirements
- no inference payload logging is included
- any cross-region delivery is evaluated (see Cross-region constraints)

Approval artifact:
- Jira internal-support ticket must include a Security comment: **“Approved for enablement”** with reviewer name + date.

## Gate C: Privacy/Legal check (conditional)
Required if any of the following are true:
- customer requests cross-region export that changes data residency guarantees
- customer requests inclusion of user email addresses or other PII fields beyond standard audit schema
- customer contract includes bespoke audit log clauses

# Cross-region constraints
- Default rule: **export must stay within the customer’s chosen region** that matches the deployment region.
- Cross-region export is allowed only when:
  1) Legal confirms it does not violate contractual residency terms
  2) Security confirms threat model is acceptable
  3) Customer acknowledges increased failure modes and latency

# Security exception process
If the customer cannot satisfy the above controls (e.g., insists on long-lived keys), file a Security exception under `security-and-compliance/risk-and-exceptions` and link it in the enablement ticket.

# Required evidence (attach/link in the Jira ticket)
- Destination policy snippet (IAM policy / role trust policy / workload identity binding)
- Confirmation of encryption at rest on destination
- Proof of ownership (account/subscription/project identifiers)
- For Private: network diagram or egress policy statement

# Related pages
- Audit log export architecture (Observability Standards)
- Hosted enablement runbook (Eng Platform)
- Dedicated customer setup runbook (Eng Infra)
- Private configuration guide (Eng Private Deployments)
- Support intake process (Customer Success & Support)

source.title: Inference Request Log Retention Policy
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
# Purpose
This policy defines Redwood Inference’s default retention for *inference request logs* and the process for customer-specific exceptions.

# Scope
Applies to Hosted API and Dedicated deployments operated by Redwood. For Private deployments, customer-managed logging may apply; however, any Redwood-operated telemetry collection still follows this policy unless explicitly excepted.

# Definitions
**Inference request logs** ("request logs") include: request/response bodies (prompt + completion), request metadata (model, tokens, latency, status code), and routing identifiers.

# Default retention
Unless otherwise approved as an exception:
- **Request/response bodies (prompt + completion): 30 days**
- **Request metadata (no prompt/completion text): 90 days**
- **Aggregated metrics (no per-request identifiers): 13 months**

# Storage locations (summary)
- Primary log store: `obs-logs-prod` (multi-region)
- Analytics warehouse: `redwood-usage-warehouse` (aggregated-only for long retention)

# When an exception is allowed
Exceptions may be approved for:
1) Regulatory / contractual requirements (e.g., customer requires shorter retention for payloads)
2) Security posture alignment (e.g., customer prohibits payload logging)
3) Legal hold / audit requirements (e.g., longer retention)

# Exception approval process
1) Open an internal support Jira issue (project: internal-support) with summary: "Log retention exception — <Customer>".
2) Include: requested retention, scope (payload vs metadata), deployment type, and impacted regions.
3) Security & Compliance reviews and either approves or rejects.
4) If approved, ensure:
   - The implementation change is tracked (service owner + rollout plan).
   - Customer confirmation is recorded (email or contract addendum).
   - The exception is added to the **Inference Log Retention Exception Register**.

# Source of truth
- Canonical policy: this page
- Canonical list of approved exceptions: **Inference Log Retention Exception Register** (Confluence)
- Execution evidence: Jira internal-support issues, Slack approval threads, customer comms (Gmail), and contract addenda (Drive)

# Notes
- An "exception" is any customer-specific retention for request/response bodies that differs from the default **30 days**.
- Metadata-only logging (no payload) is considered a retention exception if payload retention is set to **0 days**.

source.title: tenant-scoped-sdk-local-fallbacks-2024
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Overview

Purpose: This document defines recommended SDK design patterns for tenant-scoped authentication and robust local fallbacks. Its goal is to reduce support incidents and production outages in multi-tenant applications that call Redwood inference endpoints. The guidance is written for SDK implementers (client libraries and server-side helper libs) and for developer-facing code patterns used by product teams.

Intended audience:
- SDK engineers implementing Redwood client libraries
- Platform engineers integrating tenant-aware routing
- Support engineers troubleshooting tenant-impacted incidents

Why this matters
- Multi-tenant workloads commonly fail in two categories: auth/identity mispropagation and global rate-limit storms.
- Proper tenant-scoped tokens, local caches, and graceful fallbacks prevent noisy neighbor effects and provide predictable user experience when upstream capacity is constrained.

Key responsibilities
- SDK: perform tenant identity propagation, token refresh, bounded local caching, and deterministic fallback behavior.
- App: supply tenant context and define fallback UX.
- Platform: expose per-tenant rate quota headers and circuit-breaker metadata.

Recommended patterns (summary)
1) Tenant context must be explicit and immutable per request
- Each API call requires either a tenant_id header or a tenant-scoped API key. Do not rely on global process state.
2) Token lifecycle and refresh
- SDKs should implement background refresh with jittered scheduling and a single-flight refresh per tenant.
3) Local prefix caching for short prompts and embeddings
- Cache recent request/response pairs keyed by (tenant_id, prompt_hash, model, max_tokens). Keep TTL <= 5 minutes by default.
4) Rate-limit and quota-aware backoff
- Honor 429s + Retry-After header and implement exponential backoff with capped jitter.
5) Graceful degradation modes
- Define fast-fail, cached-response, degrade-to-embedding, and queued-retry strategies.

Detailed patterns and examples

1) Explicit tenant propagation
- Enforcement: SDK constructors should require a tenant context provider or explicit tenant_id for each call. Example signatures:
  - Node: client.generate({ tenantId, input, model })
  - Python: client.generate(tenant_id=..., prompt=..., model=...)

- Rationale: implicit global state leads to cross-tenant leaks and hard-to-reproduce bugs.

2) Single-flight token refresh (pseudo-code)
- Behaviour: when access token is expired or near expiry (<30s), allow only one inflight refresh operation per tenant to avoid stampeding.

Pseudo-code (Python style):
```
# per-tenant cached state
if token.near_expiry():
    await tenant_refresh_lock.wait_or_run(refresh_function)
# proceed with the fresh token
```

3) Local prefix / KV caching (config table)
- Default config values to start with (tunable):
| Key | Default | Notes |
| cache_ttl_seconds | 300 | Keep short to reduce staleness for dynamic prompts |
| cache_max_entries_per_tenant | 200 | Prevent unbounded memory growth |
| cache_key_components | tenant_id,prompt_hash,model,max_tokens | Ensures tenant isolation |

Example Node snippet (pseudo):
```
const key = `${tenantId}:${hash(prompt)}:${model}:${maxTokens}`;
const cached = cache.get(key);
if (cached) return cached;
const res = await client.call({tenantId, prompt, model});
cache.set(key, res, {ttl: config.cache_ttl_seconds});
return res;
```

4) Rate-limit handling and backoff strategy
- Behavior table:
| Response | SDK action | User-visible outcome |
| 429 + Retry-After | Respect header, jittered sleep, retry up to 3 times | May delay completion; falls back to cached response if available |
| 503 / 504 | Apply exponential backoff starting at 200ms, max 3 retries | Consider degrade-to-embedding strategy |
| Network error | Circuit-breaker increment and local short-circuit if threshold exceeded | Fast-fail mode after repeated failures |

Backoff algorithm (recommended parameters):
- base_delay_ms = 200
- max_delay_ms = 5000
- factor = 2.0
- jitter_fraction = 0.2
- max_retries = 3

5) Degradation strategies (choose one per codepath)
- Fast-fail: return a deterministic error to the caller immediately (use for synchronous user-facing flows).
- Cached-response: return last-known-good response if cached and TTL valid.
- Degrade-to-embedding: for long-form generation failures, fallback to shorter embedding-based retrieval + template reply.
- Queue-and-retry: push to durable work queue for eventual processing (async jobs).

Operational observability (what SDKs MUST emit)
- Telemetry events (logged + metric tags):
  - request.start (tags: tenant_id, model, route)
  - request.retry (tags: reason=rate_limit|network|gateway)
  - request.cached_hit (tags: tenant_id, cache_key)
  - token.refresh (tags: tenant_id, result=success|failure)
  - circuit.opened / circuit.closed (tags: tenant_id, route)
- Metrics to expose (per-tenant rollup):
  - requests_total, requests_success, requests_429, requests_503, cached_hits, token_refresh_failures, circuit_breaker_open_count
- Log context: always include tenant_id, request_id, and trace_id.

Support runbook - triage steps (for SRE / support)
1) Identify impacted tenants via logs and metrics (dashboard: SDK -> requests_429 by tenant).
2) Gather single example request: tenant_id, request_id, timestamp, model, SDK version.
3) Check token status: was a refresh attempted? (token.refresh events).
4) Check upstream headers: Retry-After, x-redwood-quota-remaining, x-redwood-tenant-quota.
5) If a tenant shows persistent 429s and no quota header: escalate to Platform with tenant config snapshot.
6) If circuit-breaker open: check SDK local metrics to confirm per-tenant threshold.

Example diagnostic CLI snippets
- Query logs (internal ELK):
```
# find all 429s for tenant abc-123 in last hour
kql: tenant_id:"abc-123" and http_status:429 and @timestamp:[now-1h TO now]
```
- Inspect SDK debug output (recommended env var):
  - REDWOOD_SDK_LOG=debug
  - REDWOOD_SDK_CACHE_DUMP=true

Compatibility and versioning notes
- New SDKs must be backward compatible for token metadata headers: the platform will accept both legacy header X-Redwood-Tenant and new header X-RW-TenantId for a transition period of 12 months.
- Feature gates: local-cache and degrade-to-embedding are opt-in per-major SDK (default off for 1.0.0 releases).

Example policy for product teams (short checklist)
1) Confirm tenant identity is explicit at call site.
2) Choose a degradation strategy appropriate for the UX (fast-fail for transactional, queue for background jobs).
3) Ensure SDK telemetry is enabled in staging and production.
4) Validate with synthetic load test: run 1k QPS multi-tenant traffic and observe per-tenant quota isolation.

Appendix A: Sample SDK config YAML
```
sdk:
  cache_ttl_seconds: 300
  cache_entries_per_tenant: 200
  backoff:
    base_delay_ms: 200
    max_delay_ms: 5000
    factor: 2.0
    jitter_fraction: 0.2
    max_retries: 3
  token:
    refresh_margin_seconds: 30
    single_flight: true
  telemetry:
    enabled: true
    log_level: INFO
```

Appendix B: Common support tickets and canonical fixes
- Symptom: Sporadic 401s for one tenant only
  - Cause: token refresh race, stale tenant-scoped API key in app config
  - Fix: Validate the app rotates tenant-scoped keys on deploy; enable single-flight refresh in SDK.
- Symptom: Sudden increase in 429s across tenants
  - Cause: platform-wide quota misconfiguration or routing degradation
  - Fix: Open platform incident, switch affected customers to reduced throughput plan, enable queued processing.

References and related docs
- SDK telemetry contract: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/docs-site/sdk-telemetry-contract-stability-playbook-2026
- Rate-limit etiquette and headers: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/rate-limit-and-quota-spec
- Multi-tenant security checklist: /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/multi-tenant-auth-checklist

Owner and maintenance
- Doc owner: Priya Natarajan (sdk-platform team)
- Review cadence: yearly or when SDK major version changes
- Contributors: Marco Silva (devx), Aisha Thompson (platform)

Feedback and changes
- To propose changes, create a PR against the docs repo and add reviewers listed above. For urgent corrections (bug in recommended behavior) tag @oncall-sdks in Slack and file a short changelog entry in the SDK release notes."

Example Node snippet (pseudo):
```
const key = `${tenantId}:${hash(prompt)}:${model}:${maxTokens}`;
const cached = cache.get(key);
if (cached) return cached;
const res = await client.call({tenantId, prompt, model});
cache.set(key, res, {ttl: config.cache_ttl_seconds});
return res;
```

4) Rate-limit handling and backoff strategy
- Behavior table:
| Response | SDK action | User-visible outcome |
| 429 + Retry-After | Respect header, jittered sleep, retry up to 3 times | May delay completion; falls back to cached response if available |
| 503 / 504 | Apply exponential backoff starting at 200ms, max 3 retries | Consider degrade-to-embedding strategy |
| Network error | Circuit-breaker increment and local short-circuit if threshold exceeded | Fast-fail mode after repeated failures |

Backoff algorithm (recommended parameters):
- base_delay_ms = 200
- max_delay_ms = 5000
- factor = 2.0
- jitter_fraction = 0.2
- max_retries = 3

5) Degradation strategies (choose one per codepath)
- Fast-fail: return a deterministic error to the caller immediately (use for synchronous user-facing flows).
- Cached-response: return last-known-good response if cached and TTL valid.
- Degrade-to-embedding: for long-form generation failures, fallback to shorter embedding-based retrieval + template reply.
- Queue-and-retry: push to durable work queue for eventual processing (async jobs).

Operational observability (what SDKs MUST emit)
- Telemetry events (logged + metric tags):
  - request.start (tags: tenant_id, model, route)
  - request.retry (tags: reason=rate_limit|network|gateway)
  - request.cached_hit (tags: tenant_id, cache_key)
  - token.refresh (tags: tenant_id, result=success|failure)
  - circuit.opened / circuit.closed (tags: tenant_id, route)
- Metrics to expose (per-tenant rollup):
  - requests_total, requests_success, requests_429, requests_503, cached_hits, token_refresh_failures, circuit_breaker_open_count
- Log context: always include tenant_id, request_id, and trace_id.

Support runbook - triage steps (for SRE / support)
1) Identify impacted tenants via logs and metrics (dashboard: SDK -> requests_429 by tenant).
2) Gather single example request: tenant_id, request_id, timestamp, model, SDK version.
3) Check token status: was a refresh attempted? (token.refresh events).
4) Check upstream headers: Retry-After, x-redwood-quota-remaining, x-redwood-tenant-quota.
5) If a tenant shows persistent 429s and no quota header: escalate to Platform with tenant config snapshot.
6) If circuit-breaker open: check SDK local metrics to confirm per-tenant threshold.

Example diagnostic CLI snippets
- Query logs (internal ELK):
```
# find all 429s for tenant abc-123 in last hour
kql: tenant_id:\"abc-123\" and http_status:429 and @timestamp:[now-1h TO now]
```
- Inspect SDK debug output (recommended env var):
  - REDWOOD_SDK_LOG=debug
  - REDWOOD_SDK_CACHE_DUMP=true

Compatibility and versioning notes
- New SDKs must be backward compatible for token metadata headers: the platform will accept both legacy header X-Redwood-Tenant and new header X-RW-TenantId for a transition period of 12 months.
- Feature gates: local-cache and degrade-to-embedding are opt-in per-major SDK (default off for 1.0.0 releases).

Example policy for product teams (short checklist)
1) Confirm tenant identity is explicit at call site.
2) Choose a degradation strategy appropriate for the UX (fast-fail for transactional, queue for background jobs).
3) Ensure SDK telemetry is enabled in staging and production.
4) Validate with synthetic load test: run 1k QPS multi-tenant traffic and observe per-tenant quota isolation.

Appendix A: Sample SDK config YAML
```
sdk:
  cache_ttl_seconds: 300
  cache_entries_per_tenant: 200
  backoff:
    base_delay_ms: 200
    max_delay_ms: 5000
    factor: 2.0
    jitter_fraction: 0.2
    max_retries: 3
  token:
    refresh_margin_seconds: 30
    single_flight: true
  telemetry:
    enabled: true
    log_level: INFO
```

Appendix B: Common support tickets and canonical fixes
- Symptom: Sporadic 401s for one tenant only
  - Cause: token refresh race, stale tenant-scoped API key in app config
  - Fix: Validate the app rotates tenant-scoped keys on deploy; enable single-flight refresh in SDK.
- Symptom: Sudden increase in 429s across tenants
  - Cause: platform-wide quota misconfiguration or routing degradation
  - Fix: Open platform incident, switch affected customers to reduced throughput plan, enable queued processing.

References and related docs
- SDK telemetry contract: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/docs-site/sdk-telemetry-contract-stability-playbook-2026
- Rate-limit etiquette and headers: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/rate-limit-and-quota-spec
- Multi-tenant security checklist: /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/multi-tenant-auth-checklist

Owner and maintenance
- Doc owner: Priya Natarajan (sdk-platform team)
- Review cadence: yearly or when SDK major version changes
- Contributors: Marco Silva (devx), Aisha Thompson (platform)

Feedback and changes
- To propose changes, create a PR against the docs repo and add reviewers listed above. For urgent corrections (bug in recommended behavior) tag @oncall-sdks in Slack and file a short changelog entry in the SDK release notes."

source.title: API deterministic output samples and canonical fixes
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Overview

This guide documents patterns, quickstart snippets, telemetry hooks, and canonical fixes for achieving reproducible (deterministic) API outputs when using Redwood SDKs and the Redwood API. It is intended for SDK authors, integration engineers, and support engineers who triage output variance regressions or write tests/benchmarks that require repeatable responses.

Why deterministic outputs matter

- Evals and regression tests depend on repeatable responses when measuring model drift or SDK changes.
- Customer integrations (prompt templates, scoring) sometimes rely on stable tokenization and sampling outputs for business logic.
- Determinism reduces flakiness in CI and helps isolate runtime vs model vs prompt issues.

Scope and assumptions

- Applies to text generation APIs (chat/generate/stream) and to SDKs wrapping those endpoints.
- Does not attempt to override model-level nondeterministic behavior (some models are inherently stochastic); instead we provide best-effort controls and debugging methods.
- Uses example endpoint: https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate and model names redwood-1.3, rw-open-7b as illustrative.

Quick checklist (short)

1) Pin model version and variant in every request (model+revision).
2) Set deterministic sampling parameters (temperature=0, top_p=1.0) where exact reproducibility is required.
3) Pin seed and random_state consistently across client and test harness.
4) Remove nondeterministic plugins/filters in the prompt pipeline.
5) Capture and replay full request metadata (headers, tokenizer_version, kv-cache flags).

Canonical request example (JSON)

{
  "model": "redwood-1.3@rev-2025-09-15",
  "input": [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are an assistant that must be deterministic; do not hallucinate."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Summarize: The process for X"}
  ],
  "temperature": 0.0,
  "top_p": 1.0,
  "max_tokens": 150,
  "seed": 123456789,
  "tokenizer_version": "v2.1.0",
  "client_sdk_version": "js-2.4.1"
}

Notes on fields:
- model: always include explicit revision hash or date to avoid silent model upgrades.
- seed: integer seed to control pseudo-random sampling in the runtime. Not all models will honor seed; check model capability doc.
- tokenizer_version: important when differences in tokenization change token-level outputs.
- client_sdk_version: helps triage SDK-side changes (serialization, newline handling).

JS SDK quickstart (deterministic generate)

// npm install @redwood/sdk
const Redwood = require('@redwood/sdk');
const client = new Redwood({ baseURL: 'https://api.redwood.ai', apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY });

(async () => {
  const res = await client.generate({
    model: 'redwood-1.3@rev-2025-09-15',
    input: [{ role: 'system', content: 'Deterministic assistant' }, { role: 'user', content: 'List three properties of quartz.' }],
    temperature: 0.0,
    top_p: 1.0,
    seed: 42,
    tokenizer_version: 'v2.1.0'
  });
  console.log(res.output_text);
})();

Repro harness (CLI)

We provide a small deterministic replay harness that:
- Accepts a JSON request payload (full headers + body).
- Replays request against a pinned model revision.
- Compares token-level output (token ids + text) to golden artifact.

Usage: 
repro-harness replay --payload ./tests/golden_request.json --golden ./tests/golden_output.json --endpoint https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate

Golden artifact format (short):
- request.json (the full request object)
- response_tokens.json (list of token ids)
- response_text.txt (the decoded text)

Telemetry hooks to collect for each determinism test

- request.id (UUID)
- model and model_revision
- seed and sampling params (temperature, top_p, top_k)
- tokenizer_version and client_sdk_version
- runtime_kv_cache_enabled (true/false)
- response.token_ids (logged as hashed array for PII safety)
- latency_ms and model_compute_ms
- environment (dedicated/dedicated-burst/hosted)

Top support issues and canonical fixes

Table: common symptom -> likely cause -> canonical fix

- Symptom: Responses differ across runs though temperature=0 and seed set.
  - Likely cause: Model revision changed or no revision pinned.
  - Fix: Pin model to @rev-YYYY-MM-DD or commit hash in request; replay against specific revision.

- Symptom: Output text differs but token ids match.
  - Likely cause: Different tokenizer_version used for decode.
  - Fix: Standardize tokenizer_version in requests and in post-processing decode; store tokenizer_version in telemetry.

- Symptom: Streaming outputs interleave with other sessions (partial tokens mismatch).
  - Likely cause: Misrouted or shared connection, incorrect session id.
  - Fix: Ensure per-request session_id and proper connection multiplexing; use SDK streaming client helpers.

- Symptom: Golden fails in CI intermittently on Windows/macOS, but passes on Linux.
  - Likely cause: Line endings or unicode normalization differences.
  - Fix: Normalize output with NFC and LF before diffing; use portable decode routines.

- Symptom: Deterministic seed ignored for rw-open-7b.
  - Likely cause: Not all model variants support seed-based determinism; rw-open-7b sampling layer uses nondeterministic kernels.
  - Fix: Use a Redwood-verified deterministic variant (redwood-1.3@deterministic) or change tests to compare metrics instead of exact text.

Investigation playbook (step-by-step)

1) Confirm model and revision: check request.model field and runtime logs for served_revision.
2) Re-run with temperature=0.0 and seed used in failing run. Capture token ids.
3) Replay golden request using repro-harness against the exact revision.
4) If token ids differ, collect environment info: hardware type, runtime kernel version, quantization profile.
5) If token ids same but decoded text differs, compare tokenizer_version and decoder normalization.
6) Open a triage ticket including: request.json, response_tokens.json, runtime logs (trace id), repro-harness output, and telemetry snapshot.

Operational guidance for SDK authors

- Always include client_sdk_version in the request metadata header: X-Redwood-SDK-Version.
- Provide helper to canonicalize newline and unicode normalization in language binding.
- Expose seed argument in all SDKs (even if not used by underlying model) and surface a capability flag: supports_seed: true/false.
- When introducing runtime changes that can affect determinism (tokenizer upgrades, quant kernel changes), publish a deterministic compatibility note in the SDK CHANGELOG and add an opt-in flag for rolling customers.

Sample compatibility matrix (text table)

Model Variant | supports_seed | tokenizer_version | deterministic_variant_available
redwood-1.3@rev-2025-09-15 | true | v2.1.0 | yes
rw-open-7b@rev-2025-06-02 | false | v1.9.4 | no

Best-practice test structure for regressions

1) Small unit test: assert stable decoding of short, deterministic prompts using seed.
2) Integration test: run repro-harness against pinned model revision in CI; compare token ids with golden.
3) Smoke CI: light-weight text-match on canonical properties (e.g., presence of expected headings) if deterministic variant not available.
4) Fallback: toleration thresholds for token variation expressed as percentage similarity of token sequences (e.g., at least 98% token overlap).

SDK examples of canonical fixes (common code patches)

1) Fix: line ending normalization in Node SDK\- before:
const out = Buffer.from(resp).toString();
// after:
const out = Buffer.from(resp).toString().replace(/\r\n/g, '\n');

2) Fix: ensure JSON serialization uses stable key order (python)\- before: json.dumps(payload)\- after: json.dumps(payload, sort_keys=True, separators=(',', ':'))

Rollout and customer communication playbook

- When a deterministic-impacting change is required:
  1) Draft a short RFC describing change, risk, and migration steps.
  2) Post to eng-developer-experience and eng-serving-runtime for review.
  3) Create a canary release targeting internal and 3 paying customers with high coverage.
  4) Provide a dedicated migration guide and repro harness artifacts for customers.
  5) Ship with a 2-week opt-in before forced migration for low-risk changes; for high-risk, provide explicit scheduling and support.

Ownership and escalation

- Primary owner: SDK Core team (eng-developer-experience) for client-side fixes.
- Secondary owner: Serving runtime (eng-serving-runtime) for model/tokenizer/runtime kernel changes.
- Escalation path: open incident in PagerDuty with tag 'determinism' -> assign runtime oncall -> involve SDK oncall for client-side artifacts.

Related resources

- Repro harness repo (internal): git.internal.redwood/repro-harness (use the repro-harness CLI documented above).
- Tokenizer compatibility table: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling/tokenizer-compat-matrix
- SDK CHANGELOG template: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/quickstarts-and-templates/sdk-patch-release-playbook-and-compat-rules-2026

Appendix: minimal golden artifact example

request.json:
{
  "model": "redwood-1.3@rev-2025-09-15",
  "input": [{"role":"user","content":"Define magnetism in one sentence."}],
  "temperature": 0.0,
  "seed": 12345,
  "tokenizer_version": "v2.1.0"
}

response_tokens.json:
[223, 45, 1982, 12, 9001]

response_text.txt:
"Magnetism is a force produced by moving electric charges and magnetic materials that exerts attraction or repulsion on other charges or materials."

If you have questions or want walkthrough pairing for integrating repro-harness into your test suite, add a comment on this page or ping @Priya in Slack (#devx-sdk).

  - Likely cause: Model revision changed or no revision pinned.
  - Fix: Pin model to @rev-YYYY-MM-DD or commit hash in request; replay against specific revision.

- Symptom: Output text differs but token ids match.
  - Likely cause: Different tokenizer_version used for decode.
  - Fix: Standardize tokenizer_version in requests and in post-processing decode; store tokenizer_version in telemetry.

- Symptom: Streaming outputs interleave with other sessions (partial tokens mismatch).
  - Likely cause: Misrouted or shared connection, incorrect session id.
  - Fix: Ensure per-request session_id and proper connection multiplexing; use SDK streaming client helpers.

- Symptom: Golden fails in CI intermittently on Windows/macOS, but passes on Linux.
  - Likely cause: Line endings or unicode normalization differences.
  - Fix: Normalize output with NFC and LF before diffing; use portable decode routines.

- Symptom: Deterministic seed ignored for rw-open-7b.
  - Likely cause: Not all model variants support seed-based determinism; rw-open-7b sampling layer uses nondeterministic kernels.
  - Fix: Use a Redwood-verified deterministic variant (redwood-1.3@deterministic) or change tests to compare metrics instead of exact text.

Investigation playbook (step-by-step)

1) Confirm model and revision: check request.model field and runtime logs for served_revision.
2) Re-run with temperature=0.0 and seed used in failing run. Capture token ids.
3) Replay golden request using repro-harness against the exact revision.
4) If token ids differ, collect environment info: hardware type, runtime kernel version, quantization profile.
5) If token ids same but decoded text differs, compare tokenizer_version and decoder normalization.
6) Open a triage ticket including: request.json, response_tokens.json, runtime logs (trace id), repro-harness output, and telemetry snapshot.

Operational guidance for SDK authors

- Always include client_sdk_version in the request metadata header: X-Redwood-SDK-Version.
- Provide helper to canonicalize newline and unicode normalization in language binding.
- Expose seed argument in all SDKs (even if not used by underlying model) and surface a capability flag: supports_seed: true/false.
- When introducing runtime changes that can affect determinism (tokenizer upgrades, quant kernel changes), publish a deterministic compatibility note in the SDK CHANGELOG and add an opt-in flag for rolling customers.

Sample compatibility matrix (text table)

Model Variant | supports_seed | tokenizer_version | deterministic_variant_available
redwood-1.3@rev-2025-09-15 | true | v2.1.0 | yes
rw-open-7b@rev-2025-06-02 | false | v1.9.4 | no

Best-practice test structure for regressions

1) Small unit test: assert stable decoding of short, deterministic prompts using seed.
2) Integration test: run repro-harness against pinned model revision in CI; compare token ids with golden.
3) Smoke CI: light-weight text-match on canonical properties (e.g., presence of expected headings) if deterministic variant not available.
4) Fallback: toleration thresholds for token variation expressed as percentage similarity of token sequences (e.g., at least 98% token overlap).

SDK examples of canonical fixes (common code patches)

1) Fix: line ending normalization in Node SDK\- before:
const out = Buffer.from(resp).toString();
// after:
const out = Buffer.from(resp).toString().replace(/\r
/g, '
');

2) Fix: ensure JSON serialization uses stable key order (python)\- before: json.dumps(payload)\- after: json.dumps(payload, sort_keys=True, separators=(',', ':'))

Rollout and customer communication playbook

- When a deterministic-impacting change is required:
  1) Draft a short RFC describing change, risk, and migration steps.
  2) Post to eng-developer-experience and eng-serving-runtime for review.
  3) Create a canary release targeting internal and 3 paying customers with high coverage.
  4) Provide a dedicated migration guide and repro harness artifacts for customers.
  5) Ship with a 2-week opt-in before forced migration for low-risk changes; for high-risk, provide explicit scheduling and support.

Ownership and escalation

- Primary owner: SDK Core team (eng-developer-experience) for client-side fixes.
- Secondary owner: Serving runtime (eng-serving-runtime) for model/tokenizer/runtime kernel changes.
- Escalation path: open incident in PagerDuty with tag 'determinism' -> assign runtime oncall -> involve SDK oncall for client-side artifacts.

Related resources

- Repro harness repo (internal): git.internal.redwood/repro-harness (use the repro-harness CLI documented above).
- Tokenizer compatibility table: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling/tokenizer-compat-matrix
- SDK CHANGELOG template: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/quickstarts-and-templates/sdk-patch-release-playbook-and-compat-rules-2026

Appendix: minimal golden artifact example

request.json:
{
  "model": "redwood-1.3@rev-2025-09-15",
  "input": [{"role":"user","content":"Define magnetism in one sentence."}],
  "temperature": 0.0,
  "seed": 12345,
  "tokenizer_version": "v2.1.0"
}

response_tokens.json:
[223, 45, 1982, 12, 9001]

response_text.txt:
"Magnetism is a force produced by moving electric charges and magnetic materials that exerts attraction or repulsion on other charges or materials."

If you have questions or want walkthrough pairing for integrating repro-harness into your test suite, add a comment on this page or ping @Priya in Slack (#devx-sdk).

source.title: Customer‑sensitive Notice Lifecycle for Degraded Inference
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Summary
This playbook defines the lifecycle and templates for communicating with high‑sensitivity customers (enterprise, regulated) during inference quality or availability degradations. It standardizes timings, ownership, escalation paths, and message content so notices are predictable, legally defensible, and aligned with SLAs/SLOs.

Scope and definitions
- Applies to incidents affecting: text generation API, chat, embeddings, reranking, or routing logic that can materially affect customer output quality or availability.
- High‑sensitivity customer: an account with contractual notice SLAs, data residency constraints, or regulated workloads.
- Severity tiers used here map to company SLOs:
  - Severity 1 (S1): Total service outage or >50% customer impact on request success for >5 minutes.
  - Severity 2 (S2): Significant degradation (latency >3x P95 or quality regressions for critical routes) impacting key customers for >15 minutes.
  - Severity 3 (S3): Minor or localized issues not materially affecting customer business.

Primary goals
1) Deliver timely, accurate, and trancheable communications for sensitive customers.
2) Minimize downstream risk to customers (data loss, compliance breaches).
3) Ensure evidence collection and signoffs needed for credits/regulatory reporting.
4) Limit repeated noise: escalate only when new material information is available.

Ownership and roles
- Bridge Lead (BL): senior oncall person running the incident bridge, owns public message cadence.
- Customer Liaison (CL): Customer Success / Technical Account Manager assigned to the customer; triages customer concerns and personalizes messages.
- SRE Investigator (SRE): owns technical triage and evidence collection.
- Product / Security SME (SME): consults for quality regressions that may require model rollbacks.
- Legal / Compliance: engaged for regulated customer notifications or incident reporting obligations.

Triage and notification flow (numbered procedure)
1. Detection: monitoring alerts or customer report triggers initial triage. If SRE determines S1/S2, create an incident in the incident tracker and open a bridge within 5 minutes.
2. BL confirms severity and whether sensitive customers are affected. If yes, notify Customer Liaison immediately (call + slack).
3. CL compiles the impacted customer list (account IDs, contacts, contract clauses).
4. BL issues the first public status update (status page + internal Slack #ops) within the timing windows below. CL independently calls/email 2–3 primary customer contacts for S1/S2 within 15 minutes.
5. SRE captures evidence (see Evidence Checklist) and produces an interim technical summary for CL to include in customer notices.
6. Every update must be reviewed by BL and CL before external send; Legal is looped when PII, contractual breaches, or credits are likely.

Timing windows and cadence (table described as text)
- Initial public status (status.redwood.com): S1 = within 5 minutes, S2 = within 15 minutes.
- Sensitive customer private notice (email/call): S1 = within 15 minutes, S2 = within 30 minutes.
- Follow‑ups: every 30 minutes for S1 until mitigation; every 60 minutes for S2 until stabilization; S3 as needed.
- Final resolution notice: within 24 hours of closure with summary and postmortem link.

Escalation criteria
- If a single high‑sensitivity customer reports a business impact (e.g., revenue loss, regulatory non‑compliance), escalate to BL -> Head of SRE -> Legal within 10 minutes.
- If evidence suggests data exposure or integrity loss, treat as security incident and follow security incident guide.

Evidence collection checklist (SRE Investigator)
- Request/response logs (10 minutes before onset to 30 minutes after resolution) for affected tenant IDs.
- P95/P99 latency graphs for impacted endpoints and model variants.
- Error rate and response code histograms.
- Routing rules / fallback activations and polyfills triggered.
- Deployment timeline (git hash, rollout percentage) correlated with incident start time.
- KV cache eviction traces and cache hit ratios.
- If model quality suspected: prompt set examples, sampling of outputs (redact PII), evaluation harness scores.
- Attach all artifacts to the incident tracker and mark as exportable for legal review.

Message templates (placeholders in CAPITALS)
1) Initial private notice (email + optional call script)
Subject: Redwood — Incident affecting inference for ACCOUNT_NAME (INCIDENT_ID)
Body: Hello CUSTOMER_CONTACT_NAME,
We are investigating an incident impacting inference availability/quality for ACCOUNT_NAME. Current status: INVESTIGATION IN PROGRESS.
What we know now:
- Start time: INCIDENT_START_TIME UTC
- Symptom: e.g., elevated 5xx errors, increased latency for ROUTE_NAME/model VARIANT
- Impacted customers: LIST (you are listed)
What we’re doing:
- Bridge open, SRE investigating, routing and fallback checks in progress
- Immediate mitigations: ROUTE_REROUTE or ROLLBACK plan in motion
Expected cadence:
- We will provide the next update in NEXT_UPDATE_WINDOW or sooner if material changes occur.
If you need a call now, please reply with preferred number/time.
Regards,
Customer Liaison (CL_NAME) / Bridge Lead (BL_NAME)

2) Status page short form (for public page)
Title: Degraded inference latency for ROUTE_NAME — INVESTIGATING
Details: We are investigating elevated latency affecting text generation APIs in REGION. Some requests may time out or return degraded responses. Engineers are actively investigating.
Impact: Customers in REGION may see higher latency or increased 5xx error rates.
Next update: TIMESTAMP

3) Resolution notice (private + public)
Subject (private): Incident INCIDENT_ID — Resolved for ACCOUNT_NAME
Body: Hi CUSTOMER_CONTACT_NAME,
Summary: The incident that started at INCIDENT_START_TIME UTC has been resolved at RESOLUTION_TIME UTC. Root cause (preliminary): e.g., misrouted model variant after an incremental rollout causing cache churn.
Customer impact: What the customer saw and for how long.
Mitigation performed: Steps taken (rollback, scaling, config fix).
Credit / remediation: If contractually required, indicate next steps and timeline for credit calculation.
Postmortem: A full postmortem will be published at LINK within POSTMORTEM_WINDOW (typically 72 hours for S1).
Thank you for your patience.
Regards,
CL_NAME, Customer Liaison

Customer escalation matrix and delivery channels
- Channel preference order: Phone call (for S1), Encrypted email (for regulatory customers), Slack direct message (if integrated), status page update.
- For customers on private deployments (Dedicated/Private): follow the private deployment contact protocol and include any on‑prem runbook steps executed.

Legal and compliance triggers (when to involve Legal)
- Data exfiltration or unauthorized access suspected.
- Regulatory customers (HIPAA, GDPR, FINRA) where notification windows are contractually defined.
- Any potential SLA credit calculations requiring invoice adjustments.

Post‑incident deliverables and deadlines
- 24h: Interim incident summary emailed to affected customers.
- 72h: Preliminary postmortem published internally and shared with affected customers (S1).
- 7 days: Final postmortem with remediation plan (owner, timeline) and evidence attached.
- 30 days: Completion report for long‑running remediation items.

Game day and runbook integration
- Quarterly game days should exercise the entire customer notice lifecycle end‑to-end: detect -> bridge -> CL call -> evidence attach -> customer follow‑up -> postmortem.
- Runbooks to validate during game days: status page publish automation, call tree for CL, export script for evidence artifacts.
- Scoring rubric: time-to-first-notice, evidence completeness score, customer follow‑up within SLA.

On‑call rotation notes
- Primary oncall must be capable of opening the bridge and identifying affected contracts. Secondary oncall supports evidence exports. For sensitive customers, prioritize warm handoffs between BL and CL.
- When scheduling rotations, ensure CL coverage during region business hours for top 25 customers.

Appendices
A. Example call script for CL (short)
Hello NAME, this is CL_NAME from Redwood. We’re investigating an issue affecting your inference requests. We have opened an incident INCIDENT_ID and will follow up within TIME_WINDOW. Are you currently experiencing outages that we should prioritize?
B. Evidence export CLI (example)
redwood-ops export-incident --id INCIDENT_ID --tenants ACCOUNT_ID --from START_TS --to END_TS --include-logs --format tar.gz
C. Postmortem template fields to include- Incident summary, timeline, root cause analysis, contributors, customer impact summary, remediation actions, compensations (if any), evidence link, lessons learned.

Related links
- Service SLO catalog: /confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets
- Incident tracker: https://incidents.redwood.internal
- Status page authoring guide: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/status-page-and-comm-templates

Revision history
- 2025-03-15: Created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2025-07-10: Added evidence checklist and legal triggers (reviewed by Legal).
- 2025-10-02: Tightened follow-up cadence for S2 and added game day scoring rubric.

- Service SLO catalog: /confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets
- Incident tracker: https://incidents.redwood.internal
- Status page authoring guide: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/status-page-and-comm-templates

Revision history
- 2025-03-15: Created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2025-07-10: Added evidence checklist and legal triggers (reviewed by Legal).
- 2025-10-02: Tightened follow-up cadence for S2 and added game day scoring rubric.

source.title: Model Serving Standard: Model Version Readiness Requirements
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
h1. Purpose
This standard defines what must be true for a model version to be considered production-ready on Redwood Inference. Rollout plans MUST reference the readiness artifacts listed here.

h1. Definitions
* Model version: immutable build of weights + tokenizer + runtime settings, identified by a version id.
* Compatibility: whether a version can serve a given request type (chat/completions/embeddings/rerank) and feature set (streaming, tool calling, structured output).

h1. Required readiness artifacts (all production versions)
A model version is READY_FOR_PROD only when the following are complete and linked in the model registry entry:

h2. A) Functional compatibility
* Supported request types: chat, completions, embeddings, rerank (list explicitly)
* Streaming support: yes/no and known limitations
* Tool/function calling: supported schemas and known differences
* Structured output: supported formats (JSON mode, grammar constraints)
* Token limits: max input context, max output, max total
* Safety behavior notes (if safety layers are applied)

h2. B) Performance profile
* Hardware compatibility: GPU types and minimum driver/runtime versions
* Throughput: tokens/sec/GPU under representative load
* Latency: p50/p95 E2E and TTFT under representative prompts
* Batching behavior: recommended max batch size and expected batch fill
* Cache behavior: prefix/KV cache hit rate expectations

h2. C) Reliability and failure modes
* Known error modes (OOM, long-tail latency spikes, kernel issues)
* Backpressure behavior: how admission control rejects are surfaced
* Safe degradation: whether the version can be used as a fallback target

h2. D) Quality baseline
* Internal eval suite results (links)
* Regression delta vs current default version
* Known quality trade-offs (e.g., better reasoning, worse formatting)

h2. E) Operational ownership
* Owning team for serving runtime compatibility issues
* Oncall escalation path
* Dashboard links (minimum set)

h1. Additional requirements for default versions
If a version will become the default behind a widely used alias, it MUST additionally have:
* A successful canary roll to at least 25% traffic for >= 2 hours
* A documented fallback chain that includes the prior default version
* A completed customer communications review for Dedicated/Private customers

h1. Exceptions
Any exception to this standard must be approved via the risk-and-exceptions process and referenced in the rollout plan with the exception id.
