source.title: Terraform policy + network canary: GPU egress validation harness
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Summary:
This document describes a repeatable, policy-driven validation harness we use during GPU tenant onboarding to ensure Terraform modules, VPC/subnet/network ACLs, and egress controls meet Redwood Inference networking and security requirements before production promotion. The harness combines: Terraform plan validation, OPA/Rego policy checks, a CI/CD canary stage that deploys lightweight network probes, and synthetic inference traffic checks to validate egress, routes, and KV cache behavior under bounded load.

Goals:
- Prevent misconfigured network paths that would allow uncontrolled egress or violate tenant isolation SLOs.
- Automate policy-as-code checks early in CI to fail unsafe changes pre-merge.
- Provide a canary runtime test that validates real network behavior and telemetry before full rollout.
- Provide clear rollback/mitigation actions and owner escalation for failures.

Scope and non-goals:
- In scope: Terraform modules that provision tenant VPCs, subnets, NAT/Egress gateways, route tables, security groups, private link endpoints, and GPU host attachments. Also CI/CD pipelines that push these modules to staging.
- Out of scope: downstream application-level authentication (handled by app teams) and tenant billing reconciliation flows.

Context and motivation:
On several past onboardings, tenants experienced unexpected outbound flows (via misconfigured NAT or route misattachments) and intermittent latency due to cross-account egress routing. We implemented a structured harness so network configuration errors are caught automatically and observable artifacts (probe traces, flow logs) are available to owners.

High-level architecture:
1) Git push -> Terraform plan stage (ci).
2) Run policy-as-code (OPA/Rego) over plan JSON (terraform show -json) to assert invariants.
3) If policy passes, CD creates a temporary canary workspace in the target account and deploys:
   - a probe pod/instance (Linux microVM) with tcpdump, curl, iperf3 and our probe agent
   - ephemeral test routing (route table entries that mirror production target path but isolated to canary)
4) The probe agent executes network checks, synthetic L2/L3 validation, and application-layer synthetic token generation to simulate inference (short prompt generation).
5) Gather telemetry: VPC flow logs, probe logs, cloud NAT / gateway metrics, and Redwood telemetry (egress bytes by tenant, latency percentiles).
6) Decision gate (pass/fail/promote) based on policy thresholds; auto-destroy on pass or alert and rollback on fail.

Terraform module contract (developer checklist):
- Module must expose: vpc_id, public_subnet_ids (for NAT gateways), private_subnet_ids (for GPU hosts), egress_nat_config (object), route_table_tags (list), and optional egress_mirror_endpoint.
- Module must include tagging policy: environment, tenant_id, owner_team, compliance_level.
- Module must include outputs for: gateway_ids, route_table_ids, and network_acl_ids.

Policy-as-code rules (examples):
- No module may create an internet-facing route for private_subnet_ids.
- NAT gateways must be tagged with allowed_burst_accounts.
- Route tables attached to GPU host subnets must reference approved transit gateway attachments or private link endpoints; direct 0.0.0.0/0 on these route tables is disallowed.
- Security groups for probe instances allow egress only to validated telemetry endpoints during canary (list provided).

Example Rego snippets (for illustration - place in policies/egress):
package redwood.iac.egress

# disallow 0.0.0.0/0 on private subnets
deny[msg] {
  input.resources[_].type == "aws_route"
  route := input.resources[_].values
  route.route_table_id == input.private_route_table
  route.destination_cidr_block == "0.0.0.0/0"
  msg = sprintf("private subnet route table %v has 0.0.0.0/0", [route.route_table_id])
}

# require nat gateway tagging
deny[msg] {
  input.resources[_].type == "aws_nat_gateway"
  nat := input.resources[_].values
  not nat.tags["allowed_burst_accounts"]
  msg = "nat gateway missing allowed_burst_accounts tag"
}

Canary test scenarios (run in CI job: canary-network-probe):
1) Basic reachability: probe pings expected internal endpoints (model store, config services) and fails if > 5% packet loss in 30s window.
2) Egress policy validation: probe attempts outbound TCP connect to three classes: allowed telemetry endpoints, blocked Internet host, and unauthorized external IP. Allowed connections must succeed; blocked must be rejected within 3s with TCP RST or no route.
3) Simulated inference: probe posts a 128-token prompt to a local inference stub behind the same VPC path to validate latency <= 250ms p50 for small prompts.
4) Bursty KV load: run 30s of KV cache warmup with synthetic keys to ensure egress flow stabilization (monitor NAT connection churn and ephemeral port usage).
5) Flow-log audit: ensure VPC flow logs contain only expected endpoints and annotate suspicious flows for manual review.

Pass/fail thresholds (canary gate):
- Reachability packet loss < 5% (fail otherwise)
- No unauthorized outbound connections observed in flow logs or probe logs
- Synthetic inference p50 latency <= 250ms and p95 <= 800ms
- NAT connection churn (new connections/min) below configured baseline (default: 120/min per canary instance)
- All policy-as-code checks returned zero denies

CI/CD pipeline (recommended GitHub Actions & Cross-account runner):
1) terraform fmt && terraform validate
2) terraform plan -out=plan.tfplan && terraform show -json plan.tfplan > plan.json
3) opa test --fail-on-external --data policies plan.json (use conftest or opal if desired)
4) if opa OK -> trigger ephemeral canary workspace (cd terraform apply -var-file=canary.tfvars) in staging account
5) deploy probe artifacts (container image built by CI) and wait for health
6) run canary-network-probe suite (script in ./ci/scripts/canary_probe.sh)
7) gather artifacts and post to the PR: probe logs, flow log digest, metric snapshot
8) decision: auto-approve/promote or fail and open incident ticket with owners

Sample ci command (can be copied into GHA job):
- name: Run policy and canary
  run: |
    terraform init -backend-config="workspace=canary-${{ github.sha }}"
    terraform plan -out=plan.tfplan
    terraform show -json plan.tfplan > plan.json
    conftest test plan.json --policy policies/egress
    if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then exit 1; fi
    ./ci/scripts/trigger-canary.sh --workspace canary-${{ github.sha }}

Observability and artifacts to collect:
- VPC flow logs (1 minute aggregation for canary runs)
- Probe stdout/stderr, tcpdump captures (pcap), and curl/iperf outputs
- NAT/gateway metrics: active connections, bytes_out, error_rate
- Terraform plan JSON and OPA/Rego evaluation result (policy logs)
- Redwood per-tenant egress counters and trace ids for synthetic inference requests

Ownership and escalation:
- Module author: primary contact for Terraform module changes and first responder for policy failures.
- Network oncall: secondary owner for canary network failures and flow log anomalies.
- SRE infra owner: responsible for CI runner, ephemeral workspace cleanup, and automation reliability.

Rollback and remediation playbook (fast-path):
1) If canary fails policy checks: block merge, tag author, and require policy fix.
2) If canary fails runtime probes (egress/latency): disable promotion, destroy canary workspace, and open a P1 with network-oncall.
3) Apply temporary circuit-breaker: attach a deny-egress security group to the tenant or remove route attachments (manual runbook steps documented in runbooks/oncall).
4) If suspected code regression in TF module: revert to last known-good module tag and re-run harness.

Cost and cleanup considerations:
- Canary workspace is ephemeral and should be auto-destroyed within 30 minutes of test completion (success or failure).
- Limit concurrent canaries per account to 4 by default to avoid NAT/Egress billing spikes; increase only with SRE approval.

Operational run cadence and metrics to track:
- Weekly: number of PRs blocked by OPA rules and top 5 policy violations.
- Per-onboarding: canary pass rate and median time to remediation.
- Monthly: upstream false positives in policy rules (policy churn) and probe flakiness rate.

Appendix A: sample minimal terraform output contract (text):
outputs = [
  { name = "vpc_id", value = "vpc-xxxx" },
  { name = "private_subnet_ids", value = ["subnet-1","subnet-2"] },
  { name = "egress_gateway_id", value = "nat-xxx" }
]

Appendix B: quick troubleshooting checklist for network-oncall:
- Verify the canary workspace exists and terraform apply completed without errors.
- Check probe pod health and logs (kubectl logs).
- Pull and inspect pcap from probe; grep for unexpected destinations.
- Cross-reference flow logs for unexpected elastic IPs or transit attachments.
- If NAT connection churn is high, escalate to infra owner to inspect connection tracking and possible port exhaustion.

References and related pages:
- See related Terraform module conventions: /confluence/eng-infra/terraform-and-iac/tf-module-compatibility-and-deprecation-plan
- Canary runner implementation examples: internal repo redwood-infra/canary-runner (link in repo index)
- Policy library: policies/egress in git infra-policy repo

Contact and next steps:
- For implementation questions or to onboard a module into this harness, contact author (Priya Natarajan) or reviewers listed above.
- Roadmap items: integrate Opal for real-time plan gating, add automated remediation playbooks for common policy violations, and surface canary artifacts in the Redwood Console onboarding checklist.

  route := input.resources[_].values
  route.route_table_id == input.private_route_table
  route.destination_cidr_block == "0.0.0.0/0"
  msg = sprintf("private subnet route table %v has 0.0.0.0/0", [route.route_table_id])
}

# require nat gateway tagging
deny[msg] {
  input.resources[_].type == "aws_nat_gateway"
  nat := input.resources[_].values
  not nat.tags["allowed_burst_accounts"]
  msg = "nat gateway missing allowed_burst_accounts tag"
}

Canary test scenarios (run in CI job: canary-network-probe):
1) Basic reachability: probe pings expected internal endpoints (model store, config services) and fails if > 5% packet loss in 30s window.
2) Egress policy validation: probe attempts outbound TCP connect to three classes: allowed telemetry endpoints, blocked Internet host, and unauthorized external IP. Allowed connections must succeed; blocked must be rejected within 3s with TCP RST or no route.
3) Simulated inference: probe posts a 128-token prompt to a local inference stub behind the same VPC path to validate latency <= 250ms p50 for small prompts.
4) Bursty KV load: run 30s of KV cache warmup with synthetic keys to ensure egress flow stabilization (monitor NAT connection churn and ephemeral port usage).
5) Flow-log audit: ensure VPC flow logs contain only expected endpoints and annotate suspicious flows for manual review.

Pass/fail thresholds (canary gate):
- Reachability packet loss < 5% (fail otherwise)
- No unauthorized outbound connections observed in flow logs or probe logs
- Synthetic inference p50 latency <= 250ms and p95 <= 800ms
- NAT connection churn (new connections/min) below configured baseline (default: 120/min per canary instance)
- All policy-as-code checks returned zero denies

CI/CD pipeline (recommended GitHub Actions & Cross-account runner):
1) terraform fmt && terraform validate
2) terraform plan -out=plan.tfplan && terraform show -json plan.tfplan > plan.json
3) opa test --fail-on-external --data policies plan.json (use conftest or opal if desired)
4) if opa OK -> trigger ephemeral canary workspace (cd terraform apply -var-file=canary.tfvars) in staging account
5) deploy probe artifacts (container image built by CI) and wait for health
6) run canary-network-probe suite (script in ./ci/scripts/canary_probe.sh)
7) gather artifacts and post to the PR: probe logs, flow log digest, metric snapshot
8) decision: auto-approve/promote or fail and open incident ticket with owners

Sample ci command (can be copied into GHA job):
- name: Run policy and canary
  run: |
    terraform init -backend-config="workspace=canary-${{ github.sha }}"
    terraform plan -out=plan.tfplan
    terraform show -json plan.tfplan > plan.json
    conftest test plan.json --policy policies/egress
    if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then exit 1; fi
    ./ci/scripts/trigger-canary.sh --workspace canary-${{ github.sha }}

Observability and artifacts to collect:
- VPC flow logs (1 minute aggregation for canary runs)
- Probe stdout/stderr, tcpdump captures (pcap), and curl/iperf outputs
- NAT/gateway metrics: active connections, bytes_out, error_rate
- Terraform plan JSON and OPA/Rego evaluation result (policy logs)
- Redwood per-tenant egress counters and trace ids for synthetic inference requests

Ownership and escalation:
- Module author: primary contact for Terraform module changes and first responder for policy failures.
- Network oncall: secondary owner for canary network failures and flow log anomalies.
- SRE infra owner: responsible for CI runner, ephemeral workspace cleanup, and automation reliability.

Rollback and remediation playbook (fast-path):
1) If canary fails policy checks: block merge, tag author, and require policy fix.
2) If canary fails runtime probes (egress/latency): disable promotion, destroy canary workspace, and open a P1 with network-oncall.
3) Apply temporary circuit-breaker: attach a deny-egress security group to the tenant or remove route attachments (manual runbook steps documented in runbooks/oncall).
4) If suspected code regression in TF module: revert to last known-good module tag and re-run harness.

Cost and cleanup considerations:
- Canary workspace is ephemeral and should be auto-destroyed within 30 minutes of test completion (success or failure).
- Limit concurrent canaries per account to 4 by default to avoid NAT/Egress billing spikes; increase only with SRE approval.

Operational run cadence and metrics to track:
- Weekly: number of PRs blocked by OPA rules and top 5 policy violations.
- Per-onboarding: canary pass rate and median time to remediation.
- Monthly: upstream false positives in policy rules (policy churn) and probe flakiness rate.

Appendix A: sample minimal terraform output contract (text):
outputs = [
  { name = "vpc_id", value = "vpc-xxxx" },
  { name = "private_subnet_ids", value = ["subnet-1","subnet-2"] },
  { name = "egress_gateway_id", value = "nat-xxx" }
]

Appendix B: quick troubleshooting checklist for network-oncall:
- Verify the canary workspace exists and terraform apply completed without errors.
- Check probe pod health and logs (kubectl logs).
- Pull and inspect pcap from probe; grep for unexpected destinations.
- Cross-reference flow logs for unexpected elastic IPs or transit attachments.
- If NAT connection churn is high, escalate to infra owner to inspect connection tracking and possible port exhaustion.

References and related pages:
- See related Terraform module conventions: /confluence/eng-infra/terraform-and-iac/tf-module-compatibility-and-deprecation-plan
- Canary runner implementation examples: internal repo redwood-infra/canary-runner (link in repo index)
- Policy library: policies/egress in git infra-policy repo

Contact and next steps:
- For implementation questions or to onboard a module into this harness, contact author (Priya Natarajan) or reviewers listed above.
- Roadmap items: integrate Opal for real-time plan gating, add automated remediation playbooks for common policy violations, and surface canary artifacts in the Redwood Console onboarding checklist.

source.title: Multi-signal Priority Lattice and Decision Runbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Summary:

This runbook defines a multi-signal prioritization lattice used by platform operators when multiple alerts and business signals fire simultaneously. The lattice converts heterogeneous signals (latency, token-errors, quality regressions, customer impact, payment deltas, and telemetry noise) into a ranked action queue with clear ownership and operator instructions. The goal is to: reduce time-to-mitigation for high-impact events, limit unnecessary escalations, and preserve customer SLAs.

When to use:
- Multi-region or multi-model incidents where more than two distinct alert streams are active concurrently.
- When the root cause is unclear and immediate mitigation decisions are required.
- During Brownouts (degraded SLOs in single region) or Multi-tenant contention events.

Key definitions:
- Priority score: a weighted integer (0-100) combining signals in the table below.
- Lattice tiers: Critical (80+), High (60-79), Medium (40-59), Low (0-39).
- Action bucket: the set of operator interventions available for each tier (see Actions section).

Signals and weightings (default canonical weights):
| Signal                    | Source (metric/event)                        | Weight |
|---------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|--------|
| Customer-facing errors    | 5xx/error-rate (per-tenant)                   | 30     |
| Latency tail              | p95 latency on route/model (region)           | 20     |
| Token surge               | tokens/s exceed baseline by > 3x              | 10     |
| Quality regression        | eval alert / regression-detector             | 15     |
| Payment/quotas impact     | quota-breach or billing alerts                | 10     |
| Capacity availability     | GPU pool utilization / preemption signal      | 8      |
| External dependency error | third-party model / storage error            | 7      |

Notes on weighting: weights are tuned by the Platform SRE guild; use the incident runbook config utility to override for customer-specific dedicated pools. The lattice assumes higher weight for direct customer error signals.

Priority lattice calculation (operator view):
1. Gather active signals for the time window t0 - t0-5m.
2. Normalize each signal to a 0-100 subscore (examples below).
3. Multiply subscores by canonical weights and sum to compute Priority score.
4. Map Priority score to Lattice tier.

Normalization examples (quick heuristics):
- Customer error-rate (per-tenant): subscore = min(100, (error_rate_pct / 2) * 100). (i.e., 2% errors -> 100)
- p95 latency: subscore = clamp(0, 100, (p95_ms - baseline_ms) / baseline_ms * 100). Baseline_ms pulled from route SLI table.
- Token surge: subscore = min(100, ((tokens/s / baseline_tokens) - 1) * 50).

Investigative triage checklist (first 10 minutes):
1. Identify the tenant(s) and region(s) affected. (Command: `rbctl incidents list --active --since 10m`)
2. Check topology map for impacted model variants and routing policies. (Dashboard: Platform -> Model Routing Map)
3. Correlate traces: run `jaeger query --service=runtime-serving --tag region=<r> --duration=10m` to find tail latencies and error traces.
4. Check GPU pool health: `kubectl get nodes -l gpu=true -o wide` and `prometheus query: node_gpu_utilization{pool="<pool>"}`.
5. Confirm whether a model-variant-fallback occurred in the last 15 minutes (`/api/audit/model-fallbacks` events).

Tier-to-action mapping (decision lattice):
- Critical (80+): immediate mitigation, page oncall, run emergency fallback policy, enable cross-region redirect, consider traffic draining, and notify Customer Success for top 10 tenants.
- High (60-79): prioritize mitigations that reduce customer-facing errors and latency, consider model swap to smaller variant, scale dedicated pool if available, open issue ticket.
- Medium (40-59): trigger targeted throttling or tenant soft-quota, schedule controlled rollbacks or config changes during 30-minute window.
- Low (0-39): monitor and collect telemetry, treat as operational noise, add to 24h correlation board if recurring.

Operator action recipes (examples):
1. Emergency model fallback (Critical):
   - Action: Route traffic to verified fallback model in same family with lower latency profile.
   - CLI: `rbctl route set --tenant <T> --route <R> --model <fallback-model:v2> --immediate`
   - Safety checks: ensure fallback model has pass/fail evaluation for tenant prompt-set (dashboard shows green).
2. Soft-quota for noisy tenant (High/Medium):
   - Action: Apply transient throttle to tenant tokens/s with 5-minute backoff window.
   - CLI: `rbctl quota patch --tenant <T> --tokens-per-sec 0.25 --ttl 300s --reason "auto-throttle"`
3. Cross-region redirect (Critical):
   - Action: Re-route eligible requests to healthy region with matching model variant.
   - Steps: Update routing policy: `rbctl routing update --policy <policy-id> --add-fallback-region <region>` then validate traffic shift histogram.

Dashboard and alert cookbook (SLO, alert thresholds and playbook hooks):
- SLOs used by lattice:
  - p95 inference latency (hosted API): 150ms (SLO target 99.95%)
  - customer-visible error rate: 0.5% (SLO target 99.9% success)
  - model-quality-regression alert: > 2 failing prompts in regression-suite within 1 hour

- Representative alert thresholds (examples):
  - platform.inference.errors.rate{region,tenant} > 0.5% over 2m -> generates "tenant-error-spike" (weight 30)
  - platform.inference.p95{route,region} > 250ms over 5m -> generates "p95-tail-exceeded" (weight 20)
  - eval.regression.alert{model} severity=high -> generates "quality-regression" (weight 15)

Alert enrichment fields to surface on the incident page (required):
- tenant id(s), region, affected model variants, recent model-fallback events, top 5 trace ids, GPU pool id, estimated customer impact (tenants impacted, percent of traffic).

Escalation matrix and contacts:
- First response: platform-operator oncall (primary) -- paging policy 1 (15m SLA for acknowledgement).
- If Critical and not acknowledged in 15m: page Platform SRE lead and Eng Platform Director.
- For regressions involving model output quality: CC Applied ML lead and Solutions Engineer owning tenant.

Command cheat-sheet (useful quick commands):
- List active lattice events: `rbctl lattice events list --since 10m`
- Compute priority score locally: `python tools/compute_lattice_score.py --events events.json`
- Force-enable fallback for tenant: `rbctl route set --tenant <T> --force-fallback true`

Recovery and rollback guidance:
- When a mitigation reduces Priority score into a lower tier, hold mitigation for 30 minutes and monitor telemetry for regression before rolling back to original configs.
- For model swaps: run synthetic prompt-set validation (CLI: `python tools/synth_validate.py --tenant <T> --model <candidate>`) and ensure pass rate >= 95% before permanent rollout.

Post-incident actions (within 48 hours):
1. Create incident ticket with lattice event id and attach the prioritization calculation output.
2. Run root-cause correlation workbook and populate the Postmortem template fields: timeline, actions taken, data used in decision, why the lattice produced the priority it did.
3. If lattice weights produced an obviously incorrect decision, submit a lattice-tuning PR to `platform-sre/lattice-config` and include regression tests.

Maintenance and tuning guidance:
- Quarterly review cadence: Platform SRE guild runs a lattice calibration session each quarter to replay top 10 multi-signal incidents and adjust canonical weights.
- Canary: changes to lattice config must be applied to a non-prod environment for 7 days and evaluated on synthetic multi-signal scenarios.

Ownership and contact list:
- Lattice config repo: platform-sre/lattice-config (owner: Priya Natarajan)
- Oncall rotation: eng-platform -> platform-operator
- Escalation leads: Platform SRE (pager), Applied ML oncall for quality regressions

Appendix A — Example priority calculation (short):
- Event: Tenant T1 in us-west triggers: 1. tenant errors 1.2% (subscore=60), 2. p95 latency 320ms baseline 120ms (subscore ~167 -> clamp 100), 3. token surge 2.1x (subscore ~55).
- Weighted sum = 60*30 + 100*20 + 55*10 = 1800 + 2000 + 550 = 4350, normalized (divide by max possible 100*(sum weights=100)) = 43.5 -> Priority score 44 -> Lattice tier = Medium.

Appendix B — Lattice testing checklist (for changes):
- Add unit tests for normalization functions.
- Add 5 synthetic multi-signal scenarios to CI.
- Run A/B simulation comparing old vs new config for 72h on production-snapshot traces.

Revision history:
- 2024-11-04 Priya Natarajan: Created initial runbook and canonical weights.
- 2025-03-15 Liam Chen: Updated action recipes for cross-region redirect automations.
- 2025-06-18 Marina Alvarez: Added synthetic validation CLI checks and post-incident actions.

- For model swaps: run synthetic prompt-set validation (CLI: `python tools/synth_validate.py --tenant <T> --model <candidate>`) and ensure pass rate >= 95% before permanent rollout.

Post-incident actions (within 48 hours):
1. Create incident ticket with lattice event id and attach the prioritization calculation output.
2. Run root-cause correlation workbook and populate the Postmortem template fields: timeline, actions taken, data used in decision, why the lattice produced the priority it did.
3. If lattice weights produced an obviously incorrect decision, submit a lattice-tuning PR to `platform-sre/lattice-config` and include regression tests.

Maintenance and tuning guidance:
- Quarterly review cadence: Platform SRE guild runs a lattice calibration session each quarter to replay top 10 multi-signal incidents and adjust canonical weights.
- Canary: changes to lattice config must be applied to a non-prod environment for 7 days and evaluated on synthetic multi-signal scenarios.

Ownership and contact list:
- Lattice config repo: platform-sre/lattice-config (owner: Priya Natarajan)
- Oncall rotation: eng-platform -> platform-operator
- Escalation leads: Platform SRE (pager), Applied ML oncall for quality regressions

Appendix A — Example priority calculation (short):
- Event: Tenant T1 in us-west triggers: 1. tenant errors 1.2% (subscore=60), 2. p95 latency 320ms baseline 120ms (subscore ~167 -> clamp 100), 3. token surge 2.1x (subscore ~55).
- Weighted sum = 60*30 + 100*20 + 55*10 = 1800 + 2000 + 550 = 4350, normalized (divide by max possible 100*(sum weights=100)) = 43.5 -> Priority score 44 -> Lattice tier = Medium.

Appendix B — Lattice testing checklist (for changes):
- Add unit tests for normalization functions.
- Add 5 synthetic multi-signal scenarios to CI.
- Run A/B simulation comparing old vs new config for 72h on production-snapshot traces.

Revision history:
- 2024-11-04 Priya Natarajan: Created initial runbook and canonical weights.
- 2025-03-15 Liam Chen: Updated action recipes for cross-region redirect automations.
- 2025-06-18 Marina Alvarez: Added synthetic validation CLI checks and post-incident actions.

source.title: Burst policy, SLA smoothing, and simulated loadbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
SUMMARY
This playbook describes a unified approach for handling traffic bursts against latency SLOs using three coordinated mechanisms: (1) burst admission policies, (2) SLA smoothing (adaptive target windows and error-budget shaping), and (3) simulated-load scenarios for verification and capacity planning. The goal is to keep P95 and P99 latency SLOs predictable across short-term spikes while limiting cost and preserving error budgets for sustained load.

Scope and audience
- Scope: inference-router, cost-aware-batcher, model-variant-fallback, and the traffic-shaper service.
- Audience: Platform SREs, Serving Runtime, Performance Engineering, and Product engineers owning route-level SLOs.

Motivation
Large variance in upstream request arrival patterns (SDK retries, client-side misconfiguration, bursty user behavior) causes repeated SLO breaches even when average utilization is within capacity. Uncoordinated burst handling either causes degraded user experience (drop/queue overflows) or large cost spikes (over-provisioning). This playbook prescribes deterministic, observable mechanisms that trade short-term admission for long-term stability.

Key concepts
1) Burst window: short interval (configurable, default 10s) where a higher-than-normal arrival rate is expected.
2) Burst budget: token-bucket capacity assigned per tenant/route that permits short bursts without immediate throttling.
3) Smoothing window: sliding window used to compute adaptive SLO targets (default 5m).
4) Admission tiers: fast-path (low latency), slow-path (batched/queued), and reject path.

SLOs and target numbers (examples)
- Global routing SLO: P95 latency <= 120ms, P99 <= 350ms for synchronous text-generation routes.
- Error budget: 0.1% of requests per 30d window.
- Burst policy defaults: burst window = 10s, burst budget = 500 tokens/tenant, refill rate = 50 tokens/s.
- Slow-path threshold: queueing allowed if estimated added latency < 50ms, otherwise reject.

Service-level roles and ownership
- inference-router: owns admission decisions and tenant-level burst accounting (owner: eng-platform).
- cost-aware-batcher: manages slow-path batching and dynamic batch sizing (owner: eng-serving-runtime).
- traffic-shaper: implements smoothing window and adaptive target adjustments (owner: eng-sre).
- monitor-slo: alerting and dashboards (owner: eng-sre).

Policy rules (operational)
1) Per-tenant burst budgets are allocated at tenant creation and can be tuned by SRE based on contract tier.
2) Admission algorithm (pseudocode):
   a) if tokens_available(tenant) > request_cost then admit to fast-path and decrement tokens.
   b) else if estimated_slow_path_added_latency(request) < slow_path_threshold then place in slow-path queue.
   c) else reject with HTTP 429 and include Retry-After header.
3) Token refill: tokens are refilled at refill_rate but capped by tenant burst budget. Suspicious sustained usage that exhausts refill for > 2m triggers capacity review.
4) Retry and idempotency: clients must include idempotency keys for safe retry behavior; routers deduplicate by idempotency key for 60s.

SLA smoothing algorithm (high level)
- Purpose: prevent SLO churn from brief spikes by adjusting the effective SLO target using a time-weighted average of observed latency with exponential decay.
- Implementation parameters: smoothing_alpha = 0.25 (configurable), smoothing_window = 5m. The traffic-shaper computes smoothed_p95 = alpha * current_p95 + (1-alpha) * previous_smoothed_p95.
- When smoothed_p95 approaches 90% of configured SLO for N consecutive samples (N default 3), the system triggers an adaptive throttle to reduce admission for new fast-path requests by a configured fraction (default 20%).

Interaction with cost-aware batching and fallback
- When smoothing reduces fast-path admission, cost-aware-batcher increases slow-path batch sizes (within latency guardrails) to amortize GPU utilization. Batcher target batch size grows by up to 2x over 30s with safeguards to avoid KV cache misses.
- If slow-path queue backlog exceeds a configured backlog_limit (default 200 reqs/route), the router begins rejecting non-critical traffic classes and failing over to lower-cost model variants for best-effort responses.

Simulation scenarios (must-run test suite)
- Scenario A: Sudden 10x spike for 20s from 1% of tenants. Verify P95 and P99 remain within < 1.1x SLO and error budget consumption does not exceed baseline projection.
- Scenario B: Gradual ramp (x2 per minute) for 10 minutes across all tenants. Verify adaptive throttle triggers and batcher adjusts batch sizes without sustained SLO breaches.
- Scenario C: Synthetic retry storm (clients retry 3x with jitter). Verify idempotency dedupe and that retry throttles prevent cascade.
- Scenario D: Region failover traffic surge (traffic-shaping disabled). Validate fallback model variant usage and on-call escalation triggers.

Simulator configuration and sample command lines
- Simulator: redwood-loadsim v0.9 (internal tool). Key flags: --tenants N --pattern {burst,ramp,retry,failover} --duration s --qps-target per-tenant.
- Example: simulate a 10x 20s burst across 50 tenants:
  redwood-loadsim --tenants 50 --pattern burst --multiplier 10 --burst-window 20 --duration 60 --qps-target 5

- Admission debug mode (runtime):
  curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" 
       "https://inference-router.internal/_debug/admit?tenant=tenant-123&recent=60"
  Response includes: tokens_available, estimated_added_latency_ms, slow_path_queue_length, last_admit_action.

Verification checks (metrics and dashboards)
- Essential metrics: fastpath_admit_rate, slowpath_enqueue_rate, reject_rate(429), p95_latency(route), p99_latency(route), burst_tokens_used, batch_size_histogram, kv_cache_hit_rate.
- Dashboards: one traffic-shaper overview per region, with a tenant heatmap for burst token consumption.
- Alerts:
  - P95 > SLO for 3m AND reject_rate > 0.1% -> Sev2 (oncall eng-sre).
  - burst_tokens_exhausted for 5% of premium tenants -> Paging (Platform SRE).
  - slowpath_queue_length > backlog_limit for 2m -> Alert to owning product + scale-runbook.

Runbook: immediate steps for an oncall engineer
1) Confirm alerts and scope by tenant and region.
2) Run admission debug endpoint for top-5 affected tenants.
3) If tokens are exhausted and traffic is legitimate: increase refill_rate for the tenant temporarily (via admin CLI), notify product for possible rate-limit changes.
4) If traffic appears malicious or retry-based: enable per-route retry-cutoff at router layer and apply network-level mitigation.
5) If slow-path queue backlog persists: scale cost-aware-batcher workers by +25% and monitor KV cache hit-rate.
6) If P99 remains above threshold after 10 minutes: execute failover to fallback model variant and follow escalation path in incident response doc.

Configuration examples (YAML snippets)
- admission-policy (example):
  tenant_default_burst_budget: 500
  tenant_default_refill_rate: 50 # tokens/s
  burst_window_seconds: 10
  slow_path_threshold_ms: 50

- smoothing-config (example):
  smoothing_alpha: 0.25
  smoothing_window_seconds: 300
  adaptive_throttle_fraction: 0.2
  consecutive_samples_threshold: 3

- batcher-config (example):
  min_batch_size: 8
  max_batch_size: 256
  batch_grow_factor: 2.0
  batch_grow_window_seconds: 30

Testing and release guidance
- All changes to admission-policy, smoothing-config, and batcher-config require:
  1) Unit tests for algorithmic behavior.
  2) Integration tests running redwood-loadsim scenarios A and B in staging.
  3) Canary rollout to 2% of tenants followed by 1h observation window.
  4) Gradual ramp to 25% after successful canary, then to 100% after 24h without SLO regressions.

Exceptions and tuning guidelines
- Premium enterprise tenants may request larger burst budgets; these must be gated by a capacity commitment or reserved GPU pool allocation.
- For low-latency routes (e.g., <40ms P95 target), slow-path is disabled; instead we scale fast-path capacity or use pre-warmed inference pools.

Security and privacy considerations
- Token accounting tables are stored in a tenant-scoped Redis cluster with encryption at rest. Access is RBAC-restricted to platform services. Audit logs are required for any manual refill operations.

Appendix A — sample incident timeline (hypothetical)
- 10:01 UTC: sudden spike detected; smoothing_p95 hits 85% SLO.
- 10:03 UTC: adaptive throttle reduces fast-path admission by 20%; slow-path enqueue increases.
- 10:07 UTC: batcher scales batch size; KV cache hit rate drops 6% triggering cache pre-warm job.
- 10:15 UTC: backlog clears and smoothing_p95 decays back under threshold.

Appendix B — FAQ
Q: Why not always reject above capacity?
A: Immediate rejection prevents tail latency degradation but harms product UX and can waste retries. Controlled admission + slow-path batching reduces repeated retries and overall cost.

Q: How should product owners set burst budgets?
A: Start with defaults; increase budgets only after reviewing traffic profile and committing capacity. For critical customer SLAs, recommend reserved capacity in Dedicated.

Related documents and references
- See traffic-smoothing-and-elastic-latency-budgets-standards (performance-standards) for formal SLO math.
- See runtime-sidecar-contract-for-private-deployments-adr for private-deploy interaction patterns.

Owners and contact
- Playbook owner: eng-sre (oncall@redwood.internal)
- Implementation owners: eng-platform (inference-router), eng-serving-runtime (cost-aware-batcher)

Change history
- 2024-11-12: Initial draft by Priya Natarajan.
- 2025-02-18: Added simulator commands and canary rollout steps (reviewed by Marco Ruiz).
- 2025-06-02: Tuned default burst budget/refill and clarified retry dedupe window (reviewed by Anika Shah).

- 10:01 UTC: sudden spike detected; smoothing_p95 hits 85% SLO.
- 10:03 UTC: adaptive throttle reduces fast-path admission by 20%; slow-path enqueue increases.
- 10:07 UTC: batcher scales batch size; KV cache hit rate drops 6% triggering cache pre-warm job.
- 10:15 UTC: backlog clears and smoothing_p95 decays back under threshold.

Appendix B — FAQ
Q: Why not always reject above capacity?
A: Immediate rejection prevents tail latency degradation but harms product UX and can waste retries. Controlled admission + slow-path batching reduces repeated retries and overall cost.

Q: How should product owners set burst budgets?
A: Start with defaults; increase budgets only after reviewing traffic profile and committing capacity. For critical customer SLAs, recommend reserved capacity in Dedicated.

Related documents and references
- See traffic-smoothing-and-elastic-latency-budgets-standards (performance-standards) for formal SLO math.
- See runtime-sidecar-contract-for-private-deployments-adr for private-deploy interaction patterns.

Owners and contact
- Playbook owner: eng-sre (oncall@redwood.internal)
- Implementation owners: eng-platform (inference-router), eng-serving-runtime (cost-aware-batcher)

Change history
- 2024-11-12: Initial draft by Priya Natarajan.
- 2025-02-18: Added simulator commands and canary rollout steps (reviewed by Marco Ruiz).
- 2025-06-02: Tuned default burst budget/refill and clarified retry dedupe window (reviewed by Anika Shah).

source.title: Buildkite GPU provisioning gate and canary strategy
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Summary

This document defines the Buildkite-based gating and canary promotion strategy for GPU provisioning pipelines. It formalizes the safety checks, Terraform module promotion steps, live canary sizing, traffic-shift policy, observable signals used to validate healthy provisioning, and the oncall runbook for remediation. The intent is to reduce blast radius from configuration or IaC changes that touch GPU host/instance pools and to provide an automated, repeatable promotion path from feature branches -> staging -> canary -> prod pools.

Goals

- Prevent accidental large-scale GPU pool churn due to IaC or pipeline changes.
- Provide deterministic canary window and success criteria for Terraform changes affecting GPU fleets (node pools, autoscaler configs, taints/labels).
- Standardize Buildkite pipeline stages and gating checks across teams.
- Automate rollback and safe promotion when metrics indicate regressions.

Scope

Includes changes to: Terraform modules under eng-infra/terraform-and-iac (gpu-*, node-pool-*), Buildkite pipelines and step templates, the "gpu-provisioner" orchestration Lambda/k8s controller, and accompanying dashboards/alerts. Excludes runtime inference scheduling logic and per-tenant billing rules.

Background and motivation

We observed two classes of failures in the last 18 months: (1) large-scale node pool misconfiguration causing mass preemption/termination during a single rollout; (2) subtle autoscaler config changes that caused evictions under peak traffic. Both were rooted in insufficient pre-promotion validation and lack of deterministic canaries. This strategy applies continuous safety checks and a 3-stage promotion with traffic shaping.

Key concepts and terminology

- Provisioning gate: a set of pre-promotion validations run in CI (unit tests, IaC plan diffs, security checks, and simulated deployments).
- Canary pool: a small, isolated GPU pool populated after promotion where live traffic is diverted at a low rate to validate behavior.
- Traffic-shift policy: percentage of traffic routed to canary pool and step durations.
- Promotion window: a minimum time window where canary metrics must remain within thresholds before widening traffic.

System components

- Buildkite pipelines: pipelines/gpu-provision.yml (step templates: "plan", "apply-canary", "promote", "rollback").
- Terraform modules: modules/gpu-node-pool, modules/autoscaler-policy, modules/egress-fencing.
- Orchestrator: service name gpu-provisioner (k8s Deployment gpu-provisioner in infra namespace) - responsible for tagging canary pools and annotating Tenancy metadata.
- Observability: Grafana dashboard infra/gpu/provisioning, Prometheus metric names infra_gpu_pool_ready_seconds, infra_gpu_eviction_rate, infra_gpu_preempt_restarts_total, and SLI infra.provisioning.latency.p50.

Promotion flow (high-level)

1) Pre-merge validation (Buildkite branch pipeline):
   - Run tfsec and tflint against module changes.
   - Run terraform plan with a diff-only reviewer: create a serialized plan artifact uploaded to s3://redwood-ci-plans/${branch}/${build_number}.plan.
   - Run unit tests that validate node-pool naming conventions and tagging rules.
   - Lint step fails on missing tags (owner, cost_center, env) or missing egress-fencing configuration.

2) Staging apply (Buildkite manual step):
   - Apply terraform to the staging account using a short-lived credential (sts assume role ci-staging-gpu) and create a staging node pool.
   - Smoke test: create 10 synthetic jobs via the eval harness pointed at the staging pool. Must pass application-level health checks.

3) Canary apply (automated Buildkite step triggered after staging sign-off):
   - Terraform apply to production account but limited to a canary node pool resource name (e.g., rdp-canary-<commit>).
   - gpu-provisioner marks canary pools with label redwood:canary=true and tenants:experimental=true.
   - Immediately begin 2-minute warm-up for container images and kernel modules.

4) Canary validation window (minimum 60 minutes):
   - Traffic-shift starts at 1% of inference traffic routed to canary, increasing to 5% after 20 minutes, 10% after 40 minutes, and 20% after 60 minutes.
   - Metrics to monitor (success criteria):
     * infra_gpu_pool_ready_seconds: median < 120s for new node readiness.
     * infra_gpu_eviction_rate: 5-min rate < 0.05 evictions per node.
     * infra_gpu_preempt_restarts_total: no more than 1 restart per 100 node-hours.
     * inference_99_latency: no > 10% increase vs baseline for canary traffic.
   - Automated checks run every 5m; any failing check triggers a rollback step immediately.

Gating and automated checks

- Gate: must pass terraform fmt, tflint, tfsec, and the plan-size check (<25 resources changed by default).
- Plan drift policy: if the plan touches more than two production node pools or changes taints/tolerations, require a manual approver from enginfra-sre.
- Canary health check agent: a lightweight job that validates container runtimes, NVIDIA driver load, MIG partitioning (if used), and kernel-level OOM logs.

Buildkite pipeline snippet

```yaml
steps:
  - label: ":terraform: plan"
    command:
      - "scripts/ci/terraform_plan.sh"
    artifact_paths:
      - "plans/*.plan"
  - wait
  - block: "Staging apply - manual"
    fields:
      - text: "Approver"
  - label: ":rocket: apply-canary"
    command: "scripts/ci/terraform_apply_canary.sh"
  - label: ":mag: canary-validate"
    command: "scripts/ci/canary_validate.sh"
    retry: 0
  - block: "Promote to prod - requires enginfra-sre signoff"
  - label: ":ship: promote"
    command: "scripts/ci/terraform_promote.sh"
```

Terraform/IaC recommendations

- Modules must implement the following variables with explicit types and descriptions: pool_type, max_nodes, min_nodes, canary_tag, egress_fencing_policy.
- Use lifecycle rule "prevent_destroy = true" on production pools unless a special deprecation path is used.
- Plan artifacts are required for any change that touches resources with lifecycle prevent_destroy or taints.

Canary sizing guidance (opinionated)

- Default canary pool size: min(4 nodes, 5% of target pool size) with at least one heterogeneous GPU of each shape used in the target pool.
- Canary duration: 60 minutes minimum, 3 hours recommended for high-change windows (e.g., driver upgrades).
- For kernel/driver changes require an extended canary of 24h with daily automated checks.

Observability and alerting

- Dashboards: infra/gpu/provisioning (tile: canary vs baseline latency, eviction rate by pool, readiness histogram).
- Alerts (PagerDuty SLO triggers):
  - P1: canary_eviction_rate_high - infra_gpu_eviction_rate > 0.2 for 10m (Pager oncall: infra-sre).
  - P1: preempt_restart_spike - infra_gpu_preempt_restarts_total increased > 3x baseline (Pager).
  - P2: canary_latency_degradation - inference_99_latency > baseline * 1.15 for 15m (Email to owners).

Runbook: oncall steps for canary failure

1) Acknowledge alert in PagerDuty and open a ticket in the provisioning channel (#infra-provisioning).
2) Check the Buildkite run that created the canary (link is in alert). Run cluster-level checks: kubectl get nodes -l redwood:canary=true -o wide.
3) Evaluate metrics: compare canary pools vs baseline for the 60m window in Grafana (use dashboard preset: Canaries -> Current Canaries).
4) If eviction or preemptions are the cause: cordon canary nodes (kubectl cordon <node>) then scale down canary pool via terraform destroy target resource or terraform apply with zero desired nodes.
5) If driver/kernel mismatch suspected: collect /var/log/nvidia-installer.log and dmesg > /tmp/canary-dmesg.log and upload to the ticket.
6) Rollback promotion by running scripts/ci/terraform_rollback.sh which uses the saved plan artifact to revert resources.
7) Postmortem: capture root cause within 72h in the runbook ticket and update the gate checks if needed.

Manual promotion and approvals

- Promotion to production beyond canary is gated by an explicit Buildkite block requiring two approvers: one from enginfra-sre and one from the owning product team.
- For any change modifying node taints/tolerations, additionally require a security review if "egress_fencing_policy" is being relaxed.

Ownership and contact list

- Primary owner: eng-infra provisioning team (team alias: enginfra-provisioning@redwood.com).
- SRE reviewer: enginfra-sre (oncall alias: infra-sre-pager).
- Buildkite pipeline owner: devops-ci (devops-ci@redwood.com).

Metrics to track (weekly)

- Number of provisioning rollouts (per month) and % that used canary path.
- Mean time to detect canary failure.
- Number of rollbacks initiated from canary.

FAQ and edge cases

Q: What if canary pool never reaches ready state?
A: The pipeline will mark the canary as failed after 30 minutes of no-ready nodes. Automatic rollback will run and notify owners.

Q: How do we handle multi-tenant pools with mixed GPU shapes?
A: Canary must include at least one node of each GPU shape used in the target pool. If cost prohibits this, escalate to enginfra-sre for an exception.

Q: Can we skip canary for urgent security patches?
A: Only if the patch is validated by enginfra-sre and a recorded exception is attached to the Buildkite run. The step template supports a "fast-path" boolean but requires a signed off approval.

Troubleshooting checklist (quick)

- Check plan artifact and diff for unintended resource changes.
- Verify that canary nodes have the correct AMI/kernel and nvidia drivers.
- Confirm network ACLs and egress-fencing applied (curl to metadata endpoints from canary nodes).
- Re-run the canary validation job locally with TEST_CANARY=true to reproduce.

Change log

- 2025-03-18: Initial draft (Priya Natarajan).
- 2025-04-10: Added canary sizing guidance and plan-size gate (reviewers Marco Liu, Dana Ortiz).
- 2025-06-02: Finalized runbook steps and alert thresholds; published.

Appendix: useful commands and scripts

- View Buildkite artifacts: bk artifacts ${BUILDKITE_BUILD_ID} plans/*.plan
- Inspect canary nodes: kubectl get nodes -l redwood:canary=true -o json | jq '.items[].metadata.name'
- Replay plan apply from artifact: scripts/ci/replay_plan.sh s3://redwood-ci-plans/${branch}/${build_number}.plan

Related pages

- See eng-infra/terraform-and-iac/gpu-spot-fallback-autoscaler-module-and-runbook for autoscaler interactions.
- See eng-infra/runbooks/preflight-gpu-deployment-validation-and-rollback-playbook for preflight checklists.

If you have feedback or want this strategy adapted for a specific product pool, comment on this page or ping enginfra-provisioning@redwood.com.

- Dashboards: infra/gpu/provisioning (tile: canary vs baseline latency, eviction rate by pool, readiness histogram).
- Alerts (PagerDuty SLO triggers):
  - P1: canary_eviction_rate_high - infra_gpu_eviction_rate > 0.2 for 10m (Pager oncall: infra-sre).
  - P1: preempt_restart_spike - infra_gpu_preempt_restarts_total increased > 3x baseline (Pager).
  - P2: canary_latency_degradation - inference_99_latency > baseline * 1.15 for 15m (Email to owners).

Runbook: oncall steps for canary failure

1) Acknowledge alert in PagerDuty and open a ticket in the provisioning channel (#infra-provisioning).
2) Check the Buildkite run that created the canary (link is in alert). Run cluster-level checks: kubectl get nodes -l redwood:canary=true -o wide.
3) Evaluate metrics: compare canary pools vs baseline for the 60m window in Grafana (use dashboard preset: Canaries -> Current Canaries).
4) If eviction or preemptions are the cause: cordon canary nodes (kubectl cordon <node>) then scale down canary pool via terraform destroy target resource or terraform apply with zero desired nodes.
5) If driver/kernel mismatch suspected: collect /var/log/nvidia-installer.log and dmesg > /tmp/canary-dmesg.log and upload to the ticket.
6) Rollback promotion by running scripts/ci/terraform_rollback.sh which uses the saved plan artifact to revert resources.
7) Postmortem: capture root cause within 72h in the runbook ticket and update the gate checks if needed.

Manual promotion and approvals

- Promotion to production beyond canary is gated by an explicit Buildkite block requiring two approvers: one from enginfra-sre and one from the owning product team.
- For any change modifying node taints/tolerations, additionally require a security review if "egress_fencing_policy" is being relaxed.

Ownership and contact list

- Primary owner: eng-infra provisioning team (team alias: enginfra-provisioning@redwood.com).
- SRE reviewer: enginfra-sre (oncall alias: infra-sre-pager).
- Buildkite pipeline owner: devops-ci (devops-ci@redwood.com).

Metrics to track (weekly)

- Number of provisioning rollouts (per month) and % that used canary path.
- Mean time to detect canary failure.
- Number of rollbacks initiated from canary.

FAQ and edge cases

Q: What if canary pool never reaches ready state?
A: The pipeline will mark the canary as failed after 30 minutes of no-ready nodes. Automatic rollback will run and notify owners.

Q: How do we handle multi-tenant pools with mixed GPU shapes?
A: Canary must include at least one node of each GPU shape used in the target pool. If cost prohibits this, escalate to enginfra-sre for an exception.

Q: Can we skip canary for urgent security patches?
A: Only if the patch is validated by enginfra-sre and a recorded exception is attached to the Buildkite run. The step template supports a "fast-path" boolean but requires a signed off approval.

Troubleshooting checklist (quick)

- Check plan artifact and diff for unintended resource changes.
- Verify that canary nodes have the correct AMI/kernel and nvidia drivers.
- Confirm network ACLs and egress-fencing applied (curl to metadata endpoints from canary nodes).
- Re-run the canary validation job locally with TEST_CANARY=true to reproduce.

Change log

- 2025-03-18: Initial draft (Priya Natarajan).
- 2025-04-10: Added canary sizing guidance and plan-size gate (reviewers Marco Liu, Dana Ortiz).
- 2025-06-02: Finalized runbook steps and alert thresholds; published.

Appendix: useful commands and scripts

- View Buildkite artifacts: bk artifacts ${BUILDKITE_BUILD_ID} plans/*.plan
- Inspect canary nodes: kubectl get nodes -l redwood:canary=true -o json | jq '.items[].metadata.name'
- Replay plan apply from artifact: scripts/ci/replay_plan.sh s3://redwood-ci-plans/${branch}/${build_number}.plan

Related pages

- See eng-infra/terraform-and-iac/gpu-spot-fallback-autoscaler-module-and-runbook for autoscaler interactions.
- See eng-infra/runbooks/preflight-gpu-deployment-validation-and-rollback-playbook for preflight checklists.

If you have feedback or want this strategy adapted for a specific product pool, comment on this page or ping enginfra-provisioning@redwood.com.

source.title: ADR-0137: Route-level enablement for Quantization Profile Library (v1)
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
## Status
**Accepted (implemented for v1).**

## Decision
For v1 of the Quantization Profile Library, Redwood Inference will expose **quantization profile enablement at the route level** (route config), with optional scoping by region/deployment tier where supported. We will **not** provide a **tenant-wide default enablement** toggle in v1.

In practice:
- Routes can specify `quantization_profile_ref = "default" | "disabled" | "profile:<id>@<version>"` (exact wire shape documented in the control-plane service spec).
- The platform will resolve the route’s effective setting deterministically during policy evaluation and/or routing.
- If a requested profile is incompatible (model/variant/hardware/context constraints) the system will **fallback to baseline precision** and emit a structured signal (metrics + trace/log annotation).
- When the feature is off (no route setting), behavior remains unchanged.

## Context / Problem Statement
Quantization profiles can materially reduce cost/token, but they introduce new risk surface:
- **Quality regression risk:** output differences can be subtle, domain-specific, and require gating via evals.
- **Compatibility risk:** profiles may only be valid for certain model variants, GPU SKUs, kernel paths, and sequence length regimes.
- **Operational risk:** a misconfiguration that affects an entire tenant can create a broad blast radius and slow down rollback.
- **Change control:** enterprise customers (and regulated workloads) require auditable, scoped changes with clear ownership and reversibility.

The product requirement for v1 is to publish a library of profiles per model variant and allow customers to **opt in** safely, with strong guardrails and fast rollback.

## Goals
1. Provide an opt-in mechanism that is **safe, auditable, and reversible**.
2. Limit blast radius and support **staged rollout** (canary/A-B per route).
3. Preserve determinism and ensure **no silent behavior change** for existing traffic.
4. Make it straightforward to compare baseline vs quantized behavior using the eval harness and dashboards.

## Non-goals (v1)
- Automatically enabling quantization by default across all traffic for a tenant.
- Fully automated per-customer recommendations or online tuning.
- A “global default profile alias” that can silently change behavior across routes without an explicit change event.

## Options Considered
### Option A: Tenant-wide default enablement (global toggle)
**Description:** A tenant setting such as `tenant.quantization_default = enabled(profile_ref)` applies to all routes unless overridden.

**Pros:**
- Simple mental model for customers that want “turn it on everywhere.”
- Faster adoption for single-route tenants.
- Reduced configuration overhead.

**Cons / Risks:**
- **High blast radius:** a single change can impact all endpoints and workloads, including heterogeneous routes with different prompts, latency SLOs, or model variants.
- **Harder rollback targeting:** operationally, we often need to disable for one route/model while leaving other routes enabled.
- **Poor fit for eval gating:** offline and route-level A/B comparisons become harder when the baseline is no longer stable across the tenant.
- **Change control ambiguity:** ownership of “global defaults” can lead to accidental enablement by teams who do not own all routes.
- Increased complexity in policy evaluation and migration semantics (global + per-route precedence, partial adoption, legacy defaults).

**Conclusion:** Not selected for v1.

### Option B: Route-level enablement (selected)
**Description:** Route config explicitly opts in, with the default behavior preserved when unset.

**Pros:**
- **Small blast radius:** impacts only the routes explicitly changed.
- Supports staged rollouts and **canary by route**.
- Enables strong auditability: “who changed what route, when, and to which profile version.”
- Easier to debug: traffic can be correlated to a specific route setting.
- Simplifies determinism: route config + resolved profile ref yields a stable decision.

**Cons:**
- More steps for customers who want broad enablement.
- More UI/API surface area (per-route controls, filtering, bulk actions).

**Conclusion:** Selected for v1.

### Option C: Header/Request-level opt-in (per request)
**Description:** Client passes a header/parameter to request quantization.

**Pros:**
- Extremely granular experimentation.

**Cons / Risks:**
- Hard to enforce guardrails; increases risk of compatibility violations.
- Moves correctness responsibility to customers and client code.
- Harder to guarantee deterministic scheduling/caching behavior.

**Conclusion:** Not selected.

## Decision Drivers
- **Safety and blast radius**: route-level is the tightest scoping that still supports meaningful customer control.
- **Determinism**: route config is stable input to the policy engine and runtime; avoids environment-dependent defaults.
- **Operational rollback**: SRE can disable a single route rapidly and verify recovery using route dashboards.
- **Enterprise change control**: route-level changes map better to org boundaries and approval flows.

## Detailed Design Notes (normative)
### Effective behavior precedence
1. Global kill-switch (platform) forces quantization off.
2. Route-level explicit setting governs behavior.
3. Unset route setting => baseline precision behavior (no change).

### Compatibility enforcement
When a route references a profile:
- Control plane/policy evaluation must validate compatibility against:
  - model_id + variant
  - deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) and region
  - GPU SKU class / capability flags
  - max context / sequence-length regimes (as encoded in the profile constraints)
- If incompatible, the request must **fallback to baseline**.
- Fallback must be **observable** (metrics + trace/log annotations) and must not loop.

### Determinism requirements
- The resolved profile reference must be deterministic for a given (route, model version, deployment, region) input.
- “Moving aliases” such as `recommended`/`latest` are not permitted as a stable ref in v1 customer-facing configs unless they are pinned to an immutable version at write time.

### Auditability and RBAC
- Enabling/disabling quantization and changing the referenced profile must emit structured audit events including:
  - actor identity
  - route identifier
  - before/after values
  - timestamp
  - change source (Console/API/automation)
- RBAC: only users with route config write permissions can change quantization settings; enterprise customers may require stricter roles.

## Safety Considerations
### Quality regressions
- Route-level enablement is required to support the official evaluation workflow:
  - Run offline eval suites comparing baseline vs quantized for the specific route prompt distribution.
  - Gate promotion based on regression thresholds and statistical tests (see quantization regression policy).
- We will treat quantization profile changes as *behavior changes* and require explicit opt-in.

### Latency and tail behavior
- Profiles can change kernel selection, KV cache behavior, and batching dynamics.
- Route-level rollout allows monitoring p95/p99 deltas on the impacted traffic slice before broadening.

### Incident response
- A global kill-switch exists for emergencies, but standard response should begin with route-level disablement.
- Route-level scoping reduces “all-tenant” emergencies caused by a mis-set default.

## Consequences
### Positive
- Safer and more controllable rollout.
- Clearer ownership boundaries for route configs.
- Better observability and debugging.

### Negative / Trade-offs
- Customers with many routes may need bulk tooling.
- Some customers will request a tenant-wide “default on” experience; we will handle via bulk route operations and templates during v1.

## Rollout Plan (v1)
1. Implement route-level config surface in control plane and Console.
2. Add compatibility guardrails + safe fallback behavior.
3. Add metrics and dashboards:
   - enabled_rate by route
   - fallback_rate (incompatible/failed apply)
   - error_rate deltas
   - latency (p50/p95/p99) deltas
   - cost/token deltas where available
4. Run perf-canary scenarios and design-partner canaries.
5. Beta: allowlist access + explicit enable/disable per route.
6. GA: publish docs, support playbook, and establish profile publishing cadence.

## Deprecation / Future Strategy
We expect requests for a tenant-level default in the future. If introduced, it must not compromise safety.

Guidelines for any future “tenant default” feature:
- Must be **explicitly opt-in** and disabled by default.
- Must preserve route-level overrides and provide clear precedence.
- Must provide a safety mechanism to prevent enabling across incompatible route/model mixes (e.g., per-route compatibility summaries and mandatory confirmations).
- Must pin any alias to an immutable version at the time of change (no silent drift).
- Must include an enterprise-grade audit trail and, where requested, approval workflows.

If/when we introduce tenant defaults, v1 route-level settings will remain supported. A migration path may optionally allow setting tenant default + removing redundant route settings, but only with tooling that produces a deterministic plan and preserves rollback semantics.

## Open Questions
- Should we support bulk route enablement actions in Console for GA (e.g., “Enable for selected routes”), or rely on API + Terraform providers initially?
- Do we need per-tenant “deny” controls (tenant-wide disable) in addition to the platform kill-switch, for customers who want a hard guarantee?

## Owners
- Platform / Control Plane: Ethan Park, Benji Okafor
- Routing / Policy determinism: Mina Haddad, Jordan Lee
- Serving Runtime: Noah Patel, Hiro Tanaka
- Applied ML (profiles + eval gating): Arjun Desai, Selene Huang
- Security / Audit requirements: Naomi Feldman, Kevin Osei

## Appendix: Rationale Summary (one-paragraph)
Route-level toggles minimize blast radius and make quantization changes auditable, testable, and reversible. This aligns with v1’s “opt-in library” positioning and supports deterministic policy + runtime behavior. Tenant-wide defaults were rejected for v1 due to elevated safety and operational risk, particularly around quality regressions, compatibility mismatches, and rollback complexity.

- Some customers will request a tenant-wide “default on” experience; we will handle via bulk route operations and templates during v1.

## Rollout Plan (v1)
1. Implement route-level config surface in control plane and Console.
2. Add compatibility guardrails + safe fallback behavior.
3. Add metrics and dashboards:
   - enabled_rate by route
   - fallback_rate (incompatible/failed apply)
   - error_rate deltas
   - latency (p50/p95/p99) deltas
   - cost/token deltas where available
4. Run perf-canary scenarios and design-partner canaries.
5. Beta: allowlist access + explicit enable/disable per route.
6. GA: publish docs, support playbook, and establish profile publishing cadence.

## Deprecation / Future Strategy
We expect requests for a tenant-level default in the future. If introduced, it must not compromise safety.

Guidelines for any future “tenant default” feature:
- Must be **explicitly opt-in** and disabled by default.
- Must preserve route-level overrides and provide clear precedence.
- Must provide a safety mechanism to prevent enabling across incompatible route/model mixes (e.g., per-route compatibility summaries and mandatory confirmations).
- Must pin any alias to an immutable version at the time of change (no silent drift).
- Must include an enterprise-grade audit trail and, where requested, approval workflows.

If/when we introduce tenant defaults, v1 route-level settings will remain supported. A migration path may optionally allow setting tenant default + removing redundant route settings, but only with tooling that produces a deterministic plan and preserves rollback semantics.

## Open Questions
- Should we support bulk route enablement actions in Console for GA (e.g., “Enable for selected routes”), or rely on API + Terraform providers initially?
- Do we need per-tenant “deny” controls (tenant-wide disable) in addition to the platform kill-switch, for customers who want a hard guarantee?

## Owners
- Platform / Control Plane: Ethan Park, Benji Okafor
- Routing / Policy determinism: Mina Haddad, Jordan Lee
- Serving Runtime: Noah Patel, Hiro Tanaka
- Applied ML (profiles + eval gating): Arjun Desai, Selene Huang
- Security / Audit requirements: Naomi Feldman, Kevin Osei

## Appendix: Rationale Summary (one-paragraph)
Route-level toggles minimize blast radius and make quantization changes auditable, testable, and reversible. This aligns with v1’s “opt-in library” positioning and supports deterministic policy + runtime behavior. Tenant-wide defaults were rejected for v1 due to elevated safety and operational risk, particularly around quality regressions, compatibility mismatches, and rollback complexity.

source.title: Authorization Safeguards and Compliance Workflow for Inference Systems
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview

This runbook defines an integrated, end-to-end workflow for authorization (RBAC/attribute-based checks), audit logging, data residency validation, secure-SDLC gating, incident response interplay, compliance evidence packaging, and risk/exception handling for Redwood Inference model-serving and control-plane systems. It is intended for cross-functional use by Security, SRE, Platform, and Product teams responsible for secure model deployments and tenant isolation.

Goals

- Ensure authorization decisions are evaluated and logged with sufficient forensic detail to support audits and incident investigation.
- Prevent unauthorized cross-residency inference or data access by combining policy checks (RBAC/ABAC), residency metadata, and runtime enforcement.
- Provide a standardized evidence bundle and manifest that maps policy decisions to artifact hashes for regulatory review.
- Integrate authorization and residency checks into the SDLC release gates and incident response procedures.

Scope

Applies to: Redwood control plane services, model-serving runtime (hosted, dedicated, private), and supporting services: identity provider (IdP), entitlement service, audit event collector, object stores for model artifacts, and customer-configured residency metadata.

Key Definitions

- Decision Context: the complete input used to compute authorization (caller identity, roles/attributes, request route, model artifact residency tags, time, tenant policy).
- Evidence Bundle: signed archive containing the decision context, audit events, log digests, relevant config snapshots, and a manifest with SHA256 hashes.
- Residency Tag: explicit metadata on model artifacts and customer tenancy that indicates allowed geographic/region usage.

Owners and Responsibilities

- Security (owner): overall policy, evidence schema, retention requirements.
- Eng-Serving-Runtime: runtime enforcement and instrumentation for decision context and KV/prefix cache handling.
- Eng-SRE: SLOs for audit delivery and pipeline availability.
- Platform/Product: SDLC gating, rollout policies, and customer-facing configuration surface.

SLOs and Retention Requirements

- Authorization decision event ingestion: 99.9% within 60s to the audit pipeline.
- Audit event tail-to-query latency (for forensic queries): 95% within 10 minutes.
- Evidence bundle generation for an incident: 1 hour SLA from incident kickoff.
- Retention: decision-level audit events retained for 2 years; evidence bundles stored for 7 years for regulated customers (configurable per contract).

Authorization and Residency Enforcement Pattern

1. Request interception: proxy/pass-through middleware captures request metadata (route, model_id, customer_id, region).
2. Attribute enrichment: call entitlement service to fetch roles and dynamic attributes (time-bound grants, JIT flags).
3. Residency check: compare model_artifact.residency_tag, tenant.residency_constraints, and request.destination_region. Fail-fast on mismatch.
4. Decision evaluation: evaluate RBAC/ABAC policies (policy engine: OPA/Rego v2) with full Decision Context.
5. Decision logging: emit canonical decision event (see Audit Event Schema below).
6. Enforcement: allow, deny, or allow-with-redaction (e.g., redact PII in payload or route to a residency-isolated runtime).

Audit Event Schema (canonical)

Use a compact canonical JSON event that is immutable after emission. Example: 

{
  "event_id": "uuid-v4",
  "timestamp": "2025-03-02T14:22:31Z",
  "service": "serving-proxy.v3",
  "customer_id": "cust-12345",
  "actor": { "id": "svc-account-42", "type": "service" },
  "decision": { "result": "DENY", "policy_id": "policy-access-model-prod-geo", "reason": "residency-mismatch" },
  "decision_context_hash": "sha256:...",
  "request_route": "/v1/generate",
  "model_id": "gpt-neox-8b-quant-v2",
  "residency": { "artifact_tag": "eu:fr:par1", "tenant_constraint": "eu-only" },
  "evidence_refs": ["manifest:sha256:..."],
  "ingest_node": "audit-collector-01",
  "signature": "detached-jws:..."
}

Notes on the schema:
- decision_context_hash is a SHA-256 of the serialized Decision Context (order-normalized) and is used to link events to evidence bundles.
- All events must be signed by the emitting service using the service signing key to prevent tampering in transit.

Evidence Bundle Format and Manifest

When an event is elevated (incident or compliance request), we assemble an Evidence Bundle with the following files:
- decision-context.json (full context used by the policy engine)
- audit-events.ndjson (all atomic audit events for the request path)
- config-snapshot.yaml (policy versions, model artifact metadata, entitlement state)
- runtime-metrics.json (span/KV-cache snapshots, latency traces)
- manifest.txt (list of files with SHA256 hashes and human-readable index)

manifest.txt example:

FILE decision-context.json SHA256:abc123...
FILE audit-events.ndjson SHA256:def456...
FILE config-snapshot.yaml SHA256:7890ab...
BUNDLE_SIGNER svc-identity:signer-01 JWS:eyJ...

Packaging rules:
- The Evidence Bundle must be sealed with an envelope signature (JWS) and recorded to the evidence index service.
- For cross-border incidents, include a residency-justification.txt that lists why any non-local data is included.

SDLC Integration: Release and Gate Checklist

Every change that affects authorization logic, residency tags, audit schemas, or evidence manifests must pass the following gates before production rollout:

1. Design review with Security (recorded ADR or PRD link).
2. Automated unit and policy tests (policy fuzzing against a standard corpus).
3. Integration test: simulate requests with different residency combinations and verify expected decision and correct audit emission.
4. Staging evidence collection: generate a sample Evidence Bundle and validate manifest and signature.
5. Preflight captain review (manual sign-off for high-risk changes).

SDLC guardrail example CLI check (pre-merge):

# Run policy fuzzing and check decision-context hash stability
policylint --path ./policies && policyfuzz --corpus ./test-corpus --assert-stable-hash

Incident Response Integration

Triggering events for evidence assembly:
- Any DENY result against a high-sensitivity model (labelled privacy:high)
- Any cross-region residency mismatch that led to fallback or denial
- Suspected exfiltration or anomalous token patterns tied to a customer account

Incident responder playbook (abridged):
1. Triage: mark incident and tag with incident.residency_scope (local/multi-region).
2. Lockdown: if high severity, apply immediate entitlement freeze for affected tenant or model.
3. Evidence capture: invoke evidence-assembler service: 
   - command: evidence-assemble --incident-id INC-20250302-007 --time-window 2025-03-02T14:00:00Z/2025-03-02T15:00:00Z
4. Quarantine artifacts: snapshot model artifact storage objects (read-only) and record object store OIDs.
5. Notify legal and customer-success if customer data appears involved.

Risk and Exception Handling Workflow

- Low-risk exceptions (developer feature flags, transient infra issues): record in Exception Register with TTL <= 7 days, require owner and mitigation plan.
- Medium-risk exceptions (temporary cross-region test access): require managerial approval, evidence of impact analysis, and a compensating control (e.g., audit hook + alerting).
- High-risk exceptions (permanent residency exception for a customer): require Security + Legal + Product sign-off, signed attestation from customer, and storage of an auditable exception package in the compliance vault.

Exception register template (fields):
- exception_id, owner, risk_level, justification, start_date, expiry_date, controls, evidence_refs

Operational Playbook: Day-to-Day Checks

Weekly:
- Verify audit pipeline backlog is < 5% windowed for 1 hour (SRE dashboard).
- Run a residency-conformance smoke test that exercises 50 sample decision paths across eu/us/apac.

Monthly:
- Evidence bundle spot audit: pick 3 incident bundles, validate manifest signatures and storage integrity.
- Access certification: review privileged roles for entitlement service and rotate signing keys as required.

Forensic query examples

- Query recent DENY events for residency mismatch for tenant X:
  curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "https://audit-query.redwood.internal/events?customer_id=cust-12345&decision.result=DENY&decision.reason=residency-mismatch&since=1680300000"
\- Reconstruct decision-context from decision_context_hash:
  - use decision-context index to fetch by hash: GET /evidence/index/{sha256} -> returns evidence bundle refs

Operational Troubleshooting Playbook

Symptom: decision events not appearing in audit index
1. Check ingest node health: systemctl status audit-collector.service on ingest node.
2. Validate outbound connectivity to audit pipeline (kafka/elastic endpoint).
3. Check local disk pressure; rotate and alert if /var/log/audit buffer > 60%.
4. If events emitted but not queryable, validate indexer consumer group lag and restart indexer with: consumerctl restart --group audit-indexer --topic audit-events

Compliance Reviewer Checklist (for auditors)

- Evidence bundles are complete and manifest signatures verify.
- Decision-level events contain signed service identity and decision_context_hash.
- Residency tags match contractual constraints and model metadata.
- Exception register contains required approvals and TTLs.
- SDLC gating records are linked to release artifacts and test results.

Appendices

A. Role -> Minimum Audit Fields Table

| Role | Minimum fields emitted in audit event | Example retention |
|------|--------------------------------------|------------------|
| Service account | event_id, timestamp, service, decision, request_route, signature | 2 years |
| Human operator | same + operator_id, reason | 7 years (if privileged ops) |
| System admin | same + privileged_action | 7 years |

B. Example policy rule (Rego-like pseudocode)

package redwood.auth

default allow = false

allow {
  input.actor.roles[_] == "tenant-admin"
  input.model_artifact.residency == input.tenant.residency_constraint
}

allow_with_redaction {
  input.actor.roles[_] == "guest-analyst"
  input.tenant.residency_constraint == "us-only"
  input.request_route == "/v1/embeddings"
}

C. Glossary and Links

- Evidence Index: internal index service link: https://evidence-index.internal/ (internal only)
- Entitlement Service API spec: /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/entitlement-service-api-v2 (internal doc)
- SDLC gate ADR template: /confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/sdlc-gates-adr-templates

Contact and escalation

Primary security on-call: security-oncall@redwood.com
Owner (policy & evidence schema): Priya Natarajan (security)
Secondary: Marco Li (eng-sre)

Change history

- 2024-11-15: Created (PN)
- 2025-02-10: Added Evidence Bundle manifest format and SDLC CLI checks (ML)
- 2025-03-02: Updated SLOs and retention guidance to reflect enterprise contracts (PN)

End of document
