- Suspected exfiltration or anomalous token patterns tied to a customer account

Incident responder playbook (abridged):
1. Triage: mark incident and tag with incident.residency_scope (local/multi-region).
2. Lockdown: if high severity, apply immediate entitlement freeze for affected tenant or model.
3. Evidence capture: invoke evidence-assembler service: 
   - command: evidence-assemble --incident-id INC-20250302-007 --time-window 2025-03-02T14:00:00Z/2025-03-02T15:00:00Z
4. Quarantine artifacts: snapshot model artifact storage objects (read-only) and record object store OIDs.
5. Notify legal and customer-success if customer data appears involved.

Risk and Exception Handling Workflow

- Low-risk exceptions (developer feature flags, transient infra issues): record in Exception Register with TTL <= 7 days, require owner and mitigation plan.
- Medium-risk exceptions (temporary cross-region test access): require managerial approval, evidence of impact analysis, and a compensating control (e.g., audit hook + alerting).
- High-risk exceptions (permanent residency exception for a customer): require Security + Legal + Product sign-off, signed attestation from customer, and storage of an auditable exception package in the compliance vault.

Exception register template (fields):
- exception_id, owner, risk_level, justification, start_date, expiry_date, controls, evidence_refs

Operational Playbook: Day-to-Day Checks

Weekly:
- Verify audit pipeline backlog is < 5% windowed for 1 hour (SRE dashboard).
- Run a residency-conformance smoke test that exercises 50 sample decision paths across eu/us/apac.

Monthly:
- Evidence bundle spot audit: pick 3 incident bundles, validate manifest signatures and storage integrity.
- Access certification: review privileged roles for entitlement service and rotate signing keys as required.

Forensic query examples

- Query recent DENY events for residency mismatch for tenant X:
  curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "https://audit-query.redwood.internal/events?customer_id=cust-12345&decision.result=DENY&decision.reason=residency-mismatch&since=1680300000"
\- Reconstruct decision-context from decision_context_hash:
  - use decision-context index to fetch by hash: GET /evidence/index/{sha256} -> returns evidence bundle refs

Operational Troubleshooting Playbook

Symptom: decision events not appearing in audit index
1. Check ingest node health: systemctl status audit-collector.service on ingest node.
2. Validate outbound connectivity to audit pipeline (kafka/elastic endpoint).
3. Check local disk pressure; rotate and alert if /var/log/audit buffer > 60%.
4. If events emitted but not queryable, validate indexer consumer group lag and restart indexer with: consumerctl restart --group audit-indexer --topic audit-events

Compliance Reviewer Checklist (for auditors)

- Evidence bundles are complete and manifest signatures verify.
- Decision-level events contain signed service identity and decision_context_hash.
- Residency tags match contractual constraints and model metadata.
- Exception register contains required approvals and TTLs.
- SDLC gating records are linked to release artifacts and test results.

Appendices

A. Role -> Minimum Audit Fields Table

| Role | Minimum fields emitted in audit event | Example retention |
|------|--------------------------------------|------------------|
| Service account | event_id, timestamp, service, decision, request_route, signature | 2 years |
| Human operator | same + operator_id, reason | 7 years (if privileged ops) |
| System admin | same + privileged_action | 7 years |

B. Example policy rule (Rego-like pseudocode)

package redwood.auth

default allow = false

allow {
  input.actor.roles[_] == "tenant-admin"
  input.model_artifact.residency == input.tenant.residency_constraint
}

allow_with_redaction {
  input.actor.roles[_] == "guest-analyst"
  input.tenant.residency_constraint == "us-only"
  input.request_route == "/v1/embeddings"
}

C. Glossary and Links

- Evidence Index: internal index service link: https://evidence-index.internal/ (internal only)
- Entitlement Service API spec: /confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/entitlement-service-api-v2 (internal doc)
- SDLC gate ADR template: /confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/sdlc-gates-adr-templates

Contact and escalation

Primary security on-call: security-oncall@redwood.com
Owner (policy & evidence schema): Priya Natarajan (security)
Secondary: Marco Li (eng-sre)

Change history

- 2024-11-15: Created (PN)
- 2025-02-10: Added Evidence Bundle manifest format and SDLC CLI checks (ML)
- 2025-03-02: Updated SLOs and retention guidance to reflect enterprise contracts (PN)

End of document

source.title: Quant profile requirements for Hosted API catalog models
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
# Overview
This page defines the minimum set of quantization profiles and validation checks required for a model to be eligible for the Hosted API catalog.

# Why we require multiple profiles
Hosted traffic varies significantly by latency sensitivity and context length. Having pre-validated quantization profiles ensures we can route cost-sensitive workloads without surprise quality regressions.

# Required profiles (per model)
For every new catalog model, you must produce and validate *at least* the following profiles:
- `fp16` (reference)
- `int8-weight-only` (cost-optimized)
- `int4-weight-only` (when supported by kernels/hardware; if not supported, document as N/A with Runtime owner signoff)

# Validation checks (required)
Each profile must pass:
1. **Correctness smoke** (Owner: Serving Runtime)
   - Generate determinism check (same seed, same output distribution tolerance)
   - Tokenizer round-trip sanity
2. **Quality delta check** (Owner: Applied ML)
   - Run Suite A from "Model launch evaluation gates" on `fp16` and on the candidate default profile
   - Pass criteria: no `p0` regressions; overall win-rate delta vs fp16 >= -5%
3. **Performance check** (Owner: Serving Runtime)
   - Meet the "Hosted model performance bar" thresholds for the default profile

# Default profile selection
- Default should be the fastest profile that passes the quality delta check and meets TTFT p95 targets.
- If `int4` passes but shows > 2% increase in error rate under load, do not ship `int4` as default.

# Naming and registry requirements
- Profile names must match the runtime config keys used by the model registry.
- The model registry PR must include the profile list and indicate which is default.

# Required artifacts
- Link to quant build logs (Drive)
- Eval harness run links for fp16 and default
- Perf suite run IDs for default

# Exceptions
Exceptions require:
- Runtime owner signoff (why kernel support is missing) and
- Applied ML signoff (why quality risk is acceptable)
Recorded on the Linear ticket with label `quant-exception`.

# Related pages
- Hosted API model intake
- Model launch evaluation gates
- Hosted model performance bar

source.title: Offline artifact bundles for Redwood Private (disconnected installs)
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
h1. Purpose
This page defines the standard approach for delivering *offline artifact bundles* for Redwood Private deployments where outbound internet access is not available (or is prohibited by policy).

h1. When to use an offline bundle
Use an offline bundle when a customer environment has one or more of the following:
* No outbound internet (no NAT / no proxy egress).
* No ability to pull from external registries (Docker Hub, GHCR, ECR public).
* Restricted certificate validation (no public OCSP/CRL).

h1. Bundle contents (minimum)
* Helm chart package (versioned).
* Container images as tar archives (or pre-pushed to a customer registry).
* File containing *image digests* (pinned) and recommended tags.
* Checksums for all bundle artifacts.
* "What needs mirroring" checklist (registry, charts, optional model weights if applicable).

h1. Installation approach (high level)
# Customer imports images into internal registry (or loads directly).
# Customer configures chart values to point to internal registry.
# Install is performed with egress blocked to validate no external dependencies.

h1. Notes
* This is not a separate product; it is a packaging path for Private.
* Any component that attempts outbound connectivity must be configurable off by default for disconnected installs.

source.title: Terraform validation harness for GPU topology changes with simulated traffic
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Summary

This playbook documents the recommended Terraform validation harness used to safely change GPU pool topology (instance types, AZ placement, placement groups, taints/tolerations) by running an automated, CI-driven simulation of representative inference traffic against a temporary environment. The goal is to detect capacity, latency, and egress regressions before Terraform changes reach production gates.

Scope

- Applies to Terraform changes that modify: GPU pool sizes, instance flavors, network egress routes, subnets, placement groups, or tenant QoS tags.
- Intended for use in the main infrastructure CI pipeline (Buildkite) and also as a manual preflight for large migration windows.

Why we run simulated traffic during TF validation

Terraform plan/validate and policy-as-code checks catch many problems (iam, security, drift). However, changes that affect topology can cause emergent behavior only visible under realistic load: KV cache warmup time, network egress hot spots, host-level forks, instance scheduling differences, or kernel-level performance regressions for quantized kernels. Running a short, deterministic traffic simulation in an isolated environment reduces risk and gives measurable pass/fail criteria tied to SLOs.

Ownership and roles

- Owner: infra/gpu-platform (pager: #infra-gpu on Slack)
- CI pipeline owner: infra-ci-team
- Reviewer(s): Perf team for benchmark definitions; Security for service identity changes.

Prerequisites

1) Feature branch containing Terraform changes targeting the gpu-allocator module.
2) CI credentials: a service account with limited create/delete rights in the sandbox AWS/GCP account. The account must be constrained to the sandbox project and tagged appropriately.
3) Canary image bundles must be published to the build artifact registry (image tag ref used by the simulation harness).
4) Telemetry exporters enabled in the test environment: metrics (Prometheus push/gateway), traces (Jaeger), and logs (ELK/Cloud Logging).

High-level flow

1. Developer opens PR with Terraform changes.
2. CI runs terraform fmt/validate, policy checks (OPA/Conftest).
3. CI creates a short-lived sandbox environment by applying a curated Terraform overlay (isolated VPC, dedicated subnets, ephemeral GPU nodes).
4. Once infra is healthy and node agents report ready, CI deploys the simulation harness and a minimal control plane (service discovery + routing).
5. CI kicks off a deterministic workload generator (see workload profiles below) for 6–20 minutes.
6. Telemetry is collected and evaluated against thresholds. If thresholds are exceeded, the run is marked failed and the TF change is blocked.

Workload profiles (examples)

- baseline-infer: 200 rps, short prompts (avg 48 tokens), 95th latency expected < 150ms, KV hit ratio 35%
- bursty-chat: 50 rps with Poisson bursts up to 400rps for 30s windows, avg seq length 256, tail P99 < 2.2s
- long-context-eval: 8 rps, sequence length 2048, measures KV growth and memory pressure

Configuration table (thresholds used by CI guard)

| metric | acceptable threshold | failure condition |
|---|---:|---|
| 95th response latency (baseline-infer) | < 150 ms | > 180 ms for 3+ sample windows |
| P99 latency (bursty-chat) | < 2.2 s | > 3.0 s for > 10% of samples |
| GPU util (avg across pool) | 25% - 85% | sustained > 95% for 2+ minutes or < 5% for 3+ mins |
| KV cache miss rate | < 20% | > 40% |
| Packet drops (instance NIC) | < 0.1% | > 1% |
| Egress errors (5xx from proxy) | < 0.5% | > 2% |

Note: CI evaluates both absolute thresholds and delta-from-baseline (the baseline is the last green canonical run for the same infra-module version). An increase > 20% relative to baseline triggers an investigator review even if absolute thresholds are still within limits.

Terraform overlay pattern

We use a small overlay module that reuses the same tf-modules as production but isolates resources: unique VPC, namespacing tags, and a shortened lifetime. Example usage within the pipeline:

```bash
# pipeline step (pseudocode)
export TF_VAR_prefix=ci-sim-${BUILDKITE_BUILD_NUMBER}
terraform init -backend-config="bucket=ci-terraform-state" \
  -backend-config="key=ci/${BUILDKITE_BUILD_NUMBER}/tfstate"
terraform apply -auto-approve -var-file=overlays/sim.vars.tfvars
```

Policy gates (pre-apply and post-apply)

- Pre-apply: OPA checks for forbidden instance types, missing tenant tags, and network exposure.
- Post-apply: Smoke tests (SSH to nodes, kube node ready, NVidia driver loaded), and an automated kernel/cuda version detector that fails if the new image/container runtime loses required drivers.

Simulation harness implementation notes

- Harness is a containerized service (go/python) that drives traffic using a combination of k6 for synthetic HTTP loads and a lightweight client that exercises the actual inference path (auth, routing, streaming).
- Test orchestration is implemented as a Buildkite step that: deploys harness, configures the target load profile, and pulls telemetry snapshots into a verification step.
- Harness supports deterministic replay from a recorded trace to ensure reproducibility across runs.

CI pipeline example (Buildkite YAML excerpt)

```yaml
steps:
  - label: ":terraform: TF validate + OPA"
    command: scripts/tf-validate.sh
  - wait: ~
  - label: ":rocket: Create sandbox infra"
    command: scripts/tf-apply-sim.sh
    timeout_in_minutes: 30
  - label: ":zap: Run sim traffic"
    command: scripts/run-sim.sh --profile baseline-infer
    timeout_in_minutes: 20
  - label: ":mag: Verify telemetry & teardown"
    command: scripts/verify-and-destroy.sh
    timeout_in_minutes: 10
    parallelism: 1
```

Pass/fail decision logic

- If any absolute threshold fails => fail the CI job, keep state for investigation (do not automatically destroy until reviewed unless configured).
- If delta-from-baseline > 20% => mark job for manual review by perf/infra owner.
- If smoke checks fail => immediate fail, skip simulation, and tear down if teardown policy allows.

Failure investigation checklist (quick triage)

1. Capture CPU/GPU profiles from the nodes (nvidia-smi stats, pid memory).
2. Check kernel and driver versions: mismatch with expected (see /ops/gpu-driver-manifest) is common root cause.
3. Inspect network telemetry (observability dashboard: egress QPS heatmap, ENI metrics).
4. Validate Terraform diff: did the change reduce pool size or modify AZ distribution?
5. If a regression is reproducible locally, escalate to runtime/kernel team.

Rollback and state cleanup policy

- By default the sandbox environment is set to auto-destroy on success or failure after a configurable hold window (default 30 minutes).
- For failing runs flagged for investigation, terraform state is preserved under a build-specific key and the infra owner is paged.

Operational runbook snippets

- Manually re-run a simulation against a historical TF plan:
  1. Check out the PR branch and download the terraform state snapshot associated with the build.
  2. Recreate the sandbox (scripts/tf-apply-sim.sh --preserve-state).
  3. Run the harness with the recorded trace that previously failed: scripts/run-sim.sh --replay trace-id.

- If an infra change requires immediate production roll (emergency):
  1. Ensure runbook owner signoff.
  2. Apply change to a single small region using the same overlay but with production credentials.
  3. Run a scaled-down sim for 10 minutes and monitor.
  4. Roll forward only if no regression; otherwise revert using the last-known-good module version.

Telemetry and artifacts retained per run

- Prometheus snapshot (Promtool export) - retained 7 days
- Trace dump (Jaeger) - retained 14 days
- Terraform state snapshot - retained 30 days for failed runs; 7 days for successful runs
- Benchmark artifacts (raw k6 output, harness logs) - retained 14 days

Limitations and known caveats

- Sandbox infra cannot perfectly mimic multi-tenant noisy neighbor patterns present in production; use the multi-tenant stress harness in conjunction with the sim for large migrations.
- GPU kernel quantization regressions that only appear at very long context lengths (> 8k) may require specialized long-run tests outside the standard CI window.

Related resources

- gpu-driver-manifest (internal): canonical mapping of OS/kernel/CUDA/cuDNN combos
- infra-ci/scripts: repo housing tf-apply-sim.sh and run-sim.sh examples
- perf-team/benchmarks: canonical baseline traces and replay IDs

Appendix: sample verification script (pseudo)

```bash
# verify-and-destroy.sh (high level)
set -e
BUCKET=s3://ci-telemetry
build=$BUILDKITE_BUILD_NUMBER
prom_snapshot=$(curl "http://prometheus/api/v1/snapshot?job=sim&build=${build}")
python tools/evaluate_thresholds.py --snapshot "$prom_snapshot" --baseline latest_green || {
  echo "Thresholds failed; preserving state for investigation"
  # keep state around and notify owners
  scripts/preserve-state.sh $build
  exit 1
}
# success path
scripts/tf-destroy.sh --state-key ci/${build}/tfstate
```

Contact and escalation

For questions about the harness, contact infra/gpu-platform. When a scaffolded run fails and the issue appears to be a runtime or kernel problem, include perf@redwood and runtime-eng in the Slack thread and create an investigation ticket with the build id, snapshot links, and trace ids.

Changelog

- 2024-11-05: Initial draft (Priya Natarajan)
- 2025-02-14: Added delta-from-baseline gating and retention policy updates (Marta Ruiz)

- If smoke checks fail => immediate fail, skip simulation, and tear down if teardown policy allows.

Failure investigation checklist (quick triage)

1. Capture CPU/GPU profiles from the nodes (nvidia-smi stats, pid memory).
2. Check kernel and driver versions: mismatch with expected (see /ops/gpu-driver-manifest) is common root cause.
3. Inspect network telemetry (observability dashboard: egress QPS heatmap, ENI metrics).
4. Validate Terraform diff: did the change reduce pool size or modify AZ distribution?
5. If a regression is reproducible locally, escalate to runtime/kernel team.

Rollback and state cleanup policy

- By default the sandbox environment is set to auto-destroy on success or failure after a configurable hold window (default 30 minutes).
- For failing runs flagged for investigation, terraform state is preserved under a build-specific key and the infra owner is paged.

Operational runbook snippets

- Manually re-run a simulation against a historical TF plan:
  1. Check out the PR branch and download the terraform state snapshot associated with the build.
  2. Recreate the sandbox (scripts/tf-apply-sim.sh --preserve-state).
  3. Run the harness with the recorded trace that previously failed: scripts/run-sim.sh --replay trace-id.

- If an infra change requires immediate production roll (emergency):
  1. Ensure runbook owner signoff.
  2. Apply change to a single small region using the same overlay but with production credentials.
  3. Run a scaled-down sim for 10 minutes and monitor.
  4. Roll forward only if no regression; otherwise revert using the last-known-good module version.

Telemetry and artifacts retained per run

- Prometheus snapshot (Promtool export) - retained 7 days
- Trace dump (Jaeger) - retained 14 days
- Terraform state snapshot - retained 30 days for failed runs; 7 days for successful runs
- Benchmark artifacts (raw k6 output, harness logs) - retained 14 days

Limitations and known caveats

- Sandbox infra cannot perfectly mimic multi-tenant noisy neighbor patterns present in production; use the multi-tenant stress harness in conjunction with the sim for large migrations.
- GPU kernel quantization regressions that only appear at very long context lengths (> 8k) may require specialized long-run tests outside the standard CI window.

Related resources

- gpu-driver-manifest (internal): canonical mapping of OS/kernel/CUDA/cuDNN combos
- infra-ci/scripts: repo housing tf-apply-sim.sh and run-sim.sh examples
- perf-team/benchmarks: canonical baseline traces and replay IDs

Appendix: sample verification script (pseudo)

```bash
# verify-and-destroy.sh (high level)
set -e
BUCKET=s3://ci-telemetry
build=$BUILDKITE_BUILD_NUMBER
prom_snapshot=$(curl "http://prometheus/api/v1/snapshot?job=sim&build=${build}")
python tools/evaluate_thresholds.py --snapshot "$prom_snapshot" --baseline latest_green || {
  echo "Thresholds failed; preserving state for investigation"
  # keep state around and notify owners
  scripts/preserve-state.sh $build
  exit 1
}
# success path
scripts/tf-destroy.sh --state-key ci/${build}/tfstate
```

Contact and escalation

For questions about the harness, contact infra/gpu-platform. When a scaffolded run fails and the issue appears to be a runtime or kernel problem, include perf@redwood and runtime-eng in the Slack thread and create an investigation ticket with the build id, snapshot links, and trace ids.

Changelog

- 2024-11-05: Initial draft (Priya Natarajan)
- 2025-02-14: Added delta-from-baseline gating and retention policy updates (Marta Ruiz)
text:
Summary

This playbook documents the recommended Terraform validation harness used to safely change GPU pool topology (instance types, AZ placement, placement groups, taints/tolerations) by running an automated, CI-driven simulation of representative inference traffic against a temporary environment. The goal is to detect capacity, latency, and egress regressions before Terraform changes reach production gates.

Scope

- Applies to Terraform changes that modify: GPU pool sizes, instance flavors, network egress routes, subnets, placement groups, or tenant QoS tags.
- Intended for use in the main infrastructure CI pipeline (Buildkite) and also as a manual preflight for large migration windows.

Why we run simulated traffic during TF validation

Terraform plan/validate and policy-as-code checks catch many problems (iam, security, drift). However, changes that affect topology can cause emergent behavior only visible under realistic load: KV cache warmup time, network egress hot spots, host-level forks, instance scheduling differences, or kernel-level performance regressions for quantized kernels. Running a short, deterministic traffic simulation in an isolated environment reduces risk and gives measurable pass/fail criteria tied to SLOs.

Ownership and roles

- Owner: infra/gpu-platform (pager: #infra-gpu on Slack)
- CI pipeline owner: infra-ci-team
- Reviewer(s): Perf team for benchmark definitions; Security for service identity changes.

Prerequisites

1) Feature branch containing Terraform changes targeting the gpu-allocator module.
2) CI credentials: a service account with limited create/delete rights in the sandbox AWS/GCP account. The account must be constrained to the sandbox project and tagged appropriately.
3) Canary image bundles must be published to the build artifact registry (image tag ref used by the simulation harness).
4) Telemetry exporters enabled in the test environment: metrics (Prometheus push/gateway), traces (Jaeger), and logs (ELK/Cloud Logging).

High-level flow

1. Developer opens PR with Terraform changes.
2. CI runs terraform fmt/validate, policy checks (OPA/Conftest).
3. CI creates a short-lived sandbox environment by applying a curated Terraform overlay (isolated VPC, dedicated subnets, ephemeral GPU nodes).
4. Once infra is healthy and node agents report ready, CI deploys the simulation harness and a minimal control plane (service discovery + routing).
5. CI kicks off a deterministic workload generator (see workload profiles below) for 6–20 minutes.
6. Telemetry is collected and evaluated against thresholds. If thresholds are exceeded, the run is marked failed and the TF change is blocked.

Workload profiles (examples)

- baseline-infer: 200 rps, short prompts (avg 48 tokens), 95th latency expected < 150ms, KV hit ratio 35%
- bursty-chat: 50 rps with Poisson bursts up to 400rps for 30s windows, avg seq length 256, tail P99 < 2.2s
- long-context-eval: 8 rps, sequence length 2048, measures KV growth and memory pressure

Configuration table (thresholds used by CI guard)

| metric | acceptable threshold | failure condition |
|---|---:|---|
| 95th response latency (baseline-infer) | < 150 ms | > 180 ms for 3+ sample windows |
| P99 latency (bursty-chat) | < 2.2 s | > 3.0 s for > 10% of samples |
| GPU util (avg across pool) | 25% - 85% | sustained > 95% for 2+ minutes or < 5% for 3+ mins |
| KV cache miss rate | < 20% | > 40% |
| Packet drops (instance NIC) | < 0.1% | > 1% |
| Egress errors (5xx from proxy) | < 0.5% | > 2% |

Note: CI evaluates both absolute thresholds and delta-from-baseline (the baseline is the last green canonical run for the same infra-module version). An increase > 20% relative to baseline triggers an investigator review even if absolute thresholds are still within limits.

Terraform overlay pattern

We use a small overlay module that reuses the same tf-modules as production but isolates resources: unique VPC, namespacing tags, and a shortened lifetime. Example usage within the pipeline:

```bash
# pipeline step (pseudocode)
export TF_VAR_prefix=ci-sim-${BUILDKITE_BUILD_NUMBER}
terraform init -backend-config="bucket=ci-terraform-state" \
  -backend-config="key=ci/${BUILDKITE_BUILD_NUMBER}/tfstate"
terraform apply -auto-approve -var-file=overlays/sim.vars.tfvars
```

Policy gates (pre-apply and post-apply)

- Pre-apply: OPA checks for forbidden instance types, missing tenant tags, and network exposure.
- Post-apply: Smoke tests (SSH to nodes, kube node ready, NVidia driver loaded), and an automated kernel/cuda version detector that fails if the new image/container runtime loses required drivers.

Simulation harness implementation notes

- Harness is a containerized service (go/python) that drives traffic using a combination of k6 for synthetic HTTP loads and a lightweight client that exercises the actual inference path (auth, routing, streaming).
- Test orchestration is implemented as a Buildkite step that: deploys harness, configures the target load profile, and pulls telemetry snapshots into a verification step.
- Harness supports deterministic replay from a recorded trace to ensure reproducibility across runs.

CI pipeline example (Buildkite YAML excerpt)

```yaml
steps:
  - label: ":terraform: TF validate + OPA"
    command: scripts/tf-validate.sh
  - wait: ~
  - label: ":rocket: Create sandbox infra"
    command: scripts/tf-apply-sim.sh
    timeout_in_minutes: 30
  - label: ":zap: Run sim traffic"
    command: scripts/run-sim.sh --profile baseline-infer
    timeout_in_minutes: 20
  - label: ":mag: Verify telemetry & teardown"
    command: scripts/verify-and-destroy.sh
    timeout_in_minutes: 10
    parallelism: 1
```

Pass/fail decision logic

- If any absolute threshold fails => fail the CI job, keep state for investigation (do not automatically destroy until reviewed unless configured).
- If delta-from-baseline > 20% => mark job for manual review by perf/infra owner.
- If smoke checks fail => immediate fail, skip simulation, and tear down if teardown policy allows.

Failure investigation checklist (quick triage)

1. Capture CPU/GPU profiles from the nodes (nvidia-smi stats, pid memory).
2. Check kernel and driver versions: mismatch with expected (see /ops/gpu-driver-manifest) is common root cause.
3. Inspect network telemetry (observability dashboard: egress QPS heatmap, ENI metrics).
4. Validate Terraform diff: did the change reduce pool size or modify AZ distribution?
5. If a regression is reproducible locally, escalate to runtime/kernel team.

Rollback and state cleanup policy

- By default the sandbox environment is set to auto-destroy on success or failure after a configurable hold window (default 30 minutes).
- For failing runs flagged for investigation, terraform state is preserved under a build-specific key and the infra owner is paged.

Operational runbook snippets

- Manually re-run a simulation against a historical TF plan:
  1. Check out the PR branch and download the terraform state snapshot associated with the build.
  2. Recreate the sandbox (scripts/tf-apply-sim.sh --preserve-state).
  3. Run the harness with the recorded trace that previously failed: scripts/run-sim.sh --replay trace-id.

- If an infra change requires immediate production roll (emergency):
  1. Ensure runbook owner signoff.
  2. Apply change to a single small region using the same overlay but with production credentials.
  3. Run a scaled-down sim for 10 minutes and monitor.
  4. Roll forward only if no regression; otherwise revert using the last-known-good module version.

Telemetry and artifacts retained per run

- Prometheus snapshot (Promtool export) - retained 7 days
- Trace dump (Jaeger) - retained 14 days
- Terraform state snapshot - retained 30 days for failed runs; 7 days for successful runs
- Benchmark artifacts (raw k6 output, harness logs) - retained 14 days

Limitations and known caveats

- Sandbox infra cannot perfectly mimic multi-tenant noisy neighbor patterns present in production; use the multi-tenant stress harness in conjunction with the sim for large migrations.
- GPU kernel quantization regressions that only appear at very long context lengths (> 8k) may require specialized long-run tests outside the standard CI window.

Related resources

- gpu-driver-manifest (internal): canonical mapping of OS/kernel/CUDA/cuDNN combos
- infra-ci/scripts: repo housing tf-apply-sim.sh and run-sim.sh examples
- perf-team/benchmarks: canonical baseline traces and replay IDs

Appendix: sample verification script (pseudo)

```bash
# verify-and-destroy.sh (high level)
set -e
BUCKET=s3://ci-telemetry
build=$BUILDKITE_BUILD_NUMBER
prom_snapshot=$(curl "http://prometheus/api/v1/snapshot?job=sim&build=${build}")
python tools/evaluate_thresholds.py --snapshot "$prom_snapshot" --baseline latest_green || {
  echo "Thresholds failed; preserving state for investigation"
  # keep state around and notify owners
  scripts/preserve-state.sh $build
  exit 1
}
# success path
scripts/tf-destroy.sh --state-key ci/${build}/tfstate
```

Contact and escalation

For questions about the harness, contact infra/gpu-platform. When a scaffolded run fails and the issue appears to be a runtime or kernel problem, include perf@redwood and runtime-eng in the Slack thread and create an investigation ticket with the build id, snapshot links, and trace ids.

- 2025-02-14: Added delta-from-baseline gating and retention policy updates (Marta Ruiz)

source.title: Activation Signals & Churn-Mitigation Runway: Early-Warning Playbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.summary: Operational playbook for detecting customer activation failures and near-term churn risk, automating alerts, running deterministic remediation steps, and standardizing outreach and QBR metrics for the first 90 days and ongoing retention efforts.
source.body:
Purpose
-------
This page codifies a repeatable, measurable process to catch customers who are failing to activate or are showing early signs of churn. It combines instrumentation, automated runbook actions, human escalation, and customer-facing outreach templates so CSMs and support can react within the first 72 hours and through the 90-day handback.

Scope
-----
Applies to: all trial and self-serve customers and newly onboarded enterprise accounts (first 90 days after initial sign-up or first production API call). Excludes legacy customers with custom contracts on >6 months cadence; those follow enterprise playbooks in enterprise-onboarding.

Definitions and Key Terms
-------------------------
- Activation window: first 30 days after account creation (primary), observed through 90 days for retention patterns.
- Active API session: any authenticated request that results in a generation/embedding response (excluding health-check-only pings).
- Primary signals: a prioritized set of telemetry used to infer activation and churn risk (see Signals table).
- CSM: Customer Success Manager assigned to the account.
- MTL: Mean time to live (internal shorthand used only for SLA windows in runbooks).

Signals and Thresholds (early-warning map)
-----------------------------------------
The platform emits signal categories that are mapped to severity levels. Severity determines automated actions and human involvement. Thresholds are conservative; adjust per-customer segment (ARR bucket): SMB / Mid-Market / Enterprise.

| Signal category | Concrete metric | Severity 1 (action) | Owner | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|
| Activation activity | < 5 API calls in first 7 days | True | CSM + Growth | Indicates likely non-starter for PLG trials |
| Feature usage | No embedding calls after enabling embeddings feature | True | CSM | Could be integration incomplete |
| Latency regressions | 95th percentile latency > 2s above baseline for 12 hours | True | Eng oncall + Support | Potential runtime regression or region capacity issue |
| Error rate | 5xx rate > 1% sustained > 30min | True | Support + Eng oncall | Triage for infra or model variant fallback |
| Cost surprises | Daily token spend > 5x projected run-rate | True | CSM + Finance | Potential misconfiguration or abuse |
| Quality regressions | Regression alerts from eval-suite on customer prompt set | True | Applied ML + CSM | May require model rollback or prompt adjustments |

Instrumentation and Example Queries
----------------------------------
We maintain shared dashboards and alerts in Grafana/Datadog + BigQuery for retrospective signals. Below are canonical queries (adapt to dataset names).

Sample BigQuery: 7-day activation check
```
SELECT customer_id,
  COUNTIF(event_type='api_request') AS api_calls_7d,
  COUNT(DISTINCT DATE(event_ts)) AS active_days_7d
FROM `redwood-prod.telemetry.events`
WHERE customer_id = @customer_id
  AND event_ts >= TIMESTAMP_SUB(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(), INTERVAL 7 DAY)
GROUP BY customer_id;
```

PromQL: 95p latency spike (Grafana alert)
```
max_over_time(histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, route))[12h:5m]) > (baseline_p95 + 2)
```

Slack alert example (format)
- channel: #cs-alerts
- message: "[ALERT] customer={{customer_id}} 95p latency spike: {{value}}ms; baseline={{baseline}}ms — CSM={{csm}}; runbook: <link>"

Automated Playbook (0–72 hour automation)
-----------------------------------------
1) Trigger: any Severity 1 signal.
2) Automated actions (system) — run within 5 minutes of alert:
   - Create a ticket in Zendesk: autotag = ew-<signal>-<customer_id>.
   - Post structured alert to #cs-alerts with runbook link and owners.
   - If signal = error-rate OR latency: automatically route traffic to fallback model variant (if available) and enable short-term caching TTL to mitigate volume.
3) System waits 30 minutes to observe whether automated mitigation reduces signal. If signal resolves, ticket annotated with automated-mitigated=true and CSM receives a summary email.
4) If signal persists after 30 minutes, escalate to human steps below.

Human Response Steps (escalation ladder)
--------------------------------------
1) First responder: CSM (primary) initiates customer outreach and opens a triage call if customer is impacted. Use the "Initial check-in" email template below. Within 60 minutes: confirm receipt in Zendesk and update Slack thread.
2) If impact = platform (latency/error): Support (Tier 2) owns technical triage; engage Eng oncall if degradation continues > 30 minutes.
3) If impact = integration or misconfiguration: CSM/Solutions Eng run guided troubleshooting (see Integration checklist).
4) If customer financial exposure > $5k/day or SLA breach likely: Immediately escalate to Customer Success Lead and Sales Engineering for executive comms.

Escalation Matrix (contact window and timeout)
---------------------------------------------
| Escalation step | Role | Contact method | Timeout |
|---|---|---|---:|
| 1 | CSM | Slack + email | 60 min |
| 2 | Support Tier 2 | Pager/Slack | 30 min |
| 3 | Eng oncall | Pager + call bridge | 15 min |
| 4 | CS Lead / Sales Eng | Phone + executive email | 60 min |
| 5 | Legal / Finance (if billing risk) | Email + Ops channel | 4 hours |

Customer Outreach Templates (use and track)
------------------------------------------
Initial check-in (short—sent after automated mitigation or first responder acknowledges):
Subject: Quick check — we saw low activity / a problem on your Redwood account
Hi {{first_name}},

Wanted to check in: our systems detected low activity / an alert for your account ({{customer_id}}) that may affect your initial setup. We ran an automated mitigation and wanted to confirm whether you see degraded responses or errors. Can you reply with: (1) whether you attempted to run a request in the last hour, and (2) any error messages you see?

If you prefer, we can jump on a 15-minute troubleshooting call — here is a link to book: {{calendar_link}}

Thanks,
{{csm_name}}

Post-resolution (explain what happened + next steps):
Subject: Incident summary and next steps — {{incident_id}}\Hi {{first_name}},

Thanks for your patience. We identified {{root_cause}} and took {{actions}} to remediate. To prevent recurrence we will: 
- Action 1: {{permanent_fix}} (owner: ENG)
- Action 2: {{guidance}} (owner: CSM)

We recommend the following product changes for your integration: {{recommendations}}.

If you’d like, we can include this item on your QBR agenda so your team has visibility.

Guided troubleshooting checklist (for CSMs / Solutions Eng)
1) Confirm API key scopes and region configuration.
2) Check ingestion of customer config flags and feature toggles.
3) Reproduce the failure with a minimal request (include request/response examples).
4) If errors are 5xx, capture request_id and escalate to Support with logs and timing.
5) For perceived low-quality outputs, run the customer prompt set through the eval harness and attach results.

30/60/90-Day Activation & Retention Playbook (CSM cadence)
---------------------------------------------------------
- Day 0–7: Activate
  - Tasks: Welcome call, verify API keys, run reference sample requests, ensure first successful generation in sandbox.
  - KPI guardrails: >= 5 successful API calls and >= 3 active days in first 7 days.
- Day 8–30: Adopt
  - Tasks: Assist with embedding/indexing flow or fine-tune integration; ship 1 internal example that uses production tokens.
  - KPI guardrails: token usage >= projected onboarding plan; at least one quality check using eval harness.
- Day 31–90: Expand
  - Tasks: QBR prep, map success metrics, recommend Dedicated/Private options if predictable throughput needed.
  - KPI guardrails: 30-day retention > 60% for target segment; expansion activity (feature upsell or reserved capacity ask) reviewed by Sales.

QBR Metrics & Dashboard (standardized deck)
------------------------------------------
Include these sections in every QBR slide set for churn-risk customers and for accounts at renewal:
- Executive summary: status (Green/Amber/Red), ARR, product usage trends.
- Adoption metrics: DAU/WAU, avg tokens/day, top API routes used.
- Reliability metrics: uptime, error rate, p95 latency, incidents since last QBR.
- Cost & efficiency: cost per 1k tokens, unusual spend patterns.
- Quality: eval harness results on customer prompt set, customer-reported quality issues.
- Action plan: next 90-day objectives, owners, and measurable targets.

Retention Experiments & Hypotheses (R&D adjacent)
------------------------------------------------
We recommend small, measurable experiments to reduce early churn. Example hypotheses and experiment designs: 
- Hypothesis: A 15-minute onboarding call within the first 48 hours increases 30-day activation by 25%. Experiment: randomized control group on new signups for 8 weeks.
- Hypothesis: Automated onboarding nudges (sample code snippets tailored to detected SDK language) reduce time-to-first-success by 40%. Experiment: A/B test within trial bucket.

Postmortem & Continuous Improvement
----------------------------------
- After any Severity 1 event affecting an active customer, complete a 1-pager postmortem: timeline, root cause, fixes, preventative actions, and owner for each action. Attach to the Zendesk ticket and QA during the weekly CS Ops review.
- Quarterly: review activation thresholds by segment; update detection logic if false positive rate > 10% for a given signal.

Ownership and Review Cadence
---------------------------
- Playbook owner: Head of Customer Success (Priya Natarajan).
- Reviewers (operational): Support Lead (Lucas Chen), Applied ML (Maya Patel), Platform SRE (Elena Rodriguez).
- Review cadence: monthly for the first 6 months after rollout, then quarterly.

Appendix A — Known integration gotchas (quick references)
-------------------------------------------------------
- Region mismatch: customer keys scoped to eu-west but calls hitting us-east leading to silent failures. Check key region claim in token metadata.
- Unbounded streaming: customers who forget to set max_tokens or streaming chunk size can incur runaway spend; recommend SDK defaults in customer onboarding templates.
- Cache TTL pitfalls: setting prefix cache TTL too long may mask upstream model regressions; prefer short TTLs during active troubleshooting.

Appendix B — Links and runbook artifacts
----------------------------------------
- Shared dashboards: Grafana:LinkToActivationDashboards (internal)
- Runbooks: /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/incident-triage-latency.json
- Ticket template: Support Ticket Template (Zendesk) link

Change log
----------
- 2024-10-03: Created by Priya Natarajan (initial draft).
- 2024-12-14: Added automated mitigation steps and Slack templates (reviewer: Lucas Chen).
- 2025-02-12: Updated thresholds for latency and error-rate; added QBR metric standardization (reviewer: Maya Patel).

source.title: Just-in-Time Privilege Activation and Audit Evidence Playbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Summary:
This playbook defines Redwood Inference's standard for just-in-time (JIT) privilege activation for privileged roles, the expected audit evidence produced by activation events, operational controls, and the investigation workflow for suspected misuse. It ties RBAC controls to audit logging, data residency/retention requirements, secure SDLC touchpoints, and risk exception handling.
source.content_2:
Purpose:
Provide a repeatable, auditable, low-risk pattern that enables engineers and on-call responders to obtain elevated privileges only when necessary, for a limited time, with end-to-end evidence suitable for internal review and external audits (SOC2/ISO/ISO27001).
source.content_3:
Scope:
Applies to: all Redwood production and customer-facing environments, admin consoles, database access, deployment pipelines, and cloud provider consoles. This playbook covers activation, approval, monitoring, logging, retention, post-activation review, and incident investigation for ephemeral privilege sessions.
source.content_4:
Definitions:
- JIT activation: temporary elevation of access granted on-demand with a limited lifespan and recorded approval.
- Privileged role: any role that allows modification of production configuration, access to customer data, or control of serving runtime (examples: prod-admin, infra-deploy, db-admin).
- Activation evidence: the set of logs and artifacts that prove who requested and approved access, when it was active, and what actions were performed.
source.content_5:
Roles and Responsibilities:
- Requester: person who requests JIT access; must provide business justification and duration.
- Approver: typically an on-call lead, manager, or automated policy engine; must validate necessity and MFA.
- Audit owner (Security): verifies evidence integrity and retention.
- SRE/Platform owner: configures JIT tooling, session proxies, and automated revocation.
- Compliance: confirms evidence meets regulatory requirements.
source.content_6:
Operational Flow (high level):
1) Request: Requester opens a JIT request via dashboard or CLI, including resource, role, justification, and desired TTL.
2) Policy evaluation: System checks risk policies (role sensitivity, geographic constraints, time-of-day, recent activity, open incidents).
3) Approval: Approver approves via console or OPA-backed automated rule. Approval requires the approver to authenticate with MFA.
4) Activation: Short-lived credentials are issued (OAuth ephemeral token / bastion session) and recorded with a unique session_id.
5) Monitoring: All session activity is proxied through audit collectors; anomaly detectors run in real time.
6) Expiry/Revoke: Session auto-expires at TTL or is manually revoked.
7) Post-review: Requester and approver must add a post-activity verification for critical actions within 48 hours.
8) Evidence assembly: Audit owner collates logs, approvals, and screenshots (if captured) for retention and potential audits.
source.content_7:
JIT Configuration Recommendations:
- Default TTL: 60 minutes for prod-admin, 15 minutes for database write roles, adjustable per role with a maximum of 8 hours under an approved exception.
- Minimum approval: one approver for low-risk roles, two independent approvers for high-risk roles (high-risk defined in role sensitivity matrix).
- MFA required for requester and approver for all activations.
- IP and geofencing: disallow activations from high-risk geographies unless emergency flag is set and recorded.
- Session isolation: route privilege sessions through a managed bastion/proxy that strips long-lived credentials and emits structured audit events.
- Immutable session_id: every activation must generate a UUID session_id attached to all downstream logs.
source.content_8:
Audit and Evidence Requirements (must be produced for every activation):
- Activation record: {timestamp, requester_id, requester_email, role, resource_scope, justification, approver_id, approval_method, session_id, requested_ttl, granted_ttl, ip}.
- Authentication proof: SSO assertion ID, MFA method (e.g., TOTP/hardware token), and timestamp of successful MFA.
- Session log stream: ordered events with timestamp, user_token_hash, session_id, action, resource, outcome, latency, client_ip.
- Command capture (for sensitive roles): command text or API call payloads (redacted for secrets) with request/response ids.
- KMS/HSM audit correlation: for operations that touch keys, include key_operation_id and key_material_handle.
- Artifact snapshot: when relevant, include deployment IDs, commit SHAs, and runbook link referenced during activation.
Retention and storage:
- Privileged activation metadata: retain for 3 years.
- Full session logs and command captures: retain for 2 years for production systems.
- Aggregated metrics and anomaly alerts: retain 5 years for trend analysis.
- Storage must respect customer data residency labels; if activation touched customer data in a region with residency constraints, evidence must be stored in that region or with an approved mirrored architecture.
Evidence integrity:
- All audit artifacts must be hashed (SHA-256) and checksum stored in an append-only ledger (e.g., internal write-once blob or external compliance service).
- Access to audit evidence is RBAC-restricted and requires separate approval for extraction.
source.content_9:
Example Structured Audit Schema (CSV/JSON friendly):
timestamp,session_id,requester_id,approver_id,role,resource,action,request_payload_id,response_code,client_ip,mfa_verified,approved_at,activated_at,revoked_at,duration_seconds,notes
2025-02-11T14:22:09Z,8f3a2b...,u123,e981,db-admin,prod-db01,ALTER TABLE,payload-777,200,34.66.10.3,true,2025-02-11T14:22:11Z,2025-02-11T14:22:15Z,2025-02-11T15:22:15Z,3600,"Schema patch for incident #INC-4521"
source.content_10:
Investigation and Incident Response for Suspected Misuse:
Detection triggers:
- Activation outside normal hours combined with large data egress.
- Rapid sequence of privilege escalations in the same session.
- Anomalous IP or geolocation change mid-session.
- Failed approval chains or missing MFA assertions.
Immediate steps:
1) Containment: Security/SRE may immediately revoke the session_id and freeze related accounts.
2) Evidence preservation: Mark all related audit artifacts as high-priority for preservation and snapshot the append-only ledger.
3) Triage: Assign incident severity using the standard incident taxonomy.
4) Forensic capture: Export session logs, command captures, KMS audit correlation records, and network flow metadata (pcap/flow logs where permitted).
5) Notification: Inform legal, compliance, and the affected product owners. Follow the escalation process in /confluence/security-and-compliance/incident-response-security/incident-process.
6) Post-incident review: Security runs a root cause analysis, documents timeline, and issues a remediation plan including role changes, policy updates, and training.
Evidence for investigations must not be deleted until legal and compliance sign-off, even if it exceeds standard retention windows.
For low-confidence anomalies, use rapid review: requestor and approver provide an explicit post-activation checklist within 48 hours. Lack of response escalates to an automatic audit review.
source.content_11:
Exception Handling and Risk Acceptance:
- Short-term exceptions: allow TTL extensions or single-approver approvals with documented business justification and a maximum 24-hour effective window. Record these exceptions in the control exception register.
- Long-term exceptions: must be reviewed quarterly by Security and Compliance and require compensating controls (continuous session recording, periodic attestation, reduced blast radius).
- Approval matrix: all exceptions above 8 hours require VP-level approval and evidence of alternative mitigation.
source.content_12:
Integration with Secure SDLC and Change Controls:
- Any code or infra change enacted under a JIT session that modifies deployment pipelines, IaC, or model serving config must reference a change ticket/PR ID in the activation justification.
- Gate: Automated CI must perform an after-action verification: build provenance check against commit SHA and a policy review that ensures no backdoors or credential leaks were introduced.
- Configuration drift: schedule a reconciliation job to run within 24 hours of any privileged deployment to detect unexpected drift and auto-rotate secrets if drift is detected.
source.content_13:
Operational Tooling and Implementation Patterns:
- Recommended stack: SSO provider (OIDC/SAML) + Privileged Access Manager (PAM) for approval UI + ephemeral token broker + centralized audit sink (clickhouse/ELK/BigQuery) + ledger service for checksums.
- Use policy-as-code (OPA/rego) to encode sensitivity matrix, TTLs, and automated approver rules.
- Proxy sessions through a managed bastion that injects session_id and strips long-lived creds.
- Real-time anomaly detection: use streaming analytics to flag abnormal command patterns and trigger immediate revocation if thresholds are breached.
source.content_14:
Sample Splunk/Kusto queries (adjust fields to your schema):
- Find activations older than 1 hour with no post-review:
  Splunk: index=audits event_type=jitaction activated_at<relative_time(now(), "-1h") AND post_review_status!=completed | table session_id, requester_id, activated_at
  Kusto: AuditEvents | where EventType == "JITActivate" and activated_at < ago(1h) and PostReviewStatus != "completed" | project session_id, requester_id, activated_at
- List commands run in a session:
  Splunk: index=session_logs session_id=8f3a2b* | table _time, user, command, outcome
  Kusto: SessionLogs | where session_id == "8f3a2b..." | project TimeGenerated, User, CommandText, Outcome
- Aggregate activations by approver to detect approval concentration:
  Kusto: AuditEvents | where EventType=="JITApprove" | summarize count() by approver_id | order by count_ desc
source.content_15:
Compliance and Audit Checklist (pre-audit):
- Can the control produce: activation records, MFA assertions, session logs, command captures, KMS correlation, and ledger checksums?
- Are retention policies applied and verified for the relevant jurisdiction?
- Do exceptions have documented approvals and compensating controls?
- Are all approvers and requesters in the HR directory (SCIM) and their attributes captured?
- Has the evidence ledger been signed/anchored to an external compliance service in the last 90 days?
source.content_16:
Appendix — Quick CLI examples:
- Request JIT via CLI (example):
  redwood-jit request --role prod-admin --resource arn:aws:rds:us-east-1:123:db:prod-db01 --ttl 60m --reason "Investigate latency spike INC-4521"
- Approve via CLI (example):
  redwood-jit approve --request-id r-9a8b --approver $(whoami) --mfa-code 987654
- Revoke session:
  redwood-jit revoke --session-id 8f3a2b... --reason "Suspicious activity detected"
source.content_17:
Contacts and Ownership:
- Policy owner: Security (security@redwood.ai) — Priya Natarajan.
- Audit owner: Compliance (compliance@redwood.ai) — Sofia Martinez.
- SRE owner: Platform (platform-sre@redwood.ai) — Ethan Park.
- Legal escalation: legal@redwood.ai.
- For support: open a ticket in the internal ServiceNow queue titled 'JIT Access Support' and tag the Platform-SRE on-call.
source.content_18:
Change History:
- 2024-11-05: Initial published version.
- 2025-01-10: Updated default TTLs and retention durations per compliance review.
- 2025-02-12: Added ledger integrity requirements and expanded incident response steps.

title: Just-in-Time Privilege Activation and Audit Evidence Playbook
preview:
Summary:
This playbook defines Redwood Inference's standard for just-in-time (JIT) privilege activation for privileged roles, the expected audit evidence produced by activation events, operational controls, and the investigation workflow for suspected misuse. It ties RBAC controls to audit logging, data residency/retention requirements, secure SDLC touchpoints, and risk exception handling.

source.title: Evidence Orchestration Playbook for Multi-Region Compliance
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook describes how Redwood orchestrates compliance evidence collection across RBAC/auth systems, audit logging, data residency boundaries, and retention policies. It defines owners, automation patterns, runbooks for pre-audit packaging and incident-led evidence collection, and the exception/risk workflow. It is intended for security engineers, platform owners, and compliance reviewers.

Scope
- Systems in-scope: auth-service (OIDC), rbac-manager (policy engine), audit-ingest (Kafka -> S3), audit-search (Elasticsearch), kms-yggdrasil (KMS/HSM integration), evidence-orchestrator (serverless job runner), compliance-indexer.
- Regions: us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1.
- Evidence types: audit records, configuration snapshots, access certification logs, key rotation proofs, data residency attestations, code-signing manifests, vulnerability scan reports.

Principles
- Least privilege: RBAC policies default to deny; roles grant only necessary perms.
- Immutability of audit records: original ingest stored immutably in write-once buckets with object-lock where available.
- Region-local evidence collection: evidence that demonstrates residency must be collected and tagged by region of origin.
- Traceability: every evidence item must include provenance metadata (collector, timestamp, selector query, signature).

Key SLOs and thresholds
- Audit ingestion SLA: 99.9% of audit events available in audit-search within 120s of generation.
- Evidence bundle creation SLA (for standard audit request): 95% completed within 24 hours.
- Retention policy adherence: 100% of regulated datasets have retention metadata and scheduled lifecycle rules configured.

Roles & RBAC model (summary)
- platform-admin: full access to infra and orchestration. Scope restricted to named admin principals and requires MFA + short-lived session.
- security-auditor: read-only access to audit-search, evidence-bucket indexes, and compliance dashboards. Cannot decrypt KMS-protected blobs.
- service-owner: manage service config, view service logs for owned namespace, request evidence bundles.
- read-only-operator: read-only access to monitoring and alerting dashboards.

Sample policy matrix (columns: resource, platform-admin, security-auditor, service-owner, read-only-operator)
| resource | platform-admin | security-auditor | service-owner | read-only-operator |
| auth-service:config | write | read | write (own namespace) | read |
| audit-search:index* | write | read | read (filtered) | read |
| evidence-bucket/* | write | list/read (metadata only) | put (evidence) | - |

RBAC enforcement patterns
1) Authorization decisions are calculated by rbac-manager using role-claims + scope tags.
2) Short-lived tokens issued by auth-service (max TTL 1 hour for privileged roles).
3) Privileged workflows require ticketing pre-approval (Jira project: RISK-EX) and recorded attestation.

Audit logging architecture and best practices
- Pipeline: services -> structured JSON events -> Kafka topic audit-events -> audit-ingest consumer -> encrypted S3 bucket (region-local) -> audit-search indexer (read-only replicas for analysis).
- Event schema: timestamp, service, host, trace_id, user_id, action, resource, outcome, region, collector_id.
- Retention tiers:
  - Hot index (audit-search): 90 days (fast queries)
  - Warm cold S3: 7 years (regulatory default)
  - Archive (glacier/deep archive): 10+ years where required
- Tamper-evidence: objects stored with checksums and periodically attested by evidence-indexer.

Data residency & retention mapping
- Default storage per region: events generated in region X are stored in S3 bucket redwood-audit-<region> with object-lock enabled. Cross-region replication is only enabled for dedicated enterprise customers that opt-in.
- Residency table (plain text):
  - US customers: us-east-1 primary. Retention: standard 7 years.
  - EU customers: eu-west-1 primary. Retention: 7 years (or per-customer contract).
  - APAC customers: ap-southeast-1 primary. Retention: 7 years.
- Data subject requests: service-owner to open DSR runbook (link: https://redwood.internal/dsr-runbook) — evidence-orchestrator will locate and extract records for the requested scope.

Secure SDLC integration points for evidence
- CI/CD: every release pipeline (build, test, deploy) must publish a build manifest and code-signature to evidence-bucket:/build-manifests/<commit>.json.
- SBOMs: generated for each build and linked to evidence manifest.
- SCA and container vulnerability scans: results uploaded to evidence-bucket and linked via compliance-indexer.
- Required gates: unit tests, security-manual-review (for privileged changes), dependency-audit-stamp.

Incident response (security) evidence flows
- Low-severity incident: collector creates an incident evidence bundle using evidence-orchestrator --incident-id INC-<id>. The bundle includes affected service logs (90-day window), RBAC change events, and deployment manifests.
- High-severity incident (data exfil): immediate snapshot policy triggers: snapshot config state, active sessions dump (redacted), audit-logs (48h pre-incident), KMS access logs, and network flow metadata. Security lead must approve cross-region evidence replication.
- CLI example (incident bundle):
  redwood-cli evidence create-bundle --incident INC-2025-041 --services auth-service,api-gateway --range -48h:now --output s3://evidence-collection/INC-2025-041/

Compliance evidence orchestration (pre-audit runbook)
1) Audit intake: compliance team files audit-request ticket with scope, timeframe, and customer IDs.
2) Triage: security-eng on-call reviews scope and assigns evidence template (standard/regulatory/enterprise-specific).
3) Template-driven collection: evidence-orchestrator executes template jobs which run region-local queries against audit-search, pulls config snapshots from service-config-store, exports KMS rotation logs (kms-yggdrasil), and packages signed manifests.
4) Packaging: bundles are signed using the compliance KMS key and uploaded to evidence-bucket:/audit-bundles/<audit-id>.zip.
5) Review & redaction: security-auditor reviews bundle for PII and redacts per DSR policy before delivery.
6) Delivery: bundles delivered via pre-authorized SFTP or secure artifact registry with one-time access tokens.

Evidence manifest spec (example JSON as text)
{
  "audit_id": "AUD-2025-021",
  "collected_at": "2025-02-08T14:32:00Z",
  "region": "eu-west-1",
  "items": [
    {"type": "audit-events", "path": "s3://redwood-audit-eu-west-1/events/2025-02-08/part-0001.json.gz", "checksum": "sha256:..."},
    {"type": "config-snapshot", "path": "s3://evidence-bucket/configs/auth-service-2025-02-08.json", "signed_by": "build-pipeline-8f3"}
  ],
  "signature": "kms-sig:..."
}

Risk and exceptions handling
- When a requirement cannot be met (e.g., region-local archival not supported by a third-party integration), an exception must be recorded in Jira (project RISK-EX) with owner, compensating controls, and an expiration date (max 180 days).
- Exception approval matrix:
  1) Service-owner creates RFC with technical workaround and compensating controls.
  2) Security reviews and either approves or requests additional mitigations.
  3) Legal/Compliance approves for customer-facing/regulatory impact.
  4) Exec sign-off for high-risk (>90 days or >$100K monthly cost).
- Exception artifacts required: risk-assessment.pdf, mitigation-plan.md, compensating-controls checklist, monitoring queries, and evidence of implementation (links to dashboard screenshots or queries).

Operational runbook snippets
- Verify audit ingestion health:
  curl -s https://observability.internal/api/health/audit-ingest | jq
  Expected: {\"uptime\": >86400, \"avg-latency_ms\": <120000, \"error_rate\": <0.001 }
- Force a region-local evidence snapshot (manual trigger):
  aws lambda invoke --function-name evidence-orchestrator-trigger --payload '{\"template\":\"standard-audit-72h\", \"region\":\"eu-west-1\" }' /tmp/out.json

Appendices
- Links:
  - Compliance indexer runbook: https://redwood.internal/runbooks/compliance-indexer
  - Evidence CLI docs: https://redwood.internal/cli/evidence
  - DSR runbook: https://redwood.internal/dsr-runbook
- Contact list:
  - Security on-call: #security-oncall (pager)
  - Compliance owners: compliance@redwood.ai

Change history
- 2024-09-12: Created by Priya Natarajan (initial draft).
- 2024-11-05: Added incident snapshot policy and CLI examples (reviewer: Ethan Caldwell).
- 2025-02-10: Revised retention mapping for EU customers and redaction step clarified (reviewer: María López).

Notes and next steps
- Next: implement automated evidence attestations (PoC scheduled Q2 2025) and add per-customer retention override UI to Console.

    {"type": "config-snapshot", "path": "s3://evidence-bucket/configs/auth-service-2025-02-08.json", "signed_by": "build-pipeline-8f3"}
  ],
  "signature": "kms-sig:..."
}

Risk and exceptions handling
- When a requirement cannot be met (e.g., region-local archival not supported by a third-party integration), an exception must be recorded in Jira (project RISK-EX) with owner, compensating controls, and an expiration date (max 180 days).
- Exception approval matrix:
  1) Service-owner creates RFC with technical workaround and compensating controls.
  2) Security reviews and either approves or requests additional mitigations.
  3) Legal/Compliance approves for customer-facing/regulatory impact.
  4) Exec sign-off for high-risk (>90 days or >$100K monthly cost).
- Exception artifacts required: risk-assessment.pdf, mitigation-plan.md, compensating-controls checklist, monitoring queries, and evidence of implementation (links to dashboard screenshots or queries).

Operational runbook snippets
- Verify audit ingestion health:
  curl -s https://observability.internal/api/health/audit-ingest | jq
  Expected: {\"uptime\": >86400, \"avg-latency_ms\": <120000, \"error_rate\": <0.001 }
- Force a region-local evidence snapshot (manual trigger):
  aws lambda invoke --function-name evidence-orchestrator-trigger --payload '{\"template\":\"standard-audit-72h\", \"region\":\"eu-west-1\" }' /tmp/out.json

Appendices
- Links:
  - Compliance indexer runbook: https://redwood.internal/runbooks/compliance-indexer
  - Evidence CLI docs: https://redwood.internal/cli/evidence
  - DSR runbook: https://redwood.internal/dsr-runbook
- Contact list:
  - Security on-call: #security-oncall (pager)
  - Compliance owners: compliance@redwood.ai

Change history
- 2024-09-12: Created by Priya Natarajan (initial draft).
- 2024-11-05: Added incident snapshot policy and CLI examples (reviewer: Ethan Caldwell).
- 2025-02-10: Revised retention mapping for EU customers and redaction step clarified (reviewer: María López).

Notes and next steps
- Next: implement automated evidence attestations (PoC scheduled Q2 2025) and add per-customer retention override UI to Console.

source.title: Failure-domain-aware capacity scheduling and SLO-safe deployments
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview:
This playbook describes a failure-domain-aware approach to capacity scheduling and deployment gating designed to protect SLOs and preserve error budget during planned and unplanned failures. It combines quantitative headroom provisioning, progressive rollouts, and automated rollback/eviction rules that are failure-domain–sensitive (region / AZ / rack / GPU-pool / node). The goal is to keep user-visible SLOs stable while making predictable capacity changes for model serving and multi-tenant workloads.

Scope:
- Applies to all production inference pools managed by Redwood (hosted, dedicated, private control plane).
- Primary targets: text generation, chat, and embedding routes where p95 latency and availability SLOs are observable.
- Excludes: offline batch/ETL jobs (handled by batch capacity playbook).

Key goals:
- Ensure SLO preservation during single and multi-domain degradations.
- Automate safe deployments with failure-domain affinity and rollback triggers.
- Provide clear operator runbooks for emergency capacity actions and tenant prioritization.

Definitions and failure domains:
- Failure domain: a unit of correlated failure (examples: AZ, rack, host, GPU pool, model shard).
- Primary domains used in scheduling: region > AZ > rack-group > gpu-pool > node.
- Tenant priority: each tenant/service must be mapped to one of: critical (SLA-backed), standard, experimental. Priority informs eviction/soft-preemption.

SLOs and headroom mapping (example):
- Service SLO matrix (recommended mapping):

  | Route class     | SLO (availability) | Headroom target (across domains) | Emergency threshold (error budget burn) |
  |-----------------|--------------------|----------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
  | Critical (SLA)   | 99.95%             | 45% reserved across domains       | 5% in 1h                                  |
  | Standard         | 99.9%              | 30% reserved                      | 10% in 1h                                 |
  | Experimental     | 99.0%              | 10% reserved                      | 25% in 1h                                 |

Capacity scheduling principles:
1) Failure-aware headroom: always provision at least the mapped headroom distributed across at least 3 independent failure domains when possible.
2) Gradual draining: when updating or draining capacity, prefer draining within a single failure domain and re-balance traffic before evicting additional domains.
3) Priority-preserving eviction: eviction order honors tenant priority and pinned reservations.
4) Observability-driven adjustments: automatically add temporary capacity when burn rate or p95 latency breaches thresholds (see automated triggers).

Capacity calculation (operator quick formula):
- Inputs: baseline_qps, p95_tokens_per_request, target_utilization (e.g., 60%), model_latency_ms (p95), domain_capacity_per_gpu (tokens/sec).
- Estimate required_gpus = ceil( (baseline_qps * p95_tokens_per_request * model_latency_ms/1000) / (domain_capacity_per_gpu * target_utilization) )

Example: baseline_qps=200, p95_tokens=120, p95_latency=450ms, domain_capacity_per_gpu=2500 tokens/sec, target_util=0.6
- required_gpus = ceil((200*120*0.45)/(2500*0.6)) = ceil(10800/1500) = ceil(7.2) = 8 GPUs

Automated triggers and thresholds:
- Autoscale-up: triggered when rolling 10-minute p95 latency exceeds target by >15% OR sustained error-budget burn-rate > 3x for 30 minutes.
- Canary abort / rollback: abort if canary error budget burn > 2% in 15 minutes OR canary p95 latency exceeds safe margin by 25%.
- Cross-domain failover: if an entire AZ loses >= 40% of capacity, immediately route 50% of affected traffic to secondary AZs with priority-aware throttling.

Deployment guardrails (pre-flight checks):
- Tracklist to run before rollout (CI/CD hook):
  1. Verify canary model artifact matches exact model hash and quantization profile.
  2. Check global headroom >= headroom target for all priority classes.
  3. Ensure telemetry pipelines (p95 latency, error rate, token counters) are green for last 60m.
  4. Validate canary scheduling spans at least two failure domains (AZs/racks) to detect correlated failures.
  5. Confirm rollback image and config pinned and accessible.

Progressive rollout recipe (recommended):
1. Launch canary in 2 small pools across distinct failure domains (2–3% of traffic).
2. Monitor 10m and 1h windows for p95 latency, error budget burn, and CPU/GPU saturation.
3. If canary metrics stable, step to 10–20% across 3 domains over 30–60 minutes.
4. Final ramp to 100% in increments of 20% with 30–60m observation windows.
5. If any threshold breach occurs, trigger automated rollback (see triggers above) and run immediate mitigation runbook.

Runbook: immediate action for capacity loss in a single failure domain
1. Detect: Alert fires: "domain_capacity_drop >= 30%". Pager to oncall.
2. Triage (5 min): identify domain (AZ/rack) and affected pools; mark impacted tenants with priority.
3. Contain (10 min): route traffic away from domain using routing policy: set routing weight to 0 for domain and enable fast cross-domain routing.
4. Re-provision (15–30 min): if spare capacity exists, instantiate warm pools in alternate domains: rictl pool scale --pool <pool> --delta +N
5. Throttle (ongoing): apply tenant-priority throttles for non-critical tenants until headroom restored.
6. Post-incident: run capacity rebalancer and submit incident review within 48 hours.

Runbook: emergency rollback during a rollout
1. Immediately set rollout state: ABORT. Stop new traffic to canary.
2. Roll back to previous pinned model/config: rictl deploy rollback --service <svc> --to-tag <previous-tag>
3. If rollback fails, cut traffic to pre-specified safe model variant (fallback) and route to dedicated backup pool.
4. Notify product & support with templated message (see "Incident comms" below).

Telemetry and operational tooling requirements:
- Mandatory dashboards: per-route p95 latency, p99, error-rate, token/sec, GPU utilization per pool, headroom ratio by priority.
- Prometheus alerts (examples):
  - name: CapacityDomainDrop
    expr: (sum by(domain) (available_gpus{pool=~".*prod.*"}) / sum by(domain) (expected_gpus{pool=~".*prod.*"})) < 0.7
    for: 5m
  - name: CanarySloBreach
    expr: increase(error_budget_burn[15m]) > 0.02

- CLI examples:
  - rictl pool scale --pool text-gen-east-1a --delta +4 --why "emergency absorb after AZ loss"
  - rictl tenant throttle --tenant-id T-1234 --p=experimental --rate 0.5

Incident review & continuous improvement:
- Every capacity-impacting incident must include in the review:
  - timeline (synchronized UTC), root cause hypothesis, evidence (telemetry links), actions taken, capacity decisions and headroom status at time of incident.
  - Owner: the oncall who executed actions + service steward.
  - Delivery: publish within 72 hours to the confluence incident folder and tag the capacity playbook.

Appendix: example capacity allocation table (ASCII):
  +----------------+-----------+-----------+----------+-----------+
  | Domain         | Expected  | Available | Reserved | Headroom  |
  |                | GPUs      | GPUs      | (critical)| (percent) |
  +----------------+-----------+-----------+----------+-----------+
  | us-east-1a     | 50        | 40        | 10       | 40%       |
  | us-east-1b     | 50        | 48        | 12       | 42%       |
  | us-west-2a     | 40        | 38        | 8        | 36%       |
  +----------------+-----------+-----------+----------+-----------+

Owners and escalation paths:
- Capacity steward: Michael Chen (eng-sre) - primary for proactive scheduling and runway planning.
- Runbook executor / oncall: SRE rotation per schedule.
- Product escalation: Elena Rodriguez (product infra) for customer-impacting incidents.

Related pages and references:
- Capacity rehearsal drills and templates: ../reliability-initiatives/capacity-drills-and-slo-recovery-cadence-playbook-2027
- Canary metrics and evaluation templates: ../operational-tooling/slo-observability-pipeline-design-and-validation-2025

Change log:
- 2024-11-03: Created initial draft.
- 2025-02-10: Added automated triggers, expanded rollback recipe, clarified headroom mapping.

Contact: Priya Natarajan (pri@redwood.ai) — reach out for clarifications or to request an addendum to tenant priority mapping.
